content: Crossover designs have patients receive multiple treatments in sequence, allowing each patient to serve as their own control and reducing inter-patient variability.

title: What is the NDA process in drug approval?
content: NDA (New Drug Application) is the formal proposal submitted to the FDA for approval to market a new drug, containing all preclinical and clinical data.

title: What is the significance of Cmax and Tmax in PK analysis?
content: Cmax is the peak drug concentration achieved, and Tmax is the time to reach that peak. They describe the rate and extent of drug absorption.

title: What is the role of an Independent Ethics Committee (IEC)?
content: IECs review and approve research protocols, informed consent documents, and ensure the protection of human subjects throughout the clinical trial.

title: What is the significance of the therapeutic window?
content: The therapeutic window is the concentration range where a drug provides efficacy without unacceptable toxicity. Drugs with narrow windows require careful monitoring.

title: What is the difference between open-label and double-blind studies?
content: Open-label studies have no blinding, while double-blind studies neither patients nor investigators know which treatment is being administered, reducing bias.

title: What is the role of pharmacovigilance in drug development?
content: Pharmacovigilance continuously monitors drug safety after market approval, detecting and assessing adverse effects to ensure ongoing patient safety.

title: What is the significance of the clearance (CL) in pharmacokinetics?
content: Clearance is the volume of plasma completely cleared of drug per unit time, determining the maintenance dose needed to achieve steady-state concentrations.

title: What is the role of GCP (Good Clinical Practice) guidelines?
content: GCP provides international ethical and scientific quality standards for designing, conducting, recording, and reporting clinical trials involving human subjects.

title: What is the difference between bioequivalence and therapeutic equivalence?
content: Bioequivalence shows similar PK profiles, while therapeutic equivalence demonstrates comparable clinical efficacy and safety, which may require additional clinical data.

title: What is the significance of the steady-state concentration (Css)?
content: Steady-state is reached when drug input equals elimination, typically after 4-5 half-lives. It represents the equilibrium concentration during repeated dosing.

title: What is the role of an orphan drug designation?
content: Orphan drug designation provides incentives (tax credits, market exclusivity) for developing drugs to treat rare diseases affecting fewer than 200,000 people in the US.

title: What is the significance of the first-pass effect?
content: The first-pass effect refers to extensive metabolism of orally administered drugs in the gut wall and liver, reducing bioavailability before reaching systemic circulation.

title: What is the role of a protocol amendment in clinical trials?
content: Protocol amendments modify the trial design based on new information or operational needs, requiring IRB/IEC approval and sometimes patient re-consent.

title: What is the significance of the volume of distribution (Vd)?
content: Vd is a theoretical volume that relates the amount of drug in the body to the plasma concentration, indicating how widely the drug distributes into tissues.

title: What is the purpose of a safety run-in period?
content: A safety run-in exposes all patients to the study drug before randomization to identify those who cannot tolerate the treatment, improving the safety of the main trial.

title: What is the significance of the number needed to harm (NNH)?
content: NNH indicates how many patients need to be exposed to a treatment to cause one additional adverse event, quantifying the risk of adverse effects.

title: What is the role of a post-marketing surveillance study?
content: Post-marketing surveillance (Phase 4) monitors drug safety and efficacy in larger, more diverse populations after approval, detecting rare adverse effects.

title: What is the primary application of mass spectrometry in peptide analysis?
content: Mass spectrometry determines peptide molecular weight and can be used for sequencing through fragmentation patterns (MS/MS), identifying post-translational modifications.

title: What is the difference between MALDI-TOF and ESI mass spectrometry?
content: MALDI-TOF uses laser desorption/ionization with time-of-flight analysis, while ESI generates ions from solution through electrospray, often coupling with LC.

title: What is the role of NMR spectroscopy in peptide analysis?
content: NMR spectroscopy provides atomic-resolution information about peptide structure, dynamics, and interactions in solution, complementing X-ray crystallography.

title: What is the principle of reversed-phase HPLC?
content: Reversed-phase HPLC separates peptides based on hydrophobicity, using nonpolar stationary phases (C18) and polar mobile phases with organic modifiers.

title: What is the advantage of LC-MS/MS over HPLC-UV for peptide analysis?
content: LC-MS/MS combines liquid chromatography separation with tandem mass spectrometry, providing molecular weight, sequence, and modification information with high specificity.

title: What is the role of capillary electrophoresis (CE) in peptide analysis?
content: CE separates peptides based on charge-to-size ratio with high resolution and efficiency, requiring minimal sample volumes and providing complementary information to HPLC.

title: What is the significance of the charge state distribution in ESI-MS?
content: The charge state distribution in ESI-MS reflects the number of basic sites available for protonation, providing information about peptide size, conformation, and composition.

title: What is the role of collision-induced dissociation (CID) in MS/MS?
content: CID fragments peptide ions through collisions with inert gas, generating product ions that reveal amino acid sequence and modification sites.

title: What is the advantage of high-resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS)?
content: HRMS (like Orbitrap or TOF) provides mass accuracy <5 ppm, enabling determination of elemental composition and identification of unknown modifications.

title: What is the principle of size-exclusion chromatography (SEC)?
content: SEC separates molecules by size, with larger molecules eluting first as they cannot enter the porous stationary phase beads.

title: What is the significance of the b and y ions in peptide fragmentation?
content: b ions are N-terminal fragments and y ions are C-terminal fragments from peptide backbone cleavage. The mass differences between consecutive ions reveal the amino acid sequence.

title: What is the advantage of nano-LC over conventional HPLC for peptide analysis?
content: Nano-LC operates at very low flow rates (nL/min), providing higher sensitivity for mass spectrometry detection and requiring minimal sample amounts.

title: What is the role of the Trypsin digestion in proteomics?
content: Trypsin specifically cleaves after lysine and arginine residues, generating predictable peptide fragments for mass spectrometric analysis and protein identification.

title: What is the difference between MRM and SRM in mass spectrometry?
content: SRM (Selected Reaction Monitoring) monitors a single precursor-product transition, while MRM (Multiple Reaction Monitoring) monitors multiple transitions simultaneously.

title: What is the advantage of ion-mobility spectrometry (IMS) in peptide analysis?
content: IMS separates ions based on their shape and charge in the gas phase, providing an additional dimension of separation that improves coverage and reduces complexity.

title: What is the role of circular dichroism (CD) in peptide analysis?
content: CD spectroscopy measures differential absorption of left and right circularly polarized light, providing information about alpha-helix, beta-sheet, and random coil content.

title: What is the significance of the retention time in HPLC?
content: Retention time indicates how long a peptide interacts with the stationary phase, providing qualitative identification and reflecting peptide hydrophobicity.

title: What is the role of the mass analyzer in mass spectrometry?
What is the advantage of Tandem MS (MS/MS) over single MS?
content: MS/MS selects specific ions and fragments them, providing sequence information and enabling identification of post-translational modifications.

title: What is the significance of the charge density in capillary electrophoresis?
content: Charge density (charge-to-size ratio) determines electrophoretic mobility in CE, with higher charge density leading to faster migration towards the electrode.

title: What is the role of the detector in HPLC?
content: The detector (UV, fluorescence, or MS) measures peptide concentration as they elute from the column, generating the chromatogram for analysis.

title: What is the advantage of high-performance capillary electrophoresis (HPCE)?
content: HPCE provides very high resolution separations with nanoliter sample volumes, complementing HPLC for peptide analysis with different selectivity.

title: What is the significance of the precursor ion in MS/MS?
content: The precursor (parent) ion is the specific ion selected in the first mass analyzer for fragmentation in MS/MS experiments.

title: What is the role of isotopic labeling in quantitative proteomics?
content: Isotopic labeling (SILAC, iTRAQ, TMT) introduces mass tags that enable relative quantification of peptides/proteins across different samples.

title: What is the advantage of MALDI-TOF for peptide mass fingerprinting?
content: MALDI-TOF is ideal for peptide mass fingerprinting due to its speed, sensitivity, and ability to measure intact peptide masses accurately.

title: What is the significance of the duty cycle in mass spectrometry?
content: Duty cycle is the fraction of time the mass analyzer is actively analyzing ions. Higher duty cycle means more ions are detected, improving sensitivity.

title: What is the role of the electron transfer dissociation (ETD) in MS/MS?
content: ETD fragments peptides through electron transfer, preserving labile modifications like phosphorylation and glycosylation that are lost in CID.

title: What is the advantage of two-dimensional chromatography for peptide separation?
content: 2D chromatography (like strong cation exchange followed by reversed-phase) provides greater peak capacity by orthogonal separation mechanisms, resolving complex mixtures.

title: What is the significance of the signal-to-noise ratio in analytical chemistry?
content: Signal-to-noise ratio indicates how well the analytical signal can be distinguished from background noise, determining detection and quantification limits.

title: What is the role of the quadrupole in mass spectrometry?
content: Quadrupoles use oscillating electric fields to filter ions, allowing only ions with specific m/z ratios to pass through, acting as mass filters.

title: What is the advantage of using an Orbitrap mass analyzer?
content: Orbitrap analyzers provide high mass resolution (>100,000) and accuracy (<2 ppm), enabling confident identification of peptides and their modifications.

title: What is the significance of the peptide mapping in quality control?
content: Peptide mapping uses enzymatic digestion and LC-MS to confirm the amino acid sequence, identify modifications, and assess purity of therapeutic peptides.

title: What is molecular dynamics (MD) simulation used for in peptide design?
content: MD simulations model the physical movements of atoms over time, providing insights into peptide folding, flexibility, and interactions with targets.

title: What is molecular docking in computational drug design?
content: Molecular docking predicts how a peptide binds to its target protein, estimating binding pose, orientation, and affinity through scoring functions.

title: What is QSAR in drug design?
content: QSAR (Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship)建立 mathematical models relating chemical structure to biological activity for predicting properties of new compounds.

title: What is the advantage of de novo peptide design?
content: De novo design uses computational methods to create novel peptide sequences with specific binding, stability, or functional properties.

title: What is the role of force fields in molecular simulations?
content: Force fields (AMBER, CHARMM, OPLS) define mathematical functions that describe the potential energy of molecular systems based on bond lengths, angles, and interactions.

title: What is homology modeling in structural biology?
content: Homology modeling predicts protein 3D structure based on sequence similarity to proteins with known structures, using the template as a guide.

title: What is the significance of binding free energy calculations?
content: Binding free energy calculations (FEP, MM-PBSA) estimate the thermodynamic favorability of peptide binding to targets, guiding lead optimization.

title: What is the role of pharmacophore modeling?
content: Pharmacophore modeling identifies the spatial arrangement of essential features (H-bond donors/acceptors, charges, hydrophobic groups) required for biological activity.

title: What is the advantage of machine learning in peptide design?
content: Machine learning algorithms can identify patterns in large datasets to predict peptide properties, stability, binding affinity, and other characteristics.

title: What is the role of genetic algorithms in peptide optimization?
content: Genetic algorithms mimic natural selection to evolve peptide sequences, selecting and mutating sequences to optimize desired properties like binding affinity.

title: What is the significance of the PDB (Protein Data Bank)?
content: The PDB is a repository of experimentally determined 3D structures of proteins and nucleic acids, providing structural data for computational modeling.

title: What is the advantage of using Rosetta for peptide design?
content: Rosetta is a comprehensive software suite for protein structure prediction, design, and optimization, including peptide design and docking.

title: What is the role of molecular mechanics in computational chemistry?
content: Molecular mechanics uses classical physics and force fields to calculate molecular energies and optimize structures, providing efficient simulations of large systems.

title: What is the significance of the Ramachandran plot in computational modeling?
content: The Ramachandran plot shows allowed backbone dihedral angles, used to validate computed structures and identify sterically unfavorable conformations.

title: What is the role of solvation models in molecular simulations?
content: Solvation models (explicit water, implicit solvent) account for solvent effects on molecular structure, dynamics, and binding, crucial for accurate simulations.

title: What is the significance of the scoring function in molecular docking?
content: Scoring functions estimate binding affinity based on van der Waals, electrostatic, and desolvation interactions between receptor and ligand.

title: What is the advantage of coarse-grained simulations?
content: Coarse-grained models group atoms into larger units, reducing computational cost and enabling simulations of larger systems and longer timescales.

title: What is the role of deep learning in peptide design?
content: Deep learning models (neural networks, transformers) can learn patterns from peptide databases to predict properties and generate novel sequences with desired functions.

title: What is the significance of molecular descriptors in QSAR?
content: Molecular descriptors (topological, geometric, electronic) quantitatively represent chemical properties, enabling mathematical modeling of structure-activity relationships.

title: What is the advantage of virtual screening in drug discovery?
content: Virtual screening computationally evaluates large numbers of compounds against a target, prioritizing candidates for experimental testing and reducing costs.

title: What is the role of conformational sampling in peptide design?
content: Conformational sampling methods (Monte Carlo, molecular dynamics) explore the range of possible peptide conformations to identify biologically relevant structures.

title: What is the advantage of fragment-based drug design?
content: Fragment-based design starts with small, low-affinity fragments that bind to target sub-pockets, then links or grows them into potent leads.

title: What is the significance of the van der Waals radius in molecular modeling?
content: Van der Waals radii define the effective atomic sizes used to calculate steric clashes and van der Waals interactions in molecular simulations.

title: What is the role of evolutionary algorithms in peptide optimization?
content: Evolutionary algorithms use selection, crossover, and mutation operations to evolve peptide populations toward improved properties like binding or stability.

title: What is the significance of the MM-PBSA method?
content: MM-PBSA (Molecular Mechanics-Poisson-Boltzmann Surface Area) estimates binding free energies by combining molecular mechanics energies with continuum solvation models.

title: What is the advantage of using AlphaFold for peptide design?
content: AlphaFold provides highly accurate protein structure predictions that can serve as templates for peptide design, especially for understanding binding interfaces.

title: What is the role of water molecules in molecular docking?
content: Conserved water molecules can form bridging hydrogen bonds between peptide and protein, significantly affecting binding affinity and specificity.

title: What is the significance of the rotamer library in peptide design?
content: Rotamer libraries contain statistically preferred side-chain conformations (chi angles) used in structure prediction and design to sample realistic conformations.

title: What is the advantage of network pharmacology in peptide design?
content: Network pharmacology analyzes how peptides affect multiple targets and pathways, providing a systems-level understanding of therapeutic effects.

title: What is the significance of the energy minimization step?
content: Energy minimization adjusts atomic coordinates to reduce the potential energy of a molecular system, removing steric clashes and optimizing geometry.

title: What is the role of the binding site analysis in peptide design?
content: Binding site analysis identifies hot-spot residues, pocket shapes, and interaction patterns that guide the design of peptides with high affinity and specificity.

title: What is the significance of transferability in machine learning models for peptides?
content: Transferability refers to the ability of ML models trained on one set of peptides to generalize predictions to new, unseen sequences, which is critical for practical design.

title: What is the main advantage of peptide cyclization?
content: Cyclization constrains peptide conformation, reducing flexibility and protecting against exopeptidase degradation, thereby improving metabolic stability and bioavailability.

title: What is the effect of incorporating D-amino acids into peptides?
content: D-amino acids are not recognized by most proteases, making peptides containing them resistant to enzymatic degradation while often maintaining biological activity.

title: What is the primary benefit of PEGylation for peptide drugs?
content: PEGylation adds polyethylene glycol chains that increase hydrodynamic radius, reduce renal clearance, shield from proteases, and reduce immune recognition.

title: What is peptide stapling?
content: Stapling introduces hydrocarbon crosslinks between side chains (typically i, i+7 positions) to stabilize alpha-helical conformation, improving binding and cell penetration.

title: What is the significance of the half-life of therapeutic peptides?
content: Half-life determines how long a peptide remains active in the body, directly impacting dosing frequency, patient compliance, and therapeutic effectiveness.

title: What is the advantage of N-methylation of peptide bonds?
content: N-methylation blocks the NH group of peptide bonds, preventing protease recognition and reducing hydrogen bonding capacity, which can enhance oral bioavailability.

title: What is the role of beta-amino acids in peptide stability?
content: Beta-amino acids have an extra carbon atom in the backbone, making them unrecognizable to most proteases and conferring enhanced stability.

title: What is the advantage of disulfide bond engineering in peptides?
content: Engineered disulfide bonds constrain peptide conformation, reducing the entropy of the unfolded state and stabilizing the bioactive conformation.

title: What is the role of albumin-binding peptides in extending half-life?
content: Albumin-binding domains or moieties link peptides to serum albumin, taking advantage of albumin's long half-life (19 days) to extend peptide circulation time.

title: What is the advantage of glycosylation for peptide stability?
content: Glycosylation adds sugar moieties that can shield peptides from proteases, improve solubility, and reduce immune recognition.

title: What is the significance of retro-inverso peptides?
content: Retro-inverso peptides reverse the sequence direction and use D-amino acids, maintaining similar side chain topology while being resistant to proteolysis.

title: What is the advantage of lipidation for peptide stability?
content: Lipidation (fatty acid addition) can enhance albumin binding, promote membrane interaction, and improve oral bioavailability of peptides.

title: What is the role of the splice cyclization in peptide synthesis?
content: Lactam (amide) cyclization forms a head-to-tail amide bond between the N-terminal amine and C-terminal carboxyl group, creating a cyclic peptide.

title: What is the advantage of thioether cyclization?
content: Thioether cyclization creates a stable, non-reducible crosslink between cysteine and a halogenated residue, providing stability without susceptibility to reduction.

title: What is the significance of half-life extension for peptide drugs?
content: Longer half-lives mean less frequent dosing, improving patient compliance and maintaining therapeutic drug levels over extended periods.

title: What is the role of protease inhibitors in peptide delivery?
content: Co-formulating peptides with protease inhibitors can protect them from degradation in the gastrointestinal tract or circulation, improving bioavailability.

title: What is the advantage of hydrocarbon stapling over disulfide stapling?
content: Hydrocarbon staples are non-reducible, metabolically stable crosslinks that also enhance cell penetration compared to disulfide bonds.

title: What is the significance of the Fc fusion for peptide half-life?
content: Fc fusion peptides bind the neonatal Fc receptor (FcRn), which protects them from lysosomal degradation and recycles them back to circulation, extending half-life.

title: What is the role of polyethylene glycol (PEG) size in PEGylation?
content: PEG molecular weight influences hydrodynamic radius, renal clearance, tissue penetration, and can affect biological activity, requiring optimization.

title: What is the advantage of using peptoids as peptide mimetics?
content: Peptoids have side chains on the nitrogen instead of alpha carbon, making them resistant to proteases while maintaining similar structural diversity.

title: What is the significance of protease-resistant peptide bonds?
content: Modified peptide bonds (e.g., reduced amides, triazoles) maintain structural features for binding while being unrecognizable to proteases.

title: What is the advantage of white blood cell-derived vesicles for peptide delivery?
content: White blood cell-derived vesicles (like exosomes) naturally evade immune detection and can target inflamed tissues or tumors through inherent tropism.

title: What is the role of cyclodextrin complexation in peptide stability?
content: Cyclodextrins form inclusion complexes with hydrophobic portions of peptides, protecting them from enzymatic degradation and improving aqueous solubility.

title: What is the significance of the Aib (alpha-aminoisobutyric acid) residue?
content: Aib is a sterically constrained amino acid that strongly promotes helical structure formation and is resistant to proteolytic degradation.

title: What is the advantage of oral peptide delivery formulations?
content: Oral formulations improve patient compliance by enabling self-administration without injection, though they face challenges with GI degradation and permeability.

title: What is the role of microneedle delivery for peptides?
content: Microneedles create transient pores in the stratum corneum, enabling transdermal peptide delivery while avoiding GI degradation and first-pass metabolism.

title: What is the significance of the Cys-Ser disulfide engineering?
content: Cys-Ser disulfide engineering can create reducible crosslinks that release active peptide in reducing intracellular environments for targeted delivery.

title: What is the advantage of spray-dried peptide formulations?
content: Spray drying converts peptide solutions to stable solid powders, improving shelf life and enabling various delivery routes including inhalation.

title: What is the significance of the iPr group in Aib?
content: The isopropyl group of Aib creates steric hindrance that restricts backbone dihedral angles, strongly favoring helical conformations.

title: What is the advantage of depot formulations for peptide drugs?
content: Depot formulations (microspheres, implants) provide sustained peptide release over extended periods, reducing dosing frequency to weekly or monthly.

title: What is the significance of the FcRn-mediated recycling pathway?
content: FcRn binds Fc domains in acidic endosomes and recycles them back to the cell surface, rescuing Fc-fusion proteins from lysosomal degradation.

title: What is the advantage of dual-drug peptide conjugates?
content: Dual-drug conjugates link two therapeutic peptides or a peptide with another drug, enabling synergistic effects by targeting multiple pathways simultaneously.

title: What is the primary function of proteases?
content: Proteases catalyze the hydrolysis of peptide bonds, breaking proteins into smaller fragments. They are essential for protein turnover, digestion, and regulatory signaling.

title: Which class of proteases uses a catalytic triad of Ser-His-Asp?
content: Serine proteases employ a Ser-His-Asp catalytic triad for nucleophilic attack on the peptide bond. Trypsin, chymotrypsin, and elastase are well-known examples.

title: Which protease cleaves after positively charged residues (Lys, Arg)?
content: Trypsin cleaves peptide bonds on the C-terminal side of lysine and arginine residues due to its specificity pocket accommodating positively charged side chains.

title: Which irreversible protease inhibitor forms a covalent bond with the active site?
content: PMSF (phenylmethylsulfonyl fluoride) forms a covalent sulfonate ester with the active site serine of serine proteases, irreversibly inactivating them.

title: What is the catalytic efficiency constant?
content: The specificity constant kcat/Km integrates substrate binding affinity and catalytic turnover into a single measure of enzyme efficiency.

title: Which protease class requires a zinc ion for catalytic activity?
content: Metalloproteases use a catalytically essential zinc ion coordinated by histidine and glutamate residues. The zinc activates a water molecule for nucleophilic attack.

title: Chymotrypsin uses which catalytic mechanism?
content: Chymotrypsin forms a transient acyl-enzyme intermediate between Ser-195 and the substrate, which is then hydrolyzed to release the product and regenerate the enzyme.

title: Non-competitive inhibition affects which kinetic parameter?
content: Non-competitive inhibitors bind equally to free enzyme and ES complex, reducing Vmax without changing Km since substrate binding affinity is unaffected.

title: Which protease converts proinsulin to active insulin?
content: Prohormone convertases PC1/3 and PC2 cleave proinsulin at dibasic residues, removing C-peptide to produce mature insulin with A and B chains linked by disulfide bonds.

title: Cysteine proteases form which type of covalent intermediate?
content: Cysteine proteases use a catalytic cysteine that attacks the peptide bond to form a thioester intermediate, which is then hydrolyzed to complete catalysis.

title: What does the Lineweaver-Burk plot represent?
content: The Lineweaver-Burk plot displays 1/V versus 1/[S], producing a linear relationship. Intercepts reveal kinetic parameters: y-intercept equals 1/Vmax, x-intercept equals -1/Km.

title: Which dipeptidyl peptidase is targeted by gliptin diabetes drugs?
content: DPP-4 degrades incretin hormones GLP-1 and GIP. Gliptins like sitagliptin inhibit DPP-4, prolonging incretin action and enhancing glucose-dependent insulin secretion.

title: Carboxypeptidase A removes which type of C-terminal amino acid?
content: Carboxypeptidase A is a pancreatic zinc metalloprotease that selectively removes C-terminal amino acids with aromatic or branched aliphatic side chains.

title: What is the physiological role of angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE)?
content: ACE converts the inactive angiotensin I to the vasoconstrictor angiotensin II and degrades vasodilatory bradykinin. ACE inhibitors treat hypertension by opposing both effects.

title: Which caspase is the initiator in the extrinsic apoptosis pathway?
content: Caspase-8 is activated by death receptor signaling at the DISC. It then cleaves and activates executioner caspases like caspase-3 and caspase-7.

title: MMP-1 cleaves fibrillar collagens at which position?
content: MMP-1 cleaves fibrillar collagens (types I, II, III) at a specific site three-quarters from the N-terminus, producing characteristic 3/4 and 1/4 fragments.

title: What is the role of furin in peptide processing?
content: Furin is a Golgi-resident proprotein convertase that cleaves precursors at (R/K)Xn(R/K) motifs, activating proinsulin, pro-BMP, and many viral glycoproteins.

title: What is SPINK1's role in the pancreas?
content: SPINK1 binds and inhibits prematurely activated trypsin in the pancreas, preventing autodigestion. Mutations in SPINK1 are linked to chronic pancreatitis.

title: What is the mechanism of signal peptidase?
content: Signal peptidase is an ER-resident serine protease that uses a Ser-Lys catalytic dyad to cleave signal peptides from nascent secretory and membrane proteins.

title: What is the Km value in Michaelis-Menten kinetics?
content: Km represents the substrate concentration at which reaction velocity is half of Vmax. A lower Km indicates higher substrate affinity for the enzyme.

title: What distinguishes exopeptidases from endopeptidases?
content: Exopeptidases sequentially remove amino acids from the N-terminus or C-terminus, while endopeptidases cleave internal peptide bonds within the chain.

title: Which pathway primarily degrades intracellular proteins via the 26S proteasome?
content: The ubiquitin-proteasome system tags proteins with polyubiquitin chains for degradation by the 26S proteasome, the primary pathway for regulated intracellular protein turnover.

title: Substrate-specific proteases recognize what feature of their substrates?
content: Substrate-specific proteases recognize defined sequence motifs within their substrates. For example, caspases recognize the DEVD motif in their target proteins.

title: Which inhibition pattern shows parallel lines on a Lineweaver-Burk plot?
content: Uncompetitive inhibition yields parallel lines because both Km and Vmax decrease by the same factor. The inhibitor binds only to the ES complex, not free enzyme.

title: What stabilizes the tetrahedral intermediate in serine protease catalysis?
content: The oxyanion hole, formed by backbone NH groups, stabilizes the negative charge on the tetrahedral intermediate via hydrogen bonding, lowering the transition state energy.

title: Which protease is essential for MHC class I antigen presentation?
content: The immunoproteasome generates 8-10 residue peptides transported into the ER by TAP and loaded onto MHC class I molecules for immune surveillance.

title: What distinguishes pure non-competitive from mixed inhibition?
content: In pure non-competitive inhibition, the inhibitor binds free enzyme and ES complex with equal affinity, leaving Km unchanged. Mixed inhibition has unequal binding affinities.

title: Which protease is activated during neutrophil degranulation to degrade extracellular matrix?
content: Neutrophil elastase is a serine protease stored in azurophilic granules. Upon activation, it degrades elastin, collagen, and proteoglycans during inflammation.

title: What is the physiological consequence of DPP-4 inhibition in type 2 diabetes?
content: DPP-4 inhibitors prevent incretin degradation, extending GLP-1 and GIP half-lives. This enhances glucose-dependent insulin secretion and suppresses glucagon release.

title: What is the kinetic mechanism of the proteasome's caspase-like activity?
content: The caspase-like (PGPH) activity of the proteasome's beta-5 subunit cleaves after acidic residues, playing a key role in generating MHC class I presentation peptides.

title: What is the minimum resolution needed to see individual atoms in X-ray crystallography?
content: Atomic resolution (~1.2 A or better) allows unambiguous determination of individual atom positions. At 3.0 A, side chain conformations may be ambiguous.

title: Which technique determines peptide structures in solution without crystallization?
content: NMR spectroscopy determines three-dimensional structures of peptides and small proteins in solution, providing information about dynamics and conformational flexibility.

title: What does cryo-EM stand for?
content: Cryo-electron microscopy flash-freezes samples in vitreous ice, preserving native conformations. It resolves structures of large complexes without crystallization.

title: What is the Ramachandran plot used for?
content: The Ramachandran plot displays phi and psi backbone dihedral angles, revealing sterically allowed conformations and validating structural model quality.

title: Which spectroscopic method measures peptide bond vibrations?
content: IR spectroscopy detects amide I (C=O stretch) and amide II (N-H bend) vibrations of peptide bonds, providing information about secondary structure content.

title: What does CD spectroscopy measure?
content: Circular dichroism measures differential absorption of left and right circularly polarized light by chiral molecules, reporting on peptide secondary structure content.

title: What is the typical bond length of a peptide bond C-N?
content: The peptide bond C-N has partial double bond character (1.33 A), shorter than a pure single bond. This restricts rotation and keeps the peptide group planar.

title: What is the B-factor in X-ray crystallography?
content: The B-factor quantifies atomic displacement from mean positions due to thermal vibration and static disorder. High B-factors indicate flexible or disordered regions.

title: What is the role of a heavy atom derivative in X-ray phasing?
content: Heavy atoms like Se, Hg, and Pt introduce anomalous scattering that enables experimental phase determination through SAD or MAD phasing methods.

title: At what resolution do side chain orientations become reliably determined?
content: At 2.0 A resolution, alpha-helices and beta-sheets are clearly visible, side chain orientations can be determined, and water molecules become apparent in electron density.

title: In cryo-EM, what is the contrast transfer function (CTF)?
content: The CTF describes how electron optics introduce oscillations and phase reversals. Correcting for CTF is essential for recovering true structural information from micrographs.

title: What is the isoelectric point (pI) of a peptide?
content: The isoelectric point is the pH at which a peptide carries no net electrical charge. Above pI the peptide is negatively charged; below pI, positively charged.

title: Which NMR experiment identifies sequentially connected backbone atoms?
content: The HNCA experiment correlates each amide proton with its own and preceding residue's C-alpha, enabling sequential backbone assignment for structure determination.

title: What is the molecular replacement method in crystallography?
content: Molecular replacement uses a known homologous structure as a search model to determine phases. It works when a sufficiently similar structure is available in the PDB.

title: What does the NOE (Nuclear Overhauser Effect) report on?
content: NOE signals arise from dipolar coupling between nuclei within ~5 A of each other in space. NOE distance constraints drive NMR structure calculations.

title: What is a key advantage of cryo-EM over X-ray crystallography?
content: Cryo-EM determines structures without crystallization, especially valuable for flexible peptide-receptor complexes that resist crystallization or lose native conformation.

title: What parameter defines the quality of a protein crystal?
content: Mosaicity measures the angular spread of lattice orientations within a crystal. Low mosaicity indicates well-ordered crystals that produce high-quality diffraction data.

title: What is chemical shift indexing (CSI) in NMR?
content: CSI compares observed C-alpha and H-alpha chemical shifts to random coil values. Deviations predict alpha-helix or beta-sheet secondary structure.

title: What is the R-factor in crystallography?
content: The R-factor quantifies agreement between observed and calculated structure factors. Values below 0.20 generally indicate a well-refined structure.

title: What does the free R-factor (R-free) assess?
content: R-free is calculated from a randomly selected 5-10% of reflections excluded from refinement, providing an unbiased measure of model quality and guarding against overfitting.

title: Which NMR parameter provides information about peptide backbone flexibility?
content: The heteronuclear NOE (15N-1H) measures local backbone dynamics. Values near +0.7 indicate rigid regions, while negative values indicate fast nanosecond-timescale motion.

title: What is the significance of the omega angle in peptide structures?
content: The omega angle (180 degrees for trans, 0 degrees for cis) defines peptide bond geometry. Proline residues have a higher propensity for cis peptide bonds than other amino acids.

title: What is small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) used for?
content: SAXS provides low-resolution information about the overall shape, size, and conformational state of peptides and proteins in solution under near-physiological conditions.

title: What is the Debye-Waller factor in crystallography?
content: The Debye-Waller factor accounts for the reduction in diffraction intensity due to atomic thermal motion. Higher B-factors mean less observed intensity.

title: What does hydrogen-deuterium exchange (HDX) measure?
content: HDX-MS measures the rate at which backbone amide hydrogens exchange with deuterium, reporting on solvent accessibility and local structural dynamics.

title: What is the correlation time in NMR?
content: The correlation time describes how fast a molecule tumbles in solution, directly affecting NMR relaxation rates. Larger peptides have longer correlation times.

title: What is the phase problem in X-ray crystallography?
content: X-ray detectors measure only intensities but not phases. Without phases, electron density cannot be directly calculated, making this the central challenge of crystallography.

title: What is cryo-electron tomography (cryo-ET) used for?
content: Cryo-ET reconstructs 3D volumes of thin cellular sections, visualizing peptide-receptor complexes and other macromolecular machines in their native cellular environment.

title: What is the Dali server used for in structural biology?
content: Dali (Distance matrix ALignment) compares 3D structures to identify structural neighbors and classify folds, useful for understanding peptide structural relationships.

title: What is sigma-A weighting used for in crystallographic refinement?
content: Sigma-A weighting optimally combines observed and calculated structure factors, reducing model bias in electron density maps used for model building and refinement.

title: What type of bond links glycans to asparagine residues?
content: N-glycosidic bonds connect glycans to the amide nitrogen of asparagine within the consensus sequence Asn-X-Ser/Thr.

title: What is the core structure of all N-linked glycans?
content: All N-linked glycans share a common chitobiose core of two GlcNAc residues followed by three mannose residues, from which diverse antennae extend.

title: Which amino acid is most commonly O-glycosylated?
content: Serine and threonine are the most common O-glycosylation sites, with the hydroxyl group forming an O-glycosidic bond with GalNAc.

title: What sugar is typically added first in mucin-type O-glycosylation?
content: Mucin-type O-glycosylation begins with the addition of GalNAc to serine or threonine by polypeptide GalNAc-transferases.

title: Glycosylation occurs primarily in which organelle?
content: N-glycosylation begins in the ER with transfer of a preformed oligosaccharide, while both N- and O-glycosylation are elaborated in the Golgi.

title: What is the role of sugar nucleotides in glycosylation?
content: Sugar nucleotides like UDP-GalNAc and CMP-Neu5Ac serve as activated donor substrates that glycosyltransferases use to transfer specific sugars to growing glycan chains.

title: What is the lectin fold?
content: Lectins are proteins with carbohydrate-recognition domains (CRDs) that specifically bind glycan structures, mediating cell-cell recognition and immune regulation.

title: Which technique is commonly used to analyze glycopeptide composition?
content: Mass spectrometry combined with lectin affinity or HILIC enables sensitive identification and characterization of glycopeptides.

title: What is a glycan antenna?
content: Glycan antennae are branching oligosaccharide chains that extend from the conserved core structure, creating enormous structural diversity in glycan epitopes.

title: What distinguishes high-mannose from complex N-glycans?
content: High-mannose glycans retain multiple mannose residues on the core, while complex glycans have been extensively processed and may contain GlcNAc, galactose, sialic acid, and fucose.

title: What is the function of dolichol phosphate in N-glycosylation?
content: Dolichol phosphate is a long-chain isoprenoid lipid in the ER membrane that serves as the anchor on which the 14-sugar precursor oligosaccharide is assembled.

title: What is the glycosidic linkage type in the chitobiose core?
content: The two GlcNAc residues in the chitobiose core are linked by a beta-1,4-glycosidic bond, forming the foundation of all N-linked glycan structures.

title: What is peptide N-glycosidase F (PNGase F) used for?
content: PNGase F hydrolyzes the bond between the innermost GlcNAc and asparagine, releasing intact N-linked glycans for structural analysis.

title: What is the consensus sequence for N-glycosylation?
content: N-glycosylation requires the sequon Asn-X-Ser/Thr, where X can be any amino acid except proline. Not all sequons are glycosylated due to local structural context.

title: Which monosaccharide is typically found at the non-reducing termini of glycans?
content: Sialic acid is commonly added to the non-reducing ends of glycan antennae, capping the chains and contributing negative charge to glycoconjugates.

title: What is the ER quality control cycle for glycoprotein folding?
content: The calnexin/calreticulin cycle monitors glycoprotein folding by binding monoglucosylated N-glycans, with ER mannosidase I and UGGT regulating cycling between folded and unfolded states.

title: What is the Tn antigen?
content: The Tn antigen consists of a single GalNAc residue O-linked to serine or threonine. It is normally masked by further glycosylation but exposed in many cancers.

title: Which enzyme adds the first GlcNAc to form hybrid glycans?
content: GnT-I adds GlcNAc to the Man alpha-1,3 arm, initiating the conversion from high-mannose to hybrid and complex N-glycans.

title: What is the reducing end of a glycan?
content: The reducing end has a free anomeric carbon that can open to the aldehyde form. In glycopeptides, this end is attached to the peptide through the glycosidic bond.

title: What is the biological significance of core fucosylation of IgG Fc glycans?
content: Core fucose on IgG Fc N-glycans reduces binding to Fc gamma receptor IIIa, decreasing ADCC. Afucosylated antibodies show dramatically enhanced ADCC activity.

title: What is O-GlcNAcylation and how does it differ from mucin-type O-glycosylation?
content: O-GlcNAcylation is a dynamic, reversible addition of a single GlcNAc to Ser/Thr in the nucleus and cytoplasm, unlike mucin-type O-glycosylation which occurs on secreted proteins.

title: What is the glycosylphosphatidylinositol (GPI) anchor?
content: GPI anchors attach proteins to the outer leaflet of cell membranes through phosphoethanolamine-linker, mannose3-GlcNAc glycan core, and phosphatidylinositol with lipid tails.

title: What is the LecT cell phenotype associated with?
content: The LecT phenotype results from loss of FUT8 activity, eliminating core fucose from N-glycans. This modification affects antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity.

title: What causes congenital disorders of glycosylation (CDG)?
content: CDG are inherited metabolic disorders caused by mutations in genes encoding glycosylation enzymes or their regulators, affecting multiple organ systems.

title: What is the sialyl Lewis X epitope involved in?
content: Sialyl Lewis X is a glycan epitope on leukocyte surface glycoproteins that binds to selectins on endothelial cells, mediating leukocyte extravasation during inflammation.

title: What analytical technique can distinguish glycan isomers with the same mass?
content: Ion mobility spectrometry-MS separates glycan isomers by collision cross-sections, while CE separates by charge-to-size ratio, resolving isomers identical by mass alone.

title: What is the concept of the glycome?
content: The glycome encompasses the full repertoire of glycans produced by an organism. Unlike the genome, it is not template-encoded and varies by cell type and disease state.

title: What structural feature distinguishes O-mannosylation from O-GalNAc glycosylation?
content: O-mannosylation adds mannose directly to serine/threonine, while mucin-type O-glycosylation adds GalNAc. O-mannosylation is particularly important in brain glycoproteins.

title: What is the role of GMPPA in GDP-fucose biosynthesis?
content: GMPPA catalyzes GDP-mannose formation, a precursor for GDP-fucose biosynthesis through a two-step pathway essential for fucosylation of glycans.

title: Which technique is the gold standard for glycan structural characterization?
content: Sequential exoglylosidase digestion systematically trims glycan termini while MS monitors mass changes, building a complete picture of glycan composition, linkage, and branching.

title: What is a peptide biomarker?
content: Peptide biomarkers are measurable indicators of normal biological processes, pathogenic processes, or pharmacological responses to therapeutic interventions.

title: Which peptide is commonly measured to diagnose myocardial infarction?
content: B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP) and NT-proBNP are released from cardiac ventricles in response to wall stress, serving as key biomarkers for heart failure.

title: What type of assay is an ELISA?
content: ELISA uses enzyme-labeled antibodies to detect and quantify specific peptides in biological samples through colorimetric, fluorescent, or chemiluminescent signals.

title: Why are peptides preferred over antibodies in some diagnostic assays?
content: Synthetic peptides offer batch-to-batch reproducibility, defined composition, and lower immunogenicity concerns compared to antibodies.

title: What does a sandwich ELISA use for detection?
content: Sandwich ELISA captures the target peptide with one antibody and detects it with a second enzyme-labeled antibody binding a different epitope, enhancing specificity.

title: What is the analytical sensitivity of an assay?
content: Analytical sensitivity refers to the minimum concentration of peptide analyte that can be reliably distinguished from zero with a stated confidence level.

title: Which technique measures peptide mass directly for diagnostic purposes?
content: Mass spectrometry can directly measure peptide mass and sequence, enabling multiplexed detection of multiple biomarkers simultaneously with high specificity.

title: What is a point-of-care (POC) diagnostic test?
content: POC tests provide rapid results at or near the site of patient care, enabling immediate clinical decisions.

title: What does 'dynamic range' mean in peptide diagnostics?
content: Dynamic range is the concentration interval between the lower and upper limits of quantitation where the assay response is proportional to analyte concentration.

title: What is the preanalytical phase in diagnostic testing?
content: The preanalytical phase encompasses all steps before analysis. Errors in this phase are the largest source of diagnostic errors.

title: What is a procalcitonin (PCT) assay used for?
content: Procalcitonin levels rise in systemic bacterial infections and sepsis. Serial PCT measurements guide antibiotic therapy duration in critical care.

title: What is MALDI used for in diagnostics?
content: MALDI-TOF is used for rapid microbial identification and peptide profiling in clinical laboratories by ionizing peptides from a solid matrix using laser pulses.

title: What is a Luminex multiplex bead assay?
content: Luminex assays use spectrally distinct microspheres coated with capture antibodies to simultaneously measure multiple peptides in a single sample using flow cytometry.

title: What is the clinical utility of amyloid-beta peptide ratios?
content: The ratio of A-beta-42 to A-beta-40 in CSF or plasma reflects brain amyloid deposition. Decreased ratios indicate amyloid pathology characteristic of Alzheimer disease.

title: What is the hook effect in immunoassays?
content: The hook effect occurs when extremely high analyte concentrations saturate both capture and detection antibodies, preventing sandwich formation and yielding falsely low results.

title: What is an aptamer in peptide diagnostic contexts?
content: Aptamers are short DNA or RNA oligonucleotides selected to bind specific targets with high affinity, serving as alternatives to antibodies in diagnostic platforms.

title: What does standardization of peptide biomarker assays require?
content: Standardization ensures comparable results across laboratories through certified reference materials, traceable calibrators, and harmonized measurement procedures.

title: What is immunohistochemistry (IHC) used for in peptide diagnostics?
content: IHC uses labeled antibodies to detect and localize specific peptides within fixed tissue sections, providing spatial information about peptide expression in cancer diagnosis.

title: What is the coefficient of variation (CV) in assay performance?
content: CV expresses the standard deviation as a percentage of the mean, quantifying the precision of repeated measurements. Lower CV values indicate better assay reproducibility.

title: What is a liquid biopsy in peptide diagnostics?
content: Liquid biopsies detect circulating tumor-derived peptides in blood, enabling non-invasive cancer diagnosis, monitoring, and treatment response assessment.

title: What is a mass spectrometry-based MRM assay?
content: MRM selects specific precursor-product ion pairs for targeted peptide quantification, achieving high sensitivity and specificity without antibodies for clinical biomarker validation.

title: What is digital ELISA (Simoa)?
content: Single molecule array (Simoa) traps individual enzyme-labeled immunocomplexes in femtoliter-volume wells, enabling single-molecule detection with 1000-fold greater sensitivity than conventional ELISA.

title: What is the clinical significance of procalcitonin monitoring?
content: Serial PCT measurements guide antibiotic therapy duration in ICU patients. Decreasing PCT levels suggest effective treatment, allowing safe antibiotic discontinuation.

title: What is the imprecision at the medical decision point?
content: Imprecision at medical decision points determines whether analytical variation could cause misclassification. Guidelines require CV below 10% at these concentrations.

title: What is harmonization vs standardization in peptide diagnostics?
content: Harmonization aims to reduce inter-laboratory differences, while standardization ensures all laboratories measure to the same international reference.

title: What is cross-reactivity and why is it problematic?
content: Cross-reactivity occurs when antibodies bind structurally similar peptides (e.g., GLP-1 vs GLP-2), producing falsely elevated results that must be characterized and minimized.

title: What is an internal standard in quantitative peptide MS?
content: Isotope-labeled internal standards are added at known concentrations to correct for ionization suppression, extraction efficiency, and matrix effects.

title: What is a companion diagnostic in peptide medicine?
content: Companion diagnostics are FDA-approved tests that identify patients likely to benefit from a specific therapeutic, like HER2 testing for trastuzumab.

title: What analytical challenge does sample matrix present?
content: Complex biological matrices cause ion suppression in MS, non-specific binding in immunoassays, and interference from structurally similar molecules.

title: What is neuropeptide measurement used for in neurological diagnostics?
content: Neuropeptide levels in CSF reflect neurotransmitter pathway function. Decreased hypocretin-1 confirms narcolepsy diagnosis, while other neuropeptides indicate neurodegeneration.

title: What is the primary target of most antimicrobial peptides?
content: Most AMPs target bacterial cell membranes through electrostatic interactions with negatively charged lipids, leading to membrane disruption and cell death.

title: What charge do most antimicrobial peptides carry?
content: Most AMPs are cationic with net positive charges from lysine and arginine residues, enabling selective binding to negatively charged bacterial membranes.

title: Defensins are classified into which groups?
content: Alpha-defensins are found in neutrophils and Paneth cells, beta-defensins in epithelial tissues, and theta-defensins (cyclic) in some primate leukocytes.

title: Which organism produces the antimicrobial peptide magainin?
content: Magainins are cationic AMPs isolated from the skin of the African clawed frog Xenopus laevis, forming pores in bacterial membranes leading to cell lysis.

title: What is the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC)?
content: MIC is the lowest concentration of an antimicrobial agent that prevents visible growth of a microorganism after overnight incubation.

title: What is the typical mechanism of pore-forming AMPs?
content: Pore-forming AMPs insert into bacterial lipid bilayers and assemble into transmembrane channels through barrel-stave, toroidal pore, or carpet mechanisms.

title: Which defense peptide is produced by Paneth cells in the gut?
content: Paneth cells at the base of intestinal crypts secrete alpha-defensins and other antimicrobial peptides that shape the gut microbiome and protect against pathogens.

title: What does AMP selectivity for bacteria over human cells depend on?
content: Bacterial membranes contain abundant negatively charged phospholipids while human membranes have predominantly neutral lipids. Cationic AMPs preferentially bind bacterial surfaces.

title: Cathelicidins are stored in which form in neutrophils?
content: Cathelicidins are stored as inactive precursor proteins in neutrophil granules. Proteolytic cleavage releases the active C-terminal antimicrobial domain upon infection.

title: What is the barrel-stave pore model?
content: In the barrel-stave model, amphipathic helices insert perpendicularly into the bilayer, with hydrophobic faces contacting lipids and hydrophilic faces lining the aqueous pore.

title: Which modification can increase AMP resistance to proteolytic degradation?
content: Substituting L-amino acids with D-enantiomers renders peptides resistant to most proteases, which recognize only L-amino acid substrates.

title: What is the therapeutic index of an AMP?
content: The therapeutic index is the ratio of hemolytic concentration (HC50) to MIC. A higher index indicates a wider safety margin between antimicrobial and toxic effects.

title: Which bacterial defense mechanism involves modification of membrane lipids?
content: Some bacteria modify their LPS with aminoarabinose or phosphoethanolamine, reducing the negative surface charge and decreasing cationic AMP binding affinity.

title: What is the carpet model of AMP action?
content: In the carpet model, cationic AMPs accumulate on the membrane surface and disrupt the bilayer in a detergent-like manner without forming defined pore structures.

title: How do AMPs contribute to wound healing?
content: AMPs like LL-37 serve dual roles: direct pathogen killing and immunomodulation including promoting angiogenesis, modulating cytokines, and stimulating epithelial cell proliferation.

title: What is the toroidal pore model?
content: In the toroidal pore model, AMPs induce continuous bending of lipid headgroups, creating a pore where peptide and lipid headgroups line the channel together.

title: Which resistance mechanism involves proteolytic destruction of AMPs?
content: Some pathogenic bacteria secrete extracellular proteases that degrade host defense peptides, neutralizing the antimicrobial effect before membrane contact.

title: What is histatin 5 and where is it found?
content: Histatin 5 is a small cationic peptide produced by salivary glands with potent antifungal activity against Candida albicans, acting through intracellular membrane targeting.

title: What is the role of LPS in AMP resistance?
content: Long O-antigen chains create a steric barrier preventing AMPs from reaching the outer membrane. Lipid A modifications reduce negative charge and cationic AMP attraction.

title: What is the mechanism of action of nisin?
content: Nisin is a lantibiotic that binds lipid II to inhibit peptidoglycan synthesis and subsequently forms pores using lipid II as a docking molecule.

title: Which AMP shows synergistic activity with conventional antibiotics?
content: AMPs can synergize with antibiotics by permeabilizing membranes and enhancing drug uptake. LL-37 synergizes with tobramycin against P. aeruginosa.

title: What is the role of two-component regulatory systems in AMP resistance?
content: Two-component systems like PmrAB and PhoPQ sense cationic AMPs and activate genes that modify LPS, reducing net negative charge and AMP binding.

title: How does the glycocalyx protect bacteria from AMPs?
content: Capsules and biofilm matrices create polysaccharide barriers that sequester AMPs through charge repulsion or steric hindrance, preventing membrane contact.

title: What is the mechanism of daptomycin's AMP-like activity?
content: Daptomycin is a lipopeptide that inserts into bacterial membranes in a calcium-dependent manner, oligomerizes, and forms ion channels causing membrane depolarization.

title: What is the clinical significance of AMP-based therapies for drug-resistant infections?
content: AMPs target fundamental membrane properties difficult for bacteria to modify without fitness costs, making resistance development less likely for multidrug-resistant infections.

title: What is the concept of antimicrobial peptide resistance thresholds?
content: Resistance thresholds define the AMP concentration at which resistance mutants can emerge. Therapeutic dosing above this threshold prevents selection of resistant populations.

title: What is the contribution of intracellular AMP targets to killing?
content: While membrane disruption is primary, some AMPs like buforin II penetrate the membrane and bind intracellular targets including DNA and RNA, contributing to cell death.

title: What is the role of membrane fluidity in AMP susceptibility?
content: Membrane fluidity affects AMP insertion. More fluid membranes allow easier penetration. Cholesterol in eukaryotic membranes reduces fluidity and AMP binding.

title: What is the immunomodulatory role of cathelicidin LL-37?
content: LL-37 modulates immune responses by binding LPS, acting as a chemoattractant, promoting wound healing, and regulating TLR-mediated inflammation.

title: What is the challenge of systemic AMP therapy?
content: Systemic AMP use faces challenges including rapid renal clearance, serum protease degradation, blood component sequestration, and potential toxicity at effective concentrations.

title: What is the primary role of neuropeptides?
content: Neuropeptides are signaling molecules released by neurons that modulate synaptic transmission, affecting mood, pain, appetite, and other neurological functions.

title: Which neuropeptide is commonly known as the 'feel-good' molecule?
content: Endorphins are endogenous opioid neuropeptides that bind mu-opioid receptors, producing analgesia and feelings of pleasure.

title: Substance P primarily functions in which process?
content: Substance P is a tachykinin neuropeptide released by sensory neurons that transmits pain signals and promotes neurogenic inflammation through NK1 receptors.

title: Neuropeptide Y is most associated with which function?
content: NPY is one of the most potent orexigenic peptides in the brain, acting on Y1 and Y5 receptors in the hypothalamus to promote feeding and energy storage.

title: What type of receptors do most neuropeptides signal through?
content: The majority of neuropeptide receptors are GPCRs with seven transmembrane domains. Binding triggers intracellular signaling cascades through G-proteins.

title: Where are neuropeptides typically synthesized?
content: Neuropeptides are synthesized as prepropeptides in the rough ER of the cell body, processed in the Golgi, and packaged into large dense-core vesicles.

title: Oxytocin is primarily associated with which behaviors?
content: Oxytocin promotes social bonding, maternal behavior, trust, and pair bonding through oxytocin receptors in limbic brain regions.

title: How do neuropeptides differ from classical neurotransmitters in release?
content: Unlike small-molecule neurotransmitters in synaptic vesicles, neuropeptides are released from large dense-core vesicles that require sustained high-frequency neuronal firing.

title: What is the role of neuropeptide processing enzymes?
content: Prohormone convertases (PC1/3, PC2) and carboxypeptidase E cleave inactive precursor proteins to generate mature, biologically active neuropeptides.

title: What is the half-life of most neuropeptides in the synaptic cleft?
content: Neuropeptides have short half-lives of seconds to minutes due to enzymatic degradation by peptidases and diffusion away from the synapse.

title: Which brain region is the primary site of neuropeptide synthesis for appetite regulation?
content: The hypothalamus contains neurons that synthesize key appetite-regulating neuropeptides including NPY, AgRP, POMC, and CART in the arcuate nucleus.

title: What is co-transmission in neuropeptide signaling?
content: Many neurons co-release classical neurotransmitters with neuropeptides. The peptide modulates the fast neurotransmitter response, providing slower, longer-lasting effects.

title: What is the role of neutral endopeptidase (NEP) in neuropeptide signaling?
content: NEP is a zinc metalloprotease on the cell surface that cleaves and inactivates neuropeptides including enkephalins and substance P, terminating signaling.

title: What distinguishes volume transmission from synaptic transmission?
content: Volume transmission allows neuropeptides to diffuse from release sites to distant receptors, affecting large neuronal populations compared to point-to-point synaptic transmission.

title: How does CRH function in the stress response?
content: CRH is released from the hypothalamus in response to stress, stimulating anterior pituitary corticotrophs to secrete ACTH, which stimulates adrenal cortisol production.

title: What is receptor internalization and why is it important?
content: After prolonged neuropeptide exposure, receptors are phosphorylated, bound by beta-arrestin, and internalized via clathrin-coated pits, preventing overstimulation.

title: Which neuropeptide system is primarily targeted by opioid analgesics?
content: Opioid analgesics mimic endogenous endorphins and enkephalins by activating mu, delta, and kappa opioid receptors, inhibiting pain transmission.

title: What is the role of galanin in the nervous system?
content: Galanin is a 29-amino acid neuropeptide that modulates acetylcholine release, regulates feeding behavior, inhibits seizures, and participates in pain processing.

title: What mechanism amplifies neuropeptide signals?
content: Neuropeptide-GPCR binding activates G-proteins that stimulate adenylyl cyclase or phospholipase C, producing second messengers that activate kinases, amplifying the signal.

title: What is the concept of neuropeptide receptor reserve?
content: Spare receptors mean maximum biological response can be achieved without occupying all available receptors, providing sensitivity and buffer against receptor loss.

title: What is the role of kisspeptin in reproductive function?
content: Kisspeptin acting through KISS1R is the most potent known stimulator of GnRH neurons, essential for puberty onset and fertility regulation.

title: What is the role of orexin in sleep-wake regulation?
content: Orexin neurons in the lateral hypothalamus stabilize wakefulness by activating wake-promoting circuits. Loss of orexin neurons causes narcolepsy type 1.

title: What is the relationship between BDNF and neuropeptide signaling?
content: BDNF modulates synaptic transmission and plasticity through TrkB receptor signaling, influencing the release and effects of multiple neuropeptide systems.

title: What is neuropeptide cross-talk?
content: Neuropeptide systems interact extensively. NPY and alpha-MSH have opposing effects on appetite, while orexin modulates multiple neurotransmitter systems simultaneously.

title: What is the significance of neuropeptide receptor dimerization?
content: GPCRs form dimers that can alter ligand binding affinity, G-protein coupling specificity, and trafficking. Mu-delta opioid heteromers show enhanced analgesia.

title: What is the role of peptidase-resistant neuropeptide analogs in research?
content: Modified neuropeptide analogs resistant to enzymatic degradation enable prolonged receptor activation, facilitating study of chronic signaling effects.

title: What is the role of the blood-brain barrier in neuropeptide signaling?
content: The BBB prevents most circulating neuropeptides from entering the brain, maintaining separate central and peripheral signaling compartments.

title: How do neuropeptide agonists and antagonists differ from classical drug targets?
content: Neuropeptide GPCRs have large, solvent-exposed orthosteric sites that accommodate peptide ligands, making them challenging to drug with small molecules.

title: What is biased agonism at neuropeptide receptors?
content: Biased agonists selectively activate specific downstream pathways at the same receptor, potentially separating therapeutic effects from side effects.

title: What is the clinical significance of orexin receptor antagonists?
content: Orexin receptor antagonists block wake-promoting orexin signaling, representing a mechanistically distinct approach to treating insomnia.

title: What is the primary function of insulin?
content: Insulin promotes glucose uptake into muscle and adipose tissue, glycogen synthesis, and lipogenesis while inhibiting gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis.

title: GLP-1 is secreted by which cells?
content: GLP-1 is an incretin hormone secreted by intestinal L-cells in response to nutrient ingestion, stimulating insulin secretion and suppressing glucagon.

title: Growth hormone is produced by which gland?
content: Growth hormone is synthesized by somatotroph cells of the anterior pituitary, regulated by hypothalamic GHRH and somatostatin.

title: Which hormone regulates water balance?
content: ADH is synthesized in the hypothalamus and released from the posterior pituitary, acting on kidney collecting ducts to increase water reabsorption.

title: Which hormone is critical for calcium homeostasis in bone?
content: PTH is secreted by parathyroid glands in response to low blood calcium, stimulating osteoclast bone resorption and enhancing renal calcium reabsorption.

title: What is the structure of insulin?
content: Mature insulin consists of an A-chain (21 residues) and B-chain (30 residues) linked by two interchain disulfide bonds, with one intrachain disulfide in the A-chain.

title: Amylin is co-secreted with which hormone?
content: Amylin is co-secreted with insulin from pancreatic beta cells, slowing gastric emptying, suppressing postprandial glucagon, and promoting satiety.

title: What is the primary role of TSH?
content: TSH binds TSH receptors on thyroid follicular cells, stimulating synthesis and release of thyroid hormones T3 and T4.

title: Leptin is produced by which tissue?
content: Leptin is secreted by adipocytes in proportion to fat mass, acting on hypothalamic receptors to suppress appetite and increase energy expenditure.

title: How is proinsulin processed to mature insulin?
content: Proinsulin undergoes proteolytic processing by PC1/3 and PC2 in pancreatic beta cell secretory granules, removing the C-peptide to yield mature insulin.

title: What is the incretin effect?
content: The incretin effect refers to oral glucose producing a greater insulin response than intravenous glucose, due to GLP-1 and GIP released from the gut.

title: What is the mechanism of action of somatostatin?
content: Somatostatin binds GPCRs (SSTR1-5) that inhibit adenylyl cyclase, reducing cAMP and suppressing secretion of multiple hormones including GH and insulin.

title: Which IGF is primarily produced by the liver in response to GH?
content: IGF-1 is produced mainly by hepatocytes under GH stimulation, mediating many growth-promoting effects of GH on target tissues throughout the body.

title: What is the half-life difference between native GLP-1 and exendin-4?
content: Native GLP-1 is rapidly degraded by DPP-4 (half-life 2-5 min). Exendin-4 is DPP-4 resistant with a 30-min half-life, forming the basis for exenatide therapy.

title: What is the role of C-peptide?
content: C-peptide is equimolarly released with insulin during proinsulin processing, serving as a clinical marker of endogenous insulin production.

title: Which hormone stimulates milk ejection?
content: Oxytocin causes contraction of myoepithelial cells surrounding mammary alveoli, ejecting milk into ducts in response to suckling.

title: What is POMC processing?
content: POMC is processed by prohormone convertases in a tissue-specific manner: anterior pituitary produces ACTH, while hypothalamus produces alpha-MSH and both produce beta-endorphin.

title: What causes the dawn phenomenon in diabetes?
content: The dawn phenomenon results from the pre-morning surge in counterregulatory hormones that increase hepatic glucose output, causing fasting hyperglycemia.

title: What is ghrelin's primary function?
content: Ghrelin is secreted by stomach oxyntic cells when fasting, acting on GHSR-1a in the hypothalamus to stimulate appetite and increase GH release.

title: What is the difference between DPP-4 inhibitors and GLP-1 receptor agonists?
content: DPP-4 inhibitors prevent degradation of native GLP-1, modestly increasing levels. GLP-1 receptor agonists are resistant analogs given at pharmacological doses with greater effects.

title: What is the role of pancreatic alpha cells?
content: Pancreatic alpha cells produce glucagon, stimulating hepatic glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis during fasting to prevent hypoglycemia.

title: What is the molecular mechanism of insulin receptor activation?
content: Insulin binds the alpha subunits, inducing conformational change that activates intrinsic tyrosine kinase activity in the beta subunits, leading to autophosphorylation and IRS recruitment.

title: What is desmopressin (DDAVP) used for?
content: Desmopressin is a synthetic ADH analog with selectivity for V2 receptors, treating central diabetes insipidus by enhancing renal water reabsorption.

title: What is the role of natriuretic peptides (ANP, BNP)?
content: ANP and BNP bind NPR-A receptors, activating guanylyl cyclase to produce cGMP, causing vasodilation, natriuresis, and diuresis to counteract the renin-angiotensin system.

title: What is the mechanism of terzepatide's activity?
content: Terzepatide is a dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist achieving superior glycemic control and weight loss through complementary metabolic effects.

title: What is the significance of pulsatile hormone release?
content: Pulsatile release prevents sustained receptor occupancy that leads to desensitization. Continuous GnRH exposure paradoxically suppresses LH/FSH, used therapeutically.

title: What is the concept of hormonal negative feedback?
content: Negative feedback loops maintain hormonal balance. T3/T4 inhibit hypothalamic TRH and pituitary TSH release, preventing overproduction of thyroid hormones.

title: What is the role of apelin in cardiovascular function?
content: Apelin acting through the APJ receptor is a positive inotrope that increases cardiac contractility while promoting vasodilation and is cardioprotective.

title: What distinguishes semaglutide's pharmacokinetics from native GLP-1?
content: Semaglutide combines DPP-4 resistance with albumin binding through a fatty diacid linker, extending half-life to approximately 165 hours for once-weekly dosing.

title: What does PET stand for?
content: Positron emission tomography detects gamma rays from positron-electron annihilation events, enabling quantitative imaging of radiotracer distribution in vivo.

title: What is the half-life of fluorine-18 (F-18)?
content: F-18 has a half-life of 109.77 minutes, making it ideal for PET imaging: long enough for synthesis and imaging but short enough to limit radiation dose.

title: What is SPECT imaging?
content: SPECT uses gamma-emitting radionuclides and rotating gamma cameras to reconstruct 3D images of radiotracer distribution in the body.

title: Why are peptides used as imaging agents?
content: Peptides targeting receptors overexpressed on tumors deliver radionuclides specifically to disease sites for sensitive imaging.

title: What is a chelator in radiopharmaceutical chemistry?
content: Chelators like DOTA and DTPA are polyaminocarboxylic acids that form stable complexes with metallic radionuclides such as Ga-68 and Lu-177.

title: What does 'specific activity' mean in radiochemistry?
content: Specific activity measures radioactivity per amount of compound. Higher specific activity means more radioactive atoms relative to cold atoms.

title: Which radionuclide is most commonly used in clinical PET imaging?
content: F-18 (as FDG) is the most widely used PET radionuclide due to its optimal half-life, low positron energy, and versatile fluorination chemistry.

title: What is the theranostic concept in peptide radiochemistry?
content: Theranostics pairs a diagnostic radionuclide (e.g., Ga-68) with a therapeutic radionuclide (e.g., Lu-177) on the same targeting peptide for personalized treatment.

title: What is a positron in nuclear medicine?
content: A positron is the antimatter counterpart of the electron. When emitted during beta-plus decay, it annihilates with an electron producing two 511 keV gamma photons.

title: What is the role of the bifunctional chelator (BFC)?
content: BFCs have two functional moieties: one for covalent peptide attachment and another for chelating the metal radionuclide, connecting targeting and imaging functions.

title: What is radiochemical purity?
content: Radiochemical purity measures the percentage of total radioactivity in the correct chemical form. Impurities can cause non-specific uptake and reduce image quality.

title: Why is gallium-68 used as a PET radionuclide?
content: Ga-68 is eluted from a Ge-68/Ga-68 generator, eliminating the need for an on-site cyclotron. Its 68-min half-life matches peptide pharmacokinetics.

title: What is DOTA?
content: DOTA is a macrocyclic chelator forming extremely stable complexes with trivalent and tetravalent metal ions, the gold standard for Lu-177, Ga-68, and Y-90.

title: What is the main advantage of PRRT?
content: PRRT delivers beta or alpha-emitting radionuclides specifically to tumor cells via peptide-receptor binding, causing DNA damage while minimizing normal tissue damage.

title: What is absorbed dose in radionuclide therapy?
content: Absorbed dose quantifies energy deposited by radiation in tissue. Accurate dosimetry for PRRT requires measuring tumor and organ uptake over time.

title: What is lyophilization in radiopharmaceutical preparation?
content: Lyophilization removes water from heat-sensitive radiopharmaceutical preparations under vacuum, producing stable dry formulations reconstituted before administration.

title: What is the role of tin(II) chloride in Tc-99m radiopharmaceuticals?
content: SnCl2 reduces Tc-99m from pertechnetate to lower oxidation states that can coordinate with chelating groups in the targeting peptide.

title: What is radiochemical stability?
content: Radiochemical stability ensures the radionuclide remains securely chelated throughout its journey in vivo. Instability causes non-specific uptake in bone or liver.

title: What is the maximum tolerated dose (MTD)?
content: MTD is determined in phase I trials as the highest administered activity producing dose-limiting toxicity in no more than a fraction of patients.

title: What is dose fractionation in PRRT?
content: Fractionated PRRT allows normal tissue repair between cycles while maintaining cumulative tumor damage, improving the therapeutic ratio.

title: What is the role of peptide mass spectrometry in radiopharmaceutical development?
content: LC-MS confirms radiolabeled peptide identity, measures radiochemical purity, and monitors in vitro stability under physiological conditions.

title: What is alpha therapy vs beta therapy?
content: Alpha particles deposit high linear energy transfer over very short distances (~50-100 um), while beta particles travel further (~1 mm) with lower LET.

title: What is cumulated activity in dosimetry?
content: Cumulated activity is the time-integral of activity, representing total nuclear transformations essential for calculating absorbed dose in radionuclide therapy.

title: What is the cross-fire effect in radionuclide therapy?
content: The cross-fire effect occurs when beta particles or alpha particles travel far enough to irradiate neighboring cells, advantageous for treating heterogeneous tumors.

title: What is the theranostic pairing of Ga-68 and Lu-177?
