content: Ga-68-DOTATATE PET identifies patients with somatostatin receptor-positive tumors for enrollment in Lu-177-DOTATATE therapy, ensuring only likely responders receive treatment.

title: What is renal dosimetry in PRRT?
content: Kidneys are dose-limiting in PRRT because filtered peptides are reabsorbed by proximal tubule cells. Amino acid co-infusion reduces renal uptake.

title: What is the advantage of alpha-emitting radionuclides for micrometastatic disease?
content: Alpha particles have a range matching micrometastases size (~50-100 um), delivering highly cytotoxic radiation while sparing surrounding normal tissue.

title: What are renal protection strategies in PRRT?
content: Infusion of lysine or arginine competitively inhibits megalin-mediated tubular reabsorption, reducing kidney absorbed dose by 20-50% without affecting tumor uptake.

title: What is the role of GMP in radiopharmaceutical production?
content: GMP regulations govern every aspect of radiopharmaceutical production from facility design to documentation, ensuring each batch meets specifications for safety.

title: What is radio-HPLC used for in quality control?
content: Radio-HPLC separates the radiolabeled peptide from free radionuclide and degradation products. The radioactivity detector quantifies the desired product fraction.

title: What is the tumor sink effect in theranostics?
content: A large tumor burden may absorb enough radiotracer to reduce availability for normal organs, beneficial for reducing toxicity but potentially masking normal organ uptake.

title: What does MALDI stand for?
content: MALDI uses a laser-absorbing matrix to desorb and ionize peptides from a solid surface, producing primarily singly charged ions ideal for mass fingerprinting.

title: What does ESI stand for in mass spectrometry?
content: ESI generates multiply charged peptide ions from solution by applying high voltage to a capillary, producing charged droplets that undergo Coulombic fission.

title: What is the mass-to-charge ratio (m/z)?
content: m/z is the fundamental observable in mass spectrometry. For multiply charged ions, m/z = (M + nH)/n, allowing large peptides to be measured.

title: What is peptide mass fingerprinting (PMF)?
content: PMF compares the set of peptide masses from enzymatic digestion with predicted masses from protein databases for identification.

title: Which enzyme is most commonly used for proteolytic digestion in proteomics?
content: Trypsin cleaves at Lys and Arg residues, producing peptides of 6-20 residues ideal for mass spectrometry analysis.

title: What is tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS)?
content: MS/MS selects a specific precursor ion, fragments it, and analyzes the resulting product ions, providing sequence information for peptide identification.

title: What is a time-of-flight (TOF) mass analyzer?
content: TOF analyzers accelerate ions with a fixed potential and measure their flight time. Lighter ions arrive first. Modern reflectron TOF achieves >20,000 resolution.

title: What is a mass spectrum?
content: A mass spectrum displays ion abundance (y-axis) versus m/z (x-axis). Each peak represents an ion species with intensity reflecting relative abundance.

title: What is the role of a matrix in MALDI?
content: The MALDI matrix co-crystallizes with the peptide, absorbs laser pulse energy, and facilitates vaporization and proton transfer to generate peptide ions.

title: What does CID produce in peptide MS/MS?
content: CID cleaves peptide backbone amide bonds, producing b-ions (N-terminal) and y-ions (C-terminal). Mass differences between consecutive ions reveal the amino acid sequence.

title: What is the role of a quadrupole mass filter?
content: Quadrupoles use oscillating DC and RF voltages to create stable trajectories for specific m/z values, filtering out other ions for selected ion monitoring.

title: What is an Orbitrap mass analyzer?
content: The Orbitrap traps ions in an electrostatic field around a spindle electrode. Ions orbit at frequencies inversely proportional to sqrt(m/z), yielding ultra-high resolution.

title: What is the role of a database search engine like Mascot?
content: Search engines correlate experimental MS/MS spectra with in silico-generated spectra from protein sequence databases, scoring matches for identification.

title: What is the y-ion series in peptide MS/MS?
content: y-ions retain the C-terminal portion of the peptide after backbone amide bond cleavage, complementary to b-ions for sequence determination.

title: What is a tryptic missed cleavage?
content: Missed cleavages occur when trypsin fails to cleave at Lys or Arg, often due to neighboring proline residues, producing longer peptides with characteristic mass shifts.

title: What is an LC-MS/MS workflow?
content: LC-MS/MS couples nano- or micro-LC separation of peptides with online tandem mass spectrometry for comprehensive proteome analysis.

title: What is false discovery rate (FDR) in proteomics?
content: FDR is estimated using target-decoy database searching. A 1% FDR means approximately 1 in 100 identifications may be incorrect.

title: What is dynamic exclusion in MS/MS acquisition?
content: Dynamic exclusion prevents redundant MS/MS of the same precursor by temporarily excluding it from selection, maximizing unique peptides fragmented.

title: What is quantitative proteomics?
content: Quantitative proteomics measures protein abundance changes using methods like label-free quantification, SILAC, TMT, or iTRAQ to discover biological differences.

title: What is the role of peptide charge state in ESI?
content: In ESI, peptides acquire multiple charges primarily at Arg, Lys, His, and the N-terminus. Larger, more basic peptides carry higher charges, reducing m/z into analyzer range.

title: What is the resolution of a mass analyzer?
content: Resolution describes the ability to separate closely spaced m/z values. Higher resolution reduces spectral complexity and improves confident peptide identification.

title: What is ETD (electron transfer dissociation)?
content: ETD transfers electrons to multiply charged peptide cations, causing N-Calpha backbone cleavage while preserving labile modifications like phosphorylation.

title: What is ion mobility spectrometry (IMS) coupled to MS used for?
content: IMS separates ions based on collision cross-section in a drift gas, providing an additional separation dimension based on peptide shape and size.

title: What is the advantage of MALDI-TOF/TOF over MALDI-TOF?
content: MALDI-TOF/TOF uses the first TOF for precursor selection and a second TOF for product ion analysis, enabling peptide sequencing from MALDI-generated ions.

title: What is de novo peptide sequencing?
content: De novo sequencing reads the amino acid sequence from consecutive b- or y-ion mass differences without requiring a reference database.

title: What is proton transfer charge reduction in ESI?
content: Proton transfer reactions reduce charge states of ESI ions, simplifying spectra of large peptides/proteins by reducing overlapping isotope envelopes.

title: What is SWATH/DIA (data-independent acquisition)?
content: DIA fragments all ions in predefined wide m/z windows, creating comprehensive spectral libraries for reproducible, quantitative proteome measurements.

title: What is trapped ion mobility spectrometry (TIMS)?
content: TIMS traps and releases ions based on mobility, providing ion focusing that concentrates ions before release, improving sensitivity and adding cross-section information.

title: What is top-down proteomics?
content: Top-down proteomics analyzes intact proteins by MS/MS, preserving information about proteoforms and combinatorial post-translational modifications.

title: What is the role of internal calibration in mass accuracy?
content: Internal calibration uses known m/z values acquired simultaneously to correct mass measurement errors, achieving sub-ppm accuracy.

title: What does SPPS stand for?
content: Solid-phase peptide synthesis builds peptides by sequential coupling reactions while anchored to an insoluble resin, enabling easy purification by filtration.

title: Who developed the original SPPS methodology?
content: Robert Bruce Merrifield invented SPPS in 1963, receiving the 1984 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for this revolutionary approach to peptide synthesis.

title: What is the role of the resin in SPPS?
content: The resin provides an insoluble solid support. The first amino acid is attached, and the peptide chain grows from C-terminus to N-terminus.

title: What is the Fmoc protecting group strategy?
content: Fmoc is removed by 20% piperidine in DMF, while side chain protecting groups (tBu, Boc, Trt) are stable to base and cleaved by TFA at the end.

title: What is the Boc protecting group strategy?
content: The Boc strategy uses TFA for repeated N-alpha deprotection and anhydrous HF for final resin cleavage, the original SPPS strategy before Fmoc became dominant.

title: What is a coupling reagent in SPPS?
content: Coupling reagents like HBTU, HATU, and DIC/HOBt activate the amino acid carboxyl group as an active ester for peptide bond formation.

title: What is piperidine used for in Fmoc-SPPS?
content: 20% piperidine in DMF removes the Fmoc group via beta-elimination, exposing the free amine for the next coupling cycle.

title: What is TFA (trifluoroacetic acid) used for in SPPS?
content: TFA simultaneously cleaves the acid-labile linker connecting the peptide to the resin and removes acid-labile side chain protecting groups like tBu and Trt.

title: What is a scavenger in SPPS cleavage cocktail?
content: Scavengers like water, triisopropylsilane, and EDT react with cationic byproducts (tert-butyl cations) generated during TFA cleavage, preventing re-attachment to the peptide.

title: What is a Rink amide resin used for?
content: Rink amide linker releases the peptide with a C-terminal amide upon TFA cleavage, mimicking the natural C-terminal amidation found in many bioactive peptides.

title: What is the concept of coupling efficiency in SPPS?
content: Coupling efficiency (typically 99-99.9%) determines overall yield. For a 30-mer, 99% efficiency per step yields ~74% overall, while 99.9% yields ~97%.

title: What is a Kaiser test?
content: The Kaiser (ninhydrin) test detects free primary amines on the resin. A negative (yellow) result confirms complete coupling; blue/purple indicates incomplete coupling.

title: What is the role of HOBt (hydroxybenzotriazole) in peptide coupling?
content: HOBt forms an active ester with the activated amino acid, reducing racemization and suppressing oxazolone formation while accelerating the coupling reaction.

title: What is pseudoproline dipeptide in SPPS?
content: Pseudoprolines are backbone-protecting dipeptides (e.g., Fmoc-Xaa-Pro-OMe) that introduce local conformational disruption, preventing aggregation during synthesis of aggregation-prone sequences.

title: What is DIC (N,N'-diisopropylcarbodiimide) used for?
content: DIC activates amino acid carboxyl groups as O-acylisourea intermediates that react with HOBt to form active esters, facilitating efficient amide bond formation.

title: What is the role of DCM (dichloromethane) in SPPS?
content: DCM is the primary swelling solvent in SPPS that expands the resin beads, making the linker and growing peptide chain accessible to incoming reagents and coupling reagents.

title: What is a Wang resin?
content: Wang resin contains a p-alkoxybenzyl alcohol linker that releases C-terminal carboxylic acid peptides upon TFA cleavage, one of the most common SPPS resins.

title: What is the concept of double coupling in SPPS?
content: Double coupling repeats the coupling step for the same amino acid when initial coupling is incomplete, as detected by the Kaiser test, ensuring maximal incorporation.

title: What is the role of DMF (dimethylformamide) in SPPS?
content: DMF is a polar aprotic solvent that dissolves amino acids, coupling reagents, and byproducts. It is used during coupling, deprotection, and washing steps.

title: What is a ChloroTrityl chloride resin?
content: ChloroTrityl chloride resin allows peptide cleavage with very mild acid (1% TFA), preserving acid-labile side chain protecting groups for fragment condensation strategies.

title: What is microwave-assisted SPPS?
content: Microwave heating accelerates SPPS reactions by providing rapid, uniform heating, reducing aggregation, improving coupling efficiency, and shortening overall synthesis time.

title: What is peptide racemization and why is it a concern in SPPS?
content: Racemization converts L-amino acids to D-enantiomers during activation, creating diastereomeric impurities. It is minimized by using HOBt and avoiding prolonged activation.

title: What is the safety-catch resin principle?
content: Safety-catch resins (e.g., trisaccharide handle) are stable to standard SPPS conditions and require specific chemical activation (e.g., diazomethane) before TFA cleavage.

title: What is the advantage of automated peptide synthesizers?
content: Automated synthesizers standardize all synthesis parameters including reagent volumes, coupling times, temperature, and mixing, improving reproducibility and throughput.

title: What is the concept of on-resin head-to-tail cyclization?
content: On-resin cyclization activates the C-terminal carboxyl group (on resin) for intramolecular reaction with the N-terminal amine, forming a cyclic peptide before cleavage.

title: What is the role of chain length in SPPS difficulty?
content: SPPS difficulty increases exponentially with chain length. A 30-mer with 99% efficiency per step yields ~74%, while a 50-mer yields only ~61%, making purification increasingly challenging.

title: What is the oxazolone problem in SPPS?
content: Oxazolones form when activated serine or threonine residues undergo intramolecular cyclization, leading to racemization at the alpha-carbon and deletion sequences.

title: What is fragment condensation in peptide synthesis?
content: Fragment condensation synthesizes protected peptide fragments (5-20 residues) by SPPS, then couples them sequentially, enabling synthesis of longer peptides with reduced deletion products.

title: What is the role of urea formation in SPPS?
content: N-acylurea (urea) forms from O-acylisourea rearrangement, representing dead-end byproducts that consume coupling reagent and reduce effective amino acid activation.

title: What is native chemical ligation (NCL)?
content: NCL joins unprotected peptide segments through a chemoselective reaction between a C-terminal thioester and N-terminal cysteine, forming a native peptide bond after S-to-N acyl shift.

title: What is the role of DMAP in SPPS esterification steps?
content: DMAP (4-dimethylaminopyridine) catalyzes esterification reactions for loading the first amino acid onto hydroxyl-containing resins like Wang or HMPB resins.

title: What is a peptide library?
content: Peptide libraries are collections of many different peptides used to identify sequences with desired binding, catalytic, or biological properties.

title: What is combinatorial peptide chemistry?
content: Combinatorial chemistry uses split-and-mix or parallel synthesis strategies to generate large numbers of peptide variants simultaneously, maximizing diversity with minimal effort.

title: What is phage display?
content: Phage display expresses peptide sequences as fusions with phage coat proteins, enabling biopanning to select high-affinity binders from billions of variants.

title: What is the split-and-mix (SPOT) synthesis method?
content: SPOT synthesis addresses each peptide at a defined position on a cellulose membrane, enabling hundreds of peptides to be synthesized simultaneously in an array format.

title: What is biopanning in phage display?
content: Biopanning involves incubating phage libraries with immobilized target, washing unbound phage, eluting bound clones, and amplifying for the next selection round.

title: What is a random peptide library?
content: Random peptide libraries use degenerate codons (e.g., NNK) at each position to encode all 20 amino acids, generating unbiased diversity for target discovery.

title: What is a cyclic peptide library?
content: Cyclic peptide libraries constrain the peptide backbone through cyclization, reducing conformational entropy and often improving binding affinity and selectivity.

title: What is a one-bead-one-compound (OBOC) library?
content: OBOC libraries use split-and-mix synthesis so each resin bead displays a single peptide variant. After screening, positive beads are sequenced by Edman degradation or MS.

title: What is the YGGFL peptide in library contexts?
content: YGGFL (Leu-enkephalin) is an endogenous opioid peptide often used as a positive control in receptor-binding screens and peptide library assays.

title: What is the advantage of peptide arrays over solution-phase libraries?
content: Peptide arrays enable spatial addressing of each variant, allowing direct identification of active sequences by their position on the array without deconvolution.

title: What is mRNA display?
content: mRNA display covalently links mRNA to its encoded peptide via a puromycin linker, enabling in vitro selection of peptides with desired properties from large libraries.

title: What is the concept of library diversity?
content: Library diversity (e.g., 10^9 variants in phage display) determines the probability of finding rare high-affinity sequences. Higher diversity increases screening success rates.

title: What is peptide SPPS library synthesis?
content: Parallel SPPS on polymer membranes or in 96/384-well plates enables simultaneous synthesis of hundreds to thousands of peptides with defined sequences.

title: What is deconvolution in peptide library screening?
content: Deconvolution identifies individual active sequences from positive pools by systematically subdividing the library into smaller sub-pools and re-screening.

title: What is DNA-encoded chemical library (DECL) technology?
content: DECL attaches unique DNA tags to each peptide, enabling selection by affinity capture and identification by PCR amplification and DNA sequencing of the barcode.

title: What is the role of T7 phage in phage display?
content: T7 phage has a robust capsid that tolerates diverse peptide insertions. The peptide is displayed as a fusion with capsid protein E10, enabling biopanning selection.

title: What is a positional scanning library?
content: Positional scanning libraries fix one position at each of the 20 amino acids while randomizing all others, identifying the preferred residue at each position for target binding.

title: What is the affinity maturation process in library screening?
content: Affinity maturation introduces random or focused mutations around initial hit sequences and re-selects, iteratively improving binding affinity from micromolar to nanomolar or better.

title: What is a linear peptide library format?
content: Linear peptide libraries allow free conformational sampling, useful for initial screening. Hits are often subsequently cyclized to improve binding properties.

title: What is the advantage of cell-free peptide library synthesis?
content: Cell-free translation systems (e.g., PURE system) enable incorporation of non-natural amino acids and avoid toxicity constraints of cell-based expression.

title: What is a peptide library counter-screen?
content: Counter-screening against closely related targets or anti-targets eliminates peptides with poor selectivity, enriching for sequences that bind specifically to the desired target.

title: What is the role of stringency washing in library selection?
content: Stringency washing increases salt concentration, detergent, or pH across rounds to remove weakly bound peptides, progressively enriching for high-affinity binders.

title: What is yeast surface display for peptide libraries?
content: Yeast display expresses peptides on the cell surface linked to Aga2p, enabling quantitative selection by FACS based on binding affinity and expression level simultaneously.

title: What is the concept of encode-decode in DNA-encoded libraries?
content: Each chemical transformation adds a DNA barcode element, building an encyclopedic tag. After selection, sequencing the tag decodes which compound was enriched.

title: What is the challenge of library bias?
content: Library bias from codon usage, synthesis errors, differential phage amplification, or aggregation can reduce the representation of certain sequences, limiting discovery potential.

title: What is the role of next-generation sequencing in peptide library analysis?
content: NGS enables deep sequencing of billions of DNA barcodes, providing quantitative enrichment data across entire libraries rather than just top hits.

title: What is a focused peptide library?
content: Focused libraries are rationally designed around known active peptides, introducing systematic substitutions to optimize binding, selectivity, or pharmacological properties.

title: What is the Klunk screening approach?
content: Klunk screening uses fluorescently labeled targets incubated with peptide arrays, then detects binding by fluorescence imaging, enabling rapid, label-free identification of active sequences.

title: What is the advantage of cyclic vs linear peptide libraries for drug discovery?
content: Cyclic peptides show improved metabolic stability, cell permeability, and binding affinity due to pre-organized conformations that reduce the entropic cost of target binding.

title: What is the role of selection pressure in phage display?
content: Selection pressure parameters determine which clones dominate after biopanning. Lower target concentrations and harsher washes select for higher affinity binders.

title: What is a peptidomimetic?
content: Peptidomimetics are designed molecules that replicate the three-dimensional presentation of pharmacophoric groups of a peptide while improving drug-like properties.

title: Why are natural peptides often poor drugs?
content: Natural peptides face challenges including enzymatic degradation, poor membrane permeability, conformational flexibility, and rapid clearance, limiting their therapeutic utility.

title: What is a beta-peptide?
content: Beta-peptides contain beta-amino acids with an extra methylene group, forming stable helices (e.g., 14-helix) that resist proteolysis while maintaining biological activity.

title: What is a non-natural (D-) amino acid?
content: D-amino acids are enantiomers of natural L-amino acids. Peptides containing D-amino acids resist proteolytic degradation while often maintaining target binding.

title: What is N-methylation of peptide bonds?
content: N-methylation replaces amide NH with N-CH3, reducing the number of H-bond donors, increasing lipophilicity and membrane permeability, and blocking protease recognition.

title: What is a peptoid?
content: Peptoids (N-substituted glycines) have side chains on the nitrogen atom rather than the alpha-carbon, making them resistant to proteases and membrane-permeable.

title: What is the concept of bioisosteric replacement in peptidomimetics?
content: Bioisosteric replacement substitutes functional groups with chemically similar moieties that maintain essential interactions while improving pharmacokinetic properties.

title: What is an alpha,alpha-disubstituted amino acid?
content: Alpha,alpha-disubstituted amino acids (e.g., Aib) restrict phi/psi angles, stabilizing specific secondary structures like helices and reducing conformational entropy.

title: What is a turn mimetic?
content: Turn mimetics are non-peptide scaffolds that reproduce the i->i+3 spatial arrangement of beta-turns, which are critical recognition elements in many bioactive peptides.

title: What is the advantage of stapled peptides?
content: Stapled peptides use a hydrocarbon bridge between i, i+4 or i, i+7 positions to lock the alpha-helical conformation, enhancing protease resistance, cell penetration, and target binding.

title: What is a macrocyclic peptidomimetic?
content: Macrocyclic scaffolds (e.g., resorc[4]arenes, oxazolidinones) present pharmacophoric groups in spatial orientations mimicking bioactive peptide conformations.

title: What is the concept of conformational restriction in peptidomimetics?
content: Conformational restriction through cyclization, stapling, or non-natural amino acids reduces the entropic cost of binding by pre-organizing the peptide in its bioactive conformation.

title: What is a dipeptide mimetic?
content: Dipeptide mimetics use small molecule scaffolds (e.g., aminomethylenebenzamides) to present two amino acid side chains in the correct spatial relationship for target recognition.

title: What is a triazole peptide mimic?
content: 1,2,3-Triazoles, formed by copper-catalyzed azide-alkyne cycloaddition, serve as amide bond mimics with improved metabolic stability, rigidity, and dipole moment similar to peptide bonds.

title: What is the concept of the pharmacophore in peptidomimetic design?
content: The pharmacophore defines essential 3D arrangement of functional groups for target binding. Peptidomimetics reproduce this arrangement on non-peptide scaffolds.

title: What is a urea or thiourea linkage in peptidomimetics?
content: Urea (NH-CO-NH) and thiourea (NH-CS-NH) linkages replace amide bonds, providing additional H-bond donors/acceptors and restricted conformational freedom.

title: What is the advantage of beta-turn peptidomimetics in drug design?
content: Beta-turn mimetics reproduce the spatial arrangement of pharmacophores in a compact, drug-like scaffold with improved oral bioavailability and protease resistance.

title: What is an spirocyclic scaffold in peptidomimetics?
content: Spirocyclic scaffolds share one spiro-atom between two rings, creating rigid three-dimensional frameworks that position pharmacophoric groups in defined spatial orientations.

title: What is the Lipinski rule of five and how do peptidomimetics address it?
content: Lipinski's rules predict oral absorption. Peptidomimetics reduce H-bond donors (N-methylation), increase lipophilicity, and constrain flexibility to improve drug-like properties.

title: What is the role of computational design in peptidomimetics?
content: Computational methods (docking, molecular dynamics, de novo design) predict peptidomimetic-target interactions, optimizing scaffold choice and substituent placement for maximal activity.

title: What is an oligoamide peptidomimetic?
content: Oligoamides (e.g., aminopyrrolinone amides, diamide oligomers) use non-amino acid building blocks connected by amide bonds, creating foldamers that mimic peptide secondary structures.

title: What is a foldamer in the context of peptidomimetics?
content: Foldamers are oligomers (beta-peptides, peptoids, aromatic amides) that spontaneously adopt defined helical, sheet, or turn structures, mimicking protein secondary structure elements.

title: What is the concept of privileged scaffolds in peptidomimetic design?
content: Privileged scaffolds (e.g., benzodiazepines, biphenyls, indoles) are versatile frameworks that repeatedly appear in bioactive molecules, providing starting points for peptidomimetic design.

title: What is a constrained amino acid in peptidomimetics?
content: Constrained amino acids (e.g., ACPC, pipecolic acid, proline analogs) restrict backbone dihedral angles, stabilizing specific secondary structures in the modified peptide.

title: What is the concept of proteomimetics?
content: Proteomimetics are small molecules that reproduce the spatial arrangement of key residues on protein surfaces (helices, sheets), disrupting disease-relevant protein-protein interactions.

title: What is the advantage of aromatic oligoamide foldamers?
content: Aromatic oligoamide foldamers form stable helical structures through steric and electronic effects, providing rigid scaffolds for presenting pharmacophoric groups while resisting enzymatic degradation.

title: What is a scaffold hopping strategy in peptidomimetic design?
content: Scaffold hopping replaces the original peptide backbone with a structurally different framework while maintaining the critical spatial arrangement of binding groups.

title: What is the role of protease resistance in peptidomimetic selection?
content: Protease resistance directly determines in vivo half-life and bioavailability. Peptidomimetics with improved resistance show longer duration of action and reduced dosing frequency.

title: What is a glycopeptide mimetic?
content: Glycopeptide mimetics use non-peptide scaffolds to present carbohydrate pharmacophores, enabling inhibition of carbohydrate-binding proteins without the instability of natural glycopeptides.

title: What does GMP stand for in pharmaceutical manufacturing?
content: Good Manufacturing Practice ensures pharmaceutical products are consistently produced and controlled according to quality standards.

title: What regulatory agency oversees drug approval in the United States?
content: The Food and Drug Administration evaluates the safety, efficacy, and quality of drugs before allowing them on the US market.

title: What is an IND application?
content: An IND application must be submitted to the FDA before initiating clinical trials, providing preclinical safety data and manufacturing information.

title: What is a NDA in drug development?
content: A New Drug Application is submitted to the FDA requesting approval to market a drug, containing comprehensive data from preclinical and clinical studies.

title: What is the role of a Qualified Person in EU manufacturing?
content: The QP is legally responsible for certifying that each batch has been manufactured in accordance with GMP and the marketing authorization.

title: What does ICH stand for?
content: ICH develops guidelines for drug development and registration across regulatory authorities worldwide.

title: What is a Certificate of Analysis?
content: A CoA provides analytical test results confirming a batch meets predefined specifications.

title: What is an excipient in peptide drug formulation?
content: Excipients are inactive ingredients that serve as vehicles, carriers, or stabilizers for the active peptide drug.

title: What is a drug substance vs drug product?
content: The drug substance is the pure active peptide. The drug product is the final formulated dosage form ready for patient administration.

title: What is a batch record in GMP manufacturing?
content: Batch records provide complete documentation of the manufacturing process for each batch produced.

title: What is a Change Control procedure?
content: Change Control ensures modifications to validated processes are properly evaluated, documented, and approved before implementation.

title: What is a Corrective and Preventive Action (CAPA)?
content: CAPA addresses root causes of quality issues and implements measures to prevent potential problems, ensuring continuous improvement.

title: What is a stability study?
content: Stability studies determine how quality varies with time under storage conditions, establishing shelf life.

title: What is process validation?
content: Process validation demonstrates consistent production of drug product meeting quality attributes through prospective, concurrent, and retrospective validation.

title: What is a Quality Management System (QMS)?
content: A QMS encompasses all activities that determine quality policy, objectives, and responsibilities.

title: What is a Deviation in GMP?
content: Deviations are unplanned departures from standard procedures that must be documented, investigated, and assessed for impact on product quality.

title: What is the role of an Ethics Committee in clinical trials?
content: Ethics Committees review clinical trial protocols to ensure participant safety, informed consent, and ethical conduct of research.

title: What is a Master Batch Record?
content: The MBR is a controlled template defining all manufacturing instructions and in-process controls for producing a specific drug product.

title: What is Cleaning Validation?
content: Cleaning validation demonstrates that cleaning procedures reliably remove previous product residues to acceptable levels.

title: What is supply chain integrity?
content: Supply chain integrity encompasses traceability, authentication, and quality assurance throughout the pharmaceutical distribution chain.

title: What is a Type II Drug Master File?
content: A Type II DMF contains detailed manufacturing, processing, packaging, and storage information for drug substances.

title: What is a Qualified Person for Pharmacovigilance?
content: The QPPV is responsible for establishing and maintaining the pharmacovigilance system and ensuring adverse drug reactions are reported.

title: What is a Risk-Based Approach to GMP compliance?
content: Risk-based approaches prioritize quality controls based on risk assessment, focusing resources on critical quality attributes with greatest impact.

title: What is QbD (Quality by Design)?
content: QbD involves defining a Quality Target Product Profile, identifying Critical Quality Attributes, and developing a design space based on process understanding.

title: What is post-marketing surveillance?
content: Post-marketing surveillance monitors drug safety in the general population, detecting rare adverse events not seen in clinical trials.

title: What is a comparability exercise for biosimilars?
content: Comparability exercises use extensive analytical, functional, and clinical studies to demonstrate no clinically meaningful differences from the reference product.

title: What is Data Integrity in GMP?
content: ALCOA+ principles ensure all GMP data is trustworthy, traceable, and suitable for regulatory review.

title: What is the EMA?
content: EMA evaluates marketing authorization applications, monitors drug safety, and provides scientific advice across EU member states.

title: What is a Pediatric Investigation Plan?
content: A PIP describes the planned pediatric clinical development program required before requesting marketing authorization for adult indications.

title: What is a Reference Listed Drug?
content: The RLD is the listed drug product that serves as the reference standard for ANDA submissions.

title: What is a biowaiver in generic drug development?
content: Biowaivers allow skipping bioequivalence studies for BCS Class I drugs with rapidly dissolving formulations.

title: What is immunogenicity in peptide therapeutics?
content: Immunogenicity refers to the tendency of a therapeutic peptide to provoke antibody formation, which can reduce efficacy or cause adverse effects.

title: What does ADME stand for?
content: ADME describes the four key pharmacokinetic processes determining drug concentration at the site of action.

title: What is the main route of peptide degradation in the body?
content: Peptides are primarily degraded by endopeptidases and exopeptidases in blood and tissues, limiting their in vivo half-life.

title: What is an acute toxicity study?
content: Acute toxicity studies determine toxic effects of a single high dose, providing initial safety information including maximum tolerated dose.

title: What is the NOAEL?
content: The NOAEL is the highest exposure level with no treatment-related adverse effects, used to set safe human doses.

title: What is a peptide's typical plasma half-life?
content: Unmodified peptides typically have half-lives of minutes to hours due to renal filtration and enzymatic degradation.

title: What is the safety factor in risk assessment?
content: Safety factors (typically 100x) are applied to the NOAEL to derive a safe starting dose for human trials.

title: What is a local tolerance study?
content: Local tolerance studies assess tissue damage and inflammation at the injection site, important for injectable formulations.

title: What is a maximum tolerated dose study?
content: MTD studies identify the dose at which unacceptable toxicity occurs, informing starting dose design for first-in-human trials.

title: What is a hypersensitivity reaction to a peptide drug?
content: Hypersensitivity reactions involve IgE-mediated or T-cell-mediated immune responses requiring discontinuation of therapy.

title: What is a therapeutic window?
content: The therapeutic window defines the concentration range where a drug is effective without being toxic.

title: What is hepatotoxicity assessment?
content: Hepatotoxicity assessment includes liver histopathology, serum ALT/AST, bilirubin measurements to detect liver damage during preclinical studies.

title: What is in silico toxicology prediction?
content: In silico methods use QSAR models and machine learning to predict potential toxicity from molecular structure alone.

title: What is a dose-response relationship?
content: Dose-response curves characterize the relationship between drug concentration and effect, yielding EC50 and Emax parameters.

title: What is a repeat-dose toxicity study?
content: Repeat-dose studies identify target organs of toxicity and establish NOAEL for chronic dosing.

title: What is pharmacovigilance?
content: Pharmacovigilance encompasses post-marketing surveillance including adverse event reporting and risk assessment.

title: What is immunotoxicity assessment?
content: Immunotoxicity studies assess unwanted immunosuppression, hypersensitivity, or autoimmune reactions through functional analyses.

title: What is a reproductive toxicity study?
content: Reproductive toxicity studies evaluate effects on fertility, teratogenicity, and pre/postnatal development required for marketing authorization.

title: What is a safety margin?
content: Safety margin = toxic dose / therapeutic dose. A larger margin indicates greater separation between effective and harmful doses.

title: What is genotoxicity testing?
content: Genotoxicity studies (Ames test, micronucleus assay) screen for DNA-damaging potential, informing risk assessment.

title: What is organ-on-a-chip in toxicology?
content: Organ-on-a-chip technology creates physiologically relevant models for predicting human toxicity without animal testing.

title: What is a reactive metabolite assessment?
content: Reactive metabolites can covalently modify cellular macromolecules, triggering immune responses or direct cytotoxicity.

title: What is the therapeutic index?
content: TI = TD50/ED50. A TI greater than 1 indicates the drug is effective at doses below toxic levels.

title: What is a micronucleus assay?
content: The micronucleus assay detects clastogenic and aneugenic events by counting extra-nuclear DNA bodies in dividing cells.

title: What is cumulative toxicity?
content: Cumulative toxicity occurs when repeated low-dose exposures progressively damage tissues through accumulation.

title: What is a dermal sensitization test?
content: Dermal sensitization tests identify chemicals causing allergic contact dermatitis, important for topical formulations.

title: What is an ADME-Tox integrated assessment?
content: Integrated ADME-Tox assessments combine PK parameters with toxicity data to predict safe human dosing.

title: What is a carcinogenicity bioassay?
content: Carcinogenicity studies are required for drugs anticipated for chronic use, unless waived based on negative genotoxicity results.

title: What is a human relevant safety biomarker?
content: Safety biomarkers (troponin for cardiotoxicity, KIM-1 for nephrotoxicity) enable early detection of organ-specific toxicity.

title: What is lyophilization?
content: Lyophilization removes water by sublimation under vacuum, producing stable dry powders of heat-sensitive peptides.

title: Why are peptides often formulated as lyophilized powders?
content: Lyophilization removes water, dramatically slowing degradation reactions and extending shelf life from weeks to years.

title: What is a lyoprotectant?
content: Lyoprotectants like sucrose and trehalose form a glassy matrix preventing peptide unfolding and aggregation during drying.

title: What is a peptide formulation excipient?
content: Excipients serve as buffers, stabilizers, tonicity agents, and preservatives for the active peptide drug.

title: What is a pre-filled syringe formulation?
content: Pre-filled syringes improve dosing accuracy, patient compliance, and reduce contamination risk.

title: What is a controlled-release peptide formulation?
content: Controlled-release formulations release peptide gradually over days to months, reducing dosing frequency.

title: What is the glass transition temperature (Tg) in lyophilization?
content: Tg is critical: the product must be kept below collapse temperature during primary drying to prevent structural collapse.

title: What is a microsphere formulation?
content: PLGA microspheres encapsulate peptides, releasing them slowly as the polymer degrades by hydrolysis.

title: What is reconstitution of a lyophilized peptide?
content: Reconstitution involves adding sterile diluent to dissolve the lyophilized cake before injection.

title: What is the role of a buffering agent?
content: Buffers maintain formulation pH. Peptides are most stable at specific pH values, and pH excursions accelerate degradation.

title: What is peptide aggregation in formulation?
content: Peptide aggregation reduces active monomer concentration, may cause immunogenicity, and is promoted by agitation and interfaces.

title: What is an adsorption isotherm in formulation?
content: Adsorption isotherms characterize peptide binding to container surfaces, which can reduce delivered dose.

title: What is the role of polysorbate in peptide formulations?
content: Polysorbates adsorb at interfaces, preventing peptide unfolding and aggregation caused by surface exposure.

title: What is a stability-indicating method?
content: Stability-indicating methods must resolve the peptide from all degradation products for accurate potency determination.

title: What is a depot injection formulation?
content: Depot injections use oil-based solutions, microspheres, or implants to provide sustained peptide release.

title: What is the role of osmolality in parenteral formulations?
content: Parenteral formulations must be isotonic to prevent pain, cell damage, and hemolysis at the injection site.

title: What are peptide-container interactions?
content: Peptides adsorb to glass and plastic surfaces, requiring careful container-closure selection and compatibility testing.

title: What is spray-drying in peptide formulation?
content: Spray drying rapidly converts peptide solutions into dry powders with controlled particle size for pulmonary delivery.

title: What is a cold chain?
content: Cold chain logistics maintain temperature-sensitive peptides throughout manufacturing, storage, and transportation.

title: What is a formulation design space?
content: The formulation design space defines ranges of parameters within which product quality is assured, following QbD principles.

title: What is a peptide implant?
content: Peptide implants provide continuous release over 6-12 months, eliminating frequent injections and improving adherence.

title: What is shelf life determination?
content: Shelf life is determined from stability studies, defined as the time the product meets all quality specifications.

title: What is the role of stabilizing excipients in lyophilization?
content: Stabilizers like sucrose form amorphous matrices that immobilize peptides, preventing molecular mobility and degradation during storage.

title: What is a forced degradation study?
content: Forced degradation studies identify degradation products and pathways under accelerated conditions, informing formulation design.

title: What is peptide formulation for pulmonary delivery?
content: Pulmonary delivery requires particles of 1-5 um aerodynamic diameter for alveolar deposition.

title: What is the role of EDTA in peptide formulations?
content: EDTA binds trace metal ions that catalyze oxidative degradation and metal-catalyzed aggregation of peptides.

title: What is a drug-device combination product?
content: Combination products like insulin pens integrate drug and device development with coordinated regulatory submissions.

title: What is process analytical technology (PAT)?
content: PAT uses in-line analytical tools to monitor critical quality attributes during manufacturing for real-time quality assurance.

title: What is the concept of peptide conformational stability in formulation?
content: Conformational stability determines whether the peptide maintains its active conformation throughout storage, affected by pH, excipients, and temperature.

title: What is the role of chelating agents beyond EDTA?
content: Chelators prevent iron and copper-catalyzed oxidative damage that can cause peptide aggregation, fragmentation, and loss of biological activity.

title: Where are conotoxins derived from?
content: Conotoxins are small, disulfide-rich peptides from cone snail venom targeting ion channels with remarkable specificity.

title: What is the primary source of marine natural product peptides?
content: Marine sponges, tunicates, bryozoans, and marine bacteria produce diverse bioactive peptides as chemical defense mechanisms.

title: What is ziconotide (Prialt)?
content: Ziconotide blocks N-type voltage-gated calcium channels, providing potent analgesia without opioid addiction risk.

title: What unique feature do many marine peptides share?
content: Marine peptides often contain multiple disulfide bonds, D-amino acids, and modifications providing stability in harsh marine environments.

title: What class of peptides do sponges produce?
content: Sponges produce diverse bioactive peptides including dolastatins (cytotoxic) and jaspamides (actin-modifying).

title: What is dolastatin-10?
content: Dolastatin-10 is a pentapeptide whose synthetic analog MMAE is the payload in the ADC brentuximab vedotin.

title: What are cyclic peptides from marine organisms used for?
content: Marine cyclic peptides like patellamides show potent anticancer and antimicrobial activities through novel mechanisms.

title: What is the role of symbiotic bacteria in marine peptide production?
content: Many marine natural products are produced by symbiotic microorganisms rather than the host organism.

title: What is patellamide?
content: Patellamides are cyanobactins from Prochloron symbionts showing anticancer activity through metal chelation.

title: What makes marine peptides attractive for drug discovery?
content: Marine organisms produce structurally unique peptides with novel mechanisms due to evolutionary adaptation to extreme environments.

title: What is the mechanism of conotoxin selectivity?
content: Conotoxins achieve selectivity through conserved disulfide frameworks presenting variable loops in precise orientations for receptor recognition.

title: What is the classification system for conotoxins?
content: Conotoxins are classified by Greek letter: alpha (nAChR), omega (CaV), kappa (Kv), mu (NaV), etc.

title: What is jaspamide (jasplakinolide)?
content: Jaspamide stabilizes F-actin, disrupting cell motility and division. It is widely used as a cell biology research tool.

title: What is a depsipeptide?
content: Depsipeptides incorporate ester bonds alongside amide bonds, creating hybrid structures common in marine natural products.

title: What is the source of trabectedin (Yondelis)?
content: Trabectedin was isolated from tunicate E. turbinata and is now produced from the symbiotic bacterium Pseudomonas.

title: What is the role of ER processing in conotoxin maturation?
content: Conotoxins undergo extensive post-translational processing including disulfide bond formation and gamma-carboxylation in the ER.

title: What is the challenge of producing marine natural product peptides?
content: Marine organisms are difficult to collect sustainably, requiring total synthesis, semi-synthesis, or heterologous expression for supply.

title: What is marine chemical ecology in drug discovery?
content: Marine organisms use bioactive peptides for defense, and these ecological functions translate to potential therapeutic activities.

title: What is the kahalalide peptide family?
content: Kahalalides are cyclic depsipeptides showing anticancer activity through disruption of lysosomal function.

title: What is the role of sponge peptides in ADC development?
content: Sponge-derived cytotoxins like auristatins are potent antimitotic agents conjugated to antibodies in ADCs.

title: What is aquaculture for marine drug supply?
content: Aquaculture and fermentation of symbiotic bacteria provide sustainable supply chains for marine natural product drugs.

title: What is biomimetic synthesis for marine peptides?
content: Biomimetic synthesis creates simplified analogs retaining essential pharmacophoric elements while improving drug developability.

title: What is domoic acid?
content: Domoic acid mimics glutamate, overactivating AMPA/kainate receptors and causing amnesic shellfish poisoning.

title: What is dereplication in marine natural product discovery?
content: Dereplication uses MS, NMR, and databases to rapidly identify known compounds, allowing focus on novel structures.

title: What is the role of genomics in marine peptide discovery?
content: Genome mining uncovers cryptic biosynthetic gene clusters encoding bioactive peptides not expressed under standard conditions.

title: What is blue biotechnology?
content: Blue biotechnology applies modern tools to marine biological resources for sustainable peptide drug discovery and production.

title: What is the iodine-rich peptide family from marine organisms?
content: Iodinated peptides from marine sponges show potent antimicrobial activity through enhanced hydrophobic interactions and oxidative damage to microbial membranes.

title: What is the concept of supply chain sustainability for marine drug peptides?
content: Sustainable supply chains require aquaculture, bacterial fermentation, or total synthesis to reduce pressure on wild marine organism populations while maintaining drug development pipelines.

title: What is the challenge of scale-up production for marine peptides?
content: Marine peptide scale-up faces challenges including complex multistep synthesis, low yields from natural sources, and difficulties in fermenting slow-growing marine organisms or their symbionts.

title: What are cyclotides?
content: Cyclotides are head-to-tail cyclic peptides found in plants with a conserved cystine knot motif providing extraordinary stability.

title: What is the primary function of plant defense peptides?
content: Plant defense peptides protect against fungal, bacterial, and viral pathogens, as well as insect herbivores.

title: What is a thionin in plant biology?
content: Thionins are small antimicrobial peptides with three or four disulfide bonds found in cereal seeds and leaves.

title: Where are plant defensins primarily located?
content: Plant defensins are concentrated in seeds where they protect the embryo, and also found in roots and leaves.

title: What makes cyclotides resistant to degradation?
content: The combination of cyclic backbone and cystine knot makes cyclotides resistant to heat, denaturation, and proteolytic degradation.

title: What is the knottin fold?
content: The knottin fold features three conserved disulfide bonds forming a compact, rigid core providing exceptional structural stability.

title: Which plant produces kalata B1?
content: Kalata B1 was first isolated from Oldenlandia affinis, the prototypical cyclotide with uterotonic activity.

title: What is the role of protease inhibitors in plant defense?
content: Plant protease inhibitors inhibit digestive enzymes in herbivore guts, reducing protein digestion.

title: What are nonspecific lipid transfer proteins in plants?
content: nsLTPs transfer lipids between membranes, contributing to cuticle formation and pathogen defense.

title: How do plant defensin precursors get processed?
content: Plant defensins are synthesized with a signal peptide and sometimes a propeptide that is cleaved to yield the active defensin.

title: How do cyclotides exert insecticidal activity?
content: Cyclotides insert into insect gut membranes through hydrophobic loops, causing membrane disruption and cell lysis.

title: What is the gene organization of cyclotides?
content: Cyclotides are encoded as precursor proteins containing signal peptide, prodomain, and one or multiple cyclotide domains.

title: What is the mechanism of cyclotide circularization?
content: Cyclization occurs post-translationally through AEP that cleaves the precursor and simultaneously ligates N- and C-termini.

title: What are sunflower trypsin inhibitors (SFTIs)?
content: SFTI-1 is a cyclic peptide with picomolar trypsin inhibitory activity being explored for oral peptide drugs.

title: What is the distribution of cyclotides across plant families?
content: Cyclotides are predominantly found in Violaceae and Rubiaceae, with occasional occurrences in other families.

title: What biological activities do cyclotides have beyond insecticidal?
content: Cyclotides show diverse bioactivities including antimicrobial, anti-HIV, cytotoxic, and uterotonic effects.

title: What are phytoalexins?
content: Phytoalexins are antimicrobial compounds synthesized rapidly at infection sites as chemical defense.

title: What are plant peptide hormones?
content: Plant peptide hormones (systemin, CLV3, CLE peptides) regulate cell proliferation, differentiation, and defense through receptor kinase signaling.

title: What is systemin?
content: Systemin was the first plant peptide hormone discovered, activating systemic wound responses including proteinase inhibitor production.

title: What is the cyclotide-based drug design concept?
content: The cyclotide scaffold presents bioactive sequences in a protease-resistant, orally bioavailable format.

title: What is gamma-thionin antifungal mechanism?
content: Plant gamma-thionins bind fungal membranes causing permeabilization and ion leakage through electrostatic interactions.

title: What is phytoremediation using plant defense peptides?
content: Transgenic plants expressing antimicrobial peptides show enhanced resistance while some systems are used for phytoremediation.

title: What is the role of cysteine-rich secretory proteins in plant defense?
content: CRiPs encompass diverse families using various mechanisms for plant defense including membrane binding and lipid transfer.

title: What is the concept of peptide-based biopesticides from plants?
content: Plant-derived peptides offer biodegradable, target-specific alternatives to chemical pesticides with lower environmental impact.

title: What is the evolutionary relationship between plant and animal defensins?
content: Plant and animal defensins share the cystine knot structural fold despite low sequence similarity, suggesting common ancestry.

title: What is peptide secretion in plant-microbe interactions?
content: Plants secrete antimicrobial peptides into the apoplast during pathogen attack and into the rhizosphere influencing soil microbiome.

title: What is the challenge of recombinant plant peptide production?
content: Recombinant production faces challenges including low yields, improper disulfide bond formation, and absence of plant-specific processing enzymes.

title: What is the role of antimicrobial peptide genes in transgenic crop protection?
content: Transgenic expression of plant or animal AMP genes (defensins, cecropins, thionins) in crops like rice, wheat, and tobacco enhances resistance to major fungal and bacterial diseases.

title: What is the concept of peptide-based plant disease resistance biomarkers?
content: Changes in defense peptide expression profiles can serve as early biomarkers of plant pathogen infection, enabling timely intervention in precision agriculture and reducing crop losses.

title: What is non-ribosomal peptide synthesis (NRPS)?
content: NRPS uses massive multi-modular enzyme complexes to assemble peptides from amino acid building blocks without ribosomes.

title: What is an intein?
content: Inteins are self-splicing protein elements that excise themselves and ligate flanking exteins, enabling protein-level recombination.

title: What is a RiPP?
content: RiPPs are ribosomally produced as precursor peptides then modified by dedicated enzymes to create diverse structures.

title: What is the role of a carrier protein in NRPS?
content: The peptidyl carrier protein uses a phosphopantetheine arm to tether intermediates and shuttle them between catalytic domains.

title: What is the split-intein method used for?
content: Split inteins express two fragments separately; when mixed, the intein halves reassemble and splice, ligating the exteins.

title: What is genetic code expansion?
content: Genetic code expansion uses engineered aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase/tRNA pairs to incorporate non-natural amino acids at amber stop codons.

title: What is a peptide synthetase module in NRPS?
content: Each NRPS module contains domains for amino acid activation, thiolation, and condensation, determining which amino acid is incorporated.

title: What is the role of the adenylation (A) domain?
content: The A domain recognizes a specific amino acid and activates it as an aminoacyl-AMP for transfer to the T domain.

title: What is a lantibiotic?
content: Lantibiotics are ribosomally synthesized peptides containing unusual thioether amino acids formed by dehydration and Cys addition.

title: What is the colinearity rule in NRPS?
content: In canonical NRPS, the linear arrangement of modules directly determines the linear sequence of amino acids in the product.

title: What is domain swapping in NRPS engineering?
content: Domain swapping alters substrate specificity, introduces modifications, or changes the peptide sequence by replacing specific domains.

title: What is the role of a thiolation (T) domain in NRPS?
content: The T domain uses a phosphopantetheine group to form a thioester with the intermediate, serving as a flexible tether.

title: What is a cryptic biosynthetic gene cluster?
content: Cryptic gene clusters encode biosynthetic machinery silent in laboratory conditions, revealing novel chemistry when activated.

title: What is directed evolution of NRPS?
content: Directed evolution introduces mutations then screens for improved activity, altered specificity, or enhanced production.

title: What is an engineered NRPS?
content: Engineered NRPS enzymes are redesigned through domain swapping, mutagenesis, or computational design to produce novel analogs.

title: What is the role of tailoring enzymes in RiPP biosynthesis?
content: Tailoring enzymes modify the ribosomally synthesized precursor, creating diverse structural features characteristic of RiPPs.

title: What is a lanthipeptide?
content: Lanthipeptides have thioether bridges formed by LanM enzymes that dehydrate Ser/Thr and catalyze Cys addition.

title: What is a minimal NRPS?
content: Minimal NRPS designs reduce complexity to essential catalytic units, making the system easier to engineer and express.

title: What is protein splicing used for in synthetic biology?
content: Intein-based splicing enables seamless protein ligation, affinity purification, and conditional protein activation.

title: What is a condensation (C) domain in NRPS?
content: The C domain catalyzes peptide bond formation between upstream and downstream carrier proteins, extending the chain.

title: What is a RiPP precursor peptide?
content: RiPP precursor peptides contain an N-terminal leader peptide recognized by tailoring enzymes and a C-terminal core peptide that becomes the final product.

title: What is the NRPS assembly line concept?
content: NRPS assembly lines function as molecular conveyors where growing intermediates are passed between modules in defined order.

title: What is a silent gene cluster activation strategy?
content: Silent cluster activation uses promoters, CRISPRa, heterologous hosts, or co-culture approaches to trigger expression of cryptic clusters.

title: What is combinatorial biosynthesis?
content: Combinatorial biosynthesis combines NRPS/PKS genes from different organisms to generate hybrid natural products with novel activities.

title: What is a stand-alone NRPS vs a mega-enzyme?
content: Stand-alone NRPS are individual proteins with single modules, while mega-enzymes are massive single polypeptides containing all modules.

title: What is the phosphopantetheine prosthetic group?
content: The 4'-phosphopantetheine arm is covalently attached to T/CP domains and provides flexible tethering for substrate positioning.

title: What is the thiotemplate mechanism in NRPS?
content: In thiotemplate mechanism, the NRPS enzyme determines peptide sequence using thioester-linked intermediates rather than codon-anticodon recognition.

title: What is the E (epimerization) domain function in NRPS?
content: E domains epimerize L-amino acids to D-configuration, introducing stereochemical diversity and protease resistance.

title: What is chemoenzymatic synthesis?
content: Chemoenzymatic synthesis combines chemical precision for fragment assembly with enzymatic selectivity for complex modified peptide natural products.

title: What is the role of genome mining in NRPS discovery?
content: Genome mining uses bioinformatics tools like antiSMASH to identify NRPS gene clusters and predict products for novel natural product discovery.

title: What is the concept of a minimal genome for peptide production?
content: Minimal genome approaches create streamlined chassis organisms (e.g., Mycoplasma JCVI-syn3.0 based systems) with only essential genes, freeing cellular resources for heterologous peptide production.

title: What is the concept of cell-free peptide biosynthesis?
content: Cell-free systems (PURE extract, E. coli S30 lysate) provide all necessary components for transcription and translation, enabling rapid peptide production without maintaining cell viability.

title: What is the molecular weight of glycine?
content: 75.03 Da — Glycine is the simplest amino acid with formula C₂H₅NO₂

title: What type of bond connects amino acids in a peptide?
content: Peptide bond (amide bond) — formed by dehydration synthesis between the carboxyl group of one amino acid and the amino group of the next

title: What is mass spectrometry (MS) and how is it used for peptide analysis?
content: MS ionizes peptides and measures mass-to-charge (m/z) ratio. MALDI-TOF: intact mass measurement. ESI: coupling to LC for sequencing. MS/MS (tandem): fragmentation for de novo sequencing. Key for identifying peptide modifications, disulfide bonds, and post-translational modifications.

title: What is HPLC and how does it separate peptides?
content: High-Performance Liquid Chromatography separates peptides based on hydrophobicity (reverse-phase), charge (ion-exchange), or size (SEC). RP-HPLC (C18 column) is most common. Elution uses acetonitrile/water gradient with TFA. Detection: UV at 214/220 nm (peptide bond absorption).

title: What is circular dichroism (CD) and what does it tell about peptides?
content: CD measures differential absorption of left/right circularly polarized light by chiral molecules. For peptides: far-UV CD (190-250 nm) reveals secondary structure (α-helix: 208/222 nm minima; β-sheet: 218 nm minimum; random coil: 200 nm minimum). Near-UV CD shows tertiary structure.

title: What is Edman degradation and what are its limitations?
content: Edman degradation sequentially removes N-terminal amino acids using phenylisothiocyanate (PITC). Each cycle yields a PTH-amino acid identified by HPLC. Limitations: 1) Fails at modified residues, 2) Poor with N-terminal pyroglutamate, 3) Limited to ~50 cycles, 4) Requires free N-terminus.

title: How is peptide purity determined by RP-HPLC?
content: Peptide purity is assessed by area-under-curve (AUC) percentage of the main peak in RP-HPLC. Requirements: ≥95% for research, ≥98% for clinical, ≥99% for injectable. System suitability: column efficiency >10,000 plates, tailing factor <2.0, resolution >2.0 between critical pairs.

title: What is ESI-MS/MS and how does it sequence peptides?
content: Electrospray ionization (ESI) produces multiply charged ions from solution. Collision-induced dissociation (CID) fragments peptides along the backbone, generating b/y or c/z ion series. De novo sequencing reads the mass differences between consecutive ions to determine the amino acid sequence.

title: What is MALDI-TOF MS and when is it used?
content: Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization Time-of-Flight MS uses a laser to desorb/ionize peptides co-crystallized with a matrix (CHCA, DHB). Produces predominantly singly charged ions. Used for: intact mass confirmation, peptide fingerprinting, quality control, and high-throughput screening.

title: What is a peptide map and how is it generated?
content: Peptide mapping is a fingerprint of a protein/peptide after enzymatic digestion (trypsin, chymotrypsin). The resulting peptide fragments are separated by LC and detected by MS. The map confirms identity, detects modifications, and verifies sequence integrity. USP <625> provides guidelines.

title: How is disulfide bond analysis performed on peptides?
content: Disulfide bonds are analyzed by: 1) Alkylation (iodoacetamide) of free cysteines before/after reduction, 2) Trypsin digestion under non-reducing conditions, 3) LC-MS/MS of disulfide-linked peptides, 4) Reduction with DTT/TCEP and comparison of mass shifts, 5) NMR for 3D structure.

title: What is Karl Fischer titration and why is it important for peptides?
content: Karl Fischer titration measures water content in peptide samples (critical for lyophilized peptides). Water content affects: stability (hydrolysis), reconstitution accuracy, and shelf life. Target: 1-5% for most lyophilized peptides. Over-drying can cause aggregation; too much water reduces stability.

title: What is recombinant peptide expression and what systems are used?
content: Recombinant expression produces peptides using biological systems: 1) E. coli (most common, cheap, but no PTMs), 2) Yeast (Pichia pastoris: secretion, some PTMs), 3) Mammalian cells (HEK293, CHO: full PTMs), 4) Insect cells (baculovirus: high yield), 5) Cell-free systems (rapid, toxic peptides).

title: What are inclusion bodies and how are they handled?
content: Inclusion bodies (IBs) are dense, insoluble aggregates of misfolded recombinant protein in E. coli. They form when expression rate exceeds folding capacity. Recovery: 1) Cell lysis, 2) IB washing (remove membrane proteins), 3) Solubilization (8M urea/GdnHCl), 4) Refolding (dialysis/dilution), 5) Purification.

title: What is the difference between solid-phase and solution-phase peptide synthesis at scale?
content: SPPS (up to ~100 aa): automated, good for research/small scale, waste from excess reagents. Solution-phase (large-scale): lower waste, higher cost upfront, better for >100 aa or GMP manufacturing. Hybrid approaches: segment condensation for large peptides. Industrial scale: solution-phase preferred.

title: What is directed evolution and how does it improve peptides?
content: Directed evolution mimics natural selection in the lab: 1) Create mutant library (error-prone PCR, DNA shuffling), 2) Screen/select for desired property, 3) Amplify winners, 4) Repeat cycles. Applications: improved binding affinity, stability, expression, or novel activities. Nobel Prize 2018 (Frances Arnold).

title: What is phage display and how is it used for peptide discovery?
content: Phage display links peptide phenotype to genotype by expressing peptides on bacteriophage coat proteins. Libraries of 10^9-10^10 variants are screened against targets (biopanning). Bound phages are eluted and amplified. Used for: antibody discovery, peptide ligand identification, and affinity maturation.

title: What is mRNA display and how does it differ from phage display?
content: mRNA display links mRNA to its encoded protein via puromycin. Advantages over phage: 1) Larger library (10^12-10^14), 2) No cell transformation bottleneck, 3) Can include non-natural amino acids, 4) In vitro selection faster. Used for: aptamer discovery, protein-protein interactions, peptide macrocycles.

title: What is cell-free protein synthesis (CFPS)?
content: CFPS produces proteins/peptides using cell extracts (E. coli, CHO, wheat germ) without living cells. Advantages: 1) Rapid (hours vs days), 2) Toxic peptide tolerance, 3) Non-natural amino acid incorporation, 4) Isotope labeling, 5) Membrane protein production. Commercial systems:PURE, TX-TL, NEB.

title: What is transpeptidation and how does it create novel peptide bonds?
content: Transpeptidation uses engineered enzymes (sortase A, subtiligase) to join peptide segments. Sortase A recognizes LPXTG motif, cleaves between T-G, and transfers to an oligoglycine nucleophile. Used for: PEGylation, antibody-drug conjugates, cyclic peptides, and protein ligation.

title: What is the GlycoFi technology for glycoengineered peptides?
content: GlycoFi (Merck) engineers yeast (Pichia pastoris) to produce human-like glycosylation patterns. Glycoengineering involves: 1) Knocking out yeast-specific glycosyltransferases, 2) Expressing human glycosylation enzymes, 3) Producing homogeneous, human-type glycans on therapeutic peptides/glycoproteins.

title: What is fermentation scale-up and what are the challenges?
content: Fermentation scale-up (shake flask → bioreactor → industrial) faces: 1) Oxygen transfer limitation, 2) Heat removal, 3) Mixing heterogeneity, 4) pH control, 5) Foam management, 6) Genetic instability at high cell density, 7) Downstream processing bottlenecks. Fed-batch is most common for peptides.

title: What is DPP-IV and its role in clinical peptide pharmacology?
content: Dipeptidyl peptidase IV (CD26) is a serine protease that cleaves Xaa-Pro/Xaa-Ala dipeptides from N-terminus. Key substrates: GLP-1, GIP, SDF-1α. Clinical relevance: DPP-IV inhibitors (gliptins) extend incretin half-life for diabetes. Also: T-cell activation marker, HIV co-receptor.

title: What is therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) for peptide drugs?
content: TDM measures drug levels to optimize dosing. For peptides: 1) Complex PK (non-linear for some), 2) Immunogenicity affects levels, 3) Limited validated assays. Examples: insulin (glucose monitoring), cyclosporine (LC-MS/MS), tacrolimus. Challenges: ADA interference, short half-lives, metabolite activity.

title: What is a peptide drug's pharmacokinetic profile?
content: Typical peptide PK: 1) Rapid absorption (SC/IM), 2) Distribution: limited by size/lipophilicity, 3) Metabolism: proteolytic degradation (not CYP450), 4) Excretion: renal (glomerular filtration + tubular degradation), 5) Half-life: minutes to hours (unmodified), days (PEGylated/lipidated).

title: What are the clinical considerations for peptide drug immunogenicity?
content: Immunogenicity assessment: 1) Anti-drug antibody (ADA) screening (bridging ELISA), 2) Neutralizing antibody (NAb) assays, 3) T-cell epitope prediction (in silico), 4) Risk factors: non-human sequences, aggregation, route. Impact: reduced efficacy, injection site reactions, anaphylaxis. Mitigation: humanization, formulation.

title: What is the dose-response relationship for peptide drugs?
content: Dose-response for peptides: 1) Linear PK peptides (small, unmodified): dose-proportional. 2) Non-linear PK (binding proteins, receptor-mediated clearance): saturable. 3) U-shaped curves possible (hormesis). Therapeutic window: defined by efficacy (EC50) vs toxicity (TD50). Dose titration common (insulin, GLP-1 agonists).

title: What is the role of clinical pharmacogenomics in peptide therapy?
content: Pharmacogenomics affects peptide response: 1) HLA typing (immunogenicity risk), 2) Receptor polymorphisms (drug sensitivity), 3) Metabolic enzyme variants (protease expression), 4) Disease subtypes (biomarker-guided therapy). Examples: HLA-B*5701 and abacavir (not peptide, but illustrates principle), OPRM1 variants and opioid response.

title: What are the phases of clinical trials for peptide drugs?
content: Phase I: Safety, PK, MTD in healthy volunteers (20-80). Phase II: Efficacy, dose-finding in patients (100-300). Phase III: Confirmatory, large-scale efficacy/safety (1,000-5,000). Phase IV: Post-marketing surveillance. Special considerations for peptides: 1) First-in-human starting dose calculation (NOAEL), 2) Immunogenicity monitoring.

title: What is a companion diagnostic for peptide therapeutics?
content: Companion diagnostics identify patients who will benefit from specific peptide drugs. Examples: 1) HER2 testing for trastuzumab, 2) PD-L1 expression for immunotherapy peptides, 3) Tumor mutational burden for neoantigen vaccines, 4) MSI/dMMR for pembrolizumab. Regulatory: FDA CDx approval required for some drugs.

title: What is the concept of bioequivalence for peptide biosimilars?
content: Biosimilar peptides must demonstrate: 1) Similarity to reference product (structure, function), 2) No clinically meaningful differences in efficacy/safety, 3) No increased immunogenicity. Steps: analytical similarity → functional assays → PK/PD equivalence → clinical confirmation. Key: higher similarity threshold than small-molecule generics.

title: What is the role of real-world evidence (RWE) in peptide drug development?
content: RWE from electronic health records, registries, and claims data supplements clinical trials. For peptides: 1) Long-term safety (immunogenicity, rare events), 2) Comparative effectiveness (head-to-head vs. standard care), 3) Dosing optimization in special populations, 4) Post-marketing surveillance, 5) Rare disease evidence generation.

title: What is amyloidosis in the context of peptide diseases?
content: A group of diseases caused by abnormal accumulation of misfolded amyloid fibrils (aggregated peptides/proteins) in organs and tissues, leading to organ dysfunction. Examples include AL amyloidosis (immunoglobulin light chain) and ATTR amyloidosis (transthyretin).

title: What is the role of amyloid-β (Aβ) peptide in Alzheimer's disease?
content: Aβ peptides (38-43 aa) are cleaved from amyloid precursor protein (APP) by β-secretase and γ-secretase. Aβ42 is particularly prone to aggregation, forming oligomers and fibrils that cause neurotoxicity, synaptic dysfunction, and plaques in the brain.

title: How do tau peptides relate to neurodegenerative disease?
content: Tau is a microtubule-associated protein. In Alzheimer's and tauopathies, tau becomes hyperphosphorylated, detaches from microtubules, and forms neurofibrillary tangles (NFTs). This disrupts axonal transport and leads to neuronal death.

title: What is prion disease and how does it involve peptide misfolding?
content: Prion diseases (CJD, BSE, Kuru) involve the conversion of normal cellular prion protein (PrPC) to a misfolded, infectious form (PrPSc). PrPSc induces further misfolding in a template-directed manner, causing spongiform encephalopathy.

title: What is the pathophysiology of diabetic amyloidosis?
content: Islet amyloid polypeptide (IAPP/amylin), co-secreted with insulin by pancreatic β-cells, can misfold and form amyloid deposits in type 2 diabetes. These deposits contribute to β-cell death and disease progression.

title: What are the major classes of peptide-based diseases?
content: 1) Amyloidosis (misfolded peptide aggregation), 2) Peptide deficiency syndromes (hormone insufficiency), 3) Peptide excess disorders (hormone overproduction), 4) Autoimmune peptide diseases (immune response against self-peptides), 5) Infectious peptide diseases (viral/bacterial peptide toxins).

title: What is systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) and which peptides are involved?
content: SLE is an autoimmune disease where the immune system attacks self-antigens, including nuclear peptides and DNA. Anti-Smith (anti-Sm) and anti-nuclear ribonucleoprotein (anti-nRNP) antibodies target specific peptide components.

title: How do natriuretic peptides function in heart failure?
content: ANP (atrial) and BNP (ventricular) are released in response to cardiac wall stress. They promote natriuresis, diuresis, and vasodilation. In heart failure, BNP levels rise as a compensatory mechanism and serve as diagnostic biomarkers (NT-proBNP).

title: What is the role of angiotensin II in hypertension?
content: Angiotensin II is an octapeptide produced from angiotensin I by ACE. It causes vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, sodium retention, and sympathetic activation. Excess angiotensin II is a key driver of essential hypertension and heart failure.

title: What is Cushing's syndrome in terms of peptide hormone excess?
content: Cushing's syndrome results from chronic excess cortisol (a steroid hormone regulated by CRP-ACTH-peptide axis). Causes include pituitary adenomas secreting excess ACTH (Cushing's disease), ectopic ACTH production, or exogenous glucocorticoid use.

title: What is the role of substance P in chronic pain?
content: Substance P (SP, neuropeptide) is an undecapeptide released from sensory neurons. It binds NK1 receptors, mediating pain signal transmission, neurogenic inflammation, and central sensitization. SP antagonists are being developed as novel analgesics.

title: What is the mechanism of opioid peptide dysfunction in addiction?
content: Endogenous opioids (endorphins, enkephalins, dynorphins) modulate pain and reward. Chronic exogenous opioid use downregulates these systems, causing tolerance. Withdrawal reveals deficient endogenous signaling, driving compulsive drug-seeking behavior.

title: How do cytokine peptides contribute to sepsis?
content: Sepsis involves a dysregulated immune response to infection. Pro-inflammatory peptides (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) trigger a cytokine storm, causing vasodilation, increased vascular permeability, DIC, and multi-organ failure. Anti-inflammatory peptides (IL-10) attempt to counterbalance.

title: What is the pathophysiology of rheumatoid arthritis at the peptide level?
content: In RA, citrullinated peptides (modified arginine residues by PAD enzymes) are recognized as neoantigens. Anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA) form immune complexes, activating complement and recruiting inflammatory cells to joints, causing synovitis and erosion.

title: What is the role of VEGF peptides in cancer angiogenesis?
content: Vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) peptides promote new blood vessel formation (angiogenesis) essential for tumor growth beyond 1-2mm. Tumor cells overexpress VEGF under hypoxia (HIF-1α activation), recruiting endothelial cells and creating tumor vasculature.

title: How do antimicrobial peptide deficiencies contribute to skin diseases?
content: Deficiencies in cathelicidin (LL-37) and β-defensins compromise skin innate immunity. In atopic dermatitis, reduced AMP levels allow bacterial colonization (S. aureus), exacerbating inflammation. Psoriasis shows paradoxically elevated LL-37, driving autoimmune activation.

title: What is the role of ghrelin in wasting syndromes?
content: Ghrelin (28 aa), produced by gastric cells, is the hunger hormone. It stimulates appetite via GHSR1a in the hypothalamus and promotes GH release. In cancer cachexia and heart failure, ghrelin signaling is disrupted, contributing to pathological weight loss.

title: What is the mechanism of amylin aggregation in type 2 diabetes?
content: Amylin (IAPP) co-aggregates with insulin in β-cell granules. In T2DM, amylin misfolds into toxic oligomers and amyloid fibrils, disrupting β-cell membranes, triggering ER stress, and inducing apoptosis. Human amylin is more amyloidogenic than rodent amylin.

title: How do neuropeptide Y (NPY) levels relate to anxiety disorders?
