content: NPY is an anxiolytic neuropeptide that counteracts CRF (corticotropin-releasing factor) in the amygdala. Low NPY levels are associated with increased anxiety, PTSD, and depression. NPY analogs are being developed as novel anxiolytic therapeutics.

title: What is the role of endothelin-1 in pulmonary hypertension?
content: Endothelin-1 (ET-1, 21 aa) is the most potent vasoconstrictor known. It acts on ETA (constriction, proliferation) and ETB (vasodilation, clearance) receptors. In pulmonary hypertension, ET-1 is overexpressed, causing vascular remodeling and elevated pulmonary pressures.

title: What is enzyme kinetics and how does it relate to peptide substrates?
content: Enzyme kinetics studies reaction rates of enzymes with substrates. Michaelis-Menten: v = Vmax[S]/(Km+[S]). For peptide substrates: Km reflects binding affinity, kcat reflects catalytic efficiency, kcat/Km reflects specificity. Protease specificity pockets determine preferred cleavage sites.

title: What are the classes of proteases that degrade peptides?
content: Four classes by catalytic mechanism: 1) Serine proteases (trypsin, chymotrypsin - serine nucleophile), 2) Metalloproteases (MMPs, ACE - metal ion catalysis), 3) Aspartyl proteases (pepsin, HIV protease - aspartate catalysis), 4) Cysteine proteases (caspases, papain - cysteine nucleophile).

title: What is a transition state analog and how is it used in drug design?
content: Transition state analogs mimic the high-energy transition state of enzymatic reactions. They bind enzymes 10^3-10^6 times tighter than substrates. Examples: HIV protease inhibitors (darunavir), renin inhibitors (aliskiren). Design: structural analysis of TS geometry → synthesis of stable analogs.

title: What is allosteric regulation of enzymes acting on peptides?
content: Allosteric regulation involves effector binding at a site distinct from the active site, causing conformational changes. For proteases: 1) Zymogen activation (trypsinogen → trypsin), 2) Product inhibition, 3) Cooperative effects (caspase activation cascades), 4) Regulatory subunit binding.

title: What is substrate-competitive vs mechanism-based inhibition?
content: Competitive inhibitors (Ki) bind the active site, competing with substrate. Mechanism-based inhibitors (kcat/KI) are processed by the enzyme to form a reactive intermediate that inactivates it. Example: irreversible protease inhibitors (pepstatin for aspartyl proteases). Advantage of mechanism-based: selectivity.

title: What is the catalytic triad and how does it work in serine proteases?
content: The catalytic triad (Ser-His-Asp) is a charge-relay system: 1) His abstracts proton from Ser (nucleophile activated), 2) Ser attacks peptide carbonyl (tetrahedral intermediate), 3) Asp stabilizes His+, 4) Amine leaving group departs (acyl-enzyme), 5) Water hydrolyzes acyl-enzyme (product release).

title: What are matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) and their role in disease?
content: MMPs are zinc-dependent endopeptidases that degrade extracellular matrix. 23 human MMPs: collagenases (MMP-1,13), gelatinases (MMP-2,9), stromelysins (MMP-3,10). Roles: wound healing, cancer metastasis, arthritis, cardiovascular remodeling. Inhibitors (MMPIs) failed in trials due to side effects.

title: What is ubiquitin-proteasome system and peptide degradation?
content: UPS degrades intracellular proteins: 1) E1 activates ubiquitin, 2) E2 transfers, 3) E3 ligase conjugates ubiquitin chains, 4) 26S proteasome recognizes poly-Ub, 5) 20S core degrades protein to peptides (8-25 aa). These peptides are further degraded by peptidases or presented on MHC class I.

title: What is angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) and its inhibitors?
content: ACE is a zinc metalloprotease that cleaves Ang I → Ang II (vasoconstrictor). Also degrades bradykinin. ACE inhibitors (captopril, enalapril, lisinopril) block active site zinc. Drug design: transition state mimics, zinc-binding groups (thiol, carboxylate, phosphonate). Major hypertension drugs.

title: What is DPP-4 and its role in incretin peptide degradation?
content: Dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) cleaves GLP-1 and GIP (incretin peptides) at the N-terminus, inactivating them. DPP-4 inhibitors (sitagliptin, vildagliptin) prolong incretin action, enhancing glucose-dependent insulin secretion. Used in type 2 diabetes treatment.

title: What is lyophilization and why is it used for peptide formulation?
content: Lyophilization (freeze-drying) removes water by sublimation under vacuum. For peptides: 1) Extends shelf life (prevents hydrolysis), 2) Improves stability (reduces aggregation), 3) Enables room temperature storage, 4) Allows precise dosing upon reconstitution. Critical parameters: freezing rate, primary/secondary drying temperatures.

title: What are the common excipients used in peptide formulations?
content: Excipients: 1) Bulking agents (mannitol, sucrose, trehalose), 2) Stabilizers (polysorbate 80, BSA), 3) Buffering agents (histidine, acetate, phosphate), 4) Tonicity modifiers (NaCl, glycine), 5) Antimicrobial preservatives (phenol, m-cresol), 6) Cryoprotectants (trehalose, sucrose).

title: What is the difference between a drug product and a drug substance?
content: Drug substance (API): the active peptide molecule itself (e.g., synthetic or recombinant peptide). Drug product: the final dosage form containing API + excipients (e.g., insulin vial, semaglutide pen). Manufacturing steps: drug substance → formulation → fill-finish → packaging → distribution.

title: What is a peptide conjugate and why is it formulated?
content: Peptide conjugates link peptides to other molecules for improved properties: 1) PEGylation (increased half-life), 2) Lipidation (albumin binding, oral bioavailability), 3) Antibody-drug conjugates (targeted delivery), 4) Fc fusion (extended circulation), 5) Prodrug forms (activation at target site).

title: What are the challenges in formulating oral peptide drugs?
content: Oral peptide challenges: 1) GI degradation (pH, proteases), 2) Poor intestinal permeability, 3) First-pass hepatic metabolism, 4) Food effects on absorption. Solutions: 1) Permeation enhancers (SNAC for semaglutide), 2) Protease inhibitors, 3) Nanoparticle encapsulation, 4) Enteric coatings, 5) Mucoadhesive systems.

title: What is a pre-filled syringe (PFS) and how is it designed for peptides?
content: PFS combines drug product and delivery device. Design considerations: 1) Silicone oil lubrication (protein adsorption), 2) Glass type (borosilicate vs COP polymer), 3) Needle shield (extractables), 4) Dosing accuracy (dead volume), 5) Stability in device (cyclic stress), 6) Ease of use (ergonomics).

title: What is the role of pH in peptide stability?
content: pH affects peptide stability through: 1) Acid/base-catalyzed hydrolysis (Asp-Pro bonds, Asn deamidation), 2) Aggregation (charge-dependent), 3) Conformational stability, 4) Solubility. Most peptides are stable at pH 4-7. Storage at 2-8°C in buffered solutions is standard.

title: What are the ICH guidelines for peptide drug product stability?
content: ICH Q1A(R2): Stability testing. Requirements: 1) Long-term (25°C/60% RH, 12-36 months), 2) Accelerated (40°C/75% RH, 6 months), 3) Stress conditions. For peptides: 4) Photostability (ICH Q1B), 5) In-use stability, 6) Freeze-thaw cycling. Critical: aggregation, deamidation, oxidation monitoring.

title: What is a depot formulation and how does it release peptides?
content: Depot formulations provide sustained peptide release over days-weeks-months. Types: 1) Microspheres (PLGA encapsulation, biodegradable), 2) Implants (non-biodegradable, removable), 3) In-situ forming depots (PLGA in NMP), 4) Lipid nanoparticles, 5) Hydrogels. Release: diffusion + erosion + degradation.

title: What is the role of surfactants in peptide formulations?
content: Surfactants (polysorbate 20/80, poloxamer 188) prevent: 1) Interfacial aggregation (air-liquid, liquid-container), 2) Surface adsorption, 3) Foam formation during filling. Concentration: 0.01-0.1%. Risk: surfactant oxidation can generate peroxides that damage peptides. Monitoring: critical micelle concentration (CMC).

title: What is CRISPR-Cas9 and how is it used in peptide research?
content: CRISPR-Cas9 is a gene editing system that creates targeted double-strand breaks using a guide RNA (gRNA) and Cas9 nuclease. For peptide research: 1) Knockout genes to study peptide function, 2) Engineer cell lines for peptide production, 3) Create disease models, 4) Study post-translational modification pathways.

title: What is RNA-seq and how does it relate to peptide discovery?
content: RNA-seq profiles transcriptome by sequencing cDNA libraries. For peptide discovery: 1) Identify novel ORFs encoding small peptides, 2) Quantify peptide precursor gene expression, 3) Discover tissue-specific peptide expression, 4) Identify splice variants encoding different peptide isoforms.

title: What is a proteogenomics approach?
content: Proteogenomics combines genomics, transcriptomics, and proteomics to discover novel peptides: 1) Genome translation creates theoretical peptide database, 2) Mass spectrometry data is searched against this database, 3) Novel peptides/PSMs are validated, 4) Integration reveals regulatory mechanisms between genome and proteome.

title: How does next-generation sequencing (NGS) impact peptide therapeutics?
content: NGS enables: 1) High-throughput screening of peptide libraries, 2) Immune repertoire analysis for immunogenicity prediction, 3) Tumor mutation profiling for neoantigen peptide vaccines, 4) Microbiome analysis for antimicrobial peptide discovery, 5) Pharmacogenomics for personalized peptide therapy.

title: What are microRNAs and how do they regulate peptide expression?
content: MicroRNAs (miRNAs, ~22 nt) bind 3'UTR of mRNAs, causing translational repression or mRNA degradation. They regulate: 1) Peptide precursor expression, 2) Processing enzyme levels, 3) Receptor expression, 4) Signal transduction pathways. miRNA mimic/antagonist therapeutics are being developed.

title: What is single-cell RNA-seq and its relevance to peptide biology?
content: scRNA-seq profiles gene expression in individual cells, revealing heterogeneity in peptide-producing cell populations. Applications: 1) Identify rare peptide-expressing cells, 2) Map peptide expression during development, 3) Characterize tumor microenvironment peptide signals, 4) Discover cell-type-specific peptide isoforms.

title: What is whole-genome sequencing (WGS) in the context of peptide discovery?
content: WGS determines complete DNA sequence of an organism. For peptides: 1) Identify all predicted ORFs encoding peptides, 2) Discover novel antimicrobial peptide genes, 3) Map venom peptide gene families, 4) Study peptide gene evolution and diversification, 5) Identify genetic variants affecting peptide function.

title: How does epigenomics influence peptide hormone expression?
content: Epigenetic modifications (DNA methylation, histone acetylation, chromatin remodeling) regulate accessibility of peptide hormone genes. Examples: 1) Insulin gene methylation in diabetes, 2) POMC gene regulation in stress response, 3) Ghrelin gene epigenetics in obesity. Epigenetic drugs can modulate peptide expression.

title: What is metagenomics and antimicrobial peptide discovery?
content: Metagenomics sequences DNA from environmental samples without culturing. For AMPs: 1) Mining uncultured microbial genomes for novel AMP genes, 2) Studying microbiome defensive peptide repertoires, 3) Discovering AMPs from extreme environments (deep sea, hot springs), 4) Functional metagenomics screening.

title: What is ribosome profiling (Ribo-seq)?
content: Ribo-seq maps ribosome positions on mRNAs by sequencing protected fragments. For peptide biology: 1) Identify actively translated ORFs, 2) Discover hidden small ORFs (sORFs) encoding bioactive peptides, 3) Measure translation efficiency, 4) Detect translation pauses affecting peptide folding.

title: What are the key milestones in peptide history?
content: 1882: First peptide synthesis (Theodor Curtius). 1901: First hormone isolation (secretin, Bayliss & Starling). 1921: Insulin isolation (Banting & Best). 1953: First total protein synthesis (Vincent du Vigneaud, oxytocin). 1963: Solid-phase peptide synthesis (Robert Merrifield). 1977: Recombinant DNA technology.

title: Who invented solid-phase peptide synthesis (SPPS) and why is it important?
content: Robert Merrifield developed SPPS in 1963 (Nobel Prize 1984). The peptide chain grows on an insoluble resin bead: 1) C-terminal AA attached to resin, 2) Deprotection, 3) Coupling with activated next AA, 4) Repeat. Automation enabled high-throughput synthesis, revolutionizing peptide chemistry and drug discovery.

title: What was the significance of the insulin discovery?
content: Banting and Best (1921) isolated insulin from canine pancreas. Sanger determined its sequence (1951, Nobel 1958). First recombinant human insulin (Humulin, 1982) was the first biotech drug. Insulin history spans: animal extraction → semi-synthetic → recombinant → analogs → biosimilars.

title: What is the opioid peptide discovery timeline?
content: 1975: Endorphins discovered (Hughes & Kosterlitz). 1976: Enkephalins identified. 1979: Dynorphin isolated. 1992: Endomorphins discovered. 1997: Orexin/hypocretin found. Key insight: endogenous opioids explain morphine's effects and led to receptor pharmacology (μ, δ, κ).

title: What was the impact of Fmoc chemistry on peptide synthesis?
content: Fmoc (9-fluorenylmethyloxycarbonyl) SPPS (1970s, Carpino) uses base-labile Fmoc for Nα-protection and acid-labile side chain protection. Advantages over Boc: 1) Milder conditions (piperidine/TFA), 2) No HF needed, 3) Orthogonal protection, 4) Automated synthesis compatible. Now dominant SPPS method.

title: What is the significance of the Human Genome Project for peptides?
content: HGP (1990-2003) mapped all human genes. Impact on peptides: 1) Identified ~20,000 protein-coding genes (many encode peptides), 2) Enabled genome-wide peptide discovery, 3) Facilitated personalized peptide therapeutics, 4) Provided reference for genetic variants affecting peptide function, 5) Accelerated biomarker discovery.

title: What is the evolution of peptide drug approval?
content: Peptide drug approvals: 1922: Insulin (first peptide drug). 1985: Calcitonin. 1996: Leuprolide (GnRH agonist). 2005: Exenatide (GLP-1 agonist). 2010s: Liraglutide, Semaglutide, Icatibant. 2020s: Oral semaglutide, tirzepatide (dual GIP/GLP-1). ~80 approved peptides, 500+ in clinical trials.

title: What was the amyloid hypothesis and how has it evolved?
content: 1984: Aβ discovered in Alzheimer's plaques. Hypothesis: Aβ accumulation causes AD. Evolution: 1) Oligomers (not plaques) are neurotoxic, 2) Tau pathology is downstream, 3) Inflammation plays key role, 4) Anti-Aβ drugs (aducanumab, lecanemab) show modest effects, 5) Multi-target approaches emerging.

title: What is the story of the first peptide vaccine?
content: 1981: First synthetic peptide vaccine (malaria, Patarroyo). 1986: Hepatitis B recombinant vaccine. 1990s: Peptide vaccines faced challenges (immunogenicity, MHC restriction). 2020s: mRNA vaccines (SARS-CoV-2 spike protein) represent modern peptide antigen delivery. Peptide vaccines remain active area for cancer immunotherapy.

title: What is the significance of Veni Vidi Vici for peptide science?
content: Veni Vidi Vici (3 peptides, 3 letters) represents the simplicity and power of peptide nomenclature. The three-letter amino acid code (IUPAC-IUBMB, 1983) standardized peptide sequences globally. Single-letter code (1960s) enabled computational analysis. Both are foundational to bioinformatics and peptide databases (UniProt, PDB).

title: What is the LD50 and how is it used in peptide toxicology?
content: LD50 (median lethal dose) is the dose of a substance that kills 50% of a test population. For peptides, it varies by route (IV, IP, oral). Example: botulinum toxin LD50 is ~1 ng/kg IV, making it the most toxic substance known.

title: What are the main mechanisms of peptide toxicity?
content: 1) Ion channel modulation (neurotoxins), 2) Membrane disruption (cytolytic peptides), 3) Enzymatic activity (phospholipases, proteases), 4) Receptor agonism/antagonism, 5) Immune activation (anaphylaxis), 6) Amyloid aggregation (organ toxicity).

title: What is the therapeutic index of a peptide drug?
content: Therapeutic index (TI) = TD50/ED50 (toxic dose / effective dose). A higher TI indicates a safer drug. Many peptide drugs have narrow TIs due to their potency. For example, insulin TI is relatively low, requiring careful dose titration.

title: How do venom peptides cause neurotoxicity?
content: Venom peptides target ion channels: α-neurotoxins bind nAChR (postsynaptic blockade), β-neurotoxins inhibit ACh release (presynaptic), δ-toxins delay Na+ channel inactivation (depolarization), κ-toxins block K+ channels (prolonged action potentials).

title: What is anaphylaxis and which peptide triggers it?
content: Anaphylaxis is a severe, systemic allergic reaction. IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation releases histamine, tryptase, and other mediators. In peptide drug allergy, preformed mediators cause bronchospasm, hypotension, and angioedema within minutes.

title: What is immunogenicity in peptide drug toxicology?
content: Immunogenicity is the ability of a peptide drug to trigger immune responses. Anti-drug antibodies (ADA) can neutralize efficacy, cause allergic reactions, or form immune complexes leading to organ damage. Risk factors: non-human sequences, aggregation, route of administration.

title: How is peptide stability in serum measured?
content: Serum stability assays incubate peptide in human/plasma at 37°C and measure degradation over time. Methods: HPLC (intact peptide), LC-MS/MS (metabolite identification). Key metric: t½ (half-life). Unmodified peptides often have t½ of minutes due to protease degradation.

title: What is the no-observed-adverse-effect level (NOAEL) for peptides?
content: NOAEL is the highest dose at which no adverse effects are observed in preclinical studies. It's used to calculate the starting dose for human trials: HED = NOAEL × (animal Km / human Km). Safety margins of 10-100x are applied depending on toxicity profile.

title: What are the common off-target effects of peptide drugs?
content: 1) Cross-reactivity with related receptor subtypes, 2) Metabolite toxicity (degradation products), 3) Hypersensitivity reactions, 4) Injection site reactions (local irritation), 5) Immunogenicity (ADA formation), 6) Off-target enzyme inhibition.

title: How do cytolytic peptides cause cell lysis?
content: Cytolytic peptides (melittin, magainins, defensins) insert into cell membranes, forming pores or disrupting lipid bilayers. Mechanisms: barrel-stave (transmembrane pores), carpet model (detergent-like disruption), toroidal model (curved pores with peptide orientation).

title: What is the difference between acute and chronic peptide toxicity?
content: Acute: single high-dose exposure causing immediate effects (e.g., venom envenomation, anaphylaxis). Chronic: repeated low-dose exposure causing cumulative damage (e.g., amyloid deposition, immunogenicity, organ fibrosis). Testing requirements differ: single-dose vs 28-day repeat-dose studies.

title: What is the role of metabolomics in peptide toxicology?
content: Metabolomics profiles small-molecule metabolites to detect toxicity biomarkers. For peptides, it identifies: 1) Degradation products, 2) Off-target metabolic effects, 3) Organ-specific toxicity signatures (hepatotoxicity, nephrotoxicity), 4) Species-specific metabolic differences.

title: What are the ICH guidelines for peptide drug safety testing?
content: ICH M3(R2): Nonclinical safety studies timing. ICH S6(R1): Preclinical safety of biotechnology-derived pharmaceuticals (includes peptides). ICH S5(R3): Reproductive toxicology. Key requirements: pharmacology, PK, single/repeat dose toxicity, genotoxicity, local tolerance.

title: What is complement activation-related pseudoallergy (CARPA)?
content: CARPA is a non-IgE-mediated hypersensitivity reaction triggered by complement activation. Certain peptides and their aggregates activate the classical/alternative complement pathways, generating C3a/C5a anaphylatoxins. It mimics anaphylaxis but without prior sensitization.

title: How do phospholipase A2 (PLA2) snake venom enzymes cause toxicity?
content: PLA2 enzymes hydrolyze the sn-2 position of membrane phospholipids, releasing arachidonic acid (pro-inflammatory) and lysophospholipids (detergent-like). Effects: myotoxicity, neurotoxicity, hemolysis, inflammation. Some PLA2s are catalytically inactive but retain toxicity through receptor binding.

title: What is the minimum lethal dose (MLD) and how does it differ from LD50?
content: MLD is the smallest dose that kills at least one animal in a test group, estimated from dose-response curves. LD50 is the statistically derived median lethal dose. MLD is used more for highly potent toxins (e.g., botulinum toxin) where LD50 testing would require impossibly small doses.

title: What is the role of CYP450 enzymes in peptide metabolism?
content: Most peptides are not primarily metabolized by CYP450 (unlike small molecules). They're degraded by proteases (endopeptidases, exopeptidases) in blood, liver, and kidneys. However, some cyclic peptides with non-natural bonds can resist proteolysis and may interact with CYP450.

title: What is phototoxicity and which peptides are associated with it?
content: Phototoxicity is light-induced cellular damage. Some peptides containing aromatic residues (Trp, Tyr, Phe) or bound to photosensitizing groups can generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon UV exposure, causing skin damage. Relevant for topical peptide formulations.

title: How are peptide degradation products characterized for safety?
content: Peptide degradation pathways are mapped using: 1) Forced degradation (heat, pH, oxidation, light), 2) LC-MS/MS for metabolite identification, 3) In vitro cytotoxicity of degradation products, 4) Structural similarity analysis to known toxins, 5) Pharmacological activity of metabolites.

title: What is the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) and how is it determined?
content: MTD is the highest dose that produces acceptable toxicity in Phase I clinical trials. Determined using accelerated titration or modified Fibonacci designs. For peptides: start at 1/10th of human equivalent dose from animal NOAEL, escalate until dose-limiting toxicity (DLT) occurs.

title: What is a cyclic peptide?
content: A peptide with a covalent bond linking two parts of the chain, forming a ring structure.

title: What is an alpha-helix mimetic?
content: A synthetic compound designed to replicate the spatial arrangement of key residues on one face of an alpha helix.

title: What are the two most commonly used E3 ligases in PROTAC design?
content: VHL (von Hippel-Lindau) and CRBN (cereblon).

title: What is a linker in PROTAC design?
content: The chemical chain connecting the target-binding ligand to the E3 ligase-binding ligand, optimized for length and flexibility.

title: What is a thioester in native chemical ligation?
content: A functional group (R-CO-SR') on the C-terminal fragment that reacts with the N-terminal cysteine thiol of the second fragment.

title: What is a depsipeptide?
content: A peptide containing at least one ester bond alongside peptide bonds, alternating amino acid and hydroxy acid residues.

title: What is the advantage of PROTACs over inhibitors?
content: PROTACs act catalytically, degrading the target rather than just blocking it, potentially achieving effects at lower doses.

title: What positions are typically stapled on an alpha helix?
content: i, i+4 or i, i+7 spacing, which places residues on the same helical face.

title: What is click chemistry in peptide modification?
content: A class of bioorthogonal reactions (e.g., CuAAC) used for site-specific conjugation of functional groups to peptides.

title: What is retro-inverso peptide design?
content: Reversing the amino acid sequence and inverting chirality at each residue to mimic the side chain topology of the original peptide.

title: What is bicyclic peptide display technology?
content: A method for selecting high-affinity bicyclic peptides from combinatorial libraries displayed on protein scaffolds.

title: What is head-to-tail cyclization?
content: Forming a peptide bond between the N-terminal amino group and C-terminal carboxyl group of the same peptide.

title: What is the hydrocarbon staple composed of?
content: An all-hydrocarbon bridge formed by ring-closing metathesis between two non-natural amino acids bearing terminal alkenes.

title: What is molecular docking used for in peptide research?
content: Predicting the preferred orientation and binding affinity of a peptide when it interacts with a target protein.

title: What is molecular dynamics simulation?
content: A computational method that simulates the physical movement of atoms and molecules over time using force fields.

title: What is a force field in molecular simulation?
content: A set of parameters and equations describing the potential energy of a molecular system as a function of atomic positions.

title: What is homology modeling?
content: A method for predicting 3D protein structures based on the known structure of a related homologous protein.

title: What is virtual screening?
content: Using computational methods to search large compound libraries and identify potential peptide or small molecule binders.

title: What is AlphaFold?
content: An AI system that predicts protein structures from amino acid sequences with high accuracy.

title: What is QSAR?
content: Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship, a computational method relating chemical structure to biological activity.

title: What is pharmacophore modeling?
content: Identifying the spatial arrangement of essential features (hydrogen bonds, charges, hydrophobic groups) required for biological activity.

title: What is de novo peptide design?
content: Computational design of novel peptides with desired properties from scratch, often using machine learning or physics-based methods.

title: What is a stapled peptide?
content: A peptide with a chemical cross-link that locks it into an alpha-helical conformation.

title: What is peptide macrocyclization screening?
content: Combinatorial approaches to identify the most efficient cyclization chemistry and linker for a given peptide sequence.

title: What is ring-closing metathesis (RCM)?
content: A chemical reaction using Grubbs catalyst to form a carbon-carbon bond between two alkene groups, creating a hydrocarbon staple.

title: What is PROTAC?
content: Proteolysis-Targeting Chimera - a bifunctional molecule that recruits an E3 ligase to a target protein for ubiquitination and degradation.

title: How does a PROTAC work?
content: It brings a target protein and an E3 ligase into proximity, leading to ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation of the target.

title: What is molecular glue degradation?
content: Small molecules that modify an E3 ligase surface to create a new interface for neosubstrate recognition and degradation.

title: What are the main advantages of stapled peptides over linear peptides?
content: Improved cell penetration, increased protease resistance, and enhanced binding affinity due to preorganized helical structure.

title: What is native chemical ligation (NCL)?
content: A method for joining unprotected peptide fragments via a chemoselective reaction between a C-terminal thioester and N-terminal cysteine.

title: What are the 20 standard amino acids?
content: Gly, Ala, Val, Leu, Ile, Pro, Phe, Tyr, Trp, Ser, Thr, Cys, Met, Asn, Gln, Asp, Glu, Lys, Arg, His

title: What makes proline structurally unique?
content: Proline is a cyclic imino acid where the side chain connects back to the backbone nitrogen, forming a pyrrolidine ring.

title: What is the one-letter code for arginine?
content: R

title: What is the three-letter code for isoleucine?
content: Ile

title: Why does tryptophan use W as its one-letter code?
content: Tryptophan is assigned W because T was already taken by threonine.

title: What is the one-letter code for asparagine?
content: N (chosen because it is the first letter of asparagine not used by aspartic acid).

title: What is the one-letter code for glutamine?
content: Q (sounds like the letter Q in glutamine).

title: What are the three aromatic amino acids and their one-letter codes?
content: Phenylalanine (F), tyrosine (Y), and tryptophan (W).

title: What is the one-letter code for lysine?
content: K (because L was taken by leucine).

title: What is the one-letter code for histidine?
content: H

title: What is the one-letter code for cysteine?
content: C

title: What are the three components of an amino acid?
content: An alpha-amino group, an alpha-carboxyl group, and a variable side chain (R group) attached to the alpha-carbon.

title: What is the one-letter code for methionine?
content: M (also the start codon AUG codes for methionine).

title: What is the approximate pKa of the alpha-carboxyl group?
content: Around 2 (typically 1.8-2.4).

title: What is the approximate pKa of the alpha-amino group?
content: Around 9-10 (typically 9.0-9.8).

title: What is a zwitterion?
content: A molecule with both positive and negative charges that are overall electrically neutral, like an amino acid at physiological pH.

title: Which amino acid side chain can form disulfide bonds?
content: Cysteine, through oxidation of two thiol groups (-SH) to form a disulfide bond (-S-S-).

title: What is the isoelectric point (pI)?
content: The pH at which a molecule carries no net electric charge, having equal numbers of positive and negative charges.

title: What are the essential amino acids for humans?
content: His, Ile, Leu, Lys, Met, Phe, Thr, Trp, Val (9 total; sometimes Arg is conditionally essential).

title: What is the pKa of the histidine side chain?
content: Approximately 6.0, making it the only standard amino acid with a side chain near physiological pH.

title: Which amino acids have hydroxyl groups in their side chains?
content: Serine (Ser), threonine (Thr), and tyrosine (Tyr).

title: Which amino acids have amide groups in their side chains?
content: Asparagine (Asn) and glutamine (Gln).

title: What makes glycine unique among the standard amino acids?
content: Glycine has two hydrogen atoms on its alpha-carbon, making it the only achiral standard amino acid.

title: What is the molecular weight of glycine (the smallest amino acid)?
content: 75.03 Da (the lightest standard amino acid).

title: What is the pKa of the cysteine thiol group?
content: Approximately 8.3 (can vary from 8.0 to 8.5 depending on environment).

title: What is the pKa of the lysine epsilon-amino group?
content: Approximately 10.5 (typically 10.0-11.0).

title: What is the pKa of the arginine guanidinium group?
content: Approximately 12.5 (the highest pKa among standard amino acid side chains).

title: What are the polar uncharged amino acids?
content: Serine, threonine, cysteine, asparagine, glutamine, and tyrosine.

title: What makes tryptophan the largest standard amino acid?
content: Tryptophan has an indole ring, a bicyclic aromatic structure, making it the bulkiest amino acid side chain.

title: Which two amino acids are commonly found in beta-turns?
content: Proline and glycine are commonly found in beta-turns due to their unique structural properties.

title: What is the pKa of the tyrosine hydroxyl group?
content: Approximately 10.1 (typically 9.6-10.4).

title: What is the pKa of the aspartate carboxyl group?
content: Approximately 3.9 (typically 3.7-4.1).

title: What is the pKa of the glutamate carboxyl group?
content: Approximately 4.2 (typically 4.1-4.4).

title: Which amino acids have nonpolar aliphatic side chains?
content: Glycine, alanine, valine, leucine, isoleucine, proline, and methionine.

title: What is the only amino acid that is a secondary amine (imino acid)?
content: Proline, where the side chain nitrogen is part of a ring.

title: Which amino acids are positively charged at physiological pH?
content: Lysine (Lys), arginine (Arg), and histidine (His).

title: Which amino acids are negatively charged at physiological pH?
content: Aspartic acid (Asp) and glutamic acid (Glu).

title: Which amino acids have aromatic side chains?
content: Phenylalanine (Phe), tyrosine (Tyr), and tryptophan (Trp).

title: Which amino acids contain sulfur?
content: Cysteine (Cys) has a thiol group; methionine (Met) has a thioether group.

title: What are the branched-chain amino acids (BCAAs)?
content: Valine (Val), leucine (Leu), and isoleucine (Ile).

title: What is selenocysteine and how does it differ from cysteine?
content: Selenocysteine (Sec, U) contains a selenol group (-SeH) instead of a thiol (-SH). It is incorporated via a UGA stop codon recoding mechanism requiring a SECIS element in mRNA. The lower pKa of selenol (~5.2 vs 8.3) makes it more reactive at physiological pH.

title: What are the mass spectral signatures for distinguishing leucine from isoleucine?
content: Leucine and isoleucine are isomeric (both 131.17 Da). Differentiation requires MS/MS fragmentation: leucine yields characteristic fragment m/z 86, while isoleucine yields m/z 86 with different fragmentation pattern. LC-MS with specialized columns or enzymatic digestion is used.

title: What is the role of N-methyl-L-arginine in nitric oxide synthesis regulation?
content: Asymmetric dimethylarginine (ADMA) and L-NMMA are endogenous inhibitors of nitric oxide synthase (NOS). ADMA accumulates in renal failure and cardiovascular disease. It is metabolized by DDAH (dimethylarginine dimethylaminohydrolase).

title: Explain the mechanism of phenylketonuria at the molecular level.
content: PKU results from mutations in phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH), preventing conversion of Phe to Tyr. Over 1000 mutations identified. Residual enzyme activity determines severity. BH4-responsive PKU can be treated with sapropterin (synthetic BH4).

title: What is the function of pyrrolysine in methanogenic archaea?
content: Pyrrolysine (Pyl, O) is the 22nd amino acid, encoded by UAG in methanogens. It is incorporated by PylRS tRNA synthetase. Found in methylamine methyltransferases where it activates methylamines for methanogenesis.

title: How does homocysteine metabolism relate to cardiovascular disease?
content: Homocysteine is remethylated to methionine by methionine synthase (B12-dependent) or betaine-homocysteine methyltransferase. Hyperhomocysteinemia causes endothelial damage through oxidative stress, inflammation, and impaired nitric oxide signaling.

title: What are the post-translational modifications of histidine in proteins?
content: Histidine undergoes phosphorylation (by kinases), methylation, and ADP-ribosylation. Phosphohistidine is labile (acid-sensitive), making it difficult to detect. Bacterial chemotaxis relies on histidine phosphorylation in two-component systems.

title: Describe the tryptophan-kynurenine pathway and its neurological implications.
content: Tryptophan is metabolized via IDO/TDO to kynurenine, which branches to neuroprotective kynurenic acid or neurotoxic quinolinic acid. Imbalance occurs in depression, schizophrenia, and neurodegenerative diseases. IDO activation by inflammation depletes tryptophan.

title: What is the role of D-amino acid oxidase (DAAO) in the central nervous system?
content: DAAO oxidizes D-serine, an NMDA receptor co-agonist, to iminoglutarate. DAAO is enriched in astrocytes. Its activity modulates NMDA receptor signaling. DAAO inhibitors are being explored for schizophrenia treatment by increasing D-serine levels.

title: How does the GCN2 pathway respond to amino acid deprivation?
content: GCN2 kinase senses uncharged tRNAs during amino acid starvation. It phosphorylates eIF2alpha, reducing global translation while selectively translating ATF4. ATF4 activates genes for amino acid biosynthesis, transport, and autophagy.

title: What analytical method is used for de novo peptide sequencing using Edman degradation?
content: Edman degradation sequentially removes N-terminal amino acids using phenylisothiocyanate (PITC). Each cycle identifies one residue via HPLC of the phenylthiohydantoin (PTH) amino acid. Limitations include N-terminal blockage and ~50 cycle maximum.

title: What is the role of 4-hydroxyproline in collagen stability?
content: 4-Hydroxyproline is formed by prolyl hydroxylase using vitamin C as a cofactor. It stabilizes the collagen triple helix through stereoelectronic effects (gauche effect) and hydrogen bonding via ordered water molecules. Deficiency causes scurvy.

title: Explain the structural role of hydroxylysine in collagen cross-linking.
content: Hydroxylysine (Hyl) is formed by lysyl hydroxylase (PLOD). Hyl residues serve as attachment sites for galactosyl-glucosyl disaccharides and participate in aldol and Schiff base cross-links. Deficiency causes Ehlers-Danlos type VI.

title: What is the significance of Nepsilon-methyllysine in histone modification?
content: Methyllysine residues on histones (H3K4, H3K9, H3K27, H3K36, H4K20) are written by methyltransferases and erased by demethylases. Methylation state determines gene activation or repression. H3K4me3 marks active promoters; H3K9me3 marks heterochromatin.

title: How do amino acid auxotrophs serve as selection markers in synthetic biology?
content: Amino acid auxotrophs (e.g., leuB-, hisB-) require supplementation for growth. They serve as selection markers in metabolic engineering, preventing plasmid loss and enabling selection without antibiotics. CRISPR-based auxotrophs enable biocontainment.

title: What is the role of arginine in the urea cycle and nitric oxide production?
content: Arginine is the substrate for both arginase (urea cycle, producing ornithine and urea) and nitric oxide synthase (NOS, producing citrulline and NO). The competition between these pathways regulates ureagenesis and NO production in hepatocytes and macrophages.

title: Describe the use of amino acid racemization dating in forensics and paleontology.
content: Aspartate racemization rate is temperature-dependent and calibrated to individual lifetimes. In teeth, D/L ratio of Asp correlates with age (plus or minus 3 years). In fossils, it estimates age up to several million years, though diagenesis can affect accuracy.

title: What is the mechanism of action of glyphosate on aromatic amino acid biosynthesis?
content: Glyphosate inhibits 5-enolpyruvylshikimate-3-phosphate synthase (EPSPS) in the shikimate pathway, blocking synthesis of Phe, Tyr, and Trp in plants and microorganisms. It does not affect mammals which lack this pathway.

title: How do D-amino acids function in bacterial peptidoglycan?
content: D-alanine and D-glutamate are incorporated into peptidoglycan by D-Ala-D-Ala ligase and D-glutamate ligase. D-amino acids resist proteases and are essential for cell wall integrity. Vancomycin binds D-Ala-D-Ala terminus.

title: What is the role of taurine in bile acid conjugation and cardiovascular health?
content: Taurine conjugates bile acids (forming taurocholate) to increase solubility. It also has cardioprotective effects through osmoregulation, calcium modulation, and antioxidant activity. Taurine deficiency causes cardiomyopathy.

title: Explain the concept of amino acid scoring for protein quality evaluation.
content: Protein Digestibility Corrected Amino Acid Score (PDCAAS) and Digestible Indispensable Amino Acid Score (DIAAS) evaluate protein quality. The limiting amino acid (lowest score relative to requirement) determines quality. Egg protein scores 1.0; plant proteins score lower.

title: What is the function of phosphoserine in signal transduction?
content: Phosphoserine residues are generated by Ser/Thr kinases (e.g., Akt, PKA, CK1/2). Dephosphorylation by PP1/PP2A phosphatases reverses signaling. Phosphoserine motifs recruit 14-3-3 proteins and FHA domain-containing effectors.

title: Describe the gamma-carboxylation of glutamate and its biological importance.
content: Gamma-carboxylation of Glu to Gla (gamma-carboxyglutamate) is catalyzed by vitamin K-dependent carboxylase. Gla residues chelate Ca2+ ions and are essential for the function of clotting factors II, VII, IX, X and anticoagulant proteins C, S, Z.

title: How does the branched-chain amino acid pathway relate to maple syrup urine disease?
content: Branched-chain aminotransferase (BCAT) and the BCKDH complex catabolize Leu, Ile, Val. BCKDH deficiency causes maple syrup urine disease with accumulation of BCAAs and their ketoacids. Symptoms include maple syrup odor, encephalopathy, and intellectual disability.

title: What is the significance of O-GlcNAcylation as a nutrient sensor?
content: O-GlcNAc transferase (OGT) adds GlcNAc to Ser/Thr residues using UDP-GlcNAc from the hexosamine biosynthetic pathway. It competes with phosphorylation and acts as a nutrient/glucose sensor. Dysregulation occurs in diabetes and cancer.

title: Describe the role of selenoproteins in antioxidant defense.
content: Selenoproteins include glutathione peroxidases (GPx1-8) and thioredoxin reductases (TrxR1-3). They reduce H2O2 and lipid hydroperoxides using glutathione or thioredoxin. Selenocysteine in the active site is essential for catalysis.

title: What is the mechanism of amino acid sensing by mTORC1?
content: mTORC1 is recruited to the lysosomal surface by Rag GTPases activated by amino acids. Leucine is sensed by Sestrin2 (inhibitor of GATOR2), arginine by CASTOR1, and methionine by SAMTOR. This pathway regulates protein synthesis and autophagy.

title: What is the role of cystathionine beta-synthase in transsulfuration?
content: Cystathionine beta-synthase (CBS, B6-dependent) condenses homocysteine with serine to form cystathionine. This irreversible step commits homocysteine to cysteine synthesis. CBS deficiency causes homocystinuria with thromboembolism and lens dislocation.

title: How do chiral amino acid columns enable enantiomeric separation?
content: Polysaccharide-based chiral columns (Chiralpak, Chiralcel) separate amino acid enantiomers via hydrogen bonding and pi-pi interactions. Crown ether columns (Crownpak CR) complex L-amino acids. Ligand-exchange chromatography uses Cu(II)-amino acid complexes.

title: What is the role of amino acids in the TCA cycle anaplerosis?
content: Glutamate and aspartate enter the TCA cycle via alpha-ketoglutarate and oxaloacetate. Glutaminolysis provides carbon for rapidly dividing cells (Warburg effect). Alanine provides pyruvate for gluconeogenesis and anaplerosis.

title: What is the clinical significance of homocystinuria screening?
content: Newborn screening measures methionine and homocysteine. Classical homocystinuria (CBS deficiency) affects 1:200,000. Treatment with pyridoxine, betaine, and dietary methionine restriction prevents thromboembolism, osteoporosis, and intellectual disability.

title: Explain the function of aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases in translation fidelity.
content: Aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases (aaRS) charge tRNAs with correct amino acids in a two-step reaction. Editing domains hydrolyze misacylated tRNAs. Errors occur at ~1:10,000 frequency. Mistranslation from aaRS mutations causes neurodegeneration.

title: What is the role of polyamine synthesis from amino acids?
content: Ornithine is decarboxylated by ODC to putrescine, then converted to spermidine and spermine. Polyamines stabilize DNA, regulate ion channels, and support cell growth. ODC is a target for anticancer therapy (DFMO).

title: What is the biosynthetic pathway for tryptophan in bacteria?
content: Tryptophan is synthesized from chorismate via anthranilate, using enzymes TrpE, TrpG, TrpD, TrpF, TrpC, and TrpA. The pathway requires glutamine, PRPP, and serine. It is one of the most energetically expensive amino acid biosynthetic pathways.

title: How do amino acid transporters in the kidney relate to Fanconi syndrome?
content: Renal proximal tubule amino acid transporters (SLC6A19 for neutral, SLC3A1/SLC7A9 for dibasic) reabsorb filtered amino acids. Fanconi syndrome causes generalized aminoaciduria with glucose, phosphate, and bicarbonate wasting.

title: What is the significance of protein amino acid racemization in Alzheimer disease?
content: D-aspartate accumulates in amyloid-beta plaques in Alzheimer brains. Racemization of Asp in A-beta increases aggregation propensity and resistance to degradation. This may contribute to plaque persistence and neurotoxicity.

title: Describe the role of tryptophan hydroxylase in serotonin biosynthesis.
content: Tryptophan hydroxylase (TPH) converts tryptophan to 5-hydroxytryptophan using BH4 as cofactor. TPH1 is peripheral; TPH2 is neuronal. Regulation occurs at transcriptional and post-translational levels. TPH2 mutations cause serotonin deficiency syndrome.

title: What is the function of asparagine-linked glycosylation (N-glycosylation)?
content: Oligosaccharyltransferase (OST) transfers Glc3Man9GlcNAc2 from dolichol-P-P to Asn-X-Ser/Thr sequons in the ER. Glycan processing in ER and Golgi determines protein folding, stability, and cell signaling. Congenital disorders of glycosylation affect multiple organs.

title: How do isotopic labeling methods quantify amino acid turnover?
content: Stable isotope tracers (13C6-leucine, D3-leucine, 15N-glutamine) measure protein synthesis rates via mass spectrometry. Deuterium oxide (D2O) labeling measures long-term synthesis. SILAC (Stable Isotope Labeling by Amino acids in Cell culture) enables quantitative proteomics.

title: What is the role of amino acid-derived neurotransmitters?
content: Glutamate (excitatory) and GABA (inhibitory) are the main CNS neurotransmitters. Glycine is inhibitory in spinal cord. Histamine (from histidine) modulates wakefulness. Serotonin (from tryptophan) regulates mood. Dopamine (from tyrosine) controls reward.

title: What is the role of asparagine synthetase in cancer cell metabolism?
content: Asparagine synthetase (ASNS) converts aspartate to asparagine. ASNS-low tumors (some ALL, solid tumors) are sensitive to L-asparaginase therapy. ASNS expression is induced by amino acid deprivation through ATF4.

title: Explain the concept of amino acid competition for transport across the BBB.
content: Large neutral amino acids compete for LAT1 transport. High-protein meals increase plasma Trp but compete with other BCAAs. Phenylalanine excess (PKU) inhibits Trp and Tyr transport, reducing dopamine and serotonin synthesis.

title: What is the mechanism of cysteine protease specificity?
content: Cysteine proteases (papain, cathepsins, caspases) use a Cys-His catalytic dyad. Substrate specificity is determined by S1-S4 pockets. Caspases recognize Asp at P1 position. Cathepsins have broader specificity. Inhibitors include E-64 and viral cystatins.

title: How do amino acid auxotrophies serve as biocontainment strategies?
content: Engineered auxotrophies for essential amino acids prevent survival outside supplemented media. Multiple knockouts or recoding essential genes for non-standard amino acids create orthogonal biocontainment.

title: Explain the concept of amino acid racemization and its clinical significance.
content: L-amino acids can racemize to D-forms over time. In aging, D-aspartate accumulates in lens crystallins and bone collagen, used for forensic age estimation. D-amino acids are found in bacterial cell walls and some peptide antibiotics.

title: What is the clinical utility of urine amino acid analysis by tandem mass spectrometry?
content: Tandem MS newborn screening quantifies amino acids from dried blood spots. Disorders detected include PKU, MSUD, homocystinuria, tyrosinemia, and maple syrup urine disease. Tandem MS has replaced chromatographic methods for population screening.

title: What are the key transporters for large neutral amino acids across the blood-brain barrier?
content: System L (LAT1/SLC7A5) transports large neutral amino acids across the BBB. It exchanges amino acids and is sodium-independent. LAT1 is also the transporter for L-DOPA in Parkinson disease treatment. Mutations cause Hartnup disease.

title: How is amino acid analysis performed using ion-exchange chromatography?
content: Classical amino acid analysis uses cation-exchange chromatography with post-column ninhydrin detection. Proteins are hydrolyzed with 6M HCl at 110C for 24h. Modern methods use pre-column derivatization with OPA or FMOC for HPLC separation.

title: What is the methylmalonyl-CoA mutase deficiency and its biochemical basis?
content: Methylmalonyl-CoA mutase (B12-dependent) converts methylmalonyl-CoA to succinyl-CoA. Deficiency causes methylmalonic acidemia, a severe metabolic disorder with accumulation of methylmalonic acid, leading to metabolic acidosis and neurological damage.

title: Describe the citrullination reaction and its role in autoimmune disease.
content: Citrullination is the conversion of arginine to citrulline by peptidylarginine deiminases (PADs). Anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA) are diagnostic for rheumatoid arthritis. Citrullination also occurs in NETosis and dead cell clearance.

title: Explain the principles of adaptive platform trials for pandemic response.
content: Platform trials: continuous enrollment, multiple arms, shared control, pre-specified adaptation rules. REMAP-CAP (pneumonia), SOLIDARITY (COVID-19). Rapid identification of effective therapies. Bayesian response-adaptive randomization. International collaboration essential.

title: What is the role of exposure-response analysis in dose selection?
content: Exposure-response analysis links PK exposure (AUC, Cmax, Cmin) to efficacy and safety outcomes. Characterizes therapeutic window. Identifies dose-response plateau. Supports dose justification for special populations. Population PK/PD modeling integrates variability.

title: Explain the concept of clinical trial simulation and its applications.
content: Clinical trial simulation uses mathematical models to predict trial outcomes. PK/PD models, patient population models, trial design parameters. Applications: sample size estimation, design optimization, sensitivity analysis, risk assessment. Software: NONMEM, Monolix, Phoenix.

title: What are the challenges of pediatric drug development?
content: Pediatric challenges: developmental PK, age-appropriate formulations, ethical considerations (assent, consent), limited sampling.法规: BPCA, PREA, Paediatric Regulation. Pediatric study plans (PSPs). Scaling approaches: allometric scaling, maturation functions.

title: Describe the role of pharmacogenomics in clinical trial design.
content: Pharmacogenomic stratification: enrich for responders, exclude non-responders. HLA testing prevents hypersensitivity (abacavir, carbamazepine). DPYD testing for 5-FU. CYP2C19 for clopidogrel. Pre-emptive PGx testing in clinical practice. CPIC guidelines.

title: What is the concept of Bayesian adaptive dose-finding?
content: Bayesian dose-finding: Continual Reassessment Method (CRM) uses dose-toxicity models. Up-and-down designs. Bayesian optimal interval (BOIN) design. Escalation with overdose control (EWOC). Balances patient safety with efficient dose identification.

title: Explain the principles of drug-drug interaction study design.
content: DDI study design: single-dose and steady-state studies. CYP3A4: midazolam or simvastatin as probe. Transporter studies: rifampin (P-gp/OATP). PBPK modeling for prediction. In vitro-in vivo extrapolation. FDA/EMA DDI guidance requirements.

title: What are the endpoints for oncology clinical trials?
content: Oncology endpoints: OS (gold standard), PFS (RECIST 1.1), ORR, DOR. Surrogate endpoints for accelerated approval: ORR, pCR (pathologic complete response). Patient-reported outcomes (PROs): QoL, symptom scales. Biomarker endpoints: MRD (minimal residual disease).

title: How is therapeutic equivalence demonstrated for biosimilars?
content: Biosimilar pathway: analytical similarity, functional similarity, PK/PD similarity, clinical comparison. Stepwise approach reduces residual uncertainty. Reference product comparison. No clinical efficacy trial required if biosimilarity demonstrated. Interchangeability requires switching studies.

title: What is the concept of model-informed drug development (MIDD)?
content: MIDD: quantitative pharmacology approaches to support drug development. Population PK, PK/PD modeling, exposure-response, trial simulation. FDA Model-Informed Drug Development Pilot Program. Supports dose selection, label claims, drug interaction predictions.

title: Explain the role of biomarkers in patient stratification for clinical trials.
content: Biomarker-based stratification: genomic (HER2, BRCA), protein (PD-L1), molecular (MSI-H). Enrichment designs include only biomarker-positive patients. Umbrella trials test multiple therapies for one disease. Basket trials test one therapy across biomarker-defined diseases.

title: Explain the role of patient-reported outcomes (PROs) in clinical trials.
content: PROs: patient-completed instruments measuring symptoms, function, and QoL. Validated instruments required (FDA PRO Guidance). ePRO devices improve compliance. PRO endpoints can support label claims. Composite endpoints (e.g., symptom-free days). Longitudinal analysis methods.

title: What are the principles of equivalence and non-inferiority trial design?
content: Non-inferiority: new treatment not worse than active control by pre-specified margin (delta). Equivalence: treatments similar within margin. Margin selection based on clinical judgment, regulatory precedent, and assay sensitivity. ITT and per-protocol populations both required.

title: Explain the concept of meta-analysis and its role in evidence synthesis.
content: Meta-analysis: statistical combination of results from multiple studies. Random effects (DerSimonian-Laird) vs fixed effects models. Heterogeneity assessment (I-squared, Q statistic). Publication bias (funnel plot, Egger test). GRADE framework for evidence quality.

title: What is the role of data monitoring committees (DMCs) in clinical trials?
content: DMCs (DSMBs): independent committees that review accumulating data. Interim analyses for efficacy and futility. Safety monitoring and adverse event review. Pre-specified stopping rules. Charter defines charter, composition, meeting schedule, and decision criteria.

title: Describe the concept of target product profile (TPP) in drug development.
content: TPP: written description of ideal drug product. Defines indication, patient population, dosing, safety profile, efficacy claims. Guides development strategy. ICH M4 CTD structure. Quality target product profile (QTPP) for CMC. Regular updates as knowledge accumulates.

title: What are the statistical methods for analyzing time-to-event data in clinical trials?
content: Kaplan-Meier estimator, log-rank test, Cox proportional hazards model. Hazard ratio as effect measure. Proportional hazards assumption testing. Accelerated failure time models. Competing risks. Landmark analysis. Number needed to treat (NNT) from survival data.

title: Explain the principles of sample size determination for clinical trials.
content: Sample size depends on: effect size, variability, alpha (Type I error), power (1 - Type II error). Continuous outcomes: two-sample t-test formula. Binary outcomes: chi-square test formula. Survival: number of events needed (Schoenfeld formula). Adaptive designs use simulation.

title: What is the concept of external control arms in clinical trials?
content: External controls: historical or real-world data as comparator. Used when randomized control is unethical or impractical (rare diseases, pediatric). Challenges: selection bias, data quality, temporal changes. Synthetic control arms using AI/ML. FDA guidance on use.

title: How are drug-drug interactions predicted using PBPK modeling?
content: PBPK modeling predicts DDIs by simulating drug concentrations at metabolic enzymes and transporters. Input: in vitro kinetic parameters (Vmax, Km, Ki), tissue distribution, blood flows. Output: AUC ratio, Cmax ratio. FDA accepts PBPK for DDI labeling decisions.

title: What are the principles of clinical pharmacology in special populations?
content: Special populations: renal/hepatic impairment, elderly, pregnant, pediatric, obese. Dose adjustment based on PK differences. Renal impairment: Cockcroft-Gault, CKD-EPI. Hepatic impairment: Child-Pugh. Pregnancy: altered Vd, clearance, placental transfer.

title: What are the principles of PK/PD modeling in drug development?
content: PK/PD modeling links drug exposure to pharmacological effect. PK: absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion. PD: concentration-effect relationship. Emax models, sigmoid Emax, direct/indirect response models. Applications: dose selection, clinical trial simulation, regulatory submissions.

title: Explain the concept of clinical trial endpoints for gene therapy.
content: Gene therapy endpoints: transgene expression, vector biodistribution, durability of effect. Safety: insertional mutagenesis monitoring, immune responses. Functional endpoints: change in biomarker, clinical improvement. Long-term follow-up (15 years for integrating vectors).

title: Describe the concept of surrogate endpoints and their validation.
content: Surrogate endpoints substitute for clinical endpoints to accelerate approval. Requirements: strong correlation, biological plausibility, treatment effect on surrogate predicts clinical benefit. Examples: tumor shrinkage ( RECIST), viral load (HCV), HbA1c (diabetes). Accelerated approval pathway.

title: What are the challenges of conducting clinical trials for rare diseases?
content: Rare disease challenges: small patient populations, heterogeneous phenotypes, limited natural history data, geographic dispersion. Solutions: natural history studies, patient registries, basket trials, international collaboration. Regulatory incentives: orphan drug designation, breakthrough therapy.

title: Explain the concept of master protocols in clinical trials.
content: Master protocols: umbrella (multiple treatments, one disease), basket (one treatment, multiple diseases), platform (add/drop arms). I-SPY 2 (breast cancer), ORR platform (COVID-19). Advantages: efficiency, shared controls, real-time adaptation.

title: What is the role of real-world evidence (RWE) in drug development?
content: RWE: evidence from real-world data (EHR, claims, registries). Post-market safety surveillance. Comparative effectiveness research. External control arms for rare diseases. FDA RWE framework. Limitations: confounding, missing data, selection bias.

title: How are Bayesian methods applied in clinical trial design?
content: Bayesian methods: prior information + data = posterior probability. Adaptive enrichment, dose-finding (CRM), interim analyses. Predictive probability of success. Bayesian credible intervals vs frequentist confidence intervals. Prior elicitation is critical.

title: What is the concept of precision dosing in clinical pharmacology?
content: Precision dosing: individualized dosing based on patient factors (weight, genotype, organ function, disease state). Model-informed precision dosing (MIPD) uses Bayesian estimation. TDM-guided dosing for narrow therapeutic index drugs. Therapeutic drug monitoring protocols.

title: What are the four phases of clinical trials?
content: Phase 1 (safety/dosing), Phase 2 (efficacy/side effects), Phase 3 (large-scale efficacy/comparison), Phase 4 (post-market surveillance).

title: What is adaptive trial design?
content: A clinical trial that allows pre-planned modifications (dose, sample size, endpoints) based on accumulating interim data.

title: What is a Phase 1 trial?
content: A trial assessing safety, tolerability, and pharmacokinetics in a small group of healthy volunteers or patients.

title: What is a randomized controlled trial (RCT)?
content: A trial where participants are randomly assigned to treatment or control groups to minimize bias.

title: What is a double-blind study?
content: Neither the participants nor the investigators know which treatment each participant is receiving.

title: What does FDA stand for?
content: Food and Drug Administration, the U.S. federal agency regulating drugs, biologics, and medical devices.

title: What is an IND application?
content: Investigational New Drug application, required by the FDA before beginning human clinical trials.

title: What is Breakthrough Therapy designation?
content: An FDA designation for drugs showing substantial improvement over existing treatments, providing intensive regulatory guidance.

title: What is the BLA pathway?
content: Biologics License Application, the regulatory pathway for approval of biologics including recombinant peptides and proteins.

title: What is the difference between FDA and EMA?
content: FDA regulates drugs in the United States; EMA (European Medicines Agency) evaluates medicines for the European Union.

title: What are cell-penetrating peptides (CPPs) and their mechanisms of uptake?
content: CPPs are short cationic or amphipathic peptides that transport cargo across membranes. Examples: TAT (GRKKRRQRRRPQ), penetratin, transportan. Uptake mechanisms: direct translocation at low concentrations, endocytosis at high concentrations. Cargo size affects mechanism.

title: Describe the concept of stimuli-responsive peptide delivery systems.
content: Stimuli-responsive release: pH (tumor microenvironment ~6.5, endosome ~5), temperature (LCST polymers), redox (GSH in cytosol), enzymes (MMP, cathepsin), light (UV, NIR). Enables spatiotemporal control of drug release at target sites.

title: What is the mechanism of pulmonary peptide delivery via inhalation?
content: Pulmonary delivery avoids first-pass metabolism and provides large surface area. Aerosol deposition: oropharyngeal (large particles), bronchial, alveolar (1-3 um particles). Challenges: mucus clearance, macrophage uptake. Dry powder inhalers and nebulizers used.

title: Explain the concept of albumin-binding for extending peptide half-life.
content: Albumin-binding peptides (e.g., AlbudAb) or small molecules (SAH, fatty acids) non-covalently associate with serum albumin. Half-life extension depends on binding affinity. Advantages over PEGylation: smaller size, no immunogenicity, natural clearance.

title: What are the principles of intranasal peptide delivery to the brain?
content: Intranasal delivery bypasses BBB via olfactory and trigeminal nerve pathways. Nose-to-brain delivery avoids first-pass metabolism and systemic distribution. Challenges: nasal mucociliary clearance, enzymatic degradation, limited bioavailability. Absorption enhancers improve delivery.

title: How do pH-sensitive liposomes achieve endosomal escape?
content: pH-sensitive liposomes (DOPE/CHEMS) destabilize at endosomal pH (5-6). Protonation of CHEMS disrupts bilayer, releasing contents into cytosol. Fusogenic peptides (GALA, KALA) enhance membrane fusion. Used for siRNA and protein delivery.

title: What is the role of transdermal iontophoresis in peptide delivery?
content: Iontophoresis uses low electrical current (0.1-0.5 mA/cm2) to drive charged peptides across skin. Electroosmosis enhances neutral molecule transport. Enables controlled, non-invasive delivery of peptides (e.g., LHRH, insulin). Patches with electrodes and reservoirs.

title: Describe the mechanism of long-acting injectable peptide formulations.
content: PLGA microspheres degrade by hydrolysis, releasing peptide over weeks to months (Risperdal Consta, Bydureon). Oil-based depots (peptide in castor oil/benzyl benzoate) dissolve slowly. Crystal suspensions (exenatide ER) dissolve gradually. Initial burst release is a challenge.

title: What are the principles of oral peptide delivery using permeation enhancers?
content: Permeation enhancers (SNAC, salcaprozate sodium) transiently open tight junctions or increase membrane fluidity. Enables oral delivery of peptides (semaglutide, octreotide). SNAC mechanism: local pH increase, transcellular absorption. Formulations: tablets with enteric coating.

title: How do polymer-drug conjugates improve peptide pharmacokinetics?
content: Polymer conjugation (PEG, polyglutamate, HPMA) increases hydrodynamic radius and renal threshold. Cleavable linkers (pH-sensitive, enzyme-cleavable) enable controlled release. HPMA-doxorubicin (PK1) was first polymer-drug conjugate in clinical trials.

title: What is the role of biomimetic nanoparticles in drug delivery?
content: Biomimetic nanoparticles: cell membrane-coated (erythrocyte, platelet, cancer cell), virus-like particles, outer membrane vesicles. Inherit source cell properties (long circulation, targeting, immune evasion). Enable combination of synthetic and biological features.

title: Explain the mechanism of liposomal drug delivery systems.
content: Liposomes are phospholipid bilayers enclosing aqueous core. PEGylated (stealth) liposomes evade RES, extend circulation. Neutral lipids: DPPC, DSPC. Cholesterol reduces permeability. Active targeting: antibody-ligand conjugation. Doxil (PEGylated doxorubicin) is a clinical example.

title: Explain the concept of targeted drug delivery using aptamers.
content: Aptamers (nucleic acid or peptide ligands) bind targets with high specificity and affinity. Anti-EpCAM aptamer-drug conjugates target tumors. Aptamer-nanoparticle conjugates enable targeted delivery. SELEX (Systematic Evolution of Ligands by EXponential enrichment) identifies aptamers.

title: What are the challenges of peptide delivery across the blood-brain barrier?
content: BBB challenges: tight junctions (TEER ~1500 V/cm), efflux transporters (P-gp, BCRP), enzymatic degradation, serum protein binding. Solutions: CPPs, transcytosis receptors, nanoparticles, intranasal delivery, focused ultrasound with microbubbles.

title: Explain the mechanism of sonoporation for intracellular peptide delivery.
content: Sonoporation uses ultrasound + microbubbles to transiently permeabilize cell membranes. Cavitation creates microjets and microstreaming that disrupt lipid bilayer. Reversible permeabilization allows cytoplasmic delivery. Used for tumor targeting with localized ultrasound.

title: What is the role of cyclodextrins in peptide solubilization?
content: Cyclodextrins (alpha, beta, gamma) have hydrophobic cavity and hydrophilic exterior. Inclusion complexes increase peptide aqueous solubility. Beta-cyclodextrin most commonly used. Derivatives (HP-beta-CD, SBE-beta-CD) improve solubility and reduce toxicity.

title: How do electroporation-based delivery systems work?
content: Electroporation applies brief electrical pulses (ms) to create transient pores in cell membranes. Reversible electroporation enables intracellular delivery of peptides, nucleic acids, and drugs. Used for tumor treatment (Irreversible Electroporation) and ex vivo cell engineering.

title: What are the principles of sustained-release ocular peptide delivery?
content: Ocular challenges: tear dilution, corneal barrier, rapid clearance. Sustained-release: intravitreal implants (Ozurdex - PLGA), punctal plugs, in situ gelling systems. Peptides: anti-VEGF (ranibizumab, aflibercept) for wet AMD. Port delivery systems enable refillable reservoirs.

title: Describe the mechanism of mucosal vaccine delivery using nanoparticles.
content: Mucosal nanoparticles target M cells in Peyer patches or nasal-associated lymphoid tissue. Chitosan and PLGA nanoparticles enhance antigen uptake. Adjuvants (CpG, MPLA) co-delivered for immune stimulation. Enables mucosal IgA response plus systemic immunity.

title: What is the role of albumin as a drug delivery vehicle?
content: Albumin (66 kDa) has long half-life (19 days) due to FcRn recycling. Abraxane (nab-paclitaxel) uses albumin nanoparticles. Albumin-drug conjugates and albumin-binding strategies extend half-life. Albumin accumulates in tumors via transcytosis (gp60) and EPR.

title: How do self-assembling peptide nanofibers function in drug delivery?
content: Self-assembling peptides (RADA16, max8) form nanofibers under physiological conditions. Drug encapsulation in nanofiber core. Injectable hydrogels form in situ. Applications: local drug delivery, wound healing, tissue engineering. Tunable fiber properties via sequence design.

title: What is the concept of multivalent ligand display on nanoparticles?
content: Multivalent display increases avidity through multiple simultaneous binding events. Ligand density and spacing affect receptor clustering and internalization. Optimal density: too low = poor binding, too high = steric hindrance. Enables targeted delivery with enhanced cellular uptake.

title: What are exosomes and their potential as drug delivery vehicles?
content: Exosomes are 30-150 nm extracellular vesicles with natural membrane composition. They cross BBB, evade immune system, and deliver cargo (proteins, RNA, drugs) to recipient cells. Engineering: electroporation, sonication, or transfection of parent cells.

title: Explain the principles of redox-responsive drug delivery systems.
content: Redox-responsive systems exploit GSH gradient (extracellular ~2 um, intracellular ~10 mM). Disulfide bonds, diselenide bonds, and thioketal linkers cleave in reducing environment. Enables intracellular drug release after endosomal escape. Tumor microenvironment has elevated GSH.

title: Describe the concept of microneedle drug delivery systems.
content: Microneedles (25-1000 um) penetrate stratum corneum without reaching nerves. Types: solid (poke and patch), coated, dissolving (sugar-based), hollow (infusion). Enable transdermal delivery of vaccines, proteins, and small molecules. Painless and self-administrable.

title: What is the role of PEGylation in extending peptide drug half-life?
content: PEGylation attaches PEG (2-40 kDa) to peptides. Increases hydrodynamic radius, reducing renal clearance. Shields from proteolysis and immune recognition. Branched PEG > linear PEG for shielding. Site-specific PEGylation improves homogeneity.

title: Explain the mechanism of nanoparticle-mediated gene delivery.
content: Cationic lipids (DOTAP, DOTMA) or polymers (PEI, chitosan) condense DNA into nanoparticles (polyplexes). Endosomal escape via proton sponge effect (PEI) or membrane fusion (lipids). Nuclear import required for non-dividing cells. PEGylation reduces opsonization.

title: What are the principles of hydrogel-based peptide delivery?
content: Hydrogels (PEG, alginate, hyaluronic acid) provide sustained release. Peptide-loaded gels release via diffusion, degradation, or stimuli-responsiveness. Injectable hydrogels (in situ gelling) enable local delivery. Applications: wound healing, tissue engineering.

title: How do antibody-drug conjugates achieve targeted delivery?
content: ADCs: monoclonal antibody + linker + cytotoxic payload. Antibody provides tumor selectivity. Linkers: cleavable (protease-sensitive, pH-sensitive) or non-cleavable. Payloads: maytansinoids, auristatins, calicheamicins. DAR (drug-to-antibody ratio) affects PK.

title: What is the role of receptor-mediated transcytosis in brain drug delivery?
content: Transferrin receptor (TfR) and insulin receptor mediate transport across BBB. Bispecific antibodies binding TfR and therapeutic target achieve brain delivery. Engineered Fc domains (brain shuttle) enhance transcytosis. Roche brain shuttle technology is in clinical trials.

title: Why is oral delivery of peptides challenging?
content: Peptides are degraded by GI proteases and have poor membrane permeability due to size and polarity.

title: What is pulmonary peptide delivery?
content: Administering peptides via inhalation to the lungs, exploiting the large surface area and thin alveolar epithelium.

title: What is PEGylation?
content: Attaching polyethylene glycol chains to a peptide to increase half-life by reducing renal clearance.

title: What is a nanoparticle in drug delivery?
content: A carrier (1-1000 nm) that encapsulates or conjugates drugs to improve stability, bioavailability, and targeting.

title: What is subcutaneous injection?
content: Injection into the tissue layer beneath the skin, the most common route for peptide drug administration.

title: What is the advantage of depot formulations for peptide drugs?
content: They provide sustained release over days to weeks, reducing dosing frequency and improving patient compliance.

title: What is nasal peptide delivery?
content: Administering peptides through the nasal mucosa for systemic or local absorption, bypassing GI degradation.

title: What is a permeation enhancer?
content: A compound that transiently increases epithelial permeability to allow peptide absorption across biological membranes.

title: What is a microneedle patch?
content: A transdermal delivery device with microscopic needles that create channels in the stratum corneum for peptide delivery.

title: What is a prodrug in peptide therapeutics?
content: An inactive form of a peptide that is converted to the active form in the body through metabolic processes.

title: What are the key principles of rational vaccine design?
content: Rational vaccine design uses structural biology to engineer immunogens that elicit specific immune responses. Goals: present conserved epitopes, focus B cells on neutralizing sites, avoid immunodominance of non-protective epitopes. Examples: RSV F protein stabilized prefusion form.

title: Explain the role of Fc engineering in therapeutic antibody design.
content: Fc modifications enhance effector functions. S239D/I332E increase ADCC. LALA mutations ablate ADCC/CDC for anti-inflammatory antibodies. Half-life extension:YTE mutations increase FcRn binding at pH 6. Bispecific antibodies engage two targets simultaneously.

title: What are the mechanisms of checkpoint inhibitor immunotherapy?
content: Checkpoint inhibitors block inhibitory receptors: anti-PD-1 (pembrolizumab), anti-PD-L1 (atezolizumab), anti-CTLA-4 (ipilimumab). Reinvigorate exhausted T cells in tumors. Biomarkers: PD-L1 expression, TMB, MSI-H. Immune-related adverse events are common.

title: Explain the concept of CAR-T cell therapy and its design principles.
content: CAR-T cells: chimeric antigen receptor combines antibody specificity with T cell activation. scFv recognition domain + CD3z signaling + costimulatory domains (CD28, 4-1BB). CD19 CARs for B cell malignancies. Manufacturing: leukapheresis, transduction, expansion.

title: What is the role of dendritic cells in initiating adaptive immunity?
content: Dendritic cells (DCs) capture, process, and present antigens to T cells. Maturation: upregulate MHC, costimulatory molecules (CD80/86), cytokines. Cross-presentation activates CD8+ T cells. DC subsets: cDC1 (cross-presentation), cDC2 (CD4+ T cell priming), pDC (IFN-alpha).

title: Describe the principles of bispecific antibody design.
content: Bispecific antibodies engage two targets simultaneously. Formats: BiTE (bispecific T cell engager), knobs-into-holes (heterodimeric Fc), DVD-Ig. Applications: redirect T cells to tumors (blinatumomab), dual receptor blockade. Manufacturing challenges: chain pairing, purification.

title: What are the applications of neoantigen-based cancer vaccines?
content: Neoantigen vaccines target tumor-specific mutations. Workflow: tumor sequencing, HLA typing, neoantigen prediction, vaccine manufacturing. Personalized mRNA vaccines (Moderna BNT122) show clinical efficacy. Combination with checkpoint inhibitors enhances responses.

title: Explain the concept of mucosal immunity and its importance for respiratory pathogens.
content: Mucosal immunity provides first-line defense at respiratory surfaces. Secretory IgA, tissue-resident memory T cells (TRM), alveolar macrophages. Intranasal vaccines induce mucosal IgA + systemic immunity. Challenges: mucosal tolerance, antigen delivery across epithelium.

title: What is the role of complement in vaccine-mediated protection?
content: Complement activation enhances antibody-mediated protection. C3d binds B cell receptor, lowering activation threshold. C5a recruits immune cells. Vaccine adjuvants can activate complement. Complement-fixing antibodies (IgG1, IgG3) are more protective than non-fixing (IgG4).

title: Describe the mechanisms of antibody-dependent cellular phagocytosis (ADCP).
content: ADCP: antibodies opsonize targets, Fc receptors on macrophages/neutrophils mediate phagocytosis. Fc-gamma receptors (Fc-gR I, II, III) have different affinities. ADCP is important for bacterial clearance and antibody-mediated tumor killing. Enhanced by complement.

title: What is the concept of trained immunity and its vaccine implications?
content: Trained immunity: innate immune memory through epigenetic reprogramming (H3K4me3, H3K27Ac). BCG vaccination induces trained monocytes/macrophages. Benefits: non-specific protection against infections. Implications for vaccine adjuvant design and heterologous protection.

title: Explain the concept of immunogenicity assessment for biologic drugs.
content: Immunogenicity = ability to elicit anti-drug antibodies (ADA). Factors: intrinsic (sequence, glycosylation), process-related (impurities, formulation), patient-related (disease, genetics). ADA can neutralize efficacy or cause adverse events. Assays: bridging ELISA, Meso Scale.

title: Explain the design of nanoparticle-based vaccines mimicking viral structure.
content: Self-assembling protein nanoparticles (ferritin, I53-50) display antigens in multivalent array. Nanoparticle vaccines enhance B cell activation through BCR cross-linking. SpFN (ferritin) displays SARS-CoV-2 spike receptor binding domain. Ferritin nanoparticles in clinical trials for influenza.

title: What are the mechanisms of T-cell exhaustion in chronic infection and cancer?
content: T-cell exhaustion: progressive loss of effector function (IL-2, TNF-alpha, IFN-gamma). Transcription factors: TOX, NR4A. Exhaustion markers: PD-1, TIM-3, LAG-3, TIGIT. Epigenetic stability of exhaustion limits checkpoint inhibitor efficacy. Strategies: early intervention, combination therapy.

title: Explain the role of cytokine engineering in immunotherapy.
content: Cytokine engineering: fusion proteins (IL-2 muteins), targeted delivery (cytokine-antibody fusions), gene therapy. IL-15 superagonist (N-803) expands NK and T cells. Engineered IL-2 (bempegaldesleukin) preferentially activates T cells over Tregs. Cytokine release syndrome is a major toxicity.

title: What is the concept of immunological memory and its durability?
content: Immunological memory: long-lived memory T and B cells provide rapid recall responses. Memory B cells undergo somatic hypermutation. Long-lived plasma cells (bone marrow) maintain antibody titers. Durability varies: measles > decades, influenza < years. Germinal center reactions generate high-affinity memory.

title: Describe the principles of tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) therapy.
content: TIL therapy: isolate T cells from tumor, expand ex vivo, reinfuse. Lifileucel (FDA approved for melanoma). Requires lymphodepletion conditioning. TILs recognize tumor neoantigens. Manufacturing challenges: tumor heterogeneity, T cell exhaustion, scalability.

title: What is the role of the microbiome in modulating anti-tumor immunity?
content: Gut microbiome affects checkpoint inhibitor efficacy. Akkermansia muciniphila, Faecalibacterium prausnitzii associated with response. Antibiotics reduce immunotherapy efficacy. Fecal microbiota transplant (FMT) can convert non-responders. Mechanism: enhanced T cell priming in lymph nodes.
