title: Explain the concept of antibody glycoengineering for enhanced effector functions.
content: Glycoengineering modifies Fc glycosylation to enhance ADCC. Afucosylation (obinutuzumab) increases Fc-gRIIIa binding 50-fold. CHO cell line engineering with FUT8 knockout. Glycan modifications: sialylation (anti-inflammatory), galactosylation (complement activation).

title: What are the mechanisms of immune-related adverse events (irAEs) from checkpoint inhibitors?
content: irAEs: autoimmune-like toxicity from enhanced immune activation. Mechanisms: loss of peripheral tolerance, pre-existing autoantibodies, molecular mimicry. Common: colitis, hepatitis, dermatitis, endocrinopathies. Management: corticosteroids, infliximab, vedolizumab.

title: Describe the design principles of universal influenza vaccines.
content: Universal influenza vaccines target conserved epitopes: HA stem, M2e, neuraminidase, internal proteins (NP, M1). Approaches: chimeric HA immunogens, headless HA constructs, mRNA vaccines with conserved antigens. Goal: protect against all influenza A and B strains.

title: What is the concept of synthetic immunology and artificial immune cells?
content: Synthetic immunology: engineer cells with synthetic receptors (synNotch, CAR). Artificial antigen-presenting cells (aAPCs) for T cell expansion. Synthetic cytokine circuits for programmable immune modulation. Applications: controlled immunotherapy, immune system engineering.

title: What are T-cell epitopes and how are they predicted?
content: T-cell epitopes are 8-25 amino acid peptides presented by MHC molecules. MHC-I presents intracellular peptides (8-10 aa) to CD8+ T cells. MHC-II presents extracellular peptides (13-25 aa) to CD4+ T cells. Prediction: NetMHCpan, IEDB consensus algorithms.

title: Explain the role of STING agonists as vaccine adjuvants.
content: STING (Stimulator of Interferon Genes) pathway detects cytosolic DNA. STING agonists (DMXAA, cGAMP, diABZI) activate type I IFN production. Enhance dendritic cell maturation and T cell priming. Formulation challenges: stability, delivery. Clinical trials for cancer and infectious disease.

title: Describe the role of adjuvants in enhancing vaccine responses.
content: Adjuvants enhance antigen immunogenicity.铝盐 (alum) promotes Th2 responses. AS01 (MPL + QS-21) for shingles vaccine. MF59 (squalene emulsion) for influenza. CpG (TLR9 agonist) for hepatitis B. TLR agonists bridge innate and adaptive immunity.

title: What is the concept of broadly neutralizing antibodies (bnAbs) for vaccine design?
content: bnAbs target conserved viral epitopes across strains. Examples: VRC01 (HIV CD4 binding site), CR9114 (influenza HA stem), S309 (SARS-CoV-2 RBD). Germline-targeting immunogen design aims to activate bnAb precursor B cells through sequential immunization.

title: Explain the mechanisms of immune tolerance and their implications for autoimmunity.
content: Central tolerance: deletion of self-reactive T cells in thymus (AIRE), B cells in bone marrow. Peripheral tolerance: anergy, regulatory T cells (Tregs), peripheral deletion. Breakdown leads to autoimmunity. Therapeutic strategies: Treg expansion, tolerogenic dendritic cells.

title: What are the applications of mRNA vaccines beyond infectious disease?
content: mRNA vaccines for cancer (personalized neoantigen vaccines), autoimmune diseases (tolerogenic mRNA), protein replacement therapy, gene editing (Cas9 mRNA). Advantages: rapid development, scalable manufacturing, no genomic integration risk. Lipid nanoparticle delivery essential.

title: Describe the role of glycosylation in viral immune evasion.
content: Viral glycan shields (HIV gp120, SARS-CoV-2 spike) mask protein epitopes from antibodies. Host-like glycans (N-linked) prevent immune recognition. Site-directed glycan engineering can expose conserved epitopes. Glycan deletion creates immunogens with improved responses.

title: What is the concept of original antigenic sin and its vaccine implications?
content: Original antigenic sin: immune response dominated by first encounter antigen, ignoring new epitopes in subsequent exposures. Implications for influenza and SARS-CoV-2 vaccines. May reduce effectiveness of updated vaccines. Universal vaccine strategies aim to overcome this.

title: What type of reaction forms a peptide bond?
content: A condensation (dehydration synthesis) reaction, releasing one water molecule per bond formed.

title: What is the omega (omega) angle?
content: The dihedral angle around the peptide bond itself (the C-N bond). It is typically near 180 degrees (trans) or 0 degrees (cis).

title: What are the hydrogen bonding partners in an alpha helix?
content: The C=O of residue i hydrogen bonds to the N-H of residue i+4.

title: What are the hydrogen bonding partners in an antiparallel beta sheet?
content: Residues hydrogen bond directly across to residues on adjacent strands, with C=O paired with N-H in a linear arrangement.

title: What is a depsipeptide?
content: A peptide that contains at least one ester bond in addition to peptide bonds, alternating amino and hydroxy acid residues.

title: What is peptidomimetics?
content: Molecules that mimic the structure and function of natural peptides but are modified to improve drug-like properties.

title: What is the nomenclature for a tripeptide with Gly-Ala-Val?
content: Glycylalanylvaline (read from N-terminus to C-terminus).

title: What distinguishes a peptide from a protein?
content: There is no strict cutoff, but peptides typically have fewer than 50 amino acids while proteins have more. The term peptide often implies shorter chains.

title: What is a thioamide bond?
content: A modified peptide bond where the carbonyl oxygen is replaced by sulfur, used to enhance protease resistance and alter electronic properties.

title: What is a retro-inverso peptide?
content: A peptide with reversed sequence and inverted chirality at each residue, designed to mimic the topology of the original peptide while resisting proteolysis.

title: What is the typical angle range for phi in an alpha helix?
content: Approximately -60 degrees (range: -45 to -80 degrees).

title: How many peptide bonds does a polypeptide of n amino acids contain?
content: n - 1 peptide bonds.

title: What is the typical angle range for psi in an alpha helix?
content: Approximately -47 degrees (range: -25 to -70 degrees).

title: What is the typical bond length of a peptide bond?
content: Approximately 1.33 Angstroms, shorter than a typical C-N single bond due to partial double bond character.

title: Why does the peptide bond have partial double bond character?
content: Resonance delocalization of the nitrogen lone pair into the carbonyl group creates partial double bond character between C and N.

title: What does trans mean in the context of peptide bonds?
content: The trans conformation has the alpha-carbons on opposite sides of the peptide bond, which is energetically favored due to reduced steric clash.

title: What is the phi (phi) angle in a Ramachandran plot?
content: The rotation angle around the N-Calpha bond in the peptide backbone.

title: What is the psi (psi) angle in a Ramachandran plot?
content: The rotation angle around the Calpha-C bond in the peptide backbone.

title: Why is the peptide bond planar?
content: Resonance delocalization restricts rotation about the C-N bond, forcing the atoms into a planar arrangement.

title: Which amino acid frequently adopts cis peptide bonds?
content: Proline, because its cyclic structure reduces the steric penalty of the cis conformation.

title: What is the preferred conformation of a peptide bond in most proteins?
content: The trans conformation is preferred, with approximately 99.6% of peptide bonds in trans. In trans, the two alpha-carbons are on opposite sides of the peptide bond.

title: What is the omega (ω) angle in a peptide bond?
content: The omega (ω) angle describes rotation around the peptide bond itself (C-N). In trans peptide bonds, ω is approximately 180°; in cis bonds, ω is approximately 0°.

title: Why are planar peptide bonds energetically favored?
content: Planar peptide bonds maximize the overlap of p-orbitals between the carbonyl carbon and nitrogen, allowing resonance delocalization. Non-planar conformations would disrupt this orbital overlap.

title: What percentage of peptide bonds are in the cis conformation?
content: Approximately 0.4% of non-proline peptide bonds are cis, while about 5-10% of X-Pro bonds are cis. Cis peptide bonds can create steric clashes that affect protein structure.

title: How does an omega peptide bond differ from phi and psi bonds?
content: The omega (ω) bond is the peptide bond itself (C-N) with restricted rotation, while phi (φ) and psi (ψ) are bonds in the backbone (N-Cα and Cα-C) that have more rotational freedom.

title: What is the bond angle at the peptide carbonyl carbon?
content: The bond angles at the peptide carbonyl carbon are approximately 120°, consistent with the sp² hybridization that creates the planar geometry of the peptide bond.

title: What role do backbone hydrogen bonds play in protein stability?
content: Backbone hydrogen bonds are the primary stabilizing force for secondary structures. In alpha-helices, each residue contributes one H-bond; in beta-sheets, H-bonds connect adjacent strands.

title: What is a cis-proline turn and where is it found?
content: A cis-proline turn is a structural motif where proline adopts a cis peptide bond, often found in beta-turns connecting antiparallel beta-strands. These turns are common in protein surface loops.

title: How does the peptide bond dipole moment affect protein structure?
content: The peptide bond has a dipole moment of approximately 3.5 D, with the oxygen partially negative and the nitrogen partially positive. In alpha-helices, these dipoles align, creating a macroscopic helix dipole.

title: What is the dihedral angle constraint for trans peptide bonds?
content: In trans peptide bonds, the dihedral angle ω is approximately 180° ± 5°, placing the two alpha-carbons on opposite sides of the peptide bond plane.

title: Why is glycine more conformationally flexible than other amino acids?
content: Glycine has only a hydrogen atom as its side chain, so it lacks steric constraints on its phi and psi angles. This allows it to adopt conformations that are sterically forbidden for other amino acids.

title: What is the energy barrier for cis-trans isomerization of a peptide bond?
content: The energy barrier for cis-trans isomerization is approximately 20 kcal/mol (84 kJ/mol). This slow rotation can be a rate-limiting step in protein folding.

title: What is the significance of the Ramachandran plot in structural biology?
content: The Ramachandran plot maps sterically allowed backbone conformations. It is used to validate protein structures, identify residues in unusual conformations, and understand the conformational preferences of different amino acids.

title: What is the significance of peptide bonds in prodrug design?
content: Peptide prodrugs use protease-cleavable linkers to release active drugs. Valacyclovir uses valine ester for improved oral bioavailability. Peptide-drug conjugates target tumors via protease overexpression in tumor microenvironment.

title: How do proteasomes recognize and process peptide bonds?
content: The 26S proteasome recognizes ubiquitinated substrates. The 20S core has trypsin-like (cleaves after basic), chymotrypsin-like (after hydrophobic), and caspase-like (after acidic) activities. Proteasome inhibitors (bortezomib) target these activities.

title: What is the role of peptide bond geometry in ion channel selectivity?
content: Ion channels use backbone carbonyls and amides to coordinate ions. Potassium channels have signature sequence TVGYG forming the selectivity filter. Backbone carbonyls mimic water hydration shells, enabling selective K+ over Na+ conduction.

title: Describe the concept of peptide bond pseudo-rotation in protein dynamics.
content: Peptide bond pseudo-rotation describes correlated changes in backbone torsion angles. Essential dynamics analysis reveals large-scale motions involving peptide bond rearrangements. These motions are functionally important for enzyme catalysis.

title: What is the mechanism of peptide bond formation using cyanuric fluoride?
content: Cyanuric fluoride activates carboxyl groups for peptide coupling without requiring racemization. It forms an active ester intermediate that reacts with amines. Developed by cyanuric chloride derivatives, it provides rapid coupling with minimal epimerization.

title: How do peptide bonds influence the mechanism of protein translocation?
content: Sec61 translocon transports polypeptides across ER membranes. Peptide bonds in the translocation channel interact with the pore. Unfolded proteins thread through a 14-60 angstrom pore. Signal sequences initiate translocation.

title: What is the role of peptide bonds in molecular recognition?
content: Peptide bonds serve as hydrogen bond donors and acceptors in protein-protein interactions. SH3 domains recognize proline-rich motifs (PxxP) where peptide bond geometry is critical. MHC molecules present peptides with specific backbone conformations.

title: Explain the role of peptide bonds in spider silk mechanical properties.
content: Spider silk beta-sheet crystallites have tightly packed hydrogen bonds between peptide backbones. The amorphous regions provide extensibility. The combination of crystalline and amorphous regions gives silk its exceptional tensile strength and elasticity.

title: What is the significance of peptide bonds in biosensor design?
content: Peptide-based biosensors use protease cleavage to release reporters. FRET sensors measure protease activity by donor-acceptor separation. Peptide aptamers bind targets with high specificity. Electrochemical sensors detect peptide binding events.

title: Which amino acid most frequently adopts the cis conformation?
content: Proline most frequently adopts the cis conformation (~5-10% of X-Pro bonds). The cyclic structure of proline reduces the energy difference between cis and trans forms.

title: How do peptide bonds affect the pharmacokinetics of peptide drugs?
content: Peptide bonds are susceptible to endopeptidases and exopeptidases. Half-life can be extended by D-amino acid substitution, cyclization, PEGylation, or albumin binding. Oral bioavailability is limited by gastric acid and intestinal proteases.

title: What are the phi (φ) and psi (ψ) torsion angles in a peptide?
content: Phi (φ) is the rotation angle around the N-Cα bond, and psi (ψ) is the rotation angle around the Cα-C bond. Together with omega (ω), they define the backbone conformation.

title: What is the allowed range of phi and psi angles in a Ramachandran plot?
content: The Ramachandran plot shows sterically allowed conformations. Alpha-helices cluster around φ=-57°, ψ=-47°, while beta-sheets cluster around φ=-135°, ψ=+135°. Glycine has much broader allowed regions.

title: Why does the peptide bond have partial double-bond character?
content: The peptide bond has partial double-bond character due to resonance between the carbonyl oxygen and the amide nitrogen. This restricts rotation around the C-N bond and makes the peptide bond planar.

title: What is peptidyl-prolyl isomerase (PPIase)?
content: PPIase is an enzyme that catalyzes the cis-trans isomerization of proline peptide bonds, accelerating protein folding. Cyclophilin and FK506-binding proteins are examples of PPIases.

title: What is the bond length of a peptide C-N bond?
content: The peptide C-N bond length is approximately 1.32 Å, which is intermediate between a single C-N bond (1.49 Å) and a double C=N bond (1.27 Å), reflecting its partial double-bond character.

title: How many hydrogen bonds stabilize a single turn of an alpha-helix?
content: Each hydrogen bond in an alpha-helix connects the C=O of residue i to the N-H of residue i+4. There are approximately 3.6 residues per turn, each contributing one hydrogen bond to stabilize the helix.

title: What does PK stand for in pharmacology?
content: Pharmacokinetics - what the body does to the drug (absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion).

title: What is the first FDA-approved recombinant peptide drug?
content: Humulin (recombinant human insulin), approved in 1982.

title: What class of drugs does semaglutide belong to?
content: GLP-1 receptor agonists, used for type 2 diabetes and obesity.

title: What is the mechanism of cyclosporine?
content: Inhibits calcineurin, blocking T-cell activation. Used as an immunosuppressant.

title: What does PEGylation do to a peptide drug?
content: Increases hydrodynamic radius, reducing renal clearance and extending plasma half-life.

title: Why are most peptide drugs given by injection?
content: Peptides are degraded by GI proteases and have poor intestinal absorption due to size and polarity.

title: What is the difference between efficacy and potency?
content: Efficacy is the maximum effect a drug can produce; potency is the dose needed to produce a given effect.

title: What is an agonist?
content: A ligand that binds to a receptor and activates it, producing a biological response.

title: What is an antagonist?
content: A ligand that binds to a receptor but does not activate it, blocking the action of agonists.

title: What is desmopressin used for?
content: Treating diabetes insipidus and nocturnal enuresis. It is a synthetic vasopressin analog.

title: What is teriparatide?
content: Recombinant PTH(1-34) used to treat severe osteoporosis by stimulating bone formation.

title: What does PD stand for in pharmacology?
content: Pharmacodynamics - what the drug does to the body (mechanism of action, dose-response relationship).

title: What is AUC in pharmacokinetics?
content: Area Under the Curve - the total drug exposure over time, calculated from the plasma concentration-time profile.

title: What is the role of cAMP in GPCR signaling?
content: cAMP acts as a second messenger that activates protein kinase A (PKA), leading to phosphorylation of downstream targets.

title: What is the phospholipase C (PLC) pathway?
content: A signaling pathway where PLC cleaves PIP2 into IP3 and DAG, leading to calcium release and protein kinase C activation.

title: What is volume of distribution (Vd)?
content: A theoretical volume relating the total amount of drug in the body to the plasma concentration, indicating drug distribution.

title: What is clearance (CL) in pharmacokinetics?
content: The volume of plasma cleared of drug per unit time, reflecting the rate of drug elimination from the body.

title: What is steady-state concentration (Css)?
content: The plasma concentration achieved when the rate of drug administration equals the rate of elimination.

title: What is octreotide?
content: A synthetic somatostatin analog used to treat acromegaly, carcinoid tumors, and certain GI bleeding conditions.

title: What is leuprolide?
content: A GnRH agonist used to treat prostate cancer, endometriosis, and precocious puberty by initially stimulating then downregulating gonadotropin release.

title: What is calcitonin used for?
content: Treating osteoporosis and Paget's disease by inhibiting osteoclast activity and reducing bone resorption.

title: What is oxytocin used for clinically?
content: Inducing labor, controlling postpartum bleeding, and facilitating milk let-down during breastfeeding.

title: What is the therapeutic index (TI)?
content: The ratio of the toxic dose (TD50) to the effective dose (ED50). A higher TI indicates a safer drug.

title: What is lanreotide?
content: A long-acting somatostatin analog used to treat acromegaly and neuroendocrine tumors, administered as a depot injection.

title: What is bioavailability?
content: The fraction of an administered drug that reaches the systemic circulation in its active form.

title: What is the half-life (t1/2) of a drug?
content: The time required for the plasma concentration of a drug to decrease by 50%.

title: What is Cmax?
content: The maximum (peak) plasma concentration of a drug after administration.

title: What is Tmax?
content: The time at which the maximum plasma concentration (Cmax) is reached after drug administration.

title: What is the first-pass effect?
content: Metabolism of a drug in the gut wall and liver before it reaches systemic circulation, reducing oral bioavailability.

title: What is the main route of elimination for most peptide drugs?
content: Renal excretion (kidneys filter and excrete peptides).

title: What is the concept of receptor reserve and its pharmacological significance?
content: Receptor reserve (spare receptors) occurs when maximal response requires only a fraction of receptors occupied. Allows potent agonists to produce full effects at low occupancy. Explains why receptor downregulation may not reduce maximal response initially.

title: How do drug transporters affect pharmacokinetics?
content: Efflux transporters (P-gp/MDR1, BCRP, MRP2) limit oral bioavailability and CNS penetration. Influx transporters (OATP1B1, OCT2, PEPT1) enhance absorption. Genetic polymorphisms in transporters cause interindividual variability in drug exposure.

title: What is the concept of pharmacogenomics and its clinical applications?
content: Pharmacogenomics studies genetic variants affecting drug response. CYP2D6 polymorphisms affect codeine activation. HLA-B*5701 predicts abacavir hypersensitivity. DPYD variants predict 5-fluorouracil toxicity. PGx testing guides dosing per CPIC guidelines.

title: Explain the concept of therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM).
content: TDM measures drug concentrations to maintain therapeutic ranges. Required for drugs with narrow therapeutic indices (aminoglycosides, vancomycin, lithium, digoxin, phenytoin). Trough levels guide dosing; AUC-based dosing is more accurate for aminoglycosides.

title: What are the principles of drug-drug interaction prediction?
content: Prediction uses in vitro CYP inhibition/induction data, physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) modeling, and clinical DDI studies. FDA guidance requires DDI studies for CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers. Time-dependent inhibition requires pre-incubation studies.

title: Describe the concept of target-mediated drug disposition (TMDD).
content: TMDD occurs when drug binding to its pharmacological target significantly affects PK. Examples: monoclonal antibodies (receptor-mediated clearance), EPO (receptor binding), warfarin (albumin binding). Models include quasi-equilibrium and rapid binding approximations.

title: What is the role of efflux transporters in blood-brain barrier penetration?
content: P-glycoprotein (P-gp/ABCB1) and BCRP (ABCG2) actively efflux drugs from brain endothelial cells. CNS drugs must overcome efflux (low P-gp substrate affinity) or be designed as poor substrates. Co-administration of P-gp inhibitors can increase brain exposure.

title: Explain the concept of drug repurposing and its advantages.
content: Drug repurposing identifies new indications for approved drugs. Advantages: known safety profile, established manufacturing, shorter development time. Examples: thalidomide (EM -> myeloma), sildenafil (angina -> ED), minoxidil (hypertension -> alopecia).

title: What are the ADMET properties and how are they assessed?
content: ADMET = Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion, Toxicity. Assessed in silico (QSAR), in vitro (Caco-2, microsomes, hERG), and in vivo (PK studies). Lipinski rule of 5 predicts oral bioavailability. hERG channel inhibition predicts cardiac toxicity.

title: How do monoclonal antibodies differ in PK from small molecules?
content: mAbs: ~150 kDa, low Vd (extracellular fluid), FcRn-mediated recycling extends half-life (21 days typical), linear PK at low doses, nonlinear at high doses due to target saturation. Small molecules: rapid distribution, hepatic metabolism, shorter half-lives.

title: What is the concept of steady-state concentration and time to reach it?
content: Css = average concentration at dosing equilibrium. Time to steady state = 4-5 half-lives. For drugs with long half-lives (amiodarone t1/2 ~40 days), loading doses achieve therapeutic levels faster. Css is independent of dose rate for linear kinetics.

title: Explain the mechanism of competitive versus non-competitive antagonism.
content: Competitive antagonists (e.g., prazosin) bind same site as agonist, shifting dose-response rightward without reducing Emax. Non-competitive antagonists (e.g., phenoxybenzamine) bind allosterically or irreversibly, reducing Emax. Surmountable vs insurmountable.

title: Describe the mechanism of drug resistance in cancer chemotherapy.
content: Resistance mechanisms: decreased drug uptake (reduced transporter expression), increased efflux (MDR1/P-gp upregulation), altered drug targets (topoisomerase mutations), enhanced DNA repair, increased drug metabolism, and anti-apoptotic pathway activation.

title: What is the principle of receptor theory in pharmacology?
content: Clark receptor theory: response is proportional to receptor occupancy. Step 2 theory: drug-receptor complex has intrinsic activity (efficacy). Operational model: response = (Em x tau^n x [D]^n) / ([D]^n x (Kd + [D])^n). Explains partial agonism and inverse agonism.

title: Explain the concept of bias signaling in GPCR pharmacology.
content: Biased agonists preferentially activate one signaling pathway over another. G-protein biased (e.g., oliceridine for Gq over beta-arrestin) may have better safety profiles. Arrestin-biased ligands may have therapeutic potential in pain without respiratory depression.

title: What are the principles of controlled drug delivery systems?
content: Controlled delivery maintains therapeutic concentrations over extended periods. Systems include: reservoir (membrane-controlled), matrix (dissolution/erosion), osmotic pumps (OROS), and depot injections (PLGA microspheres). Zero-order kinetics is ideal.

title: How does protein binding affect drug distribution and efficacy?
content: Only free (unbound) drug is pharmacologically active. Highly protein-bound drugs (warfarin 99%) are susceptible to displacement interactions. Free fraction determines Vd and clearance. Acidic drugs bind albumin; basic drugs bind alpha-1-acid glycoprotein.

title: What is the concept of chronopharmacology?
content: Chronopharmacology studies time-dependent drug effects. Corticosteroids given in morning mimic physiological rhythm. Statins given at night target nighttime cholesterol synthesis. Chronotherapy adjusts dosing to circadian patterns for improved efficacy and reduced toxicity.

title: Explain the mechanism of PEGylation and its pharmacokinetic benefits.
content: PEGylation attaches polyethylene glycol to proteins, increasing hydrodynamic radius and reducing renal clearance. Also shields from proteolysis and immune recognition. PEGylated interferons (PEG-IFN) have weekly dosing vs daily for unmodified IFN.

title: What are the mechanisms of drug-induced QT prolongation?
content: hERG (KCNH2) channel blockade by drugs prolongs cardiac action potential. Risk factors: female sex, hypokalemia, congenital long QT. In vitro hERG assays and in vivo telemetry required for safety. Many drugs withdrawn due to TdP risk.

title: Describe the concept of dose-response relationships in toxicology.
content: Paracelsus: the dose makes the poison. Dose-response curves identify NOAEL (no observed adverse effect level), LOAEL, and TD50. Safety margin = TD50/ED50. Margin of exposure (MOE) = BMDL10/TDI for carcinogens.

title: What is the role of physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) modeling?
content: PBPK models simulate drug PK using anatomical compartments, blood flows, and tissue-specific parameters. Applications: DDI prediction, pediatric dosing, special populations, regulatory submissions. Software: GastroPlus, Simcyp, PK-Sim.

title: What is the therapeutic window and how is it determined?
content: Therapeutic window = TD50/ED50 (toxic dose / effective dose). Wide windows (e.g., penicillin) are safer. Narrow windows (e.g., warfarin, digoxin, lithium) require therapeutic drug monitoring. Determined from dose-response curves in clinical trials.

title: How do antibody-drug conjugates (ADCs) combine targeting with cytotoxicity?
content: ADCs = monoclonal antibody + linker + cytotoxic payload. Antibody provides tumor selectivity. Linker stability prevents premature release (cleavable vs non-cleavable). Payload potency (DM1, MMAE) enables killing at low drug-to-antibody ratios.

title: What is the concept of therapeutic equivalence and bioequivalence?
content: Therapeutic equivalence: same clinical effect and safety. Bioequivalence: same rate and extent of absorption (AUC, Cmax within 80-125%). Generic drugs require bioequivalence studies. Narrow therapeutic index drugs require tighter bounds (90-111%).

title: Explain the mechanism of drug tolerance and tachyphylaxis.
content: Tolerance: decreased response with repeated dosing (pharmacodynamic: receptor downregulation; pharmacokinetic: enzyme induction). Tachyphylaxis: rapid tolerance after single dose or short period. Examples: opioid tolerance, nitrate tolerance, beta-agonist desensitization.

title: What are the principles of drug design for CNS penetration?
content: CNS drugs need: MW <450, logP 1-3, PSA <90 A^2, <8 H-bond donors, low P-gp efflux. Lipinski rules modified for CNS. Intranasal delivery bypasses BBB. Convection-enhanced delivery bypasses BBB for large molecules.

title: How do drug formulation strategies improve oral bioavailability?
content: Strategies: amorphous solid dispersions (increased dissolution), nanocrystals (increased surface area), lipid-based formulations (lymphatic transport), co-crystals (improved solubility), cyclodextrin complexation (increased solubility).

title: What is the mechanism of enzyme induction and its clinical significance?
content: Enzyme induction increases metabolic capacity. CYP inducers: rifampin (CYP3A4), carbamazepine, phenytoin, St. John wort. Reduces plasma levels of co-administered drugs (e.g., OCP failure with rifampin). Induction takes 1-2 weeks for full effect.

title: Describe the concept of organ-on-a-chip for drug testing.
content: Organ-on-a-chip microdevices mimic organ physiology with flowing cultures. Applications: liver-on-chip (metabolism), lung-on-chip (barrier function), heart-on-chip (cardiotoxicity). Human-relevant, reduces animal testing, enables personalized medicine.

title: What are the principles of population pharmacokinetic modeling?
content: PopPK uses sparse sampling from many individuals to estimate population PK parameters and interindividual variability. NONMEM is the gold standard. Covariates (weight, renal function, genotype) explain variability. Applications: dose individualization, special populations.

title: How does the enteric microbiome affect drug metabolism?
content: Gut bacteria perform reduction, hydrolysis, and deconjugation reactions. Digoxin inactivated by Eggerthella lenta. Irinotecan toxicity from bacterial beta-glucuronidase reactivating SN-38. Prodrugs may be activated by microbiome. Antibiotics alter drug metabolism.

title: What is the concept of synergy in combination drug therapy?
content: Synergy: combined effect > sum of individual effects (Loewe additivity, Bliss independence). Examples: trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (sequential blockade), ACE inhibitor + diuretic. Antagonism: combined effect < sum. Chou-Talalay method quantifies synergy.

title: Describe the concept of volume of distribution and its clinical implications.
content: Vd = amount of drug in body / plasma concentration. Low Vd (<0.6 L/kg) = stays in plasma. High Vd (>1 L/kg) = distributes to tissues. Vd helps calculate loading dose: Loading dose = Vd x Cp(target). Aspirin Vd ~0.2 L/kg; chloroquine Vd ~200 L/kg.

title: Explain the role of biomarkers in drug development.
content: Biomarkers validate targets, predict response, monitor efficacy and safety. Surrogate endpoints (HbA1c for diabetes, viral load for HCV) accelerate approval. FDA biomarker qualification program. Companion diagnostics identify responders (HER2, PD-L1).

title: What is the mechanism of drug-induced hepatotoxicity?
content: Direct hepatotoxins (acetaminophen - NAPQI metabolite) cause dose-dependent damage. Idiosyncratic reactions (drug-induced autoimmune hepatitis) are unpredictable. Mechanisms: reactive metabolites, mitochondrial dysfunction, bile salt export pump inhibition, immune-mediated.

title: Explain the concept of molecular mimicry in drug hypersensitivity.
content: Drug hypersensitivity involves immune recognition of drug-protein adducts. HLA alleles predict risk: HLA-B*5701 (abacavir), HLA-B*1502 (carbamazepine in Asians). T-cell mediated delayed reactions involve cross-reactive drug-specific T cells.

title: What are the principles of drug design for renal dosing adjustment?
content: Renally cleared drugs require dose adjustment based on CrCl or GFR. Cockcroft-Gault equation estimates CrCl. Drugs with >30% renal clearance need adjustment. Hemodialysis removes drugs based on MW, protein binding, Vd. Supplemental dosing guidelines available.

title: How do nucleic acid therapeutics achieve tissue-specific delivery?
content: Lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) for liver targeting (ApoE adsorption). GalNAc conjugation for hepatocyte-specific delivery via ASGPR. Antibody-oligonucleotide conjugates for non-liver targets. Exosome delivery for CNS. Chemical modifications (2'-OMe, PS) improve stability.

title: What is the concept of drug safety pharmacology?
content: Safety pharmacology assesses effects on vital organs: cardiovascular (hERG, telemetry), respiratory (plethysmography), CNS (Irwin test). ICH S7A/S7B guidelines require core battery studies. hERG liability is a major cause of drug attrition.

title: Describe the principles of antiviral drug design.
content: Antiviral targets: entry inhibitors (maraviroc), uncoating (amantadine), polymerases (sofosbuvir), proteases (ritonavir), release (oseltamivir). Resistance is a challenge - combination therapy (HAART) prevents resistance. Nucleoside analogs are a major drug class.

title: What is the mechanism of allosteric drug modulation?
content: Allosteric modulators bind distinct sites from orthosteric ligands. Positive allosteric modulators (PAMs) enhance agonist effects (benzodiazepines on GABA-A). Negative allosteric modulators (NAMs) reduce effects. Advantages: ceiling effect, subtype selectivity.

title: How do microRNA-based therapeutics work?
content: Anti-miRs inhibit pathogenic miRNAs (e.g., anti-miR-122 for HCV). miRNA mimics restore tumor suppressor miRNAs. Delivery challenges: stability, tissue targeting, off-target effects. Chemical modifications (2'-F, LNA) and nanoparticle delivery improve efficacy.

title: What is the role of physicochemical properties in drug-likeness?
content: Key properties: MW (oral <500), logP (<5), HBD (<5), HBA (<10), PSA (<140 A^2), rotatable bonds (<10). Veber rules: TPSA <140, rotatable bonds <10. Beyond Rule of 5 for macrocycles, peptide-like drugs.

title: What are the phases of drug metabolism and their roles?
content: Phase I: oxidation, reduction, hydrolysis (CYP450 enzymes). Phase II: conjugation (glucuronidation, sulfation, acetylation, glutathione). Phase III: transport (P-gp efflux). Phase I often activates prodrugs; Phase II increases water solubility for excretion.

title: Explain the concept of precision medicine in pharmacotherapy.
content: Precision medicine uses genetic, biomarker, and clinical data to tailor therapy. Pharmacogenomics guides dosing (CYP2C19 for clopidogrel). Tumor profiling selects targeted therapies. Liquid biopsies monitor treatment response. Companion diagnostics identify responders.

title: Explain the mechanism of CYP450 inhibition and drug interactions.
content: CYP inhibitors include reversible (ketoconazole - competitive) and irreversible (erythromycin - mechanism-based) types. CYP3A4 metabolizes ~50% of drugs. Co-administration with inhibitors increases AUC of substrates, potentially causing toxicity.

title: What is the concept of bioavailability and its determination?
content: Bioavailability (F) = fraction of dose reaching systemic circulation. For IV, F=1. For oral, F = (AUC_oral/AUC_IV) x (Dose_IV/Dose_oral). Affected by first-pass metabolism, solubility, permeability, and formulation. FDA requires bioequivalence testing.

title: Describe the Hill equation and its use in pharmacology.
content: Hill equation: E = Emax x [C]^n / (EC50^n + [C]^n). n (Hill coefficient) indicates cooperativity: n=1 (hyperbolic, no cooperativity), n>1 (positive cooperativity, sigmoidal), n<1 (negative cooperativity). Used for dose-response curve fitting.

title: What are the principles of prodrug design?
content: Prodrugs are pharmacologically inactive until metabolized to active forms. Strategies: ester prodrugs for improved oral absorption (valacyclovir), phosphate prodrugs for IV solubility (fosphenytoin), mutual prodrugs combining two drugs.

title: What is the primary structure of a protein?
content: The linear sequence of amino acids linked by peptide bonds.

title: What are the four levels of protein structure?
content: Primary (sequence), secondary (local folding like alpha helices and beta sheets), tertiary (overall 3D fold), and quaternary (multi-subunit assembly).

title: What stabilizes an alpha helix?
content: Hydrogen bonds between the C=O of residue i and the N-H of residue i+4.

title: How many residues per turn in an alpha helix?
content: 3.6 residues per turn.

title: What is the rise per residue in an alpha helix?
content: 1.5 Angstroms per residue.

title: What is the difference between parallel and antiparallel beta sheets?
content: In parallel sheets, adjacent strands run in the same N-to-C direction; in antiparallel sheets, adjacent strands run in opposite directions.

title: What forces maintain tertiary structure?
content: Hydrophobic interactions, hydrogen bonds, ionic bonds, disulfide bridges, and van der Waals forces between side chains.

title: What is quaternary structure?
content: The arrangement and interaction of multiple polypeptide subunits into a functional protein complex.

title: What is a Ramachandran plot used for?
content: It visualizes the allowed phi and psi backbone dihedral angles, helping identify secondary structures and verify protein structure quality.

title: What is a motif in protein structure?
content: A recognizable folding pattern involving two or more elements of secondary structure and their connecting loops.

title: What does HPLC stand for?
content: High-Performance Liquid Chromatography, used to separate and purify peptides.

title: What is lyophilization?
content: Freeze-drying, a process that removes solvent from a peptide solution under vacuum, producing a stable dry powder.

title: What is ion exchange chromatography?
content: A purification technique that separates peptides based on their net charge using charged resin.

title: What is size exclusion chromatography (SEC)?
content: A technique that separates molecules by size, with larger molecules eluting first.

title: What is a peptide map?
content: A chromatographic fingerprint of a peptide or protein obtained by enzymatic or chemical cleavage followed by HPLC analysis.

title: What is circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy?
content: A technique that measures the differential absorption of left and right circularly polarized light to determine protein secondary structure.

title: What is amino acid analysis (AAA)?
content: A technique that hydrolyzes a peptide into individual amino acids and quantifies each one to determine composition and concentration.

title: What is HPLC column temperature used for?
content: Controlling column temperature improves separation reproducibility and peak shape, typically maintained at 30-50 degrees C.

title: What is UV detection at 214 nm used for in HPLC?
content: Detection of peptide bonds (the amide bond absorbs at 214 nm), providing a universal detection method for peptides.

title: What is the advantage of UHPLC over HPLC?
content: Ultra-High Performance LC uses smaller particle sizes and higher pressures for faster, higher-resolution separations.

title: What is a deconvoluted mass spectrum?
content: A mass spectrum processed to remove the multiply charged ion series, showing the true molecular weight of the peptide.

title: What is the most common stationary phase in reversed-phase HPLC?
content: C18 (octadecylsilane), which retains hydrophobic peptides.

title: What is a TIC in mass spectrometry?
content: Total Ion Chromatogram, showing the sum of all ion intensities across the chromatographic run.

title: What is a gradient in HPLC?
content: A programmed change in solvent composition over time (e.g., increasing acetonitrile concentration) to optimize peptide separation.

title: What is ESI-MS?
content: Electrospray Ionization Mass Spectrometry, a soft ionization technique for analyzing peptides and proteins.

title: What is MALDI-TOF?
content: Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization Time-of-Flight mass spectrometry, used for rapid peptide mass determination.

title: What is Edman degradation?
content: A sequential method for determining peptide N-terminal amino acid sequence by chemically cleaving one residue at a time.

title: What is LC-MS/MS?
content: Liquid Chromatography coupled with tandem Mass Spectrometry, used for peptide identification and sequencing.

title: What is the purpose of a scavenger in TFA cleavage?
content: Scavengers (e.g., TIS, EDT, water) react with reactive cations released during cleavage to prevent them from reattaching to the peptide.

title: What is analytical HPLC used for?
content: Assessing peptide purity, identity, and quality before preparative purification or biological testing.

title: What are the two major classes of cell surface receptors?
content: G-protein coupled receptors (GPCRs) and enzyme-linked receptors (including receptor tyrosine kinases).

title: What is the role of arrestins in GPCR signaling?
content: Arrestins uncouple GPCRs from G-proteins (desensitization) and target them for internalization via endocytosis.

title: How many transmembrane domains does a GPCR have?
content: Seven transmembrane alpha helices (7TM).

title: What second messenger does adenylyl cyclase produce?
content: Cyclic AMP (cAMP) from ATP.

title: What is the difference between affinity and efficacy?
content: Affinity is the strength of binding between a ligand and receptor. Efficacy is the ability of a bound ligand to activate the receptor.

title: What is receptor desensitization?
content: Reduced cellular response to a stimulus after repeated or prolonged exposure to an agonist.

title: What does the MAPK/ERK pathway regulate?
content: Cell proliferation, differentiation, survival, and apoptosis.

title: What is signal amplification?
content: A process where one receptor-ligand interaction activates multiple downstream signaling molecules, amplifying the cellular response.

title: What is an allosteric modulator?
content: A ligand that binds at a site distinct from the orthosteric binding site, altering receptor activity without activating it directly.

title: What is receptor internalization?
content: The process by which a receptor-ligand complex is taken into the cell via endocytosis, removing it from the cell surface.

title: What is cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM) and how does it determine protein structures?
content: Cryo-EM flash-freezes proteins in vitreous ice, collecting 2D projection images. Single-particle analysis classifies and averages thousands of images to reconstruct 3D structures. Resolution revolution: direct electron detectors and software (RELION, cryoSPARC) enable near-atomic resolution.

title: Describe the principles of protein structure validation and quality metrics.
content: Ramachandran plot assesses backbone phi/psi angles. Clashscore measures steric clashes. R-factor and R-free indicate model-data agreement. MolProbity, wwPDB validation reports. Disordered regions: B-factors, occupancy. Coordinates deposited in PDB.

title: What is cryo-EM single-particle analysis workflow?
content: Workflow: sample preparation (grid freezing), data collection (microscope + camera), motion correction, CTF estimation, particle picking, 2D classification, 3D reconstruction, model building, refinement. Key challenges: preferred orientation, heterogeneity, flexibility.

title: Explain the concept of resolution in structural biology techniques.
content: Resolution: minimum distance between distinguishable features. Crystallography: d_min from diffraction. Cryo-EM: Gold-standard FSC 0.143 cutoff. NMR: atom-specific but limited molecular weight. Higher resolution = more detail: 1.5A (individual atoms), 3A (side chains), 5A (secondary structure).

title: What are the limitations of X-ray crystallography?
content: Crystallization bottleneck: ~30% of proteins crystallize. Crystal contacts may distort native structure. Radiation damage. Static picture: time-averaged over millions of unit cells. Flexible regions poorly resolved. Membrane proteins require detergent/lipid environment.

title: How does integrative structural biology combine multiple data sources?
content: Integrative modeling combines cryo-EM, crystallography, NMR, cross-linking MS, SAXS, and computational methods. IMP (Integrative Modeling Platform) and HADDOCK generate models consistent with all data. Resolves structures of large complexes beyond single-technique limits.

title: What is the role of AlphaFold in structural biology and its limitations?
content: AlphaFold provides accurate structure predictions (GDT-TS >90 for many targets). Limitations: does not predict dynamics, ligand binding, or protein-protein interactions reliably. Predicts single conformation. Experimental validation still needed. Complementary to experimental methods.

title: Explain the concept of room-temperature crystallography using XFELs.
content: X-ray free-electron lasers (XFELs) deliver femtosecond pulses, outrunning radiation damage (diffraction-before-destruction). Serial femtosecond crystallography (SFX) collects patterns from microcrystals. Room-temperature data capture native conformations and intermediate states.

title: What is solid-state NMR and how is it applied to membrane proteins?
content: Solid-state NMR uses magic-angle spinning (MAS) to average anisotropic interactions. Provides atomic-resolution data on membrane proteins in lipid bilayers. Applications: amyloid fibrils, membrane protein structure, protein-ligand interactions. DNP enhancement improves sensitivity.

title: Describe the application of cross-linking mass spectrometry (XL-MS) in structural biology.
content: XL-MS uses bifunctional cross-linkers (BS3, DSS) to capture spatial proximity. Enzymatic digestion and MS identify cross-linked peptides. Distance restraints (typically 10-30 angstrom) constrain modeling. Applications: protein complexes, conformational changes.

title: What is the role of electron cryo-microscopy in drug discovery?
content: Cryo-EM enables structure determination of drug-target complexes without crystallization. Fragment screening by cryo-EM. Membrane protein-drug complexes visualized in lipid environment. Allosteric binding sites identified. Structure-based drug design accelerated.

title: Explain the principle of X-ray crystallography for protein structure determination.
content: X-ray crystallography measures diffraction patterns from protein crystals. Bragg law relates diffraction angle to d-spacing. Phasing problem solved by molecular replacement, SAD, or MIR. Electron density maps refined to atomic models. Resolution determines detail level.

title: How do maximum entropy methods improve structural biology data analysis?
content: Maximum entropy maximizes information content while remaining consistent with data. Applied to SAXS profile deconvolution, NMR spectral analysis, cryo-EM reconstructions. Reduces bias from prior assumptions. Provides most parsimonious solution consistent with observations.

title: What is the concept of conformational ensembles in structural biology?
content: Proteins exist as conformational ensembles, not single structures. NMR relaxation, cryo-EM heterogeneity analysis, and MD simulations capture ensembles. Functional motions: hinge bending, domain rotation, loop dynamics. Ensemble models better represent solution behavior.

title: Explain the role of lipid nanodiscs in membrane protein structural biology.
content: Nanodiscs (MSP, SMA polymers) solubilize membrane proteins in discoidal lipid bilayers. Enable solution-state characterization without detergents. Compatible with cryo-EM, NMR, and functional assays. SMA (styrene-maleic acid) extracts directly from native membranes.

title: What is the principle of neutron protein crystallography?
content: Neutrons scatter from nuclei, not electrons. Reveals hydrogen positions directly. Complementary to X-ray crystallography. Essential for protonation states, water structure, and enzyme mechanisms. Requires large crystals and neutron sources (reactors, spallation).

title: How does cryo-EM handle protein flexibility and heterogeneity?
content: 3D variability analysis and multibody refinement separate conformational states. Classification algorithms (RELION, cryoSPARC) sort particles by conformation. Trajectory analysis reveals motion pathways. Complementary to NMR dynamics data.

title: What is the role of molecular replacement in X-ray crystallography?
content: Molecular replacement uses a known homologous structure as search model. Phaser and BALBES rotate and translate the model to solve the phase problem. Works when sequence identity >25%. Multi-domain complexes may require separate MR solutions.

title: Describe the concept of pseudo-atomic models from cryo-EM.
content: Cryogenic electron density at 3-5 angstrom enables pseudo-atomic model building. Secondary structure visible; side chains resolved at higher resolution. Model building: Coot, ISOLDE. Refinement: REFMAC, Phenix. Flexible fitting for lower-resolution maps.

title: What is the application of cryo-EM in studying large macromolecular complexes?
content: Cryo-EM excels for large complexes (>200 kDa) difficult to crystallize. Ribosome (2.5 MDa), spliceosome, nuclear pore complex, viral capsids resolved. Subnanometer resolution reveals subunit organization. Symmetry averaging improves resolution for symmetric complexes.

title: Explain the concept of real-space refinement in structural biology.
content: Real-space refinement minimizes difference between model and experimental density. Iterative model building and refinement. Phenix real_space_refine, REFMAC5. TLS refinement accounts for group anisotropic displacement. Validation: Ramachandran, clashscore, geometry.

title: What is the role of computational methods in understanding protein dynamics?
content: MD simulations sample conformational space. Enhanced sampling (metadynamics, replica exchange) overcomes barriers. Coarse-grained models extend timescales. Essential dynamics identifies collective motions. Markov state models connect simulations to experimental observables.

title: What are the advantages of NMR spectroscopy for studying protein dynamics?
content: NMR provides atomic-resolution information in solution state. Detects motions from picoseconds to seconds. Heteronuclear experiments (15N, 13C) provide residue-specific data. Chemical shift perturbation maps binding interfaces. Residual dipolar couplings determine long-range order.

title: How do integrated structural biology approaches solve challenging targets?
content: Combine cryo-EM (overall shape), X-ray (high-resolution regions), NMR (dynamics), XL-MS (distance restraints), SAXS (solution shape). Computation integrates data into consistent models. Examples: nuclear pore complex, ribosome biogenesis machinery.

title: What is the concept of time-resolved structural biology?
content: Time-resolved methods capture structural changes during biological processes. XFEL: femtosecond snapshots. Time-resolved cryo-EM: rapid freezing at defined time points. T-jump NMR: millisecond kinetics. SERS: nanosecond Raman. Reveals reaction mechanisms and conformational changes.

title: Explain the application of ML/AI in cryo-EM data processing.
content: Deep learning for particle picking (crYOLO, TOPAZ), 2D classification (cryoDRGN), 3D refinement. Neural networks for CTF estimation, motion correction. Generative models for density refinement. Reduces manual intervention and improves throughput.

title: What is the role of small-angle neutron scattering (SANS) in structural biology?
content: SANS provides low-resolution shape information. Deuterium contrast variation enables selective visualization of components in complexes. Applications: lipid bilayer structure, protein-nucleic acid complexes, surfactant systems. Complementary to SAXS.

title: Describe the principle of electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy for proteins.
content: EPR detects unpaired electrons (spin labels). DEER/PELDOR measures distances (1.5-8 nm) between spin labels. CW-EPR provides dynamics information. Site-directed spin labeling (SDSL) maps conformational changes. Applications: membrane protein structure, protein folding.

title: What is the concept of single-molecule FRET for studying protein dynamics?
content: smFRET measures distances between donor and acceptor fluorophores (1-10 nm). Fluorescence fluctuations report on conformational changes in real time. Applications: enzyme catalysis, protein folding, molecular motor dynamics. Provides kinetic information hidden in ensemble measurements.

title: How does cryo-EM visualize drug binding to targets?
content: Cryo-EM resolves drug molecules in density maps at 2-4 angstrom resolution. Enables structure-based drug design without crystallization. Fragment screening: identify weak binders by density. Allosteric sites visualized. Comparison of apo/holo forms reveals binding mechanism.

title: What is the role of Rosetta in protein structure prediction and design?
content: Rosetta uses physics-based and knowledge-based energy functions. Ab initio prediction: fragment assembly and Monte Carlo. Protein design: inverse folding, interface design. RosettaScripts enables custom protocols. Applications: enzyme design, vaccine immunogen design.

title: Explain the concept of conformational selection versus induced fit.
content: Conformational selection: ligand binds pre-existing conformation. Induced fit: ligand binding causes conformational change. NMR and smFRET distinguish mechanisms. Most proteins use combination. Understanding mechanism aids drug design and enzyme engineering.

title: What is the application of mass photometry in structural biology?
content: Mass photometry measures mass of single molecules in solution by interferometric scattering microscopy. Determines oligomeric state, conformational heterogeneity, and binding events. Label-free, in solution. Complementary to cryo-EM and AUC.

title: Describe the application of molecular dynamics simulations in structural biology.
content: MD simulations solve Newton equations of motion for atomic systems. Force fields (AMBER, CHARMM, OPLS) parameterize interactions. Timescales: fs to ms. Applications: protein folding, ligand binding, membrane dynamics. Enhanced sampling (metadynamics, replica exchange) overcomes kinetic barriers.

title: How do cryo-EM maps validate molecular models?
content: FSC (Fourier Shell Correlation) between two half-maps estimates resolution. Model-map FSC assesses model quality. EMRinger validates side chain rotamers. CaBLAM identifies backbone outliers. Phenix validation suite provides comprehensive metrics.

title: What is small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) and what information does it provide?
content: SAXS measures scattering intensity at small angles (0.1-5 degrees). Provides shape information, radius of gyration, maximum dimension, and molecular weight. Low-resolution envelope for flexible systems. Complementary to high-resolution crystallography and cryo-EM.

title: Explain the concept of hydrogen-deuterium exchange mass spectrometry (HDX-MS).
content: HDX-MS measures amide proton exchange rates with deuterium. Protected regions (secondary structure, interfaces) exchange slowly. Mass increase measured by MS over time. Conformational dynamics and binding interfaces mapped at peptide resolution.

title: What is cryo-electron tomography (cryo-ET) and its applications?
content: Cryo-ET tilts frozen samples to collect 3D tomograms. Visualizes proteins in native cellular context at sub-nm resolution. Subtomogram averaging improves resolution. Applications: nuclear pore complex, ribosome-membrane interactions, viral structure in cells.

title: How does AlphaFold predict protein structures?
content: AlphaFold uses deep learning with multiple sequence alignments and evolutionary covariation. Attention mechanisms capture long-range contacts. Template-based and ab initio prediction combined. AlphaFold2 achieves experimental accuracy for many proteins. AlphaFold DB provides predicted structures.

title: What is the role of synchrotron radiation in structural biology?
content: Synchrotrons produce high-brilliance X-rays for crystallography and SAXS. Anomalous scattering enables phasing (SeMet SAD). Microfocus beams enable analysis of small crystals. Time-resolved studies capture conformational changes. XFELs enable serial femtosecond crystallography.

title: What does SPPS stand for?
content: Solid-Phase Peptide Synthesis, developed by Robert Bruce Merrifield.

title: What is the advantage of Fmoc SPPS over Boc SPPS?
content: Fmoc SPPS uses milder basic conditions for deprotection (piperidine) instead of harsh acidic conditions (HF) required for Boc.

title: What is a Fmoc amino acid?
content: An amino acid protected at the alpha-amino group with a fluorenylmethyloxycarbonyl (Fmoc) group, used in SPPS.

title: What is a side-chain protecting group?
content: A chemical group that masks reactive side-chain functional groups during synthesis to prevent unwanted side reactions.

title: What is HATU?
content: A uronium-based coupling reagent (hexafluorophosphate azabenzotriazole tetramethyl uronium) used for efficient amide bond formation.

title: What is DIC in peptide synthesis?
content: N,N'-Diisopropylcarbodiimide, a carbodiimide coupling reagent that activates carboxyl groups for amide bond formation.

title: What is a Rink amide resin?
content: A solid support used in SPPS that produces a C-terminal amide upon cleavage, commonly used for bioactive peptides.

title: What is a Wang resin?
content: A solid support used in SPPS that produces a C-terminal carboxylic acid upon cleavage.

title: What is a truncated peptide?
content: A peptide shorter than the desired sequence, resulting from incomplete coupling during SPPS.

title: What is DMF used for in SPPS?
content: Dimethylformamide (DMF) is a polar aprotic solvent used to swell the resin and dissolve amino acids and reagents.

title: What is a microwave-assisted peptide synthesis?
content: Using microwave irradiation to accelerate coupling and deprotection steps in SPPS, reducing synthesis time.

title: What reagent removes the Fmoc protecting group?
content: Piperidine (typically 20% in DMF) via beta-elimination.

title: What is the maximum peptide length achievable by standard SPPS?
content: Typically 50-70 amino acids, though longer peptides can be made with optimized protocols or native chemical ligation.

title: What reagent removes the Boc protecting group?
content: TFA (trifluoroacetic acid), typically 25-50% in dichloromethane.

title: What is the role of HOBt in peptide coupling?
content: Suppresses racemization and increases coupling efficiency by activating the carboxyl group.

title: What is a coupling reagent in SPPS?
content: A chemical that activates the carboxyl group of the incoming amino acid to form an amide bond with the growing peptide chain.

title: What is the final step in Fmoc SPPS?
content: Cleavage with TFA and scavengers to remove the peptide from the resin and strip side-chain protecting groups.

title: What is a resin in SPPS?
content: An insoluble polymeric support (e.g., Wang resin, Rink amide resin) to which the first amino acid is covalently attached.

title: What is racemization in peptide synthesis?
content: Loss of stereochemical integrity at the alpha-carbon, converting L-amino acids to D-amino acids during coupling.

title: What is a Kaiser test?
content: A ninhydrin-based color test used to detect free amino groups on the resin, indicating whether coupling is complete.

title: What is the copper-catalyzed azide-alkyne cycloaddition (CuAAC) click reaction?
content: CuAAC forms 1,4-disubstituted 1,2,3-triazole from azide and terminal alkyne using Cu(I) catalyst. Reaction is regiospecific, high-yielding, and tolerates many functional groups. Copper ligands (TBTA) prevent Cu aggregation. Used extensively in bioconjugation.

title: How does microfluidics enable peptide synthesis and screening?
content: Microfluidic reactors enable rapid, controlled peptide synthesis with reduced reagent consumption. Droplet-based systems screen peptide libraries. Continuous flow enables real-time monitoring. Reduces waste and improves reproducibility compared to batch synthesis.

title: What is the mechanism of EDC/HOBt coupling in peptide synthesis?
content: EDC (carbodiimide) activates carboxyl group to O-acylisourea. HOBt forms active ester (OBt ester) preventing racemization and increasing coupling rate. NHS can substitute for HOBt. DIC is a less water-soluble alternative to EDC.

title: Explain the concept of on-resin vs solution-phase peptide synthesis.
content: On-resin (SPPS): peptide attached to solid support, reagents in excess, simple washing. Solution-phase: homogeneous reactions, easier purification of intermediates, but requires protecting groups. Hybrid approaches combine both for complex peptides.

title: What is the role of pseudoprolines in peptide synthesis?
content: Pseudoprolines (oxazolidines from Ser/Thr) act as temporary proline surrogates. They disrupt aggregation during SPPS and are cleaved during TFA deprotection. Effectively increase peptide solubility and coupling efficiency in difficult sequences.

title: How does the Merrifield resin differ from Wang resin in peptide synthesis?
content: Merrifield resin: chloromethyl polystyrene, peptide attached via ester bond, cleaved with HF/anisole. Wang resin: p-benzyloxybenzyl alcohol linker, cleaved with TFA. Wang resin is more acid-labile and compatible with Fmoc chemistry.

title: What is the mechanism of peptide ligation using sortase A?
content: Sortase A (from S. aureus) recognizes LPXTG motif. Cleaves between Thr-Gly, forms thioester intermediate with active site Cys. Transpeptidation to oligoglycine nucleophile joins fragments. Used for protein semisynthesis and labeling.

title: Describe the use of expressed protein ligation (EPL) in chemical biology.
content: EPL combines recombinant protein production with native chemical ligation. Intein-mediated thioester generation on recombinant protein. NCL with synthetic peptide containing N-terminal Cys. Enables site-specific incorporation of modified amino acids.

title: What is the role of copper-free click chemistry in live-cell imaging?
content: Tetrazine-trans-cyclooctene (Tz-TCO) ligation is the fastest copper-free click reaction (k2 ~10^6 M^-1 s^-1). IEDDA (inverse electron demand) mechanism. Used for real-time cell labeling, pretargeted imaging, and in vivo bioconjugation.

title: How does flash chemistry enable rapid peptide synthesis?
content: Flash chemistry uses flow microreactors with extremely short residence times (milliseconds). Prevents side reactions and enables unstable intermediate generation. Useful for reactive intermediate formation and immediate trapping.

title: What is the concept of peptide library synthesis for drug discovery?
content: Split-pool synthesis (Tea-bag method) generates one bead-one compound libraries. Parallel synthesis produces discrete compounds. Encoding strategies (mass tags, fluorescent tags) identify active compounds. High-throughput screening identifies hits.

title: Explain the strain-promoted azide-alkyne cycloaddition (SPAAC).
content: SPAAC uses strained cyclooctyne (e.g., DBCO, BCN) reacting with azides without metal catalyst. Ring strain (~18 kcal/mol) drives reaction. Useful for in vivo bioconjugation where copper toxicity is a concern. Second-order rate constants ~0.1-1 M^-1 s^-1.

title: Explain the use of Ugi multicomponent reaction in peptide mimetic synthesis.
content: Ugi reaction: amine + aldehyde + isocyanide + carboxylic acid -> bis-amide. One-pot, atom-economic, high-yielding. Products have peptide-like backbone. Diversity-oriented synthesis generates large libraries for biological screening.

title: What is the role of photoredox catalysis in peptide chemistry?
content: Photoredox catalysts (Ru(bpy)3, Ir(ppy)3) generate radicals under visible light. Used for C-C bond formation, decarboxylative coupling, and radical cyclization. Enables modification of peptide side chains under mild conditions.

title: How does microwave-assisted solid-phase peptide synthesis improve yield?
content: Microwave heating (60-80C) during coupling and deprotection reduces aggregation and incomplete couplings. Typically 2-5 min per cycle vs 30-60 min conventional. Improved crude purity reduces chromatographic purification needs.

title: What is the mechanism of aspartimide formation and its prevention?
content: Aspartimide: intramolecular cyclization of Asp-Gly/Asp-Ser sequences forming succinimide. Base-catalyzed (piperidine). Prevented by: pseudoprolines, Asp(tBu)-Gly sequences, reduced piperidine concentration, or Asn substitution.

title: Explain the concept of chemical protein synthesis using ligation-desulfurization.
content: Protein synthesis: NCL of peptide thioesters + Cys peptides -> desulfurization (Raney Ni or VA-044) converts Cys to Ala. Extended to other residues using Ala/Cys/Gly/Val/Phe at ligation sites. Enables total chemical synthesis of proteins up to ~200 residues.

title: What is the role of intein-mediated protein splicing in semisynthesis?
content: Inteins excise themselves and ligate flanking exteins. Split inteins enable protein trans-splicing. Used for protein purification (CBD-intein), C-terminal thioester generation (GyrA intein), and site-specific labeling. Conditional splicing enables control.

title: How do DNA-encoded chemical libraries (DEL) accelerate drug discovery?
content: DEL: small molecules tagged with DNA barcodes. Libraries of 10^9+ compounds screened against targets. Affinity selection followed by PCR amplification and sequencing identifies hits. Requires aqueous-compatible chemistry (click chemistry).

title: What is the role of flow chemistry in pharmaceutical manufacturing?
content: Flow chemistry enables continuous production with better heat/mass transfer, safety (small volumes), and reproducibility. Telescoped reactions eliminate intermediate isolation. PAT (process analytical technology) enables real-time monitoring and control.

title: Describe the mechanism of peptide macrocyclization strategies.
content: Macrocyclization: head-to-tail, side chain-to-side chain (lactam, disulfide), or side chain-to-backbone. Ring-closing metathesis, native chemical ligation, and click chemistry enable macrocyclization. Macrocycles can cross membranes and bind challenging targets.

title: What is the concept of solid-phase peptide synthesis automation?
content: Automated synthesizers (Applied Biosystems, Gyros) control reagent delivery, coupling times, and washing cycles. Pre-programmed methods for Fmoc/Boc chemistry. High-throughput synthesis: 96-384 peptides simultaneously. Reduces labor and improves reproducibility.

title: What is native chemical ligation (NCL) and its mechanism?
content: NCL joins peptide fragments via thioester-amino thiol exchange. C-terminal thioester reacts with N-terminal Cys to form thioester intermediate, then S-to-N acyl shift forms native peptide bond. Requires Cys at ligation site. Desulfurization converts Cys to Ala.

title: How does the PET (phenylalanine-tRNA synthetase) selection system work?
content: PET system selects for non-canonical amino acid incorporation. Mutant aaRS/tRNA pair incorporates ncAA at amber stop codon. Bacterial growth in presence of ncAA and absence of Phe selects active synthetase variants. Used to evolve aaRS for novel substrates.

title: What is the role of solid-phase extraction (SPE) in peptide purification?
content: SPE uses C18 or mixed-mode resins to desalt and concentrate peptides from reaction mixtures. Step gradients remove reagents and byproducts. Commonly used before HPLC purification. Reduces solvent consumption and speeds up workup.

title: Explain the concept of chiral peptide synthesis and racemization control.
content: Racemization occurs at activated C-terminal residue. HOBt/HOAt suppress racemization by reducing oxazolone formation. Chiral auxiliaries (e.g., Evans oxazolidinones) control stereochemistry. Racemization monitored by chiral HPLC or MSP.

title: What is the mechanism of peptide disulfide bond formation?
content: Disulfide bonds form via thiol-disulfide exchange. Oxidation by air, glutathione redox buffer, or iodine. Regioselective folding requires careful control of oxidation conditions. Directed folding strategies use preformed disulfide pairs or Acm protection.

title: How does cell-free protein synthesis enable rapid prototyping?
content: Cell-free systems (PURE, S30 extract) produce proteins without living cells. Enables incorporation of ncAAs, rapid screening of variants, and membrane protein production. Supplemented with amino acids, energy sources, and DNA template.

title: What is the role of tag-and-modify strategies in bioconjugation?
content: Tag-and-modify: install bioorthogonal handle (azide, alkyne, tetrazine) on protein, then conjugate via click chemistry. Genetic code expansion installs ncAA with handle. Chemical modification targets native residues (Cys, Lys). Selectivity determines approach.

title: Describe the use of N-terminus specific modification in peptide chemistry.
content: N-terminal modification: reductive alkylation (NaBH3CN), oxazolidinone formation, acylation, or sulfonylation. Selectivity based on pKa difference between N-terminal alpha-amino and side chain amino groups. Used for PEGylation and labeling.

title: What is the concept of dynamic combinatorial chemistry for peptide libraries?
content: DCL generates libraries through reversible bond formation (imines, disulfides, boronic esters). Target binding shifts equilibrium toward bound species. Identifies ligands without screening each compound. Used for discovering peptide macrocycles.

title: How does the amber suppression technique enable site-specific labeling?
content: Amber suppression: TAG codon recoded to incorporate ncAA with bioorthogonal handle. Cell-surface or intracellular proteins labeled with fluorophore-conjugated probe. Enables pulse-chase experiments, super-resolution imaging, and PTM mimics.

title: What is the role of peptide-based PROTACs in targeted protein degradation?
content: PROTACs (Proteolysis Targeting Chimeras) recruit E3 ligases to target proteins. Bifunctional molecules: target-binding ligand + linker + E3 ligase ligand. Peptide PROTACs use cell-permeable peptides. Catalytic mechanism enables substoichiometric activity.

title: How does microwave-assisted peptide synthesis improve coupling efficiency?
content: Microwave heating (60-80C) accelerates coupling reactions 10-1000x. Reduces aggregation in difficult sequences. Optimized with DIC/HOBt or HATU activation. In situ monitoring tracks coupling completion. Reduced racemization with careful temperature control.

title: Explain the synthesis of cyclic peptides using click chemistry.
content: Azide-alkyne cyclization of linear peptides with terminal azide and alkyne groups. CuAAC or SPAAC provides ring closure. Ring size and conformation affect biological activity. Head-to-tail cyclization uses native chemical ligation.

title: What is the role of protecting groups in solid-phase peptide synthesis?
content: Fmoc (base-labile) protects alpha-amino group; removed with 20% piperidine. Side chains protected with acid-labile groups (tBu, Trt, Pbf). orthogonal protection enables selective deprotection. Coupling with HATU/HOBt/DIC activates carboxyl group.

title: Describe the mechanism of enzymatic peptide synthesis using lipases.
content: Lipases (Candida antarctica B, CAL-B) catalyze peptide bond formation in organic solvents. Kinetic control: activated ester (donor) + amino acid ester (acceptor). Low water activity shifts equilibrium toward synthesis. Substrate engineering uses activated substrates.

title: What is the thiol-ene click reaction and its applications?
content: Thiol-ene reaction: R-SH + alkene -> RS-CH2-CH2-R via radical mechanism. Initiated by UV light or thermal radical initiators. Bioorthogonal, regiospecific (anti-Markovnikov), and oxygen-tolerant. Used for hydrogel formation and surface functionalization.

title: Explain the concept of peptide stapling using olefin metathesis.
content: All-hydrocarbon staples use Grubbs catalyst for ring-closing metathesis of terminal alkenes on i,i+3 or i,i+7 positions. Stapled peptides have increased helicity, protease resistance, and cell permeability. ALRN-6924 (MDM2/MDMX inhibitor) is a clinical example.

title: What is the role of solid-phase oligonucleotide synthesis?
content: Phosphoramidite chemistry: 5'-DMTr-protected nucleoside phosphoramidite + activator (tetrazole) + support-bound nucleoside. Coupling efficiency >99%. Oxidation (I2) converts phosphite to phosphate. Deprotection releases oligonucleotide. Automation enables high-throughput synthesis.

title: What is the isoelectric point (pI) of glycine?
content: The pI of glycine is approximately 5.97. At this pH, glycine carries no net electrical charge.

title: Which amino acid has the highest molecular weight?
content: Tryptophan has the highest molecular weight among the standard amino acids at 204.23 Da, due to its large indole side chain.

title: What is the hydrophobicity scale used to rank amino acid side chains?
content: The Kyte-Doolittle scale is commonly used, where positive values indicate hydrophobicity. Isoleucine (4.5) is the most hydrophobic, while arginine (-4.5) is the most hydrophilic.

title: Which amino acid is most commonly phosphorylated in eukaryotes?
content: Serine is the most commonly phosphorylated amino acid, followed by threonine and tyrosine. These three hydroxyl-containing residues are the primary targets of kinases.

title: What is the pKa of the cysteine thiol group?
content: The pKa of the cysteine thiol group is approximately 8.3, though it can range from 6.5 to 11 depending on the local protein environment.

title: Which amino acid is classified as a branched-chain amino acid (BCAA)?
content: Leucine, isoleucine, and valine are the three branched-chain amino acids. They are essential amino acids and important for muscle protein synthesis.

title: What makes proline unique among the 20 standard amino acids?
content: Proline is the only amino acid where the side chain cyclizes back to the backbone nitrogen, forming a pyrrolidine ring. This restricts its conformational flexibility and introduces kinks in peptide chains.

title: What is theGRAVY index and what does it measure?
content: GRAVY (Grand Average of Hydropathy) is the sum of hydropathy values divided by the number of residues. Positive values indicate hydrophobic proteins, negative values indicate hydrophilic proteins.

title: Which amino acid has the smallest side chain?
content: Glycine has the smallest side chain, consisting of only a single hydrogen atom. This gives it exceptional conformational flexibility.

title: What is the net charge of arginine at physiological pH (7.4)?
content: Arginine carries a net positive charge of +1 at physiological pH. Its guanidinium group has a pKa of approximately 12.5 and remains fully protonated.

title: Which amino acids are classified as acidic at physiological pH?
content: Aspartate (Asp) and glutamate (Glu) are the acidic amino acids, with side chain pKa values of approximately 3.9 and 4.2 respectively. Both carry negative charges at pH 7.4.

title: What is the molecular weight of cysteine?
content: Cysteine has a molecular weight of 121.16 Da. Its thiol group (-SH) is reactive and can form disulfide bonds with other cysteine residues.

title: How does the Chou-Fasman method predict secondary structure from amino acid composition?
content: The Chou-Fasman method assigns propensity values for each amino acid to form helices, sheets, or turns. Proline and glycine are strong helix-breakers, while alanine and leucine are strong helix-formers.

title: What is the extinction coefficient of tryptophan at 280 nm?
content: Tryptophan has a molar extinction coefficient of approximately 5,500 M⁻¹cm⁻¹ at 280 nm, which is significantly higher than tyrosine (1,490) and cysteine (125).

title: Which amino acid is optically inactive?
content: Glycine is optically inactive because its alpha-carbon is not chiral—it has two hydrogen atoms attached. All other standard amino acids are chiral and exist as L- and D-enantiomers.

title: What is the pI of lysine?
content: The pI of lysine is approximately 9.74. At physiological pH, lysine carries a positive charge due to its epsilon-amino group with a pKa of approximately 10.5.

title: What are the three aromatic amino acids?
content: Phenylalanine, tyrosine, and tryptophan are the aromatic amino acids. They contribute to UV absorption at 280 nm and can engage in pi-stacking interactions.

title: What is the hydrophobicity index of isoleucine on the Kyte-Doolittle scale?
content: Isoleucine has a Kyte-Doolittle hydrophobicity index of 4.5, making it the most hydrophobic amino acid. It is commonly found in the interior of globular proteins.

title: Which amino acid can undergo racemization under physiological conditions?
content: Aspartate can undergo racemization under physiological conditions, converting L-Asp to D-Asp. This is relevant in aging, where D-Asp accumulates in long-lived proteins.

title: What is the molecular weight of histidine?
content: Histidine has a molecular weight of 155.16 Da. Its imidazole side chain has a pKa near physiological pH (~6.0), making it important for enzyme catalysis and buffer systems.

title: What defines an alpha-helix?
content: An alpha-helix is a right-handed coiled secondary structure with 3.6 residues per turn, a rise of 1.5 Å per residue, and a pitch of 5.4 Å. It is stabilized by backbone hydrogen bonds between residues i and i+4.

title: What is a beta-sheet?
content: A beta-sheet consists of beta-strands connected by hydrogen bonds. Strands can be parallel (same N-to-C direction) or antiparallel (opposite directions). The extended strand has a rise of ~3.4 Å per residue.

title: What is a beta-turn?
content: A beta-turn is a four-residue motif that reverses the direction of a peptide chain. It is stabilized by a hydrogen bond between the C=O of residue i and the N-H of residue i+3, commonly found on protein surfaces.

title: Which amino acids are commonly found in alpha-helices?
content: Alanine, leucine, methionine, and glutamate are strong helix-formers. Proline and glycine are helix-breakers—proline disrupts the helix due to its cyclic structure, and glycine is too flexible.
