title: What is a 3-10 helix and how does it differ from an alpha-helix?
content: A 3-10 helix has 3 residues per turn with hydrogen bonds between residues i and i+3. It is tighter (pitch 6.0 Å) than the alpha-helix and is often found at the ends of alpha-helices.

title: What is a pi-helix?
content: A pi-helix has 4.1 residues per turn with hydrogen bonds between residues i and i+5. It is wider and shorter than an alpha-helix and is relatively rare in proteins.

title: What is the hydrogen bonding pattern in a parallel beta-sheet?
content: In parallel beta-strands, the N-H and C=O groups of one strand hydrogen bond to the opposite groups of the adjacent strand. The hydrogen bonds are angled rather than perpendicular to the strand direction.

title: What is the hydrogen bonding pattern in an antiparallel beta-sheet?
content: In antiparallel beta-strands, hydrogen bonds are straight and perpendicular to the strand direction. Pairs of hydrogen bonds alternate between being close together and far apart.

title: What is a coiled-coil structure?
content: A coiled-coil consists of two or more alpha-helices that twist around each other. It is characterized by a heptad repeat (a-b-c-d-e-f-g) where positions a and d are hydrophobic residues.

title: What is an omega-loop?
content: An omega-loop is a compact, non-repetitive secondary structure element with a loop-like shape resembling the Greek letter omega. It often connects secondary structure elements and can be functionally important.

title: How does proline affect alpha-helices?
content: Proline is a helix-breaker because its cyclic side chain eliminates the N-H hydrogen bond donor. Proline also introduces a kink of approximately 20-30° in the helix axis.

title: What is a beta-barrel?
content: A beta-barrel is a beta-sheet that twists and coils to form a closed structure. The strands are usually antiparallel, and the barrel can serve as a channel or binding site, as seen in porins.

title: What percentage of amino acids in globular proteins are in alpha-helices vs beta-sheets?
content: On average, alpha-helices account for about 30-40% and beta-sheets about 15-25% of residues in globular proteins. The remainder is in loops, turns, and unstructured regions.

title: What is a helix dipole and how does it affect peptide stability?
content: An alpha-helix has a macroscopic dipole with the N-terminus positive and C-terminus negative. This dipole can destabilize the helix and is often stabilized by negative charges at the C-terminus or positive charges at the N-terminus.

title: What is a left-handed alpha-helix?
content: A left-handed alpha-helix is a rare secondary structure with opposite handedness to the common right-handed alpha-helix. It has approximately 4.1 residues per turn and is energetically less favorable.

title: What determines the stability of an alpha-helix?
content: Helix stability depends on the intrinsic helix propensity of amino acids, side-chain interactions (i, i+3 and i, i+4), capping interactions at the helix termini, and the helix dipole moment.

title: What is a hairpin turn in beta-sheets?
content: A beta-hairpin is an antiparallel beta-sheet connected by a tight turn, often just two residues. The turn residues are frequently glycine and proline, which favor the tight turns needed for hairpin formation.

title: What is a mixed beta-sheet?
content: A mixed beta-sheet contains both parallel and antiparallel strands. This is less common than pure sheets but occurs in many proteins, particularly in larger beta-structures.

title: How are turns classified in protein structures?
content: Turns are classified by the number of residues in the turn (Type I, II, III, I', II') and the positions of the hydrogen bonds. Type I and II are the most common, differing in the phi/psi angles of the middle residue.

title: What is a pi-helix-like structural element in membrane proteins?
content: Some transmembrane helices adopt a pi-helix conformation rather than an alpha-helix. The extra width creates a channel-like structure that can be important for substrate binding or transport.

title: What is HATU and what role does it play in SPPS?
content: HATU (Hexafluorophosphate Azabenzotriazole Tetramethyl Uronium) is a uronium-type coupling reagent. It activates carboxylic acids by forming an active ester intermediate, enabling peptide bond formation.

title: What is the advantage of HBTU over DIC for peptide coupling?
content: HBTU forms a highly reactive HOBt ester intermediate that minimizes racemization and provides faster coupling than DIC alone. DIC is a carbodiimide that requires HOBt as an additive.

title: What is DIC in peptide synthesis?
content: DIC (N,N'-Diisopropylcarbodiimide) is a carbodiimide coupling reagent that activates carboxylic acids. It is often used with HOBt or Oxyma to suppress racemization and improve coupling efficiency.

title: What is the role of HOAt in peptide coupling?
content: HOAt (1-Hydroxy-7-azabenzotriazole) is an additive that reduces racemization during coupling. It forms a more reactive ester than HOBt, particularly effective with sterically hindered amino acids.

title: What is the Fmoc protecting group strategy?
content: Fmoc (Fluorenylmethyloxycarbonyl) is a base-labile Nα protecting group removed by 20% piperidine in DMF. It is orthogonal to acid-labile side-chain protecting groups used in SPPS.

title: What is the Boc protecting group strategy?
content: Boc (tert-Butyloxycarbonyl) is an acid-labile Nα protecting group removed by trifluoroacetic acid (TFA). The Boc strategy requires HF for final resin cleavage, which is more hazardous than Fmoc chemistry.

title: What is piperidine used for in SPPS?
content: Piperidine (typically 20% in DMF) is used to remove the Fmoc protecting group from the alpha-amino group. The mechanism involves a beta-elimination reaction that releases the dibenzofulvene derivative.

title: What is TFA (trifluoroacetic acid) used for in peptide synthesis?
content: TFA is used for cleaving acid-labile protecting groups and for final peptide cleavage from the resin in Fmoc SPPS. A typical cleavage cocktail is TFA/water/thiol (95:2.5:2.5).

title: What is TIS and why is it added to cleavage cocktails?
content: TIS (triisopropylsilane) is a scavenger added to TFA cleavage cocktails. It quenches reactive carbocations generated during deprotection, preventing them from re-attaching to the peptide.

title: What is Oxyma Pure and how does it compare to HOBt?
content: Oxyma Pure is a racemization suppressant and coupling additive. It has similar efficiency to HOBt but is non-explosive, making it a safer alternative for large-scale peptide synthesis.

title: What is the role of DIPEA in peptide coupling?
content: DIPEA (N,N-Diisopropylethylamine, Hünig's base) is a non-nucleophilic base that maintains the pH during coupling. It is typically used as 2-4 equivalents relative to the amino acid.

title: What is the advantage of PyBOP over BOP for peptide coupling?
content: PyBOP uses pyrrolidinyl instead of hexamethyleneamine phosphonium, avoiding the carcinogenic HMPA byproduct. It provides excellent coupling with minimal epimerization.

title: What is the role of HOBt in peptide synthesis?
content: HOBt (Hydroxybenzotriazole) acts as a coupling additive that suppresses racemization and accelerates the reaction by forming a more reactive active ester intermediate.

title: What is a pseudoproline dipeptide and when is it used?
content: Pseudoproline dipeptides are Fmoc-Xaa-Pro-OR derivatives where the oxazolidine ring mimics proline's structural constraints. They are used to improve coupling efficiency at difficult sequences prone to aggregation.

title: What is the Kaiser test?
content: The Kaiser test (ninhydrin test) detects free primary amines on resin-bound peptides. A blue or purple color indicates incomplete coupling, while a yellow or colorless result indicates successful coupling.

title: What resin is commonly used in Fmoc SPPS?
content: Wang resin, Rink amide resin, and 2-chlorotrityl chloride resin are commonly used. Wang resin gives C-terminal carboxylic acids, while Rink amide resin gives C-terminal amides.

title: What is COMU and what are its advantages?
content: COMU (1-[Cyano-2-ethoxy-2-oxoethylideneaminooxy)dimethylamino-morpholino]uronium) is a next-generation coupling reagent. It is highly reactive, promotes minimal epimerization, and is more soluble than HATU.

title: What is the role of thioanisole in cleavage cocktails?
content: Thioanisole is a scavenger that neutralizes reactive cations formed during TFA cleavage, particularly important for protecting groups like trityl and t-butyl. It prevents alkylation of sensitive residues.

title: What is a coupling reagent's role in preventing racemization?
content: Coupling reagents activate the carboxyl group without forming a free oxazolone intermediate, which is prone to racemization. Active esters formed with HOBt/HOAt proceed with minimal epimerization.

title: What is the significance of the tert-butyl protecting group in SPPS?
content: tert-Butyl groups protect side-chain functionalities (Asp, Glu, Cys, Ser, Thr, Lys, Tyr, Trp) and are cleaved simultaneously with Fmoc SPPS resin cleavage using TFA.

title: What is RP-HPLC and how does it purify peptides?
content: Reversed-phase HPLC separates peptides based on hydrophobicity. A C18 or C8 stationary phase retains peptides, which are eluted using an acetonitrile/water gradient with TFA as ion-pairing agent.

title: What is the role of TFA in RP-HPLC?
content: TFA (trifluoroacetic acid) serves as an ion-pairing agent that protonates basic residues and forms volatile ion pairs. This improves peak shape and resolution during peptide separation.

title: What is ion exchange chromatography (IEX)?
content: IEX separates peptides based on net charge. Cation exchange uses negatively charged resins to bind positively charged peptides, while anion exchange uses positively charged resins for negatively charged peptides.

title: What is size exclusion chromatography (SEC)?
content: SEC separates molecules by hydrodynamic radius. Larger molecules elute first as they cannot enter the pores of the stationary phase, while smaller molecules elute later.

title: What is HILIC chromatography?
content: Hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography (HILIC) separates polar peptides based on their hydrophilicity. It uses a polar stationary phase with a high organic mobile phase, complementary to RP-HPLC.

title: What is a mass spectrometer's role in peptide purification?
content: LC-MS combines HPLC separation with mass spectrometric detection. It allows real-time monitoring of purification and confirms the identity and purity of eluting peptide peaks.

title: What is preparative HPLC?
content: Preparative HPLC uses larger columns and flow rates to purify milligram to gram quantities of peptides. It typically uses gradient elution with collection of fractions that are later analyzed.

title: What is the typical gradient used in peptide RP-HPLC?
content: A linear gradient from 5-10% to 90-95% acetonitrile in water (with 0.1% TFA) over 15-60 minutes is standard. Shallow gradients (1% B/min or less) improve resolution of closely eluting peaks.

title: What is the advantage of using formic acid instead of TFA in RP-HPLC?
content: Formic acid is more compatible with LC-MS analysis and can be removed more easily than TFA. However, it provides weaker ion-pairing and may give lower resolution for some peptides.

title: What is flash chromatography in peptide purification?
content: Flash chromatography is a rapid gravity- or pressure-driven purification using silica gel. It is used for purifying peptide intermediates and small molecules, but is less common for final peptide products.

title: What are the factors affecting RP-HPLC peptide retention?
content: Peptide retention depends on hydrophobicity (number of hydrophobic residues), chain length, secondary structure, the mobile phase composition, temperature, and the type of stationary phase.

title: What is mixed-mode chromatography?
content: Mixed-mode chromatography combines two or more separation mechanisms (e.g., reversed-phase and ion exchange) in a single stationary phase. It provides unique selectivity for peptide purification.

title: What is strong cation exchange (SCX) chromatography?
content: SCX uses sulfonic acid groups that are charged at all pH values. It provides high binding capacity for basic peptides and is useful for separating peptides based on their positive charge.

title: What is weak cation exchange (WCX) chromatography?
content: WCX uses carboxyl groups with pH-dependent charge. It provides gentler elution conditions than SCX and is useful for separating proteins and large peptides with subtle charge differences.

title: What is the typical column loading for preparative RP-HPLC?
content: Column loading depends on the resolution needed. For analytical purification, 1-5 mg per injection is typical; for preparative work, 50-500 mg can be loaded on larger columns.

title: What is centrifugal filtration in peptide purification?
content: Centrifugal filtration uses membrane filters to separate peptides by molecular weight. It is useful for desalting, buffer exchange, and concentrating peptide solutions.

title: What is a C18 SPE cartridge?
content: A C18 solid-phase extraction cartridge is used for rapid peptide desalting and concentration. Peptides are bound to the C18 resin, washed to remove salts, and eluted with organic solvent.

title: What are the common detection methods in HPLC?
content: UV detection at 214 nm (peptide bond) and 280 nm (aromatic residues) is standard. RI (refractive index) and ELSD (evaporative light scattering) are also used for non-chromophoric analytes.

title: What is multidimensional chromatography?
content: Multidimensional chromatography uses two or more different separation modes in sequence (e.g., SCX followed by RP-HPLC). It provides enhanced resolution for complex peptide mixtures like proteomics samples.

title: What is the typical particle size for preparative HPLC columns?
content: Preparative columns typically use 5-10 μm particles, while analytical columns use 3-5 μm. Sub-2 μm particles are used in UHPLC for high-resolution separations.

title: What information does NMR spectroscopy provide about peptides?
content: NMR provides atomic-resolution information about peptide structure, dynamics, and interactions. It can determine secondary structure, tertiary fold, and binding sites in solution.

title: What is circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy?
content: CD spectroscopy measures the differential absorption of left and right circularly polarized light by chiral molecules. It is used to determine secondary structure content of peptides in solution.

title: What does DSC measure in peptide analysis?
content: Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) measures heat capacity changes during thermal transitions. It provides information about thermal stability, melting temperature (Tm), and unfolding thermodynamics.

title: How does SDS-PAGE separate peptides?
content: SDS-PAGE separates peptides by molecular weight. SDS denatures peptides and gives them uniform negative charge, allowing separation based solely on size during electrophoresis.

title: What is MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry?
content: MALDI-TOF (Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization Time-of-Flight) ionizes peptides using a laser with a matrix. It provides accurate molecular weight determination and is tolerant of impurities.

title: What is ESI-MS?
content: Electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (ESI-MS) ionizes peptides from solution by applying high voltage. It produces multiply charged ions, enabling analysis of large peptides and proteins.

title: How can CD spectra indicate alpha-helix content?
content: Alpha-helical peptides show characteristic CD minima at 208 nm and 222 nm, and a maximum at 192 nm. Beta-sheets show a minimum at 218 nm and maximum at 195 nm.

title: What is ATR-FTIR spectroscopy?
content: ATR-FTIR (Attenuated Total Reflectance Fourier Transform Infrared) measures infrared absorption of peptides. Amide I (1600-1700 cm⁻¹) and Amide II (1500-1600 cm⁻¹) bands indicate secondary structure.

title: What is the difference between 1D and 2D NMR?
content: 1D NMR records a single spectrum, while 2D NMR (COSY, NOESY, TOCSY) correlates different nuclei. 2D NMR provides connectivity information and is essential for peptide structure determination.

title: What is the TOF principle in mass spectrometry?
content: Time-of-flight (TOF) measures the time ions take to travel through a field-free region. Lighter ions travel faster, allowing mass determination. MALDI-TOF can achieve mass accuracy < 50 ppm.

title: What is Edman degradation?
content: Edman degradation sequentially removes and identifies N-terminal amino acids from a peptide using phenylisothiocyanate (PITC). It can sequence approximately 20-50 residues from the N-terminus.

title: What is a peptide mass fingerprint (PMF)?
content: PMF identifies proteins by comparing experimental peptide masses from enzymatic digestion with calculated masses from a database. MALDI-TOF is commonly used for PMF analysis.

title: What is UHPLC and how does it differ from HPLC?
content: Ultra-high-performance liquid chromatography (UHPLC) uses sub-2 μm particles and higher pressures (>1000 bar). It provides faster separations, higher resolution, and better sensitivity than conventional HPLC.

title: What is the significance of the m/z ratio in mass spectrometry?
content: The mass-to-charge ratio (m/z) is the fundamental measurement in mass spectrometry. For ESI, multiply charged ions allow analysis of large molecules within the mass range of the instrument.

title: What is capillary electrophoresis (CE)?
content: CE separates peptides based on their charge-to-size ratio in a narrow capillary under high voltage. It provides high resolution and requires minimal sample amounts.

title: What is TROSY in NMR?
content: Transverse relaxation-optimized spectroscopy (TROSY) exploits cross-correlation between dipole-dipole and chemical shift anisotropy relaxation mechanisms. It enables NMR studies of larger proteins.

title: What is a Tm value from DSC?
content: The melting temperature (Tm) is the temperature at which 50% of the peptide is unfolded. Higher Tm values indicate greater thermal stability and are important for formulation development.

title: What is isotope labeling in NMR?
content: Isotope labeling (¹⁵N, ¹³C) enhances NMR sensitivity and enables multi-dimensional experiments. Uniform labeling is achieved by expressing proteins in bacteria grown on ¹⁵N/¹³C media.

title: What is ion mobility spectrometry-mass spectrometry (IMS-MS)?
content: IMS-MS adds a gas-phase separation step based on ion shape and charge before mass analysis. It can separate peptide isomers and provide structural information about gas-phase conformations.

title: How is peptide purity typically assessed?
content: Peptide purity is assessed by RP-HPLC (>95% by UV area), mass spectrometry (confirming correct molecular weight), and sometimes by NMR for structural confirmation.

title: What is SAR in drug design?
content: Structure-Activity Relationship (SAR) studies how structural modifications to a molecule affect its biological activity. It guides optimization of potency, selectivity, and pharmacokinetic properties.

title: What are Lipinski's Rule of Five?
content: Lipinski's rules predict drug-likeness: MW < 500, logP < 5, H-bond donors < 5, H-bond acceptors < 10. Compounds violating more than one rule are likely to have poor oral bioavailability.

title: What does ADMET stand for?
content: ADMET describes the pharmacokinetic properties of a drug: Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion, and Toxicity. It is a critical framework for evaluating drug candidates.

title: What is a pharmacophore?
content: A pharmacophore is the spatial arrangement of functional groups necessary for molecular recognition by a biological target. It represents the minimum structural features required for activity.

title: What is lead optimization?
content: Lead optimization is the process of modifying a lead compound to improve potency, selectivity, and drug-like properties while minimizing toxicity. It involves iterative design-synthesis-test cycles.

title: What is virtual screening?
content: Virtual screening computationally searches large compound databases to identify potential active molecules. It includes structure-based (docking) and ligand-based (similarity) approaches.

title: What is fragment-based drug design?
content: Fragment-based drug design screens small molecular fragments (MW 150-250 Da) that bind weakly to a target. Fragments are then linked or grown to create potent drug candidates.

title: What is a structure-activity relationship (SAR) study?
content: SAR studies systematically modify specific positions of a molecule to determine which structural features are essential for activity. This guides rational drug design.

title: What is selectivity index in drug design?
content: The selectivity ratio compares the drug's effect on the intended target versus off-target effects. Higher selectivity indicates fewer side effects. It is calculated as IC50(off-target)/IC50(target).

title: What is the concept of bioisosterism?
content: Bioisosterism replaces functional groups with similar-sized groups that maintain biological activity. Classic bioisosteres include F for H, Cl for CH₃, and tetrazole for carboxylic acid.

title: What is a prodrug?
content: A prodrug is an inactive form that is metabolically converted to the active drug in vivo. Prodrugs can improve bioavailability, reduce toxicity, or enable targeted delivery.

title: What is the difference between potency and efficacy?
content: Potency is the concentration needed for 50% effect (EC50), while efficacy is the maximum effect achievable (Emax). A potent drug has low EC50; an efficacious drug has high Emax.

title: What is molecular docking?
content: Molecular docking predicts the binding mode and affinity of a ligand to a protein target. It samples different conformations and orientations and scores them using energy functions.

title: What is the Veber rule?
content: Veber's rules complement Lipinski's: compounds with rotatable bonds ≤ 10 and polar surface area ≤ 140 Å² tend to have good oral bioavailability, regardless of MW and logP.

title: What is the concept of ligand efficiency?
content: Ligand efficiency (LE = ΔG/heavy atom count) normalizes binding affinity by molecular size. It helps compare compounds of different sizes and guides optimization toward smaller, more efficient molecules.

title: What is PAINS in drug screening?
content: Pan-Assay Interference Compounds (PAINS) are frequent hitters that give false positives in high-throughput screens. They include reactive groups like quinones, catechols, and rhodanines.

title: What is the concept of therapeutic window?
content: The therapeutic window is the dose range between the minimum effective concentration and the maximum tolerated concentration. A wide therapeutic window is desirable for drug safety.

title: What is QSAR?
content: Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship (QSAR) uses mathematical models to predict biological activity from molecular descriptors. It enables virtual screening and activity prediction.

title: What is the role of computational chemistry in drug design?
content: Computational chemistry provides tools for molecular modeling, docking, MD simulations, and free energy calculations. It reduces experimental costs and accelerates drug discovery.

title: What is drug-likeness?
content: Drug-likeness refers to whether a compound has properties consistent with successful oral drugs. It is assessed using rules (Lipinski, Veber) and computational predictions of pharmacokinetic properties.

title: What is Kd in receptor binding?
content: The dissociation constant (Kd) measures the affinity of a ligand for its receptor. It represents the concentration at which 50% of receptors are occupied. Lower Kd indicates higher affinity.

title: What is the difference between Ki and Kd?
content: Kd measures ligand affinity directly, while Ki is the inhibition constant measured in competition assays. Ki accounts for the concentration of the competing ligand and is calculated using the Cheng-Prusoff equation.

title: What is EC50?
content: EC50 (half-maximal effective concentration) is the concentration of an agonist that produces 50% of the maximum response. It measures the potency of an agonist at a receptor.

title: What is IC50?
content: IC50 (half-maximal inhibitory concentration) is the concentration of an inhibitor that reduces a biological response by 50%. It is commonly used to compare inhibitor potency.

title: What is the Cheng-Prusoff equation?
content: Ki = IC50 / (1 + [S]/Km). This equation converts IC50 values to inhibition constants (Ki) for competitive inhibitors, accounting for the substrate concentration in the assay.

title: What is a dose-response curve?
content: A dose-response curve plots the biological response against the logarithm of drug concentration. It typically follows a sigmoidal shape and provides EC50, Emax, and Hill slope information.

title: What is the Hill coefficient?
content: The Hill coefficient (nH) describes the cooperativity of binding. nH > 1 indicates positive cooperativity, nH = 1 indicates non-cooperative binding, and nH < 1 indicates negative cooperativity.

title: What is receptor selectivity?
content: Receptor selectivity is the ability of a ligand to bind preferentially to one receptor subtype over others. It is measured by the ratio of Ki values for different receptor subtypes.

title: What is an allosteric modulator?
content: An allosteric modulator binds to a site different from the orthosteric (primary) binding site. It can enhance (positive allosteric modulator, PAM) or reduce (negative allosteric modulator, NAM) receptor activity.

title: What is competitive inhibition?
content: Competitive inhibitors bind to the same site as the substrate/agonist, competing for binding. They increase apparent Km without affecting Vmax, and can be overcome by increasing substrate concentration.

title: What is non-competitive inhibition?
content: Non-competitive inhibitors bind to an allosteric site, reducing Vmax without affecting Km. The inhibitor can bind to either the free enzyme or the enzyme-substrate complex.

title: What is inverse agonism?
content: An inverse agonist reduces the constitutive (basal) activity of a receptor. It produces the opposite effect of an agonist and has efficacy between zero (antagonist) and the minimum (inverse agonist).

title: What is the equilibrium association constant (Ka)?
content: Ka is the reciprocal of Kd (Ka = 1/Kd). It measures the association affinity, with higher Ka values indicating stronger binding. Ka = [RL]/([R][L]) at equilibrium.

title: What is a binding isotherm?
content: A binding isotherm plots the fraction of bound receptors against ligand concentration at constant temperature. It is used to determine binding constants and stoichiometry.

title: What is the difference between on-rate and off-rate?
content: The on-rate (kon or kass) is the rate of ligand-receptor association, while the off-rate (koff or kdis) is the rate of dissociation. Kd = koff/kon, so high kon and low koff give high affinity.

title: What is residence time in drug-receptor binding?
content: Residence time (τ = 1/koff) is the average time a drug remains bound to its receptor. Longer residence times often correlate with better in vivo efficacy, regardless of binding affinity.

title: What is functional selectivity (biased agonism)?
content: Functional selectivity occurs when a ligand preferentially activates specific signaling pathways through the same receptor. A biased agonist may activate G-protein signaling but not β-arrestin recruitment, or vice versa.

title: What is the Schild analysis?
content: Schild analysis determines the mechanism of antagonism and measures antagonist potency (pA2). A linear Schild plot with slope = 1 confirms competitive antagonism.

title: What is a binding assay?
content: A binding assay measures the interaction between a ligand and its receptor. Methods include radioligand binding, SPR, fluorescence polarization, and cell-based assays.

title: What is surface plasmon resonance (SPR)?
content: SPR measures real-time binding kinetics (kon, koff, Kd) by detecting changes in refractive index when molecules bind to a sensor surface. It requires immobilization of one binding partner.

title: What is phosphorylation in peptides?
content: Phosphorylation adds a phosphate group (PO₄³⁻) to serine, threonine, or tyrosine residues. It introduces negative charges and is a key regulatory modification in cell signaling.

title: What is acetylation of peptides?
content: Acetylation adds an acetyl group (CH₃CO) to the N-terminus or to lysine side chains. N-terminal acetylation protects against degradation and modifies charge, while lysine acetylation neutralizes positive charge.

title: What is methylation of peptides?
content: Methylation adds methyl groups (CH₃) to lysine or arginine residues. Lysine can be mono-, di-, or trimethylated, with trimethyllysine carrying a permanent positive charge.

title: What is ubiquitination?
content: Ubiquitination attaches ubiquitin (76 amino acid protein) to lysine residues. It serves as a signal for protein degradation via the proteasome and regulates many cellular processes.

title: What is PEGylation?
content: PEGylation covalently attaches polyethylene glycol (PEG) chains to peptides. It increases circulating half-life by reducing renal clearance and proteolytic degradation, and can improve solubility.

title: What is glycosylation?
content: Glycosylation adds sugar moieties to asparagine (N-linked) or serine/threonine (O-linked) residues. It affects protein folding, stability, and cell-cell recognition.

title: What is amidation of the C-terminus?
content: C-terminal amidation replaces the C-terminal carboxyl group with an amide. This increases stability against carboxypeptidases and is a natural modification in many bioactive peptides.

title: What is a disulfide bond?
content: Disulfide bonds form between two cysteine residues through oxidation. They stabilize protein tertiary and quaternary structure and are essential for the activity of many peptides and proteins.

title: What is palmitoylation?
content: Palmitoylation attaches palmitic acid (16-carbon fatty acid) to cysteine residues via a thioester bond. It increases membrane association and affects protein trafficking and signaling.

title: What is myristoylation?
content: Myristoylation attaches myristic acid (14-carbon fatty acid) to an N-terminal glycine. It is irreversible and anchors proteins to membranes, important for signaling proteins like Src kinases.

title: What is biotinylation?
content: Biotinylation attaches biotin to lysine residues or the N-terminus. The biotin-streptavidin interaction is used for protein purification, detection, and immunoassays.

title: What is citrullination?
content: Citrullination converts arginine to citrulline by deimination. It removes a positive charge and can disrupt hydrogen bonding. It is implicated in autoimmune diseases like rheumatoid arthritis.

title: What is SUMOylation?
content: SUMOylation covalently attaches SUMO (Small Ubiquitin-like Modifier) proteins to lysine residues. It regulates nuclear transport, transcription, and protein stability.

title: What is the role of glycosylation in therapeutic peptides?
content: Glycosylation can improve peptide stability, solubility, and pharmacokinetics. It also affects receptor binding and can modulate immune responses in glycoengineered therapeutics.

title: What is the difference between N-linked and O-linked glycosylation?
content: N-linked glycosylation occurs at asparagine in the consensus sequence Asn-X-Ser/Thr, while O-linked occurs at serine or threonine without a consensus sequence. N-linked starts in the ER; O-linked occurs in the Golgi.

title: What is cyclization of peptides?
content: Peptide cyclization connects the N-terminus to the C-terminus or links side chains. Cyclization increases metabolic stability, reduces conformational flexibility, and can improve receptor binding.

title: What is a D-amino acid substitution?
content: Substituting L-amino acids with D-enantiomers increases resistance to proteolysis. D-amino acids are not recognized by most proteases, extending the half-life of peptides in vivo.

title: What is the role of cysteine alkylation?
content: Cysteine alkylation adds alkyl groups (e.g., iodoacetamide) to free cysteines, preventing disulfide bond formation. It is used in proteomics and can modify peptide properties.

title: What is sulfation of peptides?
content: Tyrosine sulfation adds a sulfate group to tyrosine residues. It enhances protein-protein interactions and is important for receptor binding, such as CCR5 and glycoprotein hormones.

title: What is the purpose of FRET labeling?
content: FRET (Förster Resonance Energy Transfer) pairs fluorescent donors and acceptors to measure distances and interactions. In peptides, it monitors conformational changes, cleavage, and binding events.

title: What is MHC class I?
content: MHC class I molecules present endogenous peptides (8-10 amino acids) to CD8+ T cells. They are expressed on all nucleated cells and are essential for intracellular pathogen surveillance.

title: What is MHC class II?
content: MHC class II molecules present exogenous peptides (13-25 amino acids) to CD4+ T cells. They are expressed on antigen-presenting cells and activate helper T cell responses.

title: What is antigen processing?
content: Antigen processing is the mechanism by which proteins are degraded into peptides for MHC presentation. Proteasomal degradation generates peptides for MHC-I; endosomal degradation generates peptides for MHC-II.

title: What is the immunopeptidome?
content: The immunopeptidome is the complete set of peptides presented by MHC molecules on cell surfaces. Mass spectrometry-based immunopeptidomics identifies these peptides to study immune recognition.

title: What is the role of TAP in antigen presentation?
content: TAP (Transporter Associated with Antigen Processing) transports proteasomal peptides from the cytosol into the ER lumen, where they can be loaded onto MHC class I molecules.

title: What is the proteasome's role in MHC-I presentation?
content: The proteasome cleaves cytosolic proteins into peptides of 8-10 residues. The immunoproteasome (containing LMP2, LMP7, MECL-1) has altered cleavage specificity that favors MHC-I peptide generation.

title: What is peptide-MHC binding?
content: Peptide-MHC binding is the critical step in antigen presentation. Peptides bind in the MHC groove through anchor residues that fit into specific pockets, determining the repertoire of presented peptides.

title: What is HLA typing?
content: HLA (Human Leukocyte Antigen) typing determines an individual's MHC genotype. HLA molecules are highly polymorphic, affecting peptide binding and influencing immune responses and transplant compatibility.

title: What is cross-presentation?
content: Cross-presentation is the process where exogenous antigens are presented on MHC class I molecules, enabling activation of CD8+ T cells against viruses that don't directly infect antigen-presenting cells.

title: What is the tapasin protein?
content: Tapasin bridges TAP to MHC class I molecules, facilitating peptide loading and editing. It ensures that only high-affinity peptides are loaded onto MHC-I, improving presentation quality.

title: What are immunodominant epitopes?
content: Immunodominant epitopes are the peptides that elicit the strongest immune response. They are determined by MHC binding affinity, T cell receptor recognition, and peptide abundance.

title: What is neoantigen prediction?
content: Neoantigens are tumor-specific peptides arising from somatic mutations. Computational prediction of neoantigen-MHC binding is used for personalized cancer immunotherapy and vaccine design.

title: What is the MHC restriction phenomenon?
content: MHC restriction means T cells can only recognize antigens when presented by specific MHC molecules. This determines which peptides a given individual's T cells can respond to.

title: What is the invariant chain (Ii)?
content: The invariant chain (CD74) prevents premature peptide binding to MHC class II in the ER. It is degraded in endosomes, leaving CLIP in the MHC-II groove, which is then exchanged for antigenic peptides.

title: What is HLA-DM's function?
content: HLA-DM catalyzes the exchange of CLIP for antigenic peptides in MHC class II molecules. It acts as a peptide editor, selecting high-affinity peptides for stable MHC-II presentation.

title: What is the role of aminopeptidases in MHC-I presentation?
content: Aminopeptidases in the ER (ERAP1, ERAP2) trim N-terminal extensions from peptides to generate optimal 8-10 residue peptides for MHC-I binding.

title: What is peptide-MHC tetramer staining?
content: MHC tetramers are four MHC-peptide complexes bound to a streptavidin-fluorophore. They enable direct visualization and quantification of antigen-specific T cells by flow cytometry.

title: What is thymic selection?
content: Thymic selection shapes the T cell repertoire. Positive selection ensures T cells can recognize self-MHC; negative selection eliminates T cells that bind too strongly to self-peptides, preventing autoimmunity.

title: What is the role of the proteasome in generating MHC-I peptides?
content: The 20S proteasome core generates most MHC-I peptides. The 11S regulator (PA28) can replace the 19S cap to enhance MHC-I peptide generation, particularly during immune responses.

title: What is epitope spreading?
content: Epitope spreading is the diversification of the immune response to include additional epitopes beyond the initial target. It occurs in chronic infections and autoimmune diseases.

title: What is peptide aggregation?
content: Peptide aggregation is the association of peptide molecules into insoluble assemblies. It is driven by hydrophobic interactions and hydrogen bonding, and is a major challenge in therapeutic peptide development.

title: What causes oxidation in peptides?
content: Peptide oxidation primarily affects methionine, cysteine, histidine, and tryptophan residues. Reactive oxygen species (ROS), metal ions, and light can catalyze oxidation.

title: What is deamidation?
content: Deamidation converts asparagine to aspartate or isoaspartate and glutamine to glutamate. It is a non-enzymatic reaction that is pH and temperature dependent, increasing with alkaline conditions.

title: How does temperature affect peptide stability?
content: Higher temperatures accelerate degradation reactions including deamidation, oxidation, and hydrolysis. Peptide formulations are typically stored at 2-8°C or frozen at -20°C to minimize degradation.

title: What is the role of pH in peptide stability?
content: Peptides are most stable at pH 4-6. Extreme pH accelerates hydrolysis, deamidation (alkaline), and acid-catalyzed cleavage. The optimal pH depends on the specific peptide sequence.

title: What is racemization in peptide degradation?
content: Racemization converts L-amino acids to D-enantiomers. It is base-catalyzed, particularly affecting aspartate and serine. Racemization can alter peptide structure and reduce biological activity.

title: What is beta-elimination in peptides?
content: Beta-elimination removes side chains from serine, threonine, or cysteine residues, forming dehydroalanine. It is base-catalyzed and can lead to cross-linking or loss of activity.

title: What stabilizers are used in peptide formulations?
content: Common stabilizers include antioxidants (BHT, ascorbic acid), buffers (phosphate, acetate), surfactants (PS-20), cryoprotectants (sucrose, trehalose), and tonicity agents (NaCl).

title: What is the role of surfactants in peptide formulations?
content: Surfactants like polysorbate 20 (PS-20) prevent surface adsorption and aggregation at interfaces. They reduce interfacial tension and protect peptides from denaturation during agitation.

title: What is forced degradation testing?
content: Forced degradation studies expose peptides to stress conditions (heat, light, pH, oxidation) to identify degradation pathways and validate stability-indicating analytical methods.

title: What is the shelf life of a therapeutic peptide?
content: Shelf life depends on the peptide and formulation. Lyophilized peptides may be stable for 2-3 years at 2-8°C, while liquid formulations typically have shorter shelf lives of 1-2 years.

title: What is lyophilization (freeze-drying)?
content: Lyophilization removes water by sublimation under vacuum. It improves peptide stability by eliminating hydrolysis and reducing molecular mobility, but can cause aggregation during freezing.

title: What is the role of antioxidants in peptide formulations?
content: Antioxidants like ascorbic acid, BHT, and methionine protect against oxidative degradation by scavenging free radicals and chelating metal ions that catalyze oxidation.

title: What is peptide hydrolysis?
content: Peptide hydrolysis breaks peptide bonds, primarily at aspartate residues (Asp-X bonds are labile) and under extreme pH conditions. It generates shorter peptide fragments.

title: What is glass transition temperature (Tg) in lyophilization?
content: Tg is the temperature at which the amorphous freeze-dried cake transitions from a glassy to a rubbery state. The product must be stored below Tg to maintain stability.

title: What is the effect of light on peptide stability?
content: Light, especially UV, can cause photo-oxidation of aromatic residues (Trp, Tyr, Phe) and generate reactive oxygen species. Peptide formulations should be protected from light.

title: What is the role of metal chelators in peptide formulations?
content: Metal chelators like EDTA bind trace metal ions that catalyze oxidative degradation. They are particularly important for formulations sensitive to metal-catalyzed oxidation.

title: What is peptide adsorption?
content: Peptides can adsorb to container surfaces (glass, plastic) and formulation components. This reduces the effective concentration and can cause aggregation at the interface.

title: What is the role of sucrose in peptide formulations?
content: Sucrose acts as a cryoprotectant during lyophilization and a stabilizer in the dried state. It forms a glassy matrix that restricts molecular mobility and protects against aggregation.

title: What are the ICH stability guidelines for peptides?
content: ICH guidelines (Q1A-Q1E) require stability testing at multiple temperatures (5°C, 25°C, 40°C) and humidity levels. Long-term, accelerated, and stress studies define shelf life and storage conditions.

title: What are the phases of clinical trials?
content: Phase I tests safety and dosing in healthy volunteers (20-100). Phase II tests efficacy in patients (100-300). Phase III confirms efficacy in large populations (1,000-5,000). Phase IV is post-market surveillance.

title: What is a primary endpoint?
content: A primary endpoint is the main outcome measure used to determine if a clinical trial has met its objectives. It must be clearly defined, measurable, and clinically meaningful.

title: What is PK/PD in drug development?
content: Pharmacokinetics (PK) describes what the body does to a drug (ADME). Pharmacodynamics (PD) describes what the drug does to the body (efficacy, toxicity). PK/PD modeling guides dosing optimization.

title: What is a dose-escalation study?
content: Dose-escalation studies start with low doses and gradually increase to determine the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) and safety profile. They are common in Phase I trials.

title: What is a randomized controlled trial (RCT)?
content: An RCT randomly assigns participants to treatment or control groups. It is the gold standard for clinical evidence, minimizing bias and allowing causal inference about treatment effects.

title: What is a biomarker in clinical development?
content: A biomarker is a measurable indicator of biological state. Diagnostic biomarkers identify disease, prognostic markers predict outcomes, and surrogate endpoints substitute for clinical endpoints.

title: What is the difference between efficacy and effectiveness?
content: Efficacy is how well a drug works under ideal conditions (clinical trials). Effectiveness is how well it works in real-world practice. Effectiveness is typically lower due to patient variability and compliance.

title: What is an IND application?
content: An Investigational New Drug (IND) application is submitted to the FDA before starting clinical trials. It contains preclinical data, manufacturing information, and proposed clinical protocols.

title: What is a NDA?
content: A New Drug Application (NDA) is submitted to the FDA seeking approval to market a drug. It contains all preclinical, clinical, manufacturing, and labeling data.

title: What is a placebo-controlled trial?
content: A placebo-controlled trial compares the active drug against an inert substance. It establishes the drug's true effect beyond the placebo effect and is the most rigorous study design.

title: What is the half-life of a therapeutic peptide?
content: Peptide half-lives vary widely: insulin (minutes), GLP-1 agonists (hours to days), PEGylated peptides (days to weeks). Half-life determines dosing frequency and patient compliance.

title: What is a first-in-human (FIH) study?
content: An FIH study is the first clinical trial of a new drug in humans. It starts with a single dose in a few healthy volunteers, with extensive safety monitoring and dose escalation.

title: What is pharmacovigilance?
content: Pharmacovigilance is the monitoring of adverse drug reactions after market approval. It includes spontaneous reporting systems, post-marketing studies, and risk management programs.

title: What is the role of an IRB/IEC?
content: An Institutional Review Board (IRB) or Independent Ethics Committee (IEC) reviews clinical trial protocols to ensure participant safety, informed consent, and ethical conduct.

title: What is bioequivalence?
content: Bioequivalence compares the pharmacokinetics of a generic drug to the reference drug. The generic must have AUC and Cmax within 80-125% of the reference to be considered equivalent.

title: What is the Cmax in PK analysis?
content: Cmax is the maximum plasma concentration achieved after dosing. It reflects the rate of absorption and is one of the key PK parameters alongside Tmax, AUC, and half-life.

title: What is a dose-response relationship?
content: A dose-response relationship shows how increasing doses produce increasing effects. It helps determine the optimal dose that maximizes efficacy while minimizing toxicity.

title: What is an adaptive clinical trial design?
content: Adaptive designs allow modifications to the trial based on interim data without compromising integrity. Adaptations can include dose changes, sample size re-estimation, or treatment arm selection.

title: What is a safety run-in in clinical trials?
content: A safety run-in is a small initial cohort that receives the drug before enrolling the full study population. It identifies safety concerns early and refines dosing.

title: What is the difference between superiority, non-inferiority, and equivalence trials?
content: Superiority trials test if a drug is better than control. Non-inferiority trials test if it's not worse by more than a margin. Equivalence trials test if drugs produce similar results.

title: What are cell-penetrating peptides (CPPs)?
content: CPPs are short peptides (5-30 residues) that can cross cell membranes. Examples include TAT (GRKKRRQRRRPQ) and penetratin. They can carry cargo including drugs, nucleic acids, and nanoparticles.

title: How do CPPs cross cell membranes?
content: CPPs use mechanisms including direct penetration through the lipid bilayer, endocytosis, and membrane destabilization. The mechanism depends on the CPP sequence, concentration, and cell type.

title: What is the blood-brain barrier (BBB)?
content: The BBB is a highly selective barrier formed by endothelial cells with tight junctions. It prevents most drugs from entering the brain. Peptides can cross via receptor-mediated transcytosis.

title: What is oral bioavailability of peptides?
content: Oral bioavailability of peptides is typically <5% due to enzymatic degradation, poor membrane permeability, and first-pass metabolism. Strategies to improve it include cyclization and formulation approaches.

title: What is transcytosis?
content: Transcytosis is the transport of molecules across a cell by receptor-mediated endocytosis on one side and exocytosis on the other. It is a key mechanism for peptide transport across barriers.

title: What is the role of tight junctions in peptide transport?
content: Tight junctions seal the space between epithelial cells, preventing paracellular transport. Peptides generally cannot cross tight junctions and must use transcellular routes.

title: What is the role of P-glycoprotein in peptide absorption?
content: P-glycoprotein (P-gp) is an efflux pump that actively transports peptides back into the gut lumen. It reduces oral bioavailability of many peptide drugs.

title: What is the role of the proton pump in peptide absorption?
content: The gastric H+/K+ ATPase maintains stomach acidity (pH 1-3). Peptide formulations may include acid-resistant coatings or buffering agents to protect peptides from acid degradation.

title: What is the role of endocytosis in peptide delivery?
content: Endocytosis internalizes peptides via clathrin-coated pits, caveolae, or macropinocytosis. Once internalized, peptides must escape endosomes to reach their intracellular targets.

title: What is endosomal escape?
content: Endosomal escape is the process by which internalized peptides exit endosomes to reach the cytoplasm. Many CPPs and delivery systems are designed to disrupt endosomal membranes.

title: What are the strategies to improve oral peptide delivery?
content: Strategies include permeation enhancers (sodium caprate), enzyme inhibitors (bacitracin), mucoadhesive formulations, nanoparticle carriers, and chemical modifications (cyclosporine-like N-methylation).

title: What is the role of mucus in peptide absorption?
content: Mucus is a viscous layer that traps peptides and slows diffusion. Mucoadhesive formulations can enhance residence time, but excessive mucus can reduce absorption of peptide drugs.

title: What is the mechanism of the TAT peptide?
content: TAT (GRKKRRQRRRPQ) from HIV-1 enters cells through multiple mechanisms including direct penetration and endocytosis. Its arginine residues interact with membrane phospholipids.

title: What is the role of receptor-mediated transport in BBB crossing?
content: Peptides conjugated to transferrin or insulin can cross the BBB via receptor-mediated transcytosis. These receptors are expressed on brain endothelial cells.

title: What is the difference between passive and active peptide transport?
content: Passive transport depends on concentration gradients and peptide properties (size, lipophilicity). Active transport uses carrier proteins or energy-dependent mechanisms against concentration gradients.

title: What is the role of peptide lipophilicity in membrane permeability?
content: Moderate lipophilicity (logP -1 to 3) favors passive membrane permeability. Too hydrophilic peptides cannot cross membranes; too lipophilic peptides may be trapped in membranes.

title: What are permeation enhancers?
content: Permeation enhancers increase membrane permeability to peptides. Examples include fatty acids (sodium caprate), surfactants (polysorbate 80), and chelating agents (EDTA).

title: What is the role of the intestinal epithelium in peptide absorption?
content: The intestinal epithelium has microvilli for absorption, tight junctions that limit paracellular transport, and enzymes that degrade peptides. It represents a major barrier for oral peptide delivery.

title: What is a nanoparticle delivery system?
content: Nanoparticles (PLGA, chitosan, liposomes) can encapsulate peptides, protecting them from degradation and enhancing absorption. They can be designed for sustained release or targeted delivery.

title: What is the role of the Peyer's patches in oral peptide delivery?
content: Peyer's patches are lymphoid tissues in the intestine that sample antigens via M cells. Nanoparticles can be taken up by M cells, enabling oral delivery of peptide vaccines.

title: What is FRET and how is it used in peptide sensors?
content: FRET (Förster Resonance Energy Transfer) transfers energy from an excited donor fluorophore to an acceptor within 1-10 nm. Peptide sensors use FRET to detect conformational changes, cleavage, or binding.

title: What is fluorescence polarization (FP)?
content: FP measures the rotation of fluorescent molecules in solution. Small molecules rotate fast (low FP), while bound molecules rotate slowly (high FP). It is used to measure peptide-receptor binding.

title: What is an electrochemical peptide sensor?
content: Electrochemical sensors use peptides as recognition elements on electrodes. Binding events generate electrical signals through changes in impedance, current, or potential.

title: What is a molecular beacon?
content: Molecular beacons are hairpin-shaped oligonucleotides or peptides with a fluorophore and quencher. When the target binds, the hairpin opens, separating the fluorophore and quencher, generating fluorescence.

title: What is a peptide-based biosensor?
content: A peptide-based biosensor uses a peptide as the molecular recognition element to detect specific analytes. The peptide undergoes a conformational or chemical change upon binding, generating a measurable signal.

title: What is the Förster radius (R0)?
content: The Förster radius is the distance at which FRET efficiency is 50%. It depends on the spectral overlap between donor and acceptor, their orientations, and the refractive index of the medium.

title: What is surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS)?
content: SERS enhances Raman signals from molecules near metal surfaces. Peptide-functionalized nanoparticles can detect analytes with high sensitivity through changes in the SERS spectrum.

title: What is a quenched fluorescent peptide?
content: A quenched fluorescent peptide has a fluorophore and quencher in proximity. When a target enzyme cleaves the peptide, the fluorophore and quencher separate, generating a fluorescent signal.

title: What is electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)?
content: EIS measures changes in impedance at a peptide-modified electrode surface. Binding events alter the charge transfer resistance, which is detected as a change in the impedance spectrum.

title: What is a calorimetric peptide sensor?
content: Calorimetric sensors measure heat changes upon peptide-analyte binding. Isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) provides thermodynamic data including binding constants and enthalpy changes.

title: What is the role of quantum dots in peptide sensing?
content: Quantum dots are semiconductor nanocrystals with size-dependent fluorescence. They can be conjugated to peptides for multiplexed detection with narrow emission spectra and high brightness.

title: What is the role of aptamers in peptide sensors?
content: Aptamers are nucleic acid or peptide molecules that bind specific targets. Peptide aptamers can be used as recognition elements in biosensors, providing antibody-like specificity.

title: What is a lateral flow assay?
content: A lateral flow assay uses a peptide or antibody on a membrane strip. When the sample flows across, analyte binding generates a visible line. It is used for rapid point-of-care testing.

title: What is chemiluminescence in peptide sensing?
content: Chemiluminescence generates light through chemical reactions. Peptide sensors can use chemiluminescent labels for high-sensitivity detection, often in immunoassays.

title: What is an allosteric peptide sensor?
content: An allosteric peptide sensor undergoes a conformational change upon binding that modulates a signaling domain. The signal output (fluorescence, enzymatic activity) indicates analyte presence.

title: What is the dynamic range of a biosensor?
content: The dynamic range is the concentration range over which the sensor provides a measurable response. Wide dynamic ranges (several orders of magnitude) are desirable for practical applications.

title: What is the sensitivity of a peptide biosensor?
content: Sensitivity is the slope of the calibration curve (signal change per unit concentration change). Higher sensitivity enables detection of lower analyte concentrations.

title: What is a peptide-based pH sensor?
content: pH-sensitive peptides change conformation or charge with pH changes. They can be conjugated to fluorophores for intracellular pH measurement or environmental monitoring.

title: What is the role of SAMs in peptide sensors?
content: Self-assembled monolayers (SAMs) on gold electrodes provide an organized surface for peptide immobilization. They control peptide orientation and density, optimizing sensor performance.

title: What is a peptide array?
content: A peptide array is a high-throughput format where multiple peptides are immobilized on a solid surface. It enables simultaneous screening of many peptide-analyte interactions.

title: What are opioid peptides?
content: Opioid peptides are endogenous peptides that bind opioid receptors (μ, δ, κ) to modulate pain, reward, and stress responses. Examples include endorphins, enkephalins, and dynorphins.

title: What are antimicrobial peptides (AMPs)?
content: AMPs are part of innate immunity and kill bacteria, fungi, and viruses. They typically have cationic charge and amphipathic structure. Examples include defensins, magainins, and cathelicidins.

title: What are neuropeptides?
content: Neuropeptides are small proteins that act as neurotransmitters or neuromodulators in the nervous system. They include substance P, neuropeptide Y, and oxytocin.

title: What is the opioid peptide family?
content: The opioid family includes enkephalins (Met/Leu-enkephalin, 5 residues), endorphins (β-endorphin, 31 residues), and dynorphins (dynorphin A, 17 residues). They share the YGGF N-terminal motif.

title: What is the mechanism of AMPs?
content: AMPs disrupt bacterial membranes through electrostatic interactions (cationic peptide with anionic membrane) and amphipathic insertion, forming pores or causing membrane destabilization.

title: What is the oxytocin peptide?
content: Oxytocin is a 9-amino acid cyclic peptide produced in the hypothalamus. It promotes social bonding, uterine contractions during labor, and milk ejection during breastfeeding.

title: What is vasopressin?
content: Vasopressin (ADH) is a 9-amino acid peptide that regulates water balance by acting on kidney collecting ducts. It also affects blood pressure and social behavior.

title: What is the defensin family?
content: Defensins are cationic antimicrobial peptides with disulfide-stabilized β-sheet structures. α-defensins (6 cysteines) are found in neutrophils; β-defensins (6 cysteines, different pattern) are found in epithelial cells.

title: What is a cyclic peptide natural product?
content: Cyclic peptides like cyclosporine, bacitracin, and vancomycin are produced by microorganisms. Their cyclic structure confers resistance to proteolysis and can enhance membrane permeability.

title: What is the role of endorphins in pain modulation?
content: Endorphins are released during stress, exercise, and pain. They bind to opioid receptors to produce analgesia and euphoria. β-endorphin is the most potent endogenous opioid.

title: What is the glucagon-like peptide (GLP) family?
content: GLP-1 and GLP-2 are gut hormones that regulate blood glucose and intestinal growth. GLP-1 receptor agonists (exenatide, liraglutide) are used for type 2 diabetes treatment.

title: What is the role of antimicrobial peptides in wound healing?
content: AMPs not only kill bacteria but also modulate inflammation, promote cell proliferation, and enhance wound healing. They are being developed as therapeutic agents for chronic wounds.

title: What is the melanocortin peptide family?
content: Melanocortins (α-MSH, ACTH, β-MSH, γ-MSH) are derived from proopiomelanocortin (POMC). They regulate pigmentation, energy homeostasis, and inflammation.

title: What is the role of neuropeptide Y (NPY)?
content: NPY is a 36-amino acid peptide that is one of the most abundant brain peptides. It stimulates appetite, reduces anxiety, and modulates cardiovascular function.

title: What is the natriuretic peptide family?
content: The family includes ANP (atrial), BNP (brain), and CNP (C-type). They regulate blood pressure and fluid balance through vasodilation and natriuresis.

title: What is the role of substance P?
content: Substance P is an 11-amino acid tachykinin that transmits pain signals, mediates neurogenic inflammation, and regulates mood. It acts via the NK1 receptor.

title: What is the structure of defensins?
content: Defensins are 29-45 residue peptides with 6 cysteines forming 3 disulfide bonds. They adopt β-sheet structures stabilized by the disulfide framework.

title: What are the opioid receptor subtypes?
content: Three main opioid receptor subtypes exist: μ (mu) for analgesia and addiction, δ (delta) for mood and analgesia, and κ (kappa) for sedation and dysphoria.

title: What is the role of ghrelin?
content: Ghrelin is a 28-amino acid peptide produced in the stomach. It is the only known orexigenic hormone, stimulating appetite and growth hormone release.

title: What are cyclotides?
content: Cyclotides are circular plant peptides with a cystine knot motif. They have exceptional stability and are being investigated as drug scaffolds and insecticides.

title: What is directed evolution?
content: Directed evolution mimics natural selection in the laboratory. Libraries of peptide variants are created and screened for desired properties, with the best variants selected for further rounds of mutation.

title: What is rational design in peptide engineering?
content: Rational design uses structural and mechanistic knowledge to make specific modifications. It requires knowledge of the peptide's structure, binding site, and structure-activity relationships.

title: What is phage display?
content: Phage display expresses peptides on bacteriophage surfaces. Libraries of 10⁹-10¹¹ variants can be screened for binding to targets. Selected peptides are amplified and subjected to further rounds.

title: What is peptide stapling?
content: Peptide stapling introduces crosslinks (hydrocarbon, lactam) to constrain alpha-helical peptides. This enhances cell penetration, protease resistance, and target binding affinity.

title: What is computational peptide design?
content: Computational design uses algorithms (Rosetta, AlphaFold) to predict and optimize peptide structures. It can design novel peptides, predict mutations, and optimize binding affinity.

title: What is alanine scanning mutagenesis?
content: Alanine scanning systematically replaces each residue with alanine to identify residues critical for binding or activity. Alanine removes side-chain interactions without disrupting the backbone.

title: What is a peptide library?
content: A peptide library is a collection of many peptide variants. Libraries can be random, focused, or designed. They enable high-throughput screening for desired properties.

title: What is the role of machine learning in peptide design?
content: Machine learning models (deep learning, generative models) can predict peptide properties, generate novel sequences, and optimize multiple properties simultaneously.

title: What is peptide scaffolding?
content: Peptide scaffolding uses stable frameworks (knottins, affibodies, DARPins) to present binding loops. This creates small, stable proteins with target-binding capability.

title: What is site-directed mutagenesis?
content: Site-directed mutagenesis introduces specific amino acid changes at defined positions. It is used to test hypotheses about structure-function relationships and improve peptide properties.

title: What is the ribosome display technique?
content: Ribosome display links mRNA, ribosome, and peptide in a complex without cell transformation. It enables larger library sizes (10¹²-10¹⁴) than phage display.

title: What is mRNA display?
content: mRNA display covalently links mRNA to its encoded peptide via a puromycin linker. It enables selection of peptides without transformation and can include non-natural amino acids.

title: What is the role of consensus design?
content: Consensus design analyzes multiple homologous sequences to identify the most frequent amino acids at each position. Consensus sequences often have improved stability and activity.

title: What is a focused peptide library?
content: A focused library is designed based on known structure-activity relationships. It concentrates diversity at key positions rather than randomizing all positions, improving hit rates.

title: What is the yeast two-hybrid system?
content: The yeast two-hybrid system detects protein-protein interactions in vivo. Peptides are expressed as fusions with DNA-binding or activation domains to identify binding partners.

title: What is the concept of directed peptide evolution?
content: Directed peptide evolution combines random mutagenesis with screening/selection. Error-prone PCR or DNA shuffling creates diversity; functional screening identifies improved variants.

title: What is deep mutational scanning?
content: Deep mutational scanning introduces all possible single mutations at every position and measures their effects by next-generation sequencing. It creates comprehensive fitness landscapes.

title: What is computational protein design?
content: Computational protein design uses physics-based and knowledge-based methods to design novel protein structures and functions. Software like Rosetta, RosettaDesign, and AlphaFold are key tools.

title: What is the SELEX process?
content: SELEX (Systematic Evolution of Ligands by Exponential Enrichment) selects aptamers from random nucleic acid libraries through iterative rounds of binding and amplification.

title: What is peptide macrocyclization engineering?
content: Peptide macrocyclization connects distant residues to create constrained structures. Methods include head-to-tail cyclization, side-chain to side-chain, and stapling approaches.

title: What is method validation?
content: Method validation demonstrates that an analytical method meets its intended purpose. It assesses accuracy, precision, specificity, linearity, range, robustness, and detection/quantitation limits.

title: What are the ICH guidelines?
content: ICH (International Council for Harmonisation) guidelines provide standardized requirements for pharmaceutical development. Key guidelines include Q2 (validation), Q1A (stability), and Q6B (specifications).

title: What is specificity in method validation?
content: Specificity is the ability to measure the analyte without interference from other components (impurities, degradation products, excipients). It is demonstrated by resolution of all relevant peaks.

title: What is accuracy in analytical methods?
content: Accuracy is the closeness of measured values to the true value. It is assessed by analyzing samples of known concentration and expressed as percent recovery (typically 98-102%).

title: What is precision?
content: Precision is the reproducibility of measurements. It includes repeatability (same operator, same day), intermediate precision (different days/operators), and reproducibility (different laboratories).

title: What is linearity in method validation?
content: Linearity is the ability to obtain results proportional to concentration. It is demonstrated by analyzing 5+ concentrations and calculating correlation coefficients (typically r² > 0.999).

title: What is the limit of detection (LOD)?
content: LOD is the lowest concentration that can be reliably detected but not quantified. It is typically calculated as 3.3 × σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation and S is the slope of the calibration curve.

title: What is the limit of quantitation (LOQ)?
content: LOQ is the lowest concentration that can be quantified with acceptable accuracy and precision. It is typically calculated as 10 × σ/S.

title: What is robustness in method validation?
content: Robustness measures the method's ability to remain unaffected by small, deliberate variations (pH, temperature, flow rate). It identifies critical parameters that must be controlled.

title: What is a system suitability test?
content: System suitability tests verify that the chromatographic system is working properly before analysis. Parameters include resolution, tailing factor, plate count, and retention time reproducibility.

title: What is the difference between validation and verification?
content: Validation demonstrates method fitness for a specific purpose. Verification confirms that a validated method works in a specific laboratory setting under local conditions.

title: What is a stability-indicating method?
content: A stability-indicating method can resolve the intact peptide from all degradation products. It is essential for stability studies and must be validated per ICH guidelines.

title: What is the ICH Q2 guideline?
content: ICH Q2 provides harmonized requirements for validation of analytical procedures. It covers parameters including specificity, accuracy, precision, detection limit, quantitation limit, and linearity.

title: What is the role of reference standards?
content: Reference standards are well-characterized materials used to calibrate instruments and validate methods. Primary standards are purified; secondary standards are calibrated against primary standards.

title: What is method transfer?
content: Method transfer demonstrates that a validated method performs equivalently when moved between laboratories. It involves parallel testing and statistical comparison of results.

title: What is the concept of analytical target profile (ATP)?
content: The ATP defines the performance requirements of an analytical method before development. It specifies required accuracy, precision, and other performance characteristics.

title: What is the design space in analytical method development?
content: The design space is the multidimensional combination of input variables that have been demonstrated to provide quality assurance. It allows operational flexibility within validated ranges.

title: What is content uniformity testing?
content: Content uniformity testing ensures that each unit (tablet, vial) contains the declared amount of active ingredient. It is required by pharmacopeias for solid dosage forms.

title: What is a method development protocol?
content: A method development protocol outlines the approach for developing an analytical method. It includes selection of technique, column, mobile phase, and detection parameters.

title: What is the role of quality control in peptide manufacturing?
content: QC testing ensures that peptides meet specifications for identity, purity, potency, and safety. It includes in-process testing, release testing, and stability studies.

title: What is the IUPAC name for a peptide?
content: IUPAC nomenclature names peptides from N- to C-terminus using three-letter codes with hyphens. The C-terminal residue retains its full name (e.g., Gly-Ala-Val).

title: What is the three-letter code for arginine?
content: Arginine is abbreviated as Arg or R. In three-letter notation, it appears as Arg in peptide sequences.

title: What is the one-letter code for tryptophan?
content: Tryptophan is abbreviated as W in one-letter notation. It is one of the larger amino acids, with the W mnemonic relating to its double-ring indole structure.

title: How are peptides named in the IUPAC system?
content: Peptides are named from N- to C-terminus. Each amino acid except the C-terminal uses the -yl suffix (e.g., glycyl, alanyl). The C-terminal retains its amino acid name.

title: What is the three-letter code for cysteine?
content: Cysteine is abbreviated as Cys or C. It contains a thiol group that can form disulfide bonds.

title: What is a dipeptide?
content: A dipeptide consists of two amino acids joined by a single peptide bond. Examples include glycylalanine (Gly-Ala) and alanylglycine (Ala-Gly).

title: What is the three-letter code for histidine?
content: Histidine is abbreviated as His or H. Its imidazole side chain has a pKa near physiological pH.

title: What does the notation [D-Ala²] mean?
content: [D-Ala²] indicates that the alanine at position 2 has been replaced by D-alanine. This notation specifies both the position and the stereochemistry of the substitution.

title: What is the one-letter code for aspartic acid?
content: Aspartic acid is abbreviated as D in one-letter notation. Asparagine (amide form) is N.

title: What is the difference between Asp and Asn?
content: Asp (aspartic acid, D) has a carboxyl side chain; Asn (asparagine, N) has an amide side chain. Asp is negatively charged at physiological pH; Asn is neutral.

title: What is a polypeptide?
content: A polypeptide is a chain of many amino acids (typically >10) joined by peptide bonds. The terms polypeptide and protein are often used interchangeably for longer chains.

title: What is the one-letter code for glutamic acid?
content: Glutamic acid is abbreviated as E in one-letter notation. The mnemonic is that E is the first letter of 'glutamate' (the ionized form at physiological pH).

title: What does the notation Ac- mean in peptide nomenclature?
content: Ac- indicates N-terminal acetylation. This modification adds an acetyl group (CH₃CO-) to the N-terminal amino group, protecting it from degradation.

title: What is the three-letter code for leucine?
content: Leucine is abbreviated as Leu or L. It is a branched-chain amino acid.

title: What is the one-letter code for isoleucine?
content: Isoleucine is abbreviated as I in one-letter notation. Isoleucine (I) and leucine (L) are distinct despite being structurally similar.

title: How are modified amino acids indicated in sequences?
content: Modified amino acids are indicated with brackets: [pSer] for phosphoserine, [MeLys] for methyllysine, [AcK] for acetyllysine. Position numbers specify location.

title: What is the three-letter code for phenylalanine?
content: Phenylalanine is abbreviated as Phe or F. It is an aromatic amino acid with a benzyl side chain.

title: What is the one-letter code for asparagine?
content: Asparagine is abbreviated as N in one-letter notation. The mnemonic is that N is the first letter of 'asparagine'.

title: How is a peptide sequence written?
content: Peptide sequences are always written from N-terminus (left) to C-terminus (right). The convention is standard across all nomenclature systems.

title: What is the three-letter code for tyrosine?
content: Tyrosine is abbreviated as Tyr or Y. It contains a hydroxyl group on the aromatic ring and is a target for phosphorylation.

title: What are hydrogen bonds in peptide interactions?
content: Hydrogen bonds form between a hydrogen bond donor (N-H, O-H) and acceptor (C=O, N). In peptides, backbone H-bonds stabilize secondary structures, while side-chain H-bonds mediate specific recognition.

title: What are van der Waals interactions?
content: Van der Waals interactions are weak, short-range attractions between atoms (0.4-4 kJ/mol). They are numerous in protein interiors and contribute significantly to binding affinity through cumulative effects.

title: What is the hydrophobic effect?
content: The hydrophobic effect drives nonpolar residues to cluster in the protein interior, away from water. It is the dominant force in protein folding and peptide-receptor binding.

title: What is an ionic interaction (salt bridge)?
content: Ionic interactions form between oppositely charged residues (e.g., Lys⁺ and Asp⁻). Salt bridges contribute 5-10 kJ/mol and are important for protein stability and peptide-receptor binding.

title: What is pi-stacking?
content: Pi-stacking occurs between aromatic rings (Phe, Tyr, Trp, His). Parallel and perpendicular (T-shaped) arrangements are both observed and contribute to protein structure stability.

title: What is a cation-pi interaction?
content: Cation-pi interactions occur between a positively charged group (Lys, Arg) and an aromatic ring (Phe, Tyr, Trp). They can contribute 5-80 kJ/mol and are important in enzyme active sites.

title: What is the role of water in peptide interactions?
content: Water mediates many peptide interactions through bridging hydrogen bonds. It also plays a role in the hydrophobic effect by forming ordered structures around nonpolar surfaces.

title: What is a disulfide bond's contribution to stability?
content: Disulfide bonds contribute 10-20 kJ/mol of stability. They constrain the conformational entropy of the unfolded state, making the folded state more favorable.

title: What is the concept of complementarity in binding?
content: Molecular complementarity means the shape, charge, and hydrophobicity of the binding surfaces match. High complementarity leads to tight binding through optimized interactions.

title: What is the enthalpy-entropy compensation?
content: Enthalpy-entropy compensation is a phenomenon where favorable enthalpy changes (e.g., from binding) are partially offset by unfavorable entropy changes (e.g., from conformational restriction).

title: What is the role of conformational entropy in peptide binding?
content: Peptide binding often restricts conformational freedom, causing an unfavorable entropy change. This must be compensated by favorable enthalpy from interactions for net binding to occur.

title: What are the typical bond energies of non-covalent interactions?
content: Hydrogen bonds: 10-40 kJ/mol. Van der Waals: 0.4-4 kJ/mol. Ion pairs: 5-10 kJ/mol. Hydrophobic interactions: variable but significant. Cation-pi: 5-80 kJ/mol.

title: What is the concept of shape complementarity?
content: Shape complementarity measures how well two surfaces fit together. High shape complementarity maximizes van der Waals contacts and minimizes unoccupied volume in the binding interface.

title: What is the role of the desolvation penalty?
content: When a peptide binds its target, water molecules must be displaced from binding surfaces. This desolvation costs entropy and enthalpy, which must be overcome by the new interactions formed.

title: What is the difference between specific and non-specific binding?
content: Specific binding involves defined interactions at a particular site with defined stoichiometry. Non-specific binding occurs at multiple sites with low affinity and is often concentration-dependent.

title: What is the concept of induced fit?
content: Induced fit describes how both the peptide and receptor change conformation upon binding. This flexible adaptation can enhance binding affinity through optimized complementarity.

title: What is the role of electrostatic complementarity?
content: Electrostatic complementarity means opposite charges face each other at binding interfaces. It is important for initial long-range attraction and correct orientation of binding partners.

title: What is a cooperative binding event?
