content: Cooperative binding occurs when binding at one site affects binding at another. Positive cooperativity means binding is enhanced; negative cooperativity means binding is reduced.

title: What is the concept of binding thermodynamics?
content: Binding thermodynamics relates free energy (ΔG = ΔH - TΔS) to enthalpy and entropy. Favorable binding requires ΔG < 0, achieved through favorable ΔH and/or ΔS.

title: What is the role of water-mediated hydrogen bonds?
content: Water molecules can bridge between peptide and receptor, forming hydrogen bond networks. These water-mediated contacts can contribute significantly to binding affinity and specificity.

title: What is semaglutide?
content: Semaglutide is a GLP-1 receptor agonist used for type 2 diabetes and obesity. It is a 31-amino acid peptide with a C16 fatty acid chain for albumin binding and two Aib substitutions for protease resistance.

title: What is insulin?
content: Insulin is a 51-amino acid peptide hormone that regulates blood glucose. It consists of an A chain (21 residues) and B chain (30 residues) linked by disulfide bonds.

title: What is cyclosporine?
content: Cyclosporine is an 11-amino acid cyclic peptide immunosuppressant. It inhibits calcineurin, preventing T cell activation. It is used to prevent organ transplant rejection.

title: What is enalapril?
content: Enalapril is an ACE inhibitor peptide mimetic used to treat hypertension. It is a prodrug converted to enalaprilat, which inhibits angiotensin-converting enzyme.

title: What is leuprolide?
content: Leuprolide is a GnRH agonist (9 amino acids) used to treat prostate cancer, endometriosis, and central precocious puberty. It causes initial stimulation followed by downregulation of GnRH receptors.

title: What is octreotide?
content: Octreotide is a somatostatin analog (8 amino acids) used to treat acromegaly and neuroendocrine tumors. It has a longer half-life than natural somatostatin due to D-Trp and cyclization.

title: What is exenatide?
content: Exenatide is a GLP-1 receptor agonist derived from Gila monster venom. It was the first GLP-1 agonist approved for type 2 diabetes and is administered twice daily.

title: What is desmopressin?
content: Desmopressin is a synthetic vasopressin analog used to treat diabetes insipidus and nocturnal enuresis. It has increased antidiuretic activity and duration compared to vasopressin.

title: What is a peptide drug?
content: A peptide drug is a therapeutic agent consisting of amino acids linked by peptide bonds. There are approximately 80 approved peptide drugs and >500 in clinical development.

title: What is liraglutide?
content: Liraglutide is a long-acting GLP-1 receptor agonist (97% homology to human GLP-1) with a C16 fatty acid chain. It is used for type 2 diabetes and obesity.

title: What is abaloparatide?
content: Abaloparatide is a PTHrP analog used for osteoporosis. It stimulates bone formation by activating PTH1 receptors with a different signaling profile than PTH.

title: What is romiplostim?
content: Romiplostim is a thrombopoietin receptor agonist (peptide-Fc fusion) used to treat immune thrombocytopenia. It stimulates platelet production.

title: What is ziconotide?
content: Ziconotide is a synthetic ω-conotoxin (25 amino acids) used for severe chronic pain. It blocks N-type calcium channels and is administered intrathecally.

title: What is tesamorelin?
content: Tesamorelin is a growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH) analog used to reduce excess abdominal fat in HIV patients with lipodystrophy.

title: What is the advantage of peptide drugs over small molecules?
content: Peptide drugs offer high specificity, low toxicity, and predictable metabolism. They can target protein-protein interactions that are difficult for small molecules.

title: What is the disadvantage of peptide drugs?
content: Peptide drugs typically have short half-lives, poor oral bioavailability, and may require injection. They can also be immunogenic and are expensive to manufacture.

title: What is a peptide-drug conjugate (PDC)?
content: PDCs attach cytotoxic drugs to peptides that target specific receptors. The peptide provides targeted delivery, reducing systemic toxicity. Examples include tumor-homing peptides.

title: What is a peptide vaccine?
content: Peptide vaccines use synthetic peptides from pathogen proteins to elicit immune responses. They can be designed to target specific epitopes and are being developed for cancer and infectious diseases.

title: What is the role of peptides in regenerative medicine?
content: Peptides like RGD and IKVAV promote cell adhesion, migration, and differentiation. Self-assembling peptides create scaffolds for tissue engineering and wound healing.

title: What is an antimicrobial peptide therapeutic?
content: AMP therapeutics are being developed as alternatives to antibiotics for drug-resistant infections. They can kill bacteria through membrane disruption, making resistance development more difficult.

title: Residue
content: **Related Terms:** amino-acid, peptide-bond, backbone  An individual amino acid unit within a peptide chain, connected by peptide bonds. Named because what remains after water is lost during condensation.

title: Side Chain
content: **Related Terms:** amino-acid, chirality, isoelectric-point  The variable R-group attached to the alpha-carbon of an amino acid. Determines chemical properties including charge, hydrophobicity, and reactivity.

title: Backbone
content: **Related Terms:** residue, peptide-bond, phi-psi-angles  The repeating N-Calpha-C(=O) unit of a peptide chain. Provides structural framework while side chains project outward.

title: Chirality
content: **Related Terms:** alpha-carbon, stereoisomer, enantiomer  The property of a molecule being non-superimposable on its mirror image. Natural amino acids are L-configured. Critical for enzyme recognition and drug activity.

title: Zwitterion
content: **Related Terms:** isoelectric-point, pKa, amino-acid  A molecule with both positive and negative charges but net charge zero. Amino acids exist as zwitterions at physiological pH.

title: Condensation
content: **Related Terms:** hydrolysis, peptide-bond, dehydration-synthesis  A reaction joining two molecules with loss of water. Peptide bond formation is condensation between carboxyl and amino groups.

title: Hydrolysis
content: **Related Terms:** condensation, peptide-bond, protease  Breaking a bond by adding water. Reverse of condensation. Peptide bonds are hydrolyzed by proteases or strong acid/base.

title: Amide Bond
content: **Related Terms:** peptide-bond, resonance, planarity  A covalent bond between a carbonyl carbon and nitrogen. The peptide bond is a specific amide bond between amino acids.

title: Planarity
content: **Related Terms:** resonance, amide-bond, phi-psi-angles  The peptide bond group (C=O, N-H, and connecting atoms) is planar due to partial double-bond character from resonance. Limits conformational flexibility.

title: Resonance
content: **Related Terms:** planarity, amide-bond, peptide-bond  Delocalization of electrons across multiple atoms. In peptide bonds, resonance between C=O and C-N forms gives partial double-bond character and planarity.

title: Primary Structure
content: **Related Terms:** secondary-structure, amino-acid-sequence, N-terminus  The linear sequence of amino acids in a peptide chain, read from N-terminus to C-terminus. Encoded by DNA and determines all higher-order structure.

title: Secondary Structure
content: **Related Terms:** alpha-helix, beta-sheet, hydrogen-bond  Local folding patterns stabilized by hydrogen bonds between backbone atoms. Common types: alpha-helix and beta-sheet.

title: Tertiary Structure
content: **Related Terms:** quaternary-structure, fold, disulfide-bond  The overall 3D shape of a single peptide chain. Stabilized by hydrophobic interactions, hydrogen bonds, ionic bonds, and disulfide bridges.

title: Quaternary Structure
content: **Related Terms:** tertiary-structure, subunit, oligomer  The arrangement of multiple peptide subunits into a functional complex. Stabilized by same forces as tertiary structure. Example: hemoglobin (4 subunits).

title: Fold
content: **Related Terms:** domain, tertiary-structure, protein-folding  A distinctive 3D arrangement of secondary structural elements. Proteins with same fold may have different sequences. Classification by SCOP/CATH databases.

title: Solid-Phase Peptide Synthesis (SPPS)
content: **Related Terms:** fmoc, boc, resin, coupling  Method for synthesizing peptides on insoluble resin support. Amino acids added one at a time from C-to-N terminus. Enables automation and rapid purification.

title: Fmoc
content: **Related Terms:** spps, protecting-group, boc  Fluorenylmethyloxycarbonyl. A base-labile protecting group for amino groups in SPPS. Removed by piperidine. Preferred for its mild conditions.

title: Boc
content: **Related Terms:** spps, protecting-group, fmoc  tert-Butyloxycarbonyl. An acid-labile protecting group for amino groups. Removed by trifluoroacetic acid. Used in Boc-SPPS strategy.

title: Resin
content: **Related Terms:** spps, linker, cleavage  Insoluble polymer support for SPPS. Common types: Wang, Rink, Merrifield resin. Allows washing away reagents without losing peptide.

title: Coupling
content: **Related Terms:** spps, coupling-reagent, activation  Formation of peptide bond between activated amino acid and growing chain on resin. Coupling reagents: HATU, HBTU, DIC/HOBt. Efficiency critical for yield.

title: Bioavailability
content: **Related Terms:** adme, half-life, first-pass-metabolism  The fraction of administered drug that reaches systemic circulation unchanged. Oral bioavailability of peptides is typically <2% due to degradation and poor absorption.

title: Half-life
content: **Related Terms:** clearance, pharmacokinetics, elimination  Time required for drug concentration to decrease by 50%. Influenced by distribution, metabolism, and excretion. Peptide half-lives range from minutes to days.

title: Clearance
content: **Related Terms:** half-life, volume-of-distribution, pharmacokinetics  Volume of plasma cleared of drug per unit time. Total clearance = hepatic + renal clearance. Determines dosing requirements.

title: ADME
content: **Related Terms:** bioavailability, pharmacokinetics, clearance  Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion. Framework for understanding drug pharmacokinetics. Each phase affects drug concentration at target site.

title: Pharmacokinetics
content: **Related Terms:** adme, pharmacodynamics, bioavailability  Study of drug absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion over time. Answers: what does the body do to the drug? Contrast with pharmacodynamics.

title: Hormone
content: **Related Terms:** neurotransmitter, receptor, endocrine  Chemical messenger produced by endocrine glands, transported via blood to target organs. Peptide hormones include insulin, glucagon, and growth hormone.

title: Neurotransmitter
content: **Related Terms:** hormone, synapse, neuropeptide  Chemical messenger released at synapses to transmit nerve signals. Peptide neurotransmitters: substance P, endorphins, neuropeptide Y.

title: Receptor
content: **Related Terms:** ligand, agonist, antagonist  Protein that binds specific molecules (ligands) and triggers cellular response. Types: G-protein coupled, ion channel, enzyme-linked, nuclear.

title: Agonist
content: **Related Terms:** antagonist, receptor, ligand  Molecule that binds receptor and activates it, producing biological response. Full agonists produce maximum response; partial agonists produce submaximal response.

title: Antagonist
content: **Related Terms:** agonist, receptor, inhibitor  Molecule that binds receptor without activating it, blocking agonist binding. Competitive antagonists can be overcome by increasing agonist concentration.

title: HPLC
content: **Related Terms:** purification, reverse-phase, mass-spectrometry  High-Performance Liquid Chromatography. Separation technique for peptides using stationary phase and mobile phase under high pressure. Used for purification and analysis.

title: Mass Spectrometry
content: **Related Terms:** hplc, maldi, electrospray  Analytical technique measuring mass-to-charge ratio of ionized molecules. Essential for peptide identification, sequencing, and post-translational modification analysis.

title: NMR Spectroscopy
content: **Related Terms:** circular-dichroism, structure-determination, chemical-shift  Nuclear Magnetic Resonance. Uses magnetic fields to probe atomic environments. Provides 3D structure information and dynamics data for peptides in solution.

title: Circular Dichroism
content: **Related Terms:** nmr, secondary-structure, thermal-stability  Difference in absorption of left and right circularly polarized light. Used to assess secondary structure content (alpha-helix, beta-sheet) and thermal stability.

title: Edman Degradation
content: **Related Terms:** mass-spectrometry, sequencing, primary-structure  Sequential method for determining amino acid sequence from N-terminus. Each cycle removes and identifies one residue. Limited to ~50 residues.

title: Nanoparticle
content: **Related Terms:** liposome, drug-delivery, encapsulation  Particle 1-100 nm in size used for drug delivery. Types: polymeric, lipid, metallic. Can protect peptides from degradation and enable targeted delivery.

title: Liposome
content: **Related Terms:** nanoparticle, pegylated-liposome, drug-carrier  Spherical vesicle with phospholipid bilayer. Can encapsulate hydrophilic drugs in core and hydrophobic drugs in membrane. Used for peptide delivery.

title: PEGylation
content: **Related Terms:** half-life, clearance, polyethylene-glycol  Attachment of polyethylene glycol (PEG) to peptides/proteins. Increases half-life by reducing renal clearance and proteolytic degradation. May reduce immunogenicity.

title: Prodrug
content: **Related Terms:** bioavailability, drug-metabolism, activation  Inactive compound converted to active drug in vivo. Peptide prodrugs may improve oral bioavailability by protecting from degradation or enhancing absorption.

title: Enteric Coating
content: **Related Terms:** oral-delivery, gastric-stability, drug-formulation  pH-sensitive polymer coating that prevents drug release in stomach but allows release in intestine. Protects acid-labile peptides from gastric degradation.

title: Clinical Trial
content: **Related Terms:** fda, ind, gmp  Research study in human subjects to evaluate medical intervention. Phases: I (safety), II (efficacy), III (confirmation), IV (post-market). Required for drug approval.

title: FDA
content: **Related Terms:** clinical-trial, nda, ind  U.S. Food and Drug Administration. Regulatory agency responsible for drug approval, food safety, and medical device regulation.

title: GMP
content: **Related Terms:** fda, quality-control, manufacturing  Good Manufacturing Practice. Quality assurance guidelines ensuring drugs are consistently produced and controlled. Required for pharmaceutical manufacturing.

title: NDA
content: **Related Terms:** fda, clinical-trial, drug-approval  New Drug application. Submitted to FDA for marketing approval after clinical trials. Contains all efficacy, safety, and manufacturing data.

title: Bioequivalence
content: **Related Terms:** bioavailability, generic-drug, pharmacokinetics  Similarity in bioavailability between test and reference drug products. Criteria: 90% CI of AUC and Cmax ratios within 80-125%. Required for generic drugs.

title: Diabetes
content: **Related Terms:** insulin, glp-1, blood-glucose  Metabolic disorder characterized by high blood sugar. Type 1: autoimmune destruction of beta cells. Type 2: insulin resistance. GLP-1 analogs used in treatment.

title: Obesity
content: **Related Terms:** glp-1, appetite-regulation, metabolic-syndrome  Excessive body fat accumulation (BMI ≥30). Associated with increased risk of diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and certain cancers. GLP-1 agonists used for treatment.

title: Cancer
content: **Related Terms:** peptide-vaccine, targeted-therapy, tumor  Uncontrolled cell growth and division. Peptide-based approaches: targeted therapy, immunotherapy, peptide vaccines, and drug conjugates.

title: Neurodegeneration
content: **Related Terms:** amyloid, neuroprotection, blood-brain-barrier  Progressive loss of neuronal structure and function. Examples: Alzheimer's, Parkinson's. Peptide-based therapeutics under investigation for neuroprotection.

title: Inflammation
content: **Related Terms:** bradykinin, substance-p, cytokine  Immune response to injury or infection. Peptide mediators: bradykinin, substance P. Anti-inflammatory peptides under development for chronic inflammation.

title: Stereochemistry
content: **Related Terms:** chirality, enantiomer, diastereomer  The study of the spatial arrangement of atoms in molecules and how this affects chemical properties. In peptide chemistry, stereochemistry determines biological activity, receptor binding, and metabolic fate.

title: Enantiomer
content: **Related Terms:** chirality, stereochemistry, racemic-mixture  One of two non-superimposable mirror-image forms of a chiral molecule. L-amino acids and D-amino acids are enantiomers. Enantiomers have identical physical properties but differ in biological activity and optical rotation.

title: Diastereomer
content: **Related Terms:** enantiomer, stereochemistry, epimer  Stereoisomers that are NOT mirror images of each other. Unlike enantiomers, diastereomers have different physical properties (melting point, solubility). Multiple chiral centers in peptides create diastereomeric relationships.

title: Meso Compound
content: **Related Terms:** chirality, stereochemistry, optical-activity  A molecule with multiple chiral centers that is superimposable on its mirror image due to an internal plane of symmetry. Meso compounds are optically inactive despite having stereocenters. Rare in amino acids but relevant in modified peptide synthesis.

title: Racemic Mixture
content: **Related Terms:** enantiomer, resolution, optical-rotation  A 50:50 mixture of two enantiomers. Shows zero optical rotation. Synthetic amino acids may be racemic and require chiral resolution or asymmetric synthesis to obtain the desired L-enantiomer for peptide synthesis.

title: Epimer
content: **Related Terms:** diastereomer, stereochemistry, chiral-center  A diastereomer that differs in configuration at only one of multiple chiral centers. D-allo-threonine is an epimer of D-threonine. Epimerization can occur during peptide synthesis and affects biological activity.

title: Optical Rotation
content: **Related Terms:** chirality, enantiomer, polarimetry  The ability of a chiral molecule to rotate the plane of plane-polarized light. Measured by polarimetry. L-amino acids are levorotatory (negative rotation). Used to assess enantiomeric purity of synthetic amino acids.

title: Beta Barrel
content: **Related Terms:** beta-sheet, protein-folding, transmembrane  A protein structural motif formed by multiple beta-strands arranged in a cylindrical shape. Found in membrane proteins (porins) and some enzymes. The barrel interior can be hydrophilic while the exterior is hydrophobic.

title: Coiled Coil
content: **Related Terms:** alpha-helix, leucine-zipper, oligomerization  A structural motif where two or more alpha-helices wind around each other. Stabilized by hydrophobic interactions at the heptad repeat interface. Found in transcription factors, structural proteins, and designed peptide assemblies.

title: Zinc Finger
content: **Related Terms:** metalloprotein, dna-binding, transcription-factor  A small protein structural motif stabilized by a zinc ion coordinated by cysteine and/or histidine residues. Functions in DNA recognition and binding. Several types: Cys2His2, Cys4, Cys6. Used in engineered gene-editing proteins (ZFNs).

title: Leucine Zipper
content: **Related Terms:** coiled-coil, dimerization, transcription-factor  A dimerization motif where leucine residues at every seventh position of an alpha-helix interdigitate. Found in bZIP transcription factors (Jun, Fos, GCN4). Mediates protein-protein interactions and DNA binding.

title: Helix-Turn-Helix
content: **Related Terms:** dna-binding, motif, transcription-factor  A protein structural motif consisting of two alpha-helices connected by a short turn. The recognition helix inserts into the DNA major groove. Found in bacterial repressors and eukaryotic homeodomain proteins.

title: Beta Hairpin
content: **Related Terms:** beta-sheet, beta-turn, antiparallel  A simple protein structural motif where two antiparallel beta-strands are connected by a tight turn. Common building block in beta-sheet proteins. Turns often contain glycine and proline for conformational flexibility.

title: Disulfide Bond
content: **Related Terms:** cysteine, oxidation, stability  A covalent bond between two cysteine residues (S-S linkage). Stabilizes tertiary and quaternary structure. Can be intrachain or interchain. Critical for stability of many therapeutic peptides (e.g., insulin, oxytocin).

title: Orthogonal Protection
content: **Related Terms:** protecting-group, spps, selective-deprotection  A strategy using protecting groups that can be removed independently without affecting each other. In peptide synthesis, Fmoc (base-labile) and Boc (acid-labile) are orthogonal. Enables selective modification of specific residues.

title: Convergent Synthesis
content: **Related Terms:** fragment-condensation, peptide-ligation, linear-synthesis  A synthesis strategy where peptide fragments are prepared separately and then joined together. Advantages: each fragment can be purified, reduces cumulative loss of yield for long peptides. Disadvantage: potential epimerization at ligation sites.

title: Fragment Condensation
content: **Related Terms:** convergent-synthesis, peptide-ligation, coupling  The joining of two protected peptide fragments to form a larger peptide. Typically performed in solution. Requires C-terminal activation without epimerization. Preferred for peptides >50 residues where SPPS yield drops.

title: Peptide Ligation
content: **Related Terms:** native-chemical-ligation, expressed-protein-ligation, chemoselective  Chemoselective reaction joining unprotected peptide fragments. Native chemical ligation (NCL) joins C-terminal thioester with N-terminal cysteine. Enables synthesis of proteins from recombinant and synthetic fragments.

title: Native Chemical Ligation (NCL)
content: **Related Terms:** peptide-ligation, thioester, cysteine  A chemoselective reaction between a C-terminal thioester and an N-terminal cysteine, forming a native peptide bond. Developed by Kent and Dawson (1994). Gold standard for protein total synthesis and semi-synthesis.

title: Solid-Phase Synthesis Resin
content: **Related Terms:** spps, linker, rink-amide, wang-resin  The insoluble polymer support for solid-phase peptide synthesis. Rink amide resin yields C-terminal amides; Wang resin yields C-terminal acids. Choice of resin determines C-terminal modification and cleavage conditions.

title: Therapeutic Window
content: **Related Terms:** toxic-dose, effective-dose, safety-margin  The range of drug concentrations between the minimum effective concentration and the minimum toxic concentration. A narrow therapeutic window requires careful dose monitoring. Peptide drugs often have wide therapeutic windows due to specificity.

title: Dose-Response Curve
content: **Related Terms:** ec50, ic50, sigmoidal-curve  A graph showing the relationship between drug concentration and biological effect. Typically sigmoidal (S-shaped). Parameters: EC50 (potency), Emax (efficacy), Hill slope (cooperativity). Used to characterize peptide agonists and antagonists.

title: EC50
content: **Related Terms:** ic50, dose-response, potency  The concentration of drug that produces 50% of the maximum effective response. Measure of drug potency. Lower EC50 = higher potency. Used to compare peptide analogs and optimize lead compounds.

title: IC50
content: **Related Terms:** ec50, inhibition, potency  The concentration of inhibitor that reduces a biological process by 50%. Measure of inhibitory potency. Commonly measured for enzyme inhibitors, receptor antagonists, and peptide-based therapeutics.

title: Ki (Inhibition Constant)
content: **Related Terms:** ic50, binding-affinity, dissociation-constant  The equilibrium dissociation constant for an enzyme-inhibitor complex. Measure of binding affinity independent of assay conditions. Lower Ki = tighter binding. Related to IC50 by the Cheng-Prusoff equation.

title: Pharmacodynamics
content: **Related Terms:** pharmacokinetics, mechanism-of-action, dose-response  The study of the biochemical and physiological effects of drugs on the body. Answers: what does the drug do to the body? Involves receptor binding, dose-response relationships, and therapeutic effects.

title: Bioconjugation
content: **Related Terms:** antibody-drug-conjugate, pegylation, click-chemistry  The chemical joining of two molecules, typically a biomolecule (peptide, protein) with another functional molecule (drug, fluorophore, polymer). Common chemistries: NHS esters, maleimides, click chemistry, thiol-maleimide.

title: Antibody-Drug Conjugate (ADC)
content: **Related Terms:** bioconjugation, targeted-therapy, cytotoxic-payload  A targeted therapeutic consisting of a monoclonal antibody linked to a cytotoxic drug via a chemical linker. Antibody provides specificity; drug provides potency. Examples: ado-trastuzumab emtansine (Kadcyla), brentuximab vedotin (Adcetris).

title: PROTAC (Proteolysis-Targeting Chimera)
content: **Related Terms:** ubiquitin-proteasome, e3-ligase, targeted-degradation  A bifunctional molecule that recruits an E3 ubiquitin ligase to a target protein, inducing its ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation. One end binds the target; the other binds E3 ligase (e.g., cereblon, VHL). Catalytic mechanism allows sub-stoichiometric activity.

title: Molecular Glue
content: **Related Terms:** protac, protein-protein-interaction, degradation  A small molecule that stabilizes or creates a protein-protein interaction, often between a target protein and an E3 ligase, leading to target degradation. Examples: thalidomide and analogs (lenalidomide, pomalidomide) recruit neosubstrates to cereblon.

title: Cell-Penetrating Peptide (CPP)
content: **Related Terms:** drug-delivery, membrane-permeability, translocation  A short peptide (5-30 residues) that can cross cell membranes. Examples: TAT peptide, penetratin, polyarginine. Used to deliver cargo (peptides, proteins, nucleic acids) into cells. Mechanisms include direct translocation and endocytosis.

title: Collision-Induced Dissociation (CID)
content: **Related Terms:** tandem-mass-spectrometry, fragment-ion, peptide-sequencing  A fragmentation method in tandem mass spectrometry where peptide ions collide with inert gas molecules, breaking peptide bonds. Produces b-ions (N-terminal fragments) and y-ions (C-terminal fragments). Used for peptide sequencing and identification.

title: Precursor Ion
content: **Related Terms:** tandem-mass-spectrometry, fragment-ion, parent-ion  The intact peptide ion selected for fragmentation in tandem MS (MS/MS). Isolated in the first mass analyzer, fragmented by CID or other methods, and fragment ions are analyzed in the second mass analyzer. m/z value used for database searching.

title: Fragment Ion
content: **Related Terms:** collision-induced-dissociation, b-ion, y-ion  Ions produced from fragmentation of the precursor ion in tandem MS. Named by cleavage position: a/b/c ions (N-terminal side) and x/y/z ions (C-terminal side). The mass differences between consecutive fragment ions reveal the amino acid sequence.

title: De Novo Sequencing
content: **Related Terms:** tandem-mass-spectrometry, peptide-identification, database-search  Determination of peptide amino acid sequence from MS/MS spectra without database matching. Uses fragment ion mass differences to deduce the sequence. Useful for novel peptides, modified peptides, or organisms without reference genomes.

title: Tandem Mass Spectrometry (MS/MS)
content: **Related Terms:** collision-induced-dissociation, precursor-ion, peptide-mapping  A two-stage mass analysis technique. First MS selects precursor ions, which are fragmented, then second MS analyzes fragment ions. Provides sequence information, post-translational modification sites, and structural characterization of peptides.

title: Peptide Mass Fingerprinting
content: **Related Terms:** mass-spectrometry, proteomics, protein-identification  Protein identification by matching observed peptide masses (from proteolytic digestion) against theoretical masses from a database. Typically uses MALDI-TOF MS. Fast and reliable for proteins with database entries.

title: Proteasome
content: **Related Terms:** ubiquitin-proteasome-system, protein-degradation, 26s-proteasome  A large multi-subunit protease complex that degrades ubiquitinated proteins. The 26S proteasome consists of a 20S catalytic core and 19S regulatory caps. Generates peptides (8-10 residues) for MHC class I antigen presentation.

title: Ubiquitin-Proteasome System (UPS)
content: **Related Terms:** proteasome, ubiquitin, e3-ligase  The major pathway for regulated intracellular protein degradation. Proteins tagged with ubiquitin chains are recognized and degraded by the 26S proteasome. Involves E1 (activation), E2 (conjugation), and E3 (ligation) enzymes. Therapeutic target for cancer and neurodegeneration.

title: Autophagy
content: **Related Terms:** lysosome, proteasome, cellular-stress-response  A cellular degradation pathway that delivers cytoplasmic contents (proteins, organelles) to lysosomes for recycling. Types: macroautophagy, microautophagy, chaperone-mediated autophagy. Induced by starvation and cellular stress. Peptides can modulate autophagy for therapeutic benefit.

title: Apoptosis
content: **Related Terms:** caspase, programmed-cell-death, bcl-2  Programmed cell death characterized by cell shrinkage, chromatin condensation, DNA fragmentation, and membrane blebbing. Mediated by caspases (cysteine proteases). Two pathways: intrinsic (mitochondrial) and extrinsic (death receptor). Peptide-based caspase inhibitors used in research and therapy.

title: Protease
content: **Related Terms:** proteolysis, enzyme, substrate-specificity  An enzyme that cleaves peptide bonds. Types: serine proteases, cysteine proteases, aspartyl proteases, metalloproteases. Responsible for peptide degradation in vivo. Protease inhibitors are important therapeutic peptides (e.g., HIV protease inhibitors).

title: Biomarker Validation
content: **Related Terms:** biomarker, clinical-endpoint, surrogate-endpoint  The process of establishing that a biomarker reliably and accurately measures a biological process or predicts clinical outcome. Phases: analytical validation, clinical validation, clinical utility. Critical for companion diagnostics and precision medicine.

title: Surrogate Endpoint
content: **Related Terms:** clinical-endpoint, biomarker, fda-approval  A laboratory measurement or physical sign used as a substitute for a clinical endpoint. Must be reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit. Examples: HbA1c for diabetes, tumor shrinkage for cancer. Can accelerate drug approval under accelerated approval pathway.

title: Clinical Endpoint
content: **Related Terms:** surrogate-endpoint, efficacy, outcome-measure  A characteristic or variable that reflects how a patient feels, functions, or survives. Direct measure of clinical benefit. Examples: overall survival, progression-free survival, symptom improvement. Preferred for full drug approval but requires larger, longer trials.

title: Hazard Ratio
content: **Related Terms:** survival-analysis, cox-regression, clinical-trial  The ratio of hazard rates between treatment and control groups. HR < 1 indicates treatment reduces risk of event (e.g., death). Used in time-to-event analyses (survival curves). Confidence interval and p-value assess statistical significance.

title: p-Value
content: **Related Terms:** statistical-significance, null-hypothesis, confidence-interval  The probability of observing results as extreme as those obtained, assuming the null hypothesis is true. p < 0.05 is conventional threshold for statistical significance. Does NOT indicate effect size or clinical significance.

title: 510(k)
content: **Related Terms:** fda, medical-device, premarket-notification  A premarket submission to FDA demonstrating that a medical device is substantially equivalent to a legally marketed predicate device. Faster than PMA. Required for Class I and II devices. Does not require clinical trial data in most cases.

title: PMA (Premarket Approval)
content: **Related Terms:** fda, medical-device, clinical-trial  The most stringent FDA device approval pathway for Class III (high-risk) devices. Requires scientific evidence of safety and effectiveness, including clinical trial data. Longer review time than 510(k). Examples: implantable devices, diagnostic tests for serious conditions.

title: CE Marking
content: **Related Terms:** eu-mdr, conformity-assessment, european-market  A certification mark indicating conformity with EU health, safety, and environmental protection standards. Required for products sold in the European Economic Area. Based on EU Medical Device Regulation (MDR 2017/745) for medical devices.

title: ISO 13485
content: **Related Terms:** quality-management-system, medical-device, gmp  An international standard for quality management systems specific to medical devices. Based on ISO 9001 but with additional requirements for medical devices. Covers design, development, production, installation, and servicing. Required for CE marking and many regulatory approvals.

title: Accelerated Approval
content: **Related Terms:** fda, surrogate-endpoint, unmet-medical-need  An FDA approval pathway allowing earlier approval of drugs for serious conditions that fill unmet medical needs. Based on surrogate endpoints reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit. Requires post-marketing confirmatory trials. Can be withdrawn if benefit not confirmed.

title: Prion
content: **Related Terms:** amyloid, neurodegeneration, misfolded-protein  A misfolded form of the prion protein (PrPSc) that can convert normal PrPC to the pathogenic form. Causes transmissible spongiform encephalopathies (TSEs): Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease, mad cow disease, kuru. Resistant to normal sterilization methods. No effective treatment exists.

title: Amyloid Beta
content: **Related Terms:** alzheimers-disease, amyloid-plaque, aggregation  A peptide (36-43 amino acids) cleaved from amyloid precursor protein (APP) by beta- and gamma-secretase. Aβ42 is particularly aggregation-prone. Forms amyloid plaques in Alzheimer's disease brain. Target of anti-amyloid immunotherapies (aducanumab, lecanemab).

title: Tau Protein
content: **Related Terms:** alzheimers-disease, neurofibrillary-tangle, microtubule  A microtubule-associated protein highly expressed in neurons. Hyperphosphorylated tau forms neurofibrillary tangles in Alzheimer's disease and other tauopathies (Pick disease, progressive supranuclear palsy). Spreading of misfolded tau follows prion-like mechanisms.

title: Alpha-Synuclein
content: **Related Terms:** parkinsons-disease, lewy-body, aggregation  A presynaptic protein of 140 amino acids. Misfolding and aggregation into Lewy bodies is the hallmark of Parkinson's disease and other synucleinopathies (dementia with Lewy bodies, multiple system atrophy). Prion-like propagation between neurons has been demonstrated.

title: Transthyretin (TTR)
content: **Related Terms:** amyloidosis, protein-misfolding, tetramer  A transport protein for thyroxine and retinol-binding protein. Tetramer dissociation and misfolding leads to transthyretin amyloidosis (ATTR) affecting heart and peripheral nerves. Stabilizers (tafamidis, diflunisal) and gene silencers (patisiran, inotersen) are approved therapies.

title: Expression Vector
content: **Related Terms:** transformation, promoter, selection-marker  A DNA molecule used to introduce and express a gene in a host cell. Contains elements for replication, transcription, and translation including promoter, origin of replication, selection marker, and cloning site. Used to produce recombinant peptides and proteins in bacteria, yeast, or mammalian cells.

title: Transformation
content: **Related Terms:** expression-vector, transfection, competent-cell  The process of introducing foreign DNA into a bacterial cell. Methods include heat shock (CaCl2 treatment) and electroporation. Transformants are identified using selection markers on the introduced plasmid.

title: Transfection
content: **Related Terms:** transformation, expression-vector, mammalian-cell-culture  The process of introducing nucleic acids into eukaryotic cells. Methods include lipofection, electroporation, and viral transduction. Used for recombinant protein production in mammalian and insect cell systems.

title: Selection Marker
content: **Related Terms:** expression-vector, transformation, antibiotic-resistance  A gene on an expression vector that allows identification of cells containing the vector. Common markers: antibiotic resistance genes (ampicillin, kanamycin), auxotrophic complementation (HIS3, LEU2), or fluorescent proteins.

title: Promoter
content: **Related Terms:** expression-vector, transcription, gene-expression  A DNA sequence that initiates transcription of a gene. Controls when, where, and how much protein is produced. Types: constitutive (CMV, SV40), inducible (T7, tet-on), tissue-specific. Choice affects expression level and regulation.

title: Sequence Alignment
content: **Related Terms:** homology, multiple-alignment, blast  Arrangement of two or more sequences to identify regions of similarity. Types: global (Needleman-Wunsch) and local (Smith-Waterman). Used to infer functional, structural, or evolutionary relationships between proteins.

title: Homology
content: **Related Terms:** sequence-alignment, evolutionary-relationship, ortholog  Similarity between sequences due to shared ancestry. Homologous proteins may have similar structure and function. Types: orthologs (different species, same gene) and paralogs (same species, gene duplication). Homology is inferred from sequence similarity but is a qualitative statement.

title: E-Value
content: **Related Terms:** bit-score, blast, statistical-significance  The expected number of database hits with a given score or better, assuming random sequences. Lower E-values indicate more significant matches. E-value < 0.001 is typically considered significant for homology detection. Depends on database size and scoring matrix.

title: Bit Score
content: **Related Terms:** e-value, sequence-alignment, scoring-matrix  A normalized score derived from the raw alignment score using the substitution matrix and gap penalties. Higher bit scores indicate better alignments. Independent of database size, unlike E-values. Used to compare alignment quality across different searches.

title: Multiple Alignment
content: **Related Terms:** sequence-alignment, phylogenetic-tree, conserved-residue  Alignment of three or more sequences simultaneously. Reveals conserved regions, evolutionary patterns, and family relationships. Tools: Clustal Omega, MUSCLE, MAFFT. Used for phylogenetic analysis, primer design, and identifying functionally important residues.

title: Hydrogel
content: **Related Terms:** scaffold, biomaterial, swelling  A three-dimensional network of hydrophilic polymers that can absorb large amounts of water (10-99% water content). Used for drug delivery, tissue engineering, and wound healing. Peptide-functionalized hydrogels can mimic extracellular matrix properties.

title: Scaffold
content: **Related Terms:** hydrogel, biomaterial, tissue-engineering  A three-dimensional structure that provides mechanical support and biological signals for cell attachment and tissue formation. Materials: natural (collagen, fibrin) or synthetic (PLGA, PEG). Can be loaded with peptides for cell signaling.

title: Biomaterial
content: **Related Terms:** biocompatibility, scaffold, implant  A material designed to interact with biological systems for medical purposes. Types: metals, ceramics, polymers, and composites. Must be biocompatible and meet mechanical requirements. Peptide coatings can improve biomaterial integration with host tissue.

title: Biocompatibility
content: **Related Terms:** biomaterial, cytotoxicity, immunogenicity  The ability of a material to perform with an appropriate host response in a specific application. Evaluated by cytotoxicity, sensitization, irritation, and systemic toxicity tests. Peptide modifications can enhance biocompatibility of synthetic materials.

title: Biodegradation
content: **Related Terms:** biomaterial, hydrolysis, erosion  The breakdown of materials by biological agents (enzymes, microorganisms) or chemical processes (hydrolysis, oxidation). Rate of biodegradation must match tissue regeneration rate for scaffolds. Peptide-based materials can be designed with tunable degradation rates.

title: Nanoparticle
content: **Related Terms:** nanofiber, liposome, drug-delivery  A particle with at least one dimension between 1-100 nm. Types: polymeric, metallic (gold, silver), lipid, and silica. Properties differ from bulk material due to surface area-to-volume ratio and quantum effects. Used for drug delivery, imaging, and sensing.

title: Nanofiber
content: **Related Terms:** nanoparticle, scaffold, electrospinning  A fiber with diameter in the nanometer range (typically 10-1000 nm). Produced by electrospinning, self-assembly, or phase separation. High surface area and porosity make them ideal for tissue scaffolds, filters, and drug delivery. Peptide nanofibers can mimic extracellular matrix.

title: Nanotube
content: **Related Terms:** nanoparticle, carbon-nanotube, self-assembly  A cylindrical nanostructure. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are the most common type. Peptide nanotubes form by self-assembly of cyclic peptides with alternating D and L amino acids. Used in drug delivery, biosensing, and as templates for nanofabrication.

title: Quantum Dot
content: **Related Terms:** nanoparticle, fluorescence, imaging  Semiconductor nanocrystals (2-10 nm) with size-dependent optical properties due to quantum confinement. Emit narrow, tunable fluorescence with high photostability. Used for multiplexed imaging and tracking. Peptide-conjugated quantum dots enable targeted cell labeling.

title: Liposome
content: **Related Terms:** nanoparticle, drug-delivery, lipid-bilayer  A spherical vesicle composed of one or more phospholipid bilayers enclosing an aqueous core. Can encapsulate hydrophilic drugs in the core and hydrophobic drugs in the bilayer. PEGylated liposomes (stealth liposomes) have extended circulation time.

title: Fluorescence
content: **Related Terms:** phosphorescence, fret, fluorophore  Emission of light by a molecule after absorbing photons. The emitted photon has lower energy (longer wavelength) than the absorbed photon. Fluorescent labels (FITC, rhodamine, GFP) attached to peptides enable visualization of cellular processes.

title: Phosphorescence
content: **Related Terms:** fluorescence, triplet-state, luminescence  Emission of light from an excited triplet state after intersystem crossing from the singlet excited state. Longer lifetime than fluorescence (microseconds to seconds). Useful for time-gated imaging to eliminate background autofluorescence.

title: FRET (Förster Resonance Energy Transfer)
content: **Related Terms:** fret, bret, donor-acceptor  Non-radiative energy transfer between two fluorophores (donor and acceptor) when in close proximity (1-10 nm). Efficiency depends on distance (1/r6). Used to measure protein-protein interactions, conformational changes, and enzyme activity in real time.

title: BRET (Bioluminescence Resonance Energy Transfer)
content: **Related Terms:** fret, bioluminescence, luciferase  Energy transfer from a bioluminescent donor (luciferase) to a fluorescent acceptor. No external excitation light required, reducing background. Used to study protein-protein interactions in living cells. NanoLuc luciferase is commonly used as donor.

title: Bioluminescence
content: **Related Terms:** fluorescence, luciferase, chemiluminescence  Light production by living organisms through enzyme-catalyzed reactions. Luciferase enzymes oxidize luciferin substrates to produce light. Used as a reporter gene in molecular biology and for in vivo imaging of gene expression and cell tracking.

title: Chemical Shift
content: **Related Terms:** nmr-spectroscopy, ppm, shielding  The resonance frequency of a nucleus relative to a reference standard, expressed in parts per million (ppm). Reflects the electronic environment around the nucleus. In peptide NMR, 1H and 13C chemical shifts provide information about secondary structure and hydrogen bonding.

title: Coupling Constant (J-Coupling)
content: **Related Terms:** chemical-shift, nmr-spectroscopy, spin-spin-coupling  The splitting of NMR signals due to through-bond interactions between neighboring nuclei. Measured in Hz. 3J coupling constants (Karplus equation) relate to dihedral angles and are used to determine peptide backbone conformation.

title: NOE (Nuclear Overhauser Effect)
content: **Related Terms:** nmr-spectroscopy, noe-distance, structure-determination  Through-space dipolar coupling between nuclei that are close in space (<5 Å). NOE intensity is proportional to 1/r6, providing distance restraints for 3D structure determination. NOESY experiments are essential for NMR-based peptide structure elucidation.

title: Relaxation
content: **Related Terms:** nmr-spectroscopy, t1, t2  The return of excited nuclear spins to equilibrium after radiofrequency excitation. T1 (spin-lattice) relaxation involves energy transfer to the environment. T2 (spin-spin) relaxation involves loss of coherence. Provides information about molecular dynamics and flexibility.

title: Diffusion
content: **Related Terms:** nmr-spectroscopy, diffusion-coefficient, molecular-size  The random movement of molecules in solution. Measured by diffusion-ordered spectroscopy (DOSY) in NMR. Diffusion coefficient relates to molecular size and shape through the Stokes-Einstein equation. Used to estimate peptide oligomerization state.

title: Retention Time
content: **Related Terms:** selectivity, resolution, hplc  The time elapsed between injection of a sample and detection of a peak at the detector. Depends on analyte-stationary phase interactions. Used for qualitative identification when compared to standards under identical conditions.

title: Selectivity
content: **Related Terms:** retention-time, resolution, stationary-phase  The ability of a chromatographic system to distinguish between two analytes. Quantified by the selectivity factor (alpha) = ratio of retention factors. Affected by stationary phase chemistry, mobile phase composition, and temperature.

title: Resolution
content: **Related Terms:** selectivity, retention-time, peak-width  A measure of how well two adjacent peaks are separated. Rs = 2(tR2 - tR1)/(w1 + w2). Resolution >= 1.5 indicates baseline separation. Affected by efficiency (plate count), selectivity, and retention. Critical for peptide purification.

title: Plate Count (N)
content: **Related Terms:** resolution, peak-width, column-efficiency  A measure of column efficiency. N = 16(tR/w)2 or N = 5.54(tR/w0.5)2. Higher plate count means narrower peaks and better separation. Affected by particle size, column length, flow rate, and diffusion coefficients of analytes.

title: Peak Capacity
content: **Related Terms:** resolution, gradient-elution, proteomics  The maximum number of peaks that can be resolved with a specified resolution in a given time window. Increases with gradient time and column efficiency. Critical for proteomics applications where thousands of peptides must be separated.

title: m/z (Mass-to-Charge Ratio)
content: **Related Terms:** charge-state, mass-spectrum, ion  The mass of an ion divided by its charge state. The fundamental measurement in mass spectrometry. For peptides, multiply-charged ions (z > 1) are common in electrospray ionization. The mass can be calculated from multiple charge states.

title: Charge State
content: **Related Terms:** m/z, electrospray-ionization, multiply-charged-ion  The number of charges (protons) carried by an ion. In electrospray ionization, peptides typically acquire multiple charges (z = 2-5+). Higher charge states produce lower m/z values. Charge state distribution reflects peptide structure and solution conditions.

title: Isotope Pattern
content: **Related Terms:** m/z, monoisotopic-peak, mass-accuracy  The distribution of peaks resulting from natural isotopic abundance (13C, 15N, 18O, 34S). For peptides, the monoisotopic peak (all light isotopes) and isotope envelope provide information about molecular formula and charge state.

title: Fragmentation
content: **Related Terms:** collision-induced-dissociation, b-ion, y-ion, tandem-ms  The breaking of chemical bonds in ions to produce smaller fragment ions. In peptide MS, fragmentation primarily occurs at peptide bonds producing b-ions (N-terminal) and y-ions (C-terminal). Fragmentation patterns reveal amino acid sequence.

title: Collision Energy
content: **Related Terms:** collision-induced-dissociation, fragmentation, normalized-collision-energy  The kinetic energy converted to internal energy during ion-molecule collisions in CID. Higher collision energy produces more extensive fragmentation. Normalized collision energy (NCE) accounts for charge state and m/z. Optimal energy balances sequence coverage and signal intensity.

title: Enthalpy (ΔH)
content: **Related Terms:** entropy, free-energy, calorimetry  The heat absorbed or released during a process at constant pressure. Measured by isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC). For peptide binding reactions, enthalpy reflects hydrogen bonding, van der Waals, and electrostatic interactions. Negative ΔH indicates exothermic binding.

title: Entropy (ΔS)
content: **Related Terms:** enthalpy, free-energy, disorder  A measure of the disorder or randomness of a system. Positive ΔS increases favorability of binding (TΔS contribution). Hydrophobic interactions are entropy-driven at room temperature. Conformational entropy loss occurs when peptides adopt ordered structures upon binding.

title: Free Energy (ΔG)
content: **Related Terms:** enthalpy, entropy, equilibrium-constant  The thermodynamic potential that determines spontaneity: ΔG = ΔH - TΔS. Negative ΔG indicates a spontaneous process. Related to equilibrium constant by ΔG = -RT ln K. Binding free energy determines affinity of peptide-protein interactions.

title: Equilibrium Constant (K)
content: **Related Terms:** free-energy, dissociation-constant, association-constant  The ratio of product to reactant concentrations at equilibrium. For binding: Ka = [AB]/[A][B]. Larger K indicates stronger binding. Related to free energy by ΔG = -RT ln K. Kd (dissociation constant) = 1/Ka is commonly used for peptide-receptor binding.

title: Van't Hoff Analysis
content: **Related Terms:** equilibrium-constant, enthalpy, temperature-dependence  A method to determine binding enthalpy from the temperature dependence of the equilibrium constant. Plot of ln K vs 1/T gives slope = -ΔH/R. Assumption of temperature-independent ΔH may not hold for peptide-protein interactions with large heat capacity changes.

title: Rate Constant (k)
content: **Related Terms:** reaction-rate, activation-energy, arrhenius  The proportionality constant between reaction rate and reactant concentrations. Units depend on reaction order (s-1 for first order, M-1s-1 for second order). For peptide-protein binding: kon (association) and koff (dissociation) rate constants determine affinity (Kd = koff/kon).

title: Activation Energy (Ea)
content: **Related Terms:** arrhenius, rate-constant, transition-state  The minimum energy required for a reaction to occur. Higher activation energy means slower reaction rate at a given temperature. Enzymes lower activation energy of peptide bond hydrolysis. Measured from temperature dependence of rate constants.

title: Arrhenius Equation
content: **Related Terms:** activation-energy, rate-constant, temperature-dependence  Relates rate constant to temperature: k = A·e^(-Ea/RT), where A is the pre-exponential factor and Ea is activation energy. Plot of ln k vs 1/T gives slope = -Ea/R. Used to predict reaction rates at different temperatures for peptide degradation and synthesis.

title: Michaelis-Menten Kinetics
content: **Related Terms:** catalytic-efficiency, vmax, km  A model describing enzyme-catalyzed reaction rates. v = Vmax[S]/(Km + [S]). Parameters: Vmax (maximum rate), Km (substrate concentration at half-Vmax). Used to characterize protease activity on peptide substrates and to compare enzyme variants.

title: Catalytic Efficiency (kcat/Km)
content: **Related Terms:** michaelis-menten, rate-constant, enzyme-kinetics  The second-order rate constant for enzyme action at low substrate concentrations. kcat/Km combines catalytic rate (kcat) and substrate binding (Km). Upper limit is diffusion-controlled (~108-109 M-1s-1). Used to evaluate protease specificity for different peptide substrates.

title: LD50 (Lethal Dose 50%)
content: **Related Terms:** noael, loael, toxicity, therapeutic-index  The dose of a substance that kills 50% of a test population. Used as a measure of acute toxicity. Determined in animal studies during preclinical development. Lower LD50 values indicate higher toxicity.

title: NOAEL (No Observed Adverse Effect Level)
content: **Related Terms:** loael, therapeutic-index, safety-margin  The highest dose at which no adverse effects are observed in a study. Used to set human starting doses for clinical trials. Determined from repeat-dose toxicity studies in two species (typically rodent and non-rodent).

title: LOAEL (Lowest Observed Adverse Effect Level)
content: **Related Terms:** noael, toxicity, dose-response  The lowest dose at which adverse effects are observed. Always higher than or equal to NOAEL. Used when NOAEL cannot be determined. Helps establish dose-response relationships for safety assessment.

title: Therapeutic Index
content: **Related Terms:** ld50, ed50, safety-margin  Ratio of toxic dose to therapeutic dose (TD50/ED50). A higher therapeutic index indicates a wider margin of safety. Peptide drugs generally have favorable therapeutic indices due to high target specificity and predictable clearance.

title: Adverse Event
content: **Related Terms:** adverse-reaction, side-effect, safety-monitoring  Any untoward medical occurrence in a patient administered a pharmaceutical product, regardless of causality. Monitored throughout clinical development and post-market surveillance. Graded by severity using CTCAE criteria.

title: Toxicity
content: **Related Terms:** ld50, noael, adverse-event  The degree to which a substance can cause damage to an organism. Types include acute (single dose), chronic (repeated dose), organ-specific (hepatotoxicity, nephrotoxicity), and systemic. Assessed through in vitro and in vivo studies.

title: Genotoxicity
content: **Related Terms:** mutagenicity, carcinogenicity, ames-test  The ability of a substance to damage DNA, causing mutations or chromosomal aberrations. Assessed by Ames test, micronucleus assay, and chromosomal aberration tests. Positive genotoxicity results are a major red flag in drug development.

title: Carcinogenicity
content: **Related Terms:** genotoxicity, tumor, chronic-toxicity  The ability of a substance to cause cancer. Assessed in 2-year rodent bioassays. Required for drugs intended for chronic use. Peptide drugs are generally considered low risk due to rapid metabolism and degradation to natural amino acids.

title: Teratogenicity
content: **Related Terms:** developmental-toxicity, embryo, fetus  The ability of a substance to cause birth defects during fetal development. Assessed in embryo-fetal development studies in two species. Pregnancy category assigned based on animal and human data. Most peptide drugs lack teratogenic potential.

title: Immunotoxicity
content: **Related Terms:** immunogenicity, hypersensitivity, immune-suppression  Adverse effects on the immune system including immunosuppression, immunostimulation, or hypersensitivity. Peptide therapeutics may elicit anti-drug antibodies (ADAs) that reduce efficacy or cause immune-mediated adverse reactions.

title: Excipient
content: **Related Terms:** formulation, preservative, stabilizer  An inactive ingredient added to a drug formulation for purposes such as stability, delivery, or patient acceptability. Common excipients for peptide drugs: mannitol (tonicity), polysorbate 80 (surfactant), histidine (buffer).

title: Preservative
content: **Related Terms:** excipient, antimicrobial, multi-dose  An agent added to prevent microbial growth in multi-dose formulations. Examples: phenol, m-cresol, benzyl alcohol. Not required in single-use vials. Preservative-free formulations preferred for reduced injection site reactions.

title: Surfactant
content: **Related Terms:** excipient, aggregation, stability  A surface-active agent that reduces interfacial tension. In peptide formulations, surfactants (polysorbate 20, polysorbate 80) prevent aggregation and adsorption to container surfaces. Critical for maintaining stability of liquid peptide formulations.

title: Buffer
content: **Related Terms:** ph, excipient, stability  A solution that resists changes in pH when acid or base is added. Peptide formulations typically buffered at pH 4-7 depending on peptide stability. Common buffers: phosphate, acetate, histidine, citrate. pH affects peptide charge, solubility, and degradation rate.

title: Lyophilization
content: **Related Terms:** freeze-drying, reconstitution, stability  Freeze-drying process that removes water from a frozen product by sublimation. Produces a stable dry powder. Common for peptide drugs to improve shelf life. Requires reconstitution before administration. Cryoprotectants (trehalose, sucrose) added to protect during freezing.

title: Reconstitution
content: **Related Terms:** lyophilization, diluent, parenteral  The process of dissolving a lyophilized powder in a suitable diluent to prepare an injectable solution. Diluent choice affects concentration, pH, and osmolarity. Reconstituted products have limited stability and must be used within specified timeframes.

title: Aggregation
content: **Related Terms:** fibrillation, stability, surfactant  The association of peptide molecules into higher-order species (dimers, oligomers, insoluble particles). Driven by hydrophobic interactions, concentration, temperature, and agitation. Aggregates can reduce potency and increase immunogenicity risk.

title: Fibrillation
content: **Related Terms:** aggregation, amyloid, stability  The formation of ordered fibrillar aggregates with cross-beta structure. Some peptides (e.g., insulin, GLP-1 analogs) are prone to fibrillation under certain conditions. Fibrils can be immunogenic and are a significant formulation challenge.

title: Stability
content: **Related Terms:** shelf-life, degradation, formulation  The ability of a drug product to maintain its chemical, physical, and microbiological properties within specified limits throughout its shelf life. Stability testing follows ICH guidelines (Q1A-Q1E). Parameters: assay, purity, pH, appearance, particulate matter.

title: Shelf Life
content: **Related Terms:** stability, expiration-date, ich  The period during which a drug product is expected to remain within approved specifications when stored under recommended conditions. Determined from real-time and accelerated stability studies. Expiration date marks the end of shelf life.

title: Phase I Trial
content: **Related Terms:** phase-ii, safety, first-in-human  The first stage of testing in human subjects. Typically 20-100 healthy volunteers or patients. Primary objective: safety, tolerability, and pharmacokinetics. Determines maximum tolerated dose (MTD) and dose-limiting toxicities. Duration: several months.

title: Phase II Trial
content: **Related Terms:** phase-i, phase-iii, efficacy  Second stage of clinical testing in a larger patient population (100-300). Evaluates therapeutic efficacy, optimal dosing, and side effects. Often randomized, controlled trials. Failure rate is high (~70%). Duration: months to 2 years.

title: Phase III Trial
content: **Related Terms:** phase-ii, pivotal, registration  Large-scale efficacy and safety studies (300-3000+ patients) required for regulatory approval. Randomized, controlled, often multi-center and multi-national. Provides definitive evidence of benefit-risk profile. Duration: 1-4 years. Basis for NDA/BLA submission.

title: Phase IV Trial
content: **Related Terms:** post-market, pharmacovigilance, safety-monitoring  Post-marketing studies conducted after drug approval. Monitor long-term safety, rare adverse events, drug interactions, and outcomes in special populations. May be required by regulatory agencies. Can lead to label changes or withdrawal.

title: Informed Consent
content: **Related Terms:** ethics, irb, clinical-trial  The process of providing potential trial participants with all relevant information about a study before they agree to participate. Must include purpose, procedures, risks, benefits, and alternatives. Documented by signed consent form. Required by GCP and the Declaration of Helsinki.

title: Randomization
content: **Related Terms:** controlled-trial, bias, stratification  The process of assigning trial participants to treatment groups by chance. Minimizes selection bias and balances known and unknown confounding factors. Methods: simple, block, stratified, adaptive. Essential for establishing causal relationships.

title: Blinding (Masking)
content: **Related Terms:** double-blind, placebo, bias  A procedure to prevent knowledge of treatment assignment from influencing study outcomes. Single-blind: participant unaware. Double-blind: participant and investigator unaware. Triple-blind: participant, investigator, and analyst unaware. Reduces observation and reporting bias.

title: Endpoint
content: **Related Terms:** primary-endpoint, surrogate-endpoint, outcome-measure  A precisely defined variable intended to measure an outcome of interest. Primary endpoint is the main result measured to determine efficacy. Secondary endpoints provide additional information. Choice of endpoint is critical for trial design and regulatory acceptance.

title: Statistical Significance
content: **Related Terms:** p-value, confidence-interval, null-hypothesis  The likelihood that a result is not due to chance alone. Conventionally defined as p < 0.05. Does not imply clinical significance. Dependent on sample size and effect size. Must be interpreted alongside confidence intervals and clinical relevance.

title: Confidence Interval
content: **Related Terms:** p-value, statistical-significance, precision  A range of values that is likely to contain the true population parameter with a specified probability (typically 95%). Narrower intervals indicate greater precision. If 95% CI for a treatment effect does not include the null value, the result is statistically significant at p < 0.05.

title: FDA (Food and Drug Administration)
content: **Related Terms:** ema, nda, ind  U.S. federal agency responsible for protecting public health by regulating drugs, biologics, medical devices, food, and cosmetics. Reviews and approves drug applications. Headquartered in Silver Spring, Maryland. Commissioner appointed by the President.

title: EMA (European Medicines Agency)
content: **Related Terms:** fda, chmp, centralized-procedure  EU regulatory agency responsible for evaluation and supervision of medicinal products. Coordinates scientific evaluation of applications through its committees. Headquartered in Amsterdam. Does not approve drugs directly; the European Commission makes final decisions.

title: IND (Investigational New Drug Application)
content: **Related Terms:** fda, nda, clinical-trial  An application submitted to FDA to begin clinical testing of a new drug in humans. Must include preclinical data, manufacturing information, and clinical protocol. FDA has 30 days to place a clinical hold if safety concerns exist.

title: NDA (New Drug Application)
content: **Related Terms:** fda, bla, drug-approval  The formal request to FDA for approval to market a new drug in the United States. Contains all preclinical and clinical data, manufacturing details, and labeling. Standard review: 10 months. Priority review: 6 months.

title: GMP (Good Manufacturing Practice)
content: **Related Terms:** gcp, glp, quality-control  Regulations ensuring drugs are consistently produced and controlled according to quality standards. Covers facilities, equipment, personnel, documentation, and processes. Violations can result in warning letters, consent decrees, or product seizure.

title: GCP (Good Clinical Practice)
content: **Related Terms:** gmp, glp, ich  International ethical and scientific quality standard for designing, conducting, recording, and reporting clinical trials. Ensures data integrity and subject protection. Based on the Declaration of Helsinki. Compliance required by FDA, EMA, and most regulatory authorities.

title: ICH (International Council for Harmonisation)
content: **Related Terms:** guidelines, regulatory-harmonization, tripartite  Organization that develops guidelines for pharmaceutical development to harmonize regulatory requirements across regions (US, EU, Japan). Key guidelines: Q1 (stability), Q3 (impurities), Q7 (API manufacturing), S (safety), E (efficacy).

title: Pharmacovigilance
content: **Related Terms:** post-market-surveillance, adverse-event-reporting, safety-monitoring  The science and activities relating to detection, assessment, understanding, and prevention of adverse effects of pharmaceutical products. Encompasses pre- and post-marketing surveillance. Responsible for signal detection and risk management.

title: Post-Market Surveillance
content: **Related Terms:** pharmacovigilance, phase-iv, risk-management  Ongoing monitoring of a drug's safety and effectiveness after regulatory approval. Includes spontaneous reporting systems (MedWatch, EudraVigilance), registries, and required post-market studies. Can identify rare or long-term adverse effects not detected in clinical trials.

title: Adverse Event Reporting
content: **Related Terms:** pharmacovigilance, medwatch, spontaneous-reporting  The mandatory reporting of adverse events to regulatory authorities. Manufacturers must report serious and unexpected events within 15 days. Healthcare professionals and consumers can submit voluntary reports. Data used for signal detection and safety assessments.

title: Biomarker
content: **Related Terms:** surrogate-endpoint, diagnostic, companion-diagnostic  A defined characteristic measured as an indicator of normal biological processes, pathogenic processes, or pharmacological responses to therapy. Types: diagnostic, prognostic, predictive, pharmacodynamic. Can be molecules, genes, imaging features, or physiological measures.

title: Surrogate Endpoint
content: **Related Terms:** biomarker, clinical-endpoint, accelerated-approval  A laboratory measurement or physical sign used as a substitute for a clinical endpoint. Must be reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit based on epidemiological, therapeutic, pathophysiological, or other evidence. Enables smaller, shorter trials.

title: Clinical Endpoint
content: **Related Terms:** surrogate-endpoint, outcome, efficacy  A characteristic or variable that reflects how a patient feels, functions, or survives. Direct measure of clinical benefit. Examples: overall survival, progression-free survival, symptom severity. Gold standard for demonstrating efficacy but requires longer follow-up.

title: Hazard Ratio
content: **Related Terms:** survival-analysis, confidence-interval, cox-regression  The ratio of hazard rates between two groups at any given time. HR = 1 indicates no difference. HR < 1 favors treatment. HR > 1 favors control. Derived from Cox proportional hazards model. Reported with 95% confidence interval and p-value.

title: Odds Ratio
content: **Related Terms:** relative-risk, case-control, cross-sectional  The ratio of odds of an event occurring in the treatment group to the odds in the control group. Used in case-control studies where incidence cannot be calculated. OR ≈ 1 indicates no association. Interpretation differs from relative risk when events are common.

title: Relative Risk
content: **Related Terms:** odds-ratio, risk-ratio, cohort-study  The ratio of the probability of an event in the treatment group to the probability in the control group. RR = 1 indicates no difference. RR < 1 indicates reduced risk. RR > 1 indicates increased risk. Used in cohort studies and randomized controlled trials.

title: Meta-Analysis
content: **Related Terms:** systematic-review, forest-plot, heterogeneity  A statistical method that combines results from multiple independent studies to produce a single estimate of effect. Increases statistical power and precision. Requires assessment of heterogeneity (I² statistic). Results displayed in forest plots.

title: Systematic Review
content: **Related Terms:** meta-analysis, prisma, evidence-synthesis  A comprehensive, structured review of all relevant studies on a specific question using predefined methods. Follows PRISMA guidelines for reporting. Includes systematic search, quality assessment, and evidence synthesis. Foundation for evidence-based guidelines.

title: Evidence-Based Medicine
content: **Related Terms:** systematic-review, clinical-guideline, hierarchy-of-evidence  The integration of best research evidence with clinical expertise and patient values. Hierarchy: systematic reviews > RCTs > cohort studies > case-control > case reports > expert opinion. Clinical practice guidelines are developed from the best available evidence.

title: Odds Ratio (vs. Relative Risk)
content: **Related Terms:** relative-risk, epidemiology, study-design  A measure of association commonly used in case-control studies. When the outcome is rare (<10%), odds ratio approximates relative risk. When the outcome is common, odds ratio overestimates relative risk. Important for interpreting meta-analyses that combine different study designs.

title: Michaelis-Menten Kinetics
content: **Related Terms:** turnover-number, substrate-specificity, catalytic-efficiency  A model describing the rate of enzymatic reactions as a function of substrate concentration [S]. The Michaelis-Menten equation: v = (V_max × [S]) / (K_M + [S]), where v is the initial reaction velocity, V_max is the maximum velocity, and K_M is the Michaelis constant (the substrate concentration at which v = V_max/2). Assumes steady-state conditions where the rate of substrate conversion to product is constant after an initial transient phase.

title: Allosteric Regulation
content: **Related Terms:** cooperativity, binding-constant, catalytic-efficiency  Regulation of enzyme activity through binding of an effector molecule at a site distinct from the active site. Allosteric effectors induce conformational changes that alter catalytic activity. Positive allosteric modulators increase activity; negative allosteric modulators decrease activity. Described by the Monod-Wyman-Changeux (MWC) concerted model or the Koshland-Némethy-Filmer (KNF) sequential model.

title: Cooperativity
content: **Related Terms:** hill-coefficient, allosteric-regulation, michaelis-menten-kinetics  A phenomenon where binding of a ligand to one site on a macromolecule affects binding at other sites. Positive cooperativity increases subsequent binding affinity (Hill coefficient n > 1); negative cooperativity decreases it (n < 1). Described quantitatively by the Hill equation: Y = [L]^n / (K_d^n + [L]^n), where Y is fractional saturation, [L] is ligand concentration, and n is the Hill coefficient.

title: Substrate Specificity
content: **Related Terms:** catalytic-efficiency, michaelis-menten-kinetics, turnover-number  The ability of an enzyme to selectively catalyze a reaction with particular substrates. Determined by the geometric and electronic complementarity between the active site and substrate (lock-and-key or induced-fit models). Quantified by the specificity constant k_cat/K_M, where higher values indicate greater specificity. Enzymes may exhibit absolute specificity (one substrate), group specificity (a class of substrates), or stereochemical specificity (one enantiomer).

title: Catalytic Efficiency
content: **Related Terms:** turnover-number, michaelis-menten-kinetics, substrate-specificity  The ratio k_cat/K_M (units: M⁻¹s⁻¹), representing the efficiency of an enzyme in converting substrate to product. The upper limit is the diffusion-controlled rate (~10⁸–10⁹ M⁻¹s⁻¹). Enzymes operating near this limit are considered catalytically perfect. k_cat/K_M integrates both binding affinity (1/K_M) and catalytic rate (k_cat) into a single parameter.

title: Turnover Number
content: **Related Terms:** catalytic-efficiency, michaelis-menten-kinetics, substrate-specificity  The maximum number of substrate molecules converted to product per enzyme molecule per unit time under saturating substrate conditions. Symbol: k_cat. Units: s⁻¹. Calculated as k_cat = V_max / [E]_T, where [E]_T is the total enzyme concentration. Typical values range from 1 s⁻¹ (lysozyme) to 10⁷ s⁻¹ (carbonic anhydrase).

title: Binding Constant
content: **Related Terms:** dissociation-constant, association-constant, cooperativity  The equilibrium constant for the binding interaction between a macromolecule and a ligand. The association constant K_A = [ML]/([M][L]) (units: M⁻¹); the dissociation constant K_D = [M][L]/[ML] (units: M). K_D = 1/K_A. A smaller K_D indicates tighter binding. Related to free energy by ΔG° = RT ln(K_D).

title: Dissociation Constant
content: **Related Terms:** binding-constant, association-constant, cooperativity  The equilibrium constant K_D for the dissociation of a macromolecule-ligand complex ML into free macromolecule M and free ligand L. K_D = [M][L]/[ML] (units: M). At [L] = K_D, half of the macromolecule is bound (Y = 0.5). Experimentally determined by isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC), surface plasmon resonance (SPR), or fluorescence polarization.

title: Association Constant
content: **Related Terms:** binding-constant, dissociation-constant, cooperativity  The equilibrium constant K_A for the formation of a macromolecule-ligand complex ML from free macromolecule M and free ligand L. K_A = [ML]/([M][L]) (units: M⁻¹). K_A = 1/K_D. A larger K_A indicates tighter binding. Used in Scatchard analysis and binding isotherm fitting.

title: Hill Coefficient
content: **Related Terms:** cooperativity, allosteric-regulation, binding-constant  A parameter n in the Hill equation quantifying the degree of cooperativity in ligand binding. n = 1 indicates no cooperativity (hyperbolic binding); n > 1 indicates positive cooperativity (sigmoidal binding); n < 1 indicates negative cooperativity. The maximum Hill coefficient equals the number of binding sites. Experimentally determined from the slope of a Hill plot (log(Y/(1−Y)) vs log[L]) at Y = 0.5.

title: Ramachandran Plot
content: **Related Terms:** phi-angle, psi-angle, omega-angle  A plot of the backbone dihedral angles φ (phi) and ψ (psi) of amino acid residues in a protein structure. Regions of allowed conformational space are determined by steric clashes between backbone atoms. Most residues fall in favorable regions corresponding to α-helices (φ ≈ −60°, ψ ≈ −45°) and β-sheets (φ ≈ −120°, ψ ≈ 135°). Used to validate protein structures determined by X-ray crystallography or NMR.

title: Phi Angle
content: **Related Terms:** ramachandran-plot, psi-angle, omega-angle  The backbone dihedral angle φ defined by the atoms C(i-1)–N(i)–Cα(i)–C(i). Rotation about the N–Cα bond. One of the three backbone dihedral angles (φ, ψ, ω) that define the conformation of a polypeptide chain. Restricted to specific ranges in secondary structures: approximately −60° in α-helices and −120° in β-sheets.

title: Psi Angle
content: **Related Terms:** ramachandran-plot, phi-angle, omega-angle  The backbone dihedral angle ψ defined by the atoms N(i)–Cα(i)–C(i)–N(i+1). Rotation about the Cα–C bond. Together with φ, defines the backbone conformation at each residue. Approximately −45° in α-helices and 135° in β-sheets. The allowed regions of φ/ψ space are visualized on a Ramachandran plot.

title: Omega Angle
content: **Related Terms:** ramachandran-plot, phi-angle, psi-angle  The backbone dihedral angle ω defined by the atoms Cα(i-1)–C(i-1)–N(i)–Cα(i). Rotation about the peptide bond (C–N). Unlike φ and ψ, ω is constrained near 180° (trans, planar peptide bond) or 0° (cis, rare except before proline). Deviations from planarity indicate strained geometry. The partial double-bond character of the peptide bond restricts rotation.

title: Dihedral Angle
content: **Related Terms:** phi-angle, psi-angle, bond-angle  The angle between two planes formed by four consecutive atoms A–B–C–D. Measured as the angle between the plane A-B-C and the plane B-C-D. Ranges from −180° to +180°. In protein structures, the backbone dihedral angles φ, ψ, and ω define the polypeptide chain conformation. Side-chain dihedral angles (χ1, χ2, etc.) define rotamer states.

title: Bond Angle
content: **Related Terms:** bond-length, dihedral-angle, van-der-waals-radius  The angle formed between three bonded atoms A–B–C, measured at the central atom B. Determined by electron pair repulsion (VSEPR theory). Typical values: tetrahedral carbon 109.5°, trigonal planar carbon 120°, linear arrangement 180°. Deviations from ideal angles indicate strain or electronic effects. In proteins, backbone bond angles (N–Cα–C) are approximately 111°.

title: Bond Length
content: **Related Terms:** bond-angle, van-der-waals-radius, dihedral-angle  The equilibrium distance between the nuclei of two bonded atoms. Determined by the balance of attractive (bonding) and repulsive (electron-electron, nuclear-nuclear) forces. Typical values: C–C single bond 1.54 Å, C=C double bond 1.34 Å, C–N peptide bond 1.33 Å. Bond lengths are refined during structure determination and reported with estimated standard deviations.

title: Van der Waals Radius
content: **Related Terms:** bond-length, hydrogen-bond, salt-bridge  The effective radius of an atom in non-bonded interactions. Represents the distance at which attractive van der Waals forces balance repulsive electron cloud overlap. Values: hydrogen 1.20 Å, carbon 1.70 Å, nitrogen 1.55 Å, oxygen 1.52 Å. The sum of van der Waals radii defines the minimum approach distance between non-bonded atoms. Used in molecular modeling and steric clash analysis.

title: Hydrogen Bond
content: **Related Terms:** salt-bridge, disulfide-bond, van-der-waals-radius  A non-covalent interaction between a hydrogen atom bonded to an electronegative donor (D–H) and an electronegative acceptor (A). Typical geometry: D···A distance 2.5–3.5 Å, D–H···A angle 150–180°. Energy: 2–20 kJ/mol. In proteins, backbone N–H···O=C hydrogen bonds stabilize α-helices and β-sheets. Stronger than van der Waals interactions but weaker than covalent bonds.

title: Salt Bridge
